definition: A sentence is meaningful if and only if (i) it is grammatically well-formed, (ii) each of its constituent words has a definite sense, and (iii) the senses of the words combine to yield a determinate proposition.
conditions: grammatically well-formed
words have definite sense
senses combine to yield determinate proposition
counterexample: The sentence 'Colourless green ideas sleep furiously' is grammatically well-formed, each word has a definite sense, yet the senses do not combine to yield any determinate proposition. Nevertheless, the sentence is not without meaning: it is understood as absurd, and its absurdity is itself a meaningful property.
missing_condition: Meaningfulness may not require that the combined senses yield a determinate proposition; it may be sufficient that the sentence be interpretable as a linguistic act.
passage: A natural account of linguistic meaning proposes that a sentence is meaningful if and only if it is grammatically well-formed, each of its constituent words has a definite sense, and the senses of the words combine to yield a determinate proposition. Yet this analysis may be challenged. Consider the sentence 'Colourless green ideas sleep furiously.' It is grammatically impeccable, conforming to the syntactic rules of English. Each of its constituent words has a perfectly definite sense: we know what 'colourless' means, what 'green' means, what 'ideas' are, and so forth. But the senses do not combine to yield any determinate proposition — one cannot say what state of affairs would make the sentence true. The third condition fails. Yet we should hesitate to pronounce the sentence meaningless. It is understood by every competent speaker of English, and its absurdity is itself a meaningful property that distinguishes it from mere gibberish. The definition has drawn the boundary of meaningfulness too narrowly, excluding sentences which, though they express no determinate proposition, are nonetheless intelligible.

definition: A name refers to an object if and only if (i) the speaker intends to pick out that object, (ii) there is a causal-historical chain connecting the speaker's use of the name to the object, and (iii) the name is used in accordance with the conventions of the linguistic community.
conditions: speaker intends to pick out object
causal-historical chain to object
used according to community conventions
counterexample: A speaker uses the name 'Madagascar' intending to refer to the island, and the name is used in accordance with current convention, but the causal-historical chain traces back to a mainland region that bore the name originally. The reference succeeds despite the deviant causal chain.
missing_condition: Current communal convention may override the original causal-historical chain when the two diverge.
passage: A theory of reference might hold that a name refers to an object if and only if the speaker intends to pick out that object, there is a causal-historical chain connecting the speaker's use of the name to the object, and the name is used in accordance with the conventions of the linguistic community. This theory, though powerful, encounters a difficulty. Consider the name 'Madagascar.' When a European speaker today uses this name, he intends to refer to the large island off the south-eastern coast of Africa, and his usage accords with the conventions of his linguistic community. But historical evidence suggests that the name was originally applied by Marco Polo, or his sources, to a region of the African mainland, and was only later transferred to the island through a geographical error. The causal-historical chain thus connects the name not to the island but to the mainland. Yet the speaker's reference plainly succeeds — he refers to the island, not to the mainland. The definition has failed to account for the possibility that current convention may override the original causal-historical chain.

definition: A translation is adequate if and only if (i) it preserves the reference of the original, (ii) it preserves the sense, and (iii) it preserves the illocutionary force.
conditions: preserves reference
preserves sense
preserves illocutionary force
counterexample: A translation of a poem preserves the reference, sense, and illocutionary force of each sentence, yet loses the rhyme, metre, and phonetic texture that constitute much of the poem's literary value. The translation is adequate by the three conditions but inadequate as a translation of the poem.
missing_condition: For literary works, adequacy of translation requires preserving aesthetic and formal properties, not only semantic and pragmatic ones.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a translation is adequate if and only if it preserves the reference of the original, preserves the sense, and preserves the illocutionary force. By this account, a translation that refers to the same objects, expresses the same thoughts, and performs the same speech acts as the original is a fully adequate translation. Yet consider the translation of a poem. One may render each line into the target language in such a way that the reference is preserved — the same objects are mentioned — the sense is preserved — the same thoughts are expressed — and the illocutionary force is preserved — assertions remain assertions, questions remain questions. The three conditions are met. Yet if the translation has lost the rhyme, the metre, the assonance, and the rhythmic structure of the original, we should be reluctant to call it an adequate translation of the poem. For a poem is not merely a sequence of propositions; it is a verbal artefact whose meaning is inseparable from its form. The definition has failed to account for the aesthetic dimension of translation, which in literary contexts may be paramount.

definition: A proper name is a word that (i) denotes a particular individual, (ii) has no descriptive content, and (iii) is assigned by an act of baptism or convention.
conditions: denotes particular individual
no descriptive content
assigned by baptism or convention
counterexample: The name 'Dartmouth' denotes a particular town and was assigned by convention, but it has descriptive content: it describes the town's location at the mouth of the River Dart. The second condition fails, yet 'Dartmouth' is plainly a proper name.
missing_condition: Proper names may have etymological descriptive content without this content being part of their current semantic function.
passage: One might propose that a proper name is a word that denotes a particular individual, has no descriptive content, and is assigned by an act of baptism or convention. This analysis has the merit of distinguishing names from descriptions, but it proves too restrictive. Consider the name 'Dartmouth.' It denotes a particular town in Devon; it was assigned, one may suppose, by convention. But it plainly has descriptive content: it describes the town as being situated at the mouth of the River Dart. The second condition — that a proper name have no descriptive content — is violated. Yet no one would deny that 'Dartmouth' is a proper name. Indeed, the town might have moved away from the river's mouth, and the name would persist. The definition has confused two things: the etymological origin of a name, which may be descriptive, and its current semantic function, which may have nothing to do with that origin. A proper name may bear descriptive content on its surface while functioning, in use, as a non-descriptive tag.

definition: An utterance is metaphorical if and only if (i) the speaker says something literally false, (ii) the speaker intends the hearer to recognise the literal falsity, and (iii) the speaker intends to communicate a resemblance between the literal subject and the metaphorical vehicle.
conditions: literally false
speaker intends recognition of falsity
intends to communicate resemblance
counterexample: A man says 'No man is an island,' which is literally true (no man is, in fact, an island). Yet the utterance is plainly metaphorical. The first condition — literal falsity — fails.
missing_condition: Metaphor need not involve literal falsity; it may involve the application of a term or frame from one domain to another, regardless of truth-value.
passage: A familiar theory of metaphor holds that an utterance is metaphorical if and only if the speaker says something literally false, intends the hearer to recognise the literal falsity, and intends to communicate a resemblance between the literal subject and the metaphorical vehicle. This theory accounts well for cases such as 'Juliet is the sun,' which is literally false and communicates a resemblance between Juliet and the sun. But consider the utterance 'No man is an island.' This is literally true — no man is, in fact, a body of land surrounded by water. The first condition, literal falsity, is not satisfied. Yet the utterance is plainly metaphorical: it communicates something about human interconnection by invoking the image of an island's isolation. The definition has erred in requiring literal falsity as a necessary condition of metaphor. Metaphorical utterance may proceed by applying a frame or vocabulary from one domain to another, and this may result in statements that are literally true as well as literally false.

definition: A speaker implicates a proposition if and only if (i) he says something that, taken literally, does not express the proposition, (ii) the hearer is intended to infer the proposition from the saying, and (iii) the inference is recoverable by any competent speaker in the context.
conditions: literal content does not express the proposition
hearer intended to infer the proposition
inference recoverable by any competent speaker
counterexample: A man, asked whether he enjoyed his colleague's lecture, replies 'The weather was splendid today.' He does not literally say the lecture was poor; the hearer is intended to infer as much; and any competent speaker in the context would recover the implication. Yet suppose the speaker is a notorious eccentric whose conversational gambits are wholly unpredictable. One competent speaker might recover the intended inference, another might not — the third condition is met only for some hearers. Nevertheless, the implicature is present.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the dependence of implicature upon specific mutual assumptions between speaker and hearer, not merely upon general competence.
passage: A natural account of implicature proposes that a speaker implicates a proposition if and only if his literal utterance does not express that proposition, the hearer is intended to infer it from the saying, and the inference is recoverable by any competent speaker in the context. This analysis has considerable appeal, yet it may be shown to be too restrictive in one direction and too permissive in another. Consider a man who, asked at a dinner party whether he enjoyed his colleague's lecture, replies only 'The weather was splendid today.' He does not literally say the lecture was poor, yet the hearer is plainly intended to draw that inference. The third condition, however, proves troublesome. If the speaker is a notorious eccentric whose conversational habits are unpredictable, it may be that only those acquainted with his manner would recover the implication; a stranger, however competent in the language, might simply be puzzled. Yet the implicature is surely present — it does not vanish merely because some competent speakers fail to detect it. The definition has identified a general condition of recoverability where what is truly required is a more localised one: that the implicature be recoverable given the mutual assumptions operative between this speaker and this hearer in this context.

definition: A sentence presupposes a proposition if and only if (i) the truth of the proposition is taken for granted in the utterance, (ii) the negation of the sentence also takes the proposition for granted, and (iii) if the presupposed proposition is false, the sentence is neither true nor false.
conditions: truth of the proposition taken for granted
negation also takes proposition for granted
if presupposition false then sentence neither true nor false
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'The king of France is bald.' Its negation, 'The king of France is not bald,' equally takes for granted that there is a king of France. The first two conditions are satisfied. Yet one might argue, as Russell did, that the sentence is straightforwardly false when France has no king, rather than truth-valueless. The third condition fails on the Russellian analysis, though we should still wish to say that the sentence presupposes the existence of a king of France.
missing_condition: Presupposition may be a pragmatic phenomenon — a condition on the felicity of utterance — rather than a semantic one governing truth-value, and the definition conflates these two levels.
passage: It is commonly held that a sentence presupposes a proposition if and only if the truth of that proposition is taken for granted in the utterance, the negation of the sentence also takes it for granted, and if the presupposed proposition is false the sentence is neither true nor false. This third condition gives the analysis its semantic bite: it distinguishes presupposition from mere implication by tying it to truth-value gaps. Yet the condition is by no means beyond dispute. Consider the sentence 'The king of France is bald.' Both this sentence and its negation take for granted that France has a king; the first two conditions are satisfied. But when France has no king, is the sentence truth-valueless, or is it simply false? One influential analysis maintains that it is false, treating the existence claim not as a presupposition but as part of the asserted content. On this view the third condition fails, yet we should still wish to say that the sentence presupposes the existence of a king of France — the utterance would be infelicitous, pointless, if no king existed. The definition, it appears, has welded together a pragmatic phenomenon — the conditions under which an utterance is felicitous — with a semantic one — the conditions under which it has a truth-value — and these may come apart.

definition: An expression is indexical if and only if (i) its referent varies with the context of utterance, (ii) its linguistic meaning provides a rule for determining the referent from context, and (iii) competent speakers can identify the referent in any context of use.
conditions: referent varies with context of utterance
linguistic meaning provides rule for determining referent
competent speakers can identify referent in any context
counterexample: The word 'I' is paradigmatically indexical: its referent varies with the speaker, and its linguistic meaning — that it refers to the utterer — provides a clear rule. Yet consider the case of a parrot that has been trained to say 'I am hungry.' The referent of 'I,' according to the rule, should be the utterer — the parrot. But competent speakers cannot straightforwardly identify the referent: they may take it to refer to the parrot's owner, or to no one at all. The third condition fails, yet 'I' remains an indexical expression.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between the rule that fixes the referent and the practical ability to apply that rule, which may break down when the utterer is not a genuine language-user.
passage: One might propose that an expression is indexical if and only if its referent varies with the context of utterance, its linguistic meaning provides a rule for determining the referent from context, and competent speakers can identify the referent in any context of use. The first two conditions seem unimpeachable: 'I' refers to the speaker, 'here' to the place of utterance, and 'now' to the time of utterance, and these rules are part of the linguistic meaning of the expressions. The third condition, however, proves too strong. Consider a parrot trained to utter the sentence 'I am hungry.' The linguistic rule for 'I' dictates that it refers to the utterer. But who is the utterer? The parrot, one supposes — yet competent speakers who hear the parrot will not take 'I' to refer to the bird in the way they would take it to refer to a human speaker. They may suppose it refers to the parrot's owner, or to nobody at all, or they may simply decline to assign a referent. The third condition fails in this context, yet 'I' does not on that account cease to be an indexical expression. The definition has confused the rule that constitutes the indexical character of an expression with the practical conditions under which the rule can be straightforwardly applied.

definition: An utterance is performative if and only if (i) it accomplishes an act by being uttered, (ii) it cannot be assessed as true or false, and (iii) it employs a performative verb in the first person present indicative.
conditions: accomplishes an act by being uttered
cannot be assessed as true or false
employs performative verb in first person present indicative
counterexample: Suppose a man, upon being introduced to a stranger, simply says 'How do you do?' He accomplishes the act of greeting by uttering the words. The utterance cannot sensibly be assessed as true or false. Yet it employs no performative verb in the first person present indicative — there is no 'I greet you' or 'I hereby welcome you.' The third condition fails, yet the utterance is plainly performative.
missing_condition: Performative force may be carried by convention, intonation, or context, not only by the explicit presence of a performative verb; the definition mistakes one syntactic vehicle for the general phenomenon.
passage: It has been proposed that an utterance is performative if and only if it accomplishes an act by being uttered, it cannot be assessed as true or false, and it employs a performative verb in the first person present indicative. This definition fits well the paradigm cases: 'I promise to return the book,' 'I hereby name this ship the Queen Elizabeth,' 'I apologise for the delay.' In each instance, a performative verb in the first person present indicative appears, and the utterance does something rather than merely describing something. Yet the definition is too narrow. Consider the utterance 'How do you do?' spoken upon being introduced to a stranger. It accomplishes the act of greeting; it cannot be assessed as true or false; but it contains no performative verb whatever. There is no 'I greet you' lurking within it. Similarly, a chairman who simply says 'Next item' thereby moves the meeting forward — an act accomplished by utterance, truth-valueless, yet devoid of any explicit performative verb. The definition has elevated one syntactic form to the status of a necessary condition when in truth performative force may be conveyed by convention, by tone, or by the circumstances of utterance alone.

definition: Two expressions are synonymous if and only if (i) they can be substituted for one another in all contexts without change of truth-value, (ii) a competent speaker would assent to 'A means the same as B,' and (iii) no context exists in which one is appropriate and the other is not.
conditions: substitutable in all contexts without change of truth-value
competent speaker assents to identity of meaning
no context where one appropriate and other not
counterexample: Consider 'ophthalmologist' and 'eye doctor.' A competent speaker would assent to their meaning the same thing. They can be substituted in most contexts without change of truth-value. Yet in a legal or medical document, 'ophthalmologist' is appropriate whilst 'eye doctor' may not be — the third condition fails. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to deny that the expressions are synonymous.
missing_condition: The definition conflates semantic synonymy with pragmatic interchangeability; two expressions may share their meaning whilst differing in register, formality, or connotation.
passage: One might hold that two expressions are synonymous if and only if they can be substituted for one another in all contexts without change of truth-value, a competent speaker would assent to the claim that they mean the same thing, and no context exists in which one is appropriate and the other is not. This definition, though apparently comprehensive, proves too demanding. Consider the expressions 'ophthalmologist' and 'eye doctor.' Any competent speaker of English would agree that they mean the same thing: they apply to the same class of medical practitioners. In any factual statement about such practitioners, one may be substituted for the other without altering the truth-value of the sentence. The first two conditions are satisfied. Yet the third condition fails, for there are contexts — a formal medical report, a legal deposition, a university examination — in which 'ophthalmologist' is appropriate and 'eye doctor' is not, the latter being too colloquial for the occasion. We should not, however, wish to conclude that the expressions are not synonymous. The definition has confused identity of meaning with identity of use, and these are distinct: two expressions may denote the same thing and carry the same sense whilst differing in the register or formality of their employment.

definition: A statement is analytic if and only if (i) it is true, (ii) its truth is determined solely by the meanings of its constituent terms, and (iii) its denial entails a contradiction.
conditions: the statement is true
truth determined solely by meanings of terms
denial entails contradiction
counterexample: Consider the statement 'All bachelors are unmarried.' It is true; its truth depends upon the meanings of 'bachelor' and 'unmarried'; and its denial seems to entail a contradiction. Now consider 'Nothing is both red and green all over at the same time.' This too appears true and its denial contradictory. Yet whether its truth is determined solely by the meanings of 'red' and 'green' or by some extra-linguistic fact about the incompatibility of colours is a matter of considerable dispute. The second condition is indeterminate.
missing_condition: The definition presupposes a sharp boundary between meaning and fact, between the linguistic and the empirical, which may not obtain; analyticity requires a clear criterion for 'determined by meaning alone' that the definition does not provide.
passage: It is often said that a statement is analytic if and only if it is true, its truth is determined solely by the meanings of its constituent terms, and its denial entails a contradiction. This tripartite definition captures the standard cases admirably: 'All bachelors are unmarried' is the paradigm. It is true, its truth rests upon the meanings of 'bachelor' and 'unmarried,' and to deny it would be to assert that some unmarried man is married, which is contradictory. Yet consider the statement 'Nothing is both red and green all over at the same time.' Its truth seems indisputable, and its denial does appear to involve some form of contradiction. But whether the truth of this statement is determined solely by the meanings of 'red' and 'green' is far from obvious. One might argue that it rests upon a fact about colour — an empirical incompatibility rather than a semantic one. If so, the second condition is not clearly satisfied, and the statement occupies an uncomfortable middle position: apparently necessary, yet not obviously analytic. The definition has assumed that there is a sharp and principled distinction between truths that depend upon meaning and truths that depend upon fact, and this assumption has itself been powerfully challenged.

definition: A definition is adequate if and only if (i) the definiens and definiendum are co-extensional, (ii) the definiens does not employ the definiendum or any synonym of it, and (iii) the definiens is shorter or simpler than a mere enumeration of instances.
conditions: definiens and definiendum co-extensional
definiens does not employ definiendum or synonym
definiens shorter or simpler than enumeration
counterexample: Define 'creature with a heart' as 'creature with kidneys.' The two expressions are co-extensional: every creature with a heart has kidneys, and vice versa. The definiens does not employ the definiendum or a synonym, and it is simpler than a list of all hearted creatures. All three conditions are met. Yet the definition is plainly inadequate, for having a heart and having kidneys are distinct properties that merely happen to coincide.
missing_condition: Co-extensionality is necessary but not sufficient; an adequate definition must capture an intensional or explanatory connection between definiens and definiendum, not merely an accidental coincidence of extension.
passage: One might propose that a definition is adequate if and only if the definiens and the definiendum are co-extensional — that is, they apply to precisely the same things — the definiens does not employ the definiendum or any synonym of it, and the definiens is shorter or simpler than a mere enumeration of instances. These conditions seem reasonable enough. Yet they are met by definitions that we should on reflection reject. Consider the proposal to define 'creature with a heart' as 'creature with kidneys.' As a matter of biological fact, every creature that possesses a heart also possesses kidneys, and conversely. The two expressions are thus co-extensional. The definiens 'creature with kidneys' does not employ the definiendum 'creature with a heart,' nor is it a synonym. And it is certainly simpler than listing all hearted creatures individually. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet the definition is plainly inadequate, for having a heart and having kidneys are different properties that happen, by a contingency of evolutionary biology, to be found together. To define one by the other is to mistake coincidence for conceptual connection. The definition has required only extensional equivalence where what is truly needed is some stronger, intensional relation.

definition: A sentence is ambiguous if and only if (i) it has more than one possible interpretation, (ii) the distinct interpretations arise from the linguistic form of the sentence, and (iii) no contextual information suffices to determine which interpretation is intended.
conditions: more than one possible interpretation
distinct interpretations arise from linguistic form
no context suffices to determine intended interpretation
counterexample: The sentence 'Visiting relatives can be tiresome' has two interpretations arising from its grammatical structure: relatives who visit, or the act of visiting relatives. In most contexts of utterance, however, the intended reading is perfectly clear. The third condition fails — context does disambiguate — yet the sentence remains ambiguous. Ambiguity is a property of the sentence, not of the utterance.
missing_condition: The definition conflates sentence-ambiguity with utterance-ambiguity; a sentence may be structurally ambiguous even when every particular utterance of it is contextually resolved.
passage: A natural definition holds that a sentence is ambiguous if and only if it has more than one possible interpretation, the distinct interpretations arise from the linguistic form of the sentence, and no contextual information suffices to determine which interpretation is intended. The first two conditions seem correct: ambiguity is a matter of multiple interpretations generated by linguistic structure, whether lexical or syntactic. Yet the third condition proves too strong. Consider the sentence 'Visiting relatives can be tiresome.' It admits of two readings: one in which the relatives are doing the visiting, and another in which someone is visiting the relatives. The distinct interpretations arise from the grammatical structure of the sentence; the first two conditions are satisfied. But in nearly every actual context of utterance, the intended reading is perfectly clear — the preceding conversation, the tone, the occasion will determine which is meant. The third condition fails: context does suffice to determine the intended interpretation. Yet we should not wish to say that the sentence is therefore unambiguous. It is ambiguous as a sentence, though not as an utterance. The definition has failed to distinguish between these two levels, requiring irresolvability where all that is needed is multiplicity of structural interpretation.

definition: An expression is context-dependent if and only if (i) its semantic content varies across contexts of use, (ii) the variation is systematic and governed by rule, and (iii) the expression belongs to a recognised class of context-sensitive terms such as indexicals, demonstratives, or tense markers.
conditions: semantic content varies across contexts
variation systematic and governed by rule
belongs to recognised class of context-sensitive terms
counterexample: Consider the adjective 'tall.' A man described as 'tall' in a conversation about jockeys might be five feet eight inches; the same man described as 'tall' in a conversation about basketball players would not be so considered. The semantic content of 'tall' varies with context, and the variation is systematic — it depends upon the comparison class. Yet 'tall' does not belong to any of the recognised classes of context-sensitive terms; it is an ordinary adjective. The third condition fails, yet the expression is plainly context-dependent.
missing_condition: Context-dependence is not confined to a closed class of linguistic expressions; gradable adjectives, quantifier domains, and many other ordinary expressions exhibit systematic context-sensitivity that falls outside the traditional categories.
passage: One might define an expression as context-dependent if and only if its semantic content varies across contexts of use, the variation is systematic and governed by rule, and the expression belongs to a recognised class of context-sensitive terms such as indexicals, demonstratives, or tense markers. This definition captures the familiar cases — 'I,' 'here,' 'now,' 'that' — but it is too narrow. Consider the adjective 'tall.' When one says 'John is tall' in a conversation about jockeys, one means something different from what one means by the same words in a conversation about basketball players. The content of 'tall' varies with the context, specifically with the comparison class that the context makes salient, and this variation is entirely systematic. The first two conditions are satisfied. Yet 'tall' is not an indexical, not a demonstrative, and not a tense marker; it is an ordinary gradable adjective. The third condition fails. The definition has assumed that context-dependence is a property of a closed and enumerable class of expressions, when in fact it pervades the language far more widely. Gradable adjectives, quantifier expressions, and many other apparently simple terms exhibit systematic sensitivity to context that no list of categories can exhaust.

definition: Two sentences express the same propositional attitude if and only if (i) they attribute the same psychological relation to the subject, (ii) the embedded proposition is the same in both, and (iii) substituting co-referential terms in the embedded clause preserves the truth of the attitude report.
conditions: same psychological relation attributed
same embedded proposition
co-referential substitution preserves truth
counterexample: Consider 'Lois Lane believes that Superman can fly' and 'Lois Lane believes that Clark Kent can fly.' The psychological relation is the same — believing. The embedded propositions concern the same individual, since Superman is Clark Kent, and so by one reckoning the proposition is the same. Yet the substitution of 'Clark Kent' for 'Superman' transforms a true report into a false one: Lois does not believe that Clark Kent can fly. The third condition fails despite the co-referentiality of the terms.
missing_condition: The definition neglects the role of the mode of presentation under which the subject apprehends the object; co-referential terms may present the same object differently, and attitude reports are sensitive to this difference.
passage: It is sometimes maintained that two sentences express the same propositional attitude if and only if they attribute the same psychological relation to the subject, the embedded proposition is the same in both, and substituting co-referential terms in the embedded clause preserves the truth of the attitude report. This third condition is meant to ensure that the identity of the proposition is robust. Yet it is precisely this condition that fails in a well-known class of cases. Consider the sentences 'Lois Lane believes that Superman can fly' and 'Lois Lane believes that Clark Kent can fly.' The psychological relation is the same in both cases — viz., believing. And since Superman is Clark Kent, one might hold that the embedded proposition is the same: the proposition that a certain individual can fly. But the substitution of the co-referential term 'Clark Kent' for 'Superman' transforms a true attitude report into a false one, for Lois Lane does not believe that the bespectacled reporter can fly. The third condition fails. The definition has treated propositions as individuated solely by their objects and properties, neglecting the mode of presentation under which the subject apprehends the object — a difference to which attitude reports are acutely sensitive.

definition: An expression is used in quotation if and only if (i) it is mentioned rather than used, (ii) it is enclosed in quotation marks, and (iii) it refers to the expression itself rather than to what the expression ordinarily denotes.
conditions: expression mentioned rather than used
enclosed in quotation marks
refers to expression itself rather than ordinary denotation
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'He said he was feeling "fine."' The word 'fine' is enclosed in quotation marks and might appear to be mentioned rather than used. Yet the speaker is not referring to the word 'fine' itself; he is using the quotation marks to signal irony or distance — to indicate that 'fine' is the other person's word, whilst reserving judgement about its aptness. The third condition fails: the expression still denotes its ordinary meaning, albeit with an added layer of attitude.
missing_condition: The definition does not accommodate scare quotes or distancing quotation, in which quotation marks signal attitudinal reserve rather than a shift from use to mention.
passage: One might suppose that an expression is used in quotation if and only if it is mentioned rather than used, it is enclosed in quotation marks, and it refers to the expression itself rather than to what the expression ordinarily denotes. This analysis captures what logicians call pure quotation: when we say that 'cat' has three letters, we mention the word rather than using it, and the quotation marks signal this shift. Yet the definition is too narrow. Consider the sentence 'He said he was feeling "fine."' The word 'fine' is enclosed in quotation marks, and one might think it is thereby mentioned. But in fact the speaker is not referring to the word 'fine' as a linguistic object; he is using it to report what another person said, whilst simultaneously distancing himself from the word — suggesting, perhaps, that the person was not fine at all. The expression retains its ordinary denotation; the quotation marks serve not to effect a shift from use to mention but to signal an attitude of irony or scepticism. The third condition fails, yet quotation marks are undeniably in play. The definition has identified one function of quotation marks — the logical function of mention — whilst overlooking the pragmatic functions of distancing, irony, and attitudinal reserve.

definition: An utterance is ironic if and only if (i) the speaker says the opposite of what he means, (ii) the speaker intends the hearer to recognise that the opposite is meant, and (iii) the intended meaning is conveyed by the manifest disparity between what is said and what is the case.
conditions: speaker says opposite of what he means
speaker intends recognition of opposite meaning
meaning conveyed by disparity between saying and fact
counterexample: A man arrives at work drenched from the rain and says 'Lovely weather we are having.' He does not strictly say the opposite of what he means — 'lovely' is not the logical opposite of any particular weather-description. Rather, he says something inappropriate to the situation. The first condition, strictly construed, is not satisfied; yet the utterance is unmistakably ironic.
missing_condition: Irony does not require strict logical opposition between what is said and what is meant; it requires only a recognisable incongruity or inappropriateness, of which opposition is merely one species.
passage: A standard account of irony holds that an utterance is ironic if and only if the speaker says the opposite of what he means, intends the hearer to recognise that the opposite is meant, and conveys his intended meaning by the manifest disparity between what is said and what is the case. This analysis has the virtue of simplicity, but it rests upon the notion of 'saying the opposite,' which proves less clear than one might suppose. Consider a man who arrives at the office thoroughly drenched from a violent downpour and says 'Lovely weather we are having.' The utterance is plainly ironic. Yet in what sense does 'lovely' say the opposite of what the man means? He means something like 'the weather is dreadful,' but 'lovely' is not the logical contradictory of 'dreadful' — it is merely inappropriate to the circumstances. If we insist upon strict opposition, the first condition is not satisfied; the utterance says something false and incongruous, but not the precise opposite of the intended meaning. The definition has mistaken one form of incongruity — logical opposition — for the general mechanism of irony, which may equally proceed by understatement, overstatement, or the application of an expression that is merely unsuitable to the occasion.

definition: A language is compositional if and only if (i) the meaning of every complex expression is determined by the meanings of its parts, (ii) the mode of combination is syntactic, and (iii) no complex expression has a meaning that cannot be derived from its parts and their arrangement.
conditions: meaning of complex expression determined by meanings of parts
mode of combination is syntactic
no complex expression has underivable meaning
counterexample: The idiom 'kick the bucket,' meaning to die, is a complex expression whose meaning cannot be derived from the meanings of 'kick,' 'the,' and 'bucket' together with their syntactic arrangement. The third condition fails, yet the idiom is a legitimate expression of the language and is understood by competent speakers.
missing_condition: The definition requires strict compositionality for every expression, but natural languages contain idioms, fixed phrases, and conventionalised collocations whose meanings are not compositionally derivable, and the principle of compositionality must be understood as holding for the productive core of the language rather than for every expression without exception.
passage: A standard formulation of the principle of compositionality holds that a language is compositional if and only if the meaning of every complex expression is determined by the meanings of its parts, the mode of combination is syntactic, and no complex expression has a meaning that cannot be derived from its parts and their arrangement. This principle, often attributed to Frege, explains how speakers can understand sentences they have never encountered before: they know the meanings of the words and the rules of combination, and this suffices. Yet natural languages furnish abundant counterexamples. Consider the English idiom 'kick the bucket.' Syntactically, it is a verb phrase consisting of a verb and a direct object. The meaning of 'kick' is known, and the meaning of 'the bucket' is known. But combining them yields 'to strike the pail with one's foot,' not 'to die,' which is what the idiom means. The third condition fails. The meaning of the whole is not derivable from the meanings of the parts and their syntactic arrangement. The definition has stated the principle of compositionality as an exceptionless law, when in practice it holds for the productive, rule-governed core of the language but admits of systematic exceptions in the domain of idiom, collocation, and conventionalised metaphor.

definition: To know the meaning of a sentence is to know its truth-conditions if and only if (i) every meaningful sentence has determinate truth-conditions, (ii) the truth-conditions are specifiable independently of the context of utterance, and (iii) understanding a sentence consists in the ability to state the conditions under which it would be true.
conditions: every meaningful sentence has determinate truth-conditions
truth-conditions specifiable independently of context
understanding consists in ability to state truth-conditions
counterexample: The sentence 'It is raining' is perfectly meaningful, yet its truth-conditions cannot be specified independently of context: one must know when and where the sentence is uttered in order to determine what state of affairs would make it true. The second condition fails, for the truth-conditions of the sentence shift with every utterance.
missing_condition: The definition requires that truth-conditions be context-independent, but a vast class of meaningful sentences — those containing demonstratives, tense markers, and other context-sensitive elements — have truth-conditions that depend essentially upon the circumstances of utterance.
passage: A familiar thesis in the philosophy of language holds that to know the meaning of a sentence is to know its truth-conditions, and this is sometimes elaborated as follows: every meaningful sentence has determinate truth-conditions, the truth-conditions are specifiable independently of the context of utterance, and understanding a sentence consists in the ability to state the conditions under which it would be true. This account works well for sentences of mathematics and logic, whose truth-conditions do not shift with the circumstances of their assertion. Yet it encounters a pervasive difficulty with ordinary language. Consider the sentence 'It is raining.' It is a perfectly meaningful sentence, understood by every competent speaker of English. But its truth-conditions cannot be specified without reference to context: it is raining where? and when? An utterance of the sentence in London on a wet Tuesday has different truth-conditions from an utterance in Cairo on a dry Thursday. The second condition fails. The truth-conditions are not fixed by the sentence alone but depend upon the time and place of utterance. The definition has required that meaning be wholly intrinsic to the sentence, when in practice a great deal of the meaning of ordinary sentences is completed by the context in which they are used.

definition: The logical form of a sentence is its real structure if and only if (i) the logical form is revealed by translating the sentence into a formal language, (ii) the formal translation preserves all and only the inferential relations of the original, and (iii) each sentence has exactly one logical form.
conditions: logical form revealed by translation into formal language
translation preserves all and only inferential relations
each sentence has exactly one logical form
counterexample: The sentence 'Every man loves some woman' has two readings: one in which there is a single woman loved by all men, and another in which each man loves a possibly different woman. Each reading has a distinct logical form. The third condition fails, since the sentence does not have exactly one logical form but at least two, corresponding to the two scopal orderings of the quantifiers.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that logical form is unique, but structurally ambiguous sentences admit of multiple logical forms corresponding to different readings, and the disambiguation is a matter of pragmatic context rather than of syntax alone.
passage: It is natural to suppose that the logical form of a sentence is its real structure, revealed by translating the sentence into a formal language, where the translation preserves all and only the inferential relations of the original, and each sentence has exactly one logical form. This account captures the motivation behind the project of logical analysis: to strip away the misleading surface grammar of natural language and exhibit the underlying structure that governs inference. Yet the third condition encounters a familiar difficulty. Consider the sentence 'Every man loves some woman.' Uttered in one context, it might mean that there is a particular woman — perhaps a film star — whom every man loves. Uttered in another, it might mean merely that each man loves at least one woman, possibly a different one in each case. These two readings correspond to two distinct logical forms, distinguished by the relative scope of the universal and existential quantifiers. The sentence does not have exactly one logical form; it has at least two. The third condition fails. The definition has presupposed a one-to-one correspondence between sentences and logical forms, when in practice natural-language sentences are frequently ambiguous in ways that yield multiple distinct logical representations.

definition: A speech act is an assertion if and only if (i) the speaker utters a declarative sentence, (ii) the speaker intends the hearer to accept the proposition expressed, and (iii) the speaker presents the proposition as true.
conditions: speaker utters declarative sentence
speaker intends hearer to accept proposition
speaker presents proposition as true
counterexample: A witness in court who says 'I do solemnly swear that the defendant was at home that evening' utters a declarative sentence, intends the court to accept the proposition, and presents it as true. Yet if the witness is lying — and knows he is lying — he presents the proposition as true without himself believing it. The three conditions are all satisfied, yet the speech act fails to be a genuine assertion in the fullest sense, for it lacks the sincerity that is normally taken to be a constitutive norm of assertion.
missing_condition: The definition captures the external form of assertion but omits the sincerity condition: that a genuine assertion requires the speaker to believe the proposition he presents as true, not merely to present it as such.
passage: A plausible account holds that a speech act is an assertion if and only if the speaker utters a declarative sentence, intends the hearer to accept the proposition expressed, and presents the proposition as true. This account captures the typical case of assertion: the speaker says that P, he wants his hearer to believe that P, and he puts forward P as something that is the case. Yet the definition admits of a troublesome case. Consider a witness in a court of law who utters the sentence 'The defendant was at home that evening.' The witness speaks in declarative form, he intends the court to accept what he says, and he presents the proposition as true. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the witness is lying: he knows perfectly well that the defendant was not at home. He presents the proposition as true while himself believing it to be false. The definition counts his utterance as an assertion, and in one sense it is — it has the form and force of an assertion. But it violates a norm that many philosophers regard as constitutive of the practice of asserting, namely the requirement of sincerity. The definition has specified what an assertion looks like from the outside but has omitted the internal condition that the speaker must believe what he asserts.

definition: The negation of a proposition is the proposition that is true when and only when the original is false, such that (i) every proposition has exactly one negation, (ii) double negation restores the original proposition, and (iii) a proposition and its negation are jointly exhaustive and mutually exclusive.
conditions: every proposition has exactly one negation
double negation restores original
proposition and negation jointly exhaustive and mutually exclusive
counterexample: In intuitionistic logic, double negation does not restore the original proposition. The double negation of P — it is not the case that it is not the case that P — is weaker than P itself: one cannot in general infer P from not-not-P. The second condition fails within this logical framework. Yet intuitionistic negation is a perfectly coherent and well-studied form of negation, employed in constructive mathematics.
missing_condition: The definition assumes classical logic, in which double negation elimination holds, but in intuitionistic and other non-classical logics, negation behaves differently, and the principle of double negation elimination is not valid.
passage: A standard account holds that the negation of a proposition is the proposition that is true when and only when the original is false, and that every proposition has exactly one negation, double negation restores the original proposition, and a proposition and its negation are jointly exhaustive and mutually exclusive. This account describes negation as it behaves in classical logic, and within that framework it is entirely correct. Yet logic is not monolithic. In intuitionistic logic — developed by Brouwer and formalised by Heyting — double negation does not restore the original proposition. The intuitionistic logician accepts that from P one may infer not-not-P, but he denies the converse: from not-not-P one cannot in general infer P. The second condition fails. The intuitionist regards a proposition as established only when a constructive proof has been given, and the mere refutation of the negation does not constitute such a proof. Yet intuitionistic negation is a coherent, well-motivated, and widely studied notion, employed throughout constructive mathematics. The definition has identified negation with its classical behaviour, excluding the non-classical negations that are equally legitimate within their own logical frameworks.

definition: A sentence expresses a modal claim if and only if (i) it contains an explicit modal operator such as 'necessarily' or 'possibly,' (ii) its truth-value differs from that of the unmodalized sentence, and (iii) the modal operator governs the entire sentence.
conditions: contains explicit modal operator
truth-value differs from unmodalized sentence
modal operator governs entire sentence
counterexample: The sentence 'A bachelor is an unmarried man' expresses a necessary truth, yet it contains no explicit modal operator: there is no 'necessarily' in the sentence. The first condition fails. The modality is conveyed by the semantic content — the analytic connection between 'bachelor' and 'unmarried man' — rather than by any overt modal word.
missing_condition: The definition requires an explicit modal operator, but modal force may be conveyed implicitly through analyticity, semantic relations, or the nature of the predication, without any overt modal vocabulary.
passage: A plausible account holds that a sentence expresses a modal claim if and only if it contains an explicit modal operator such as 'necessarily' or 'possibly,' its truth-value differs from that of the unmodalized sentence, and the modal operator governs the entire sentence. This account identifies modal content with the presence of modal vocabulary: one looks for the words 'necessarily,' 'possibly,' 'must,' 'might,' and when they appear one knows that a modal claim is being made. Yet modal content is not always signalled by overt vocabulary. Consider the sentence 'A bachelor is an unmarried man.' It contains no modal operator. The sentence is in the simple present indicative. But it expresses a necessary truth: in every possible circumstance, a bachelor is an unmarried man. The necessity is conveyed not by an explicit modal word but by the semantic relation between 'bachelor' and 'unmarried man' — a relation of synonymy or definitional equivalence that holds of necessity. The first condition fails. The definition has looked for modality in the surface vocabulary of the sentence and has missed the modality that resides in the semantic content, where analyticity and definition do the work that overt modal operators do elsewhere.

definition: A linguistic context is intensional if and only if (i) substitution of co-referential expressions may alter the truth-value of the containing sentence, (ii) the context is created by a psychological verb such as 'believes' or 'desires,' and (iii) the expressions involved are singular terms.
conditions: substitution of co-referential expressions may alter truth-value
context created by psychological verb
expressions involved are singular terms
counterexample: Modal contexts are intensional without involving any psychological verb. The sentence 'Necessarily, nine is greater than seven' is true, but 'Necessarily, the number of planets is greater than seven' may be false, even though nine is the number of planets. Substitution of co-referential terms alters truth-value, yet the context is modal rather than psychological. The second condition fails.
missing_condition: The definition restricts intensional contexts to those created by psychological verbs, but modal operators, counterfactual conditionals, and temporal operators also create intensional contexts in which co-referential substitution may fail.
passage: A natural account holds that a linguistic context is intensional if and only if substitution of co-referential expressions may alter the truth-value of the containing sentence, the context is created by a psychological verb such as 'believes' or 'desires,' and the expressions involved are singular terms. This account captures the paradigmatic cases: 'John believes that Hesperus is bright' may differ in truth-value from 'John believes that Phosphorus is bright,' even though Hesperus and Phosphorus are the same celestial body. Yet the definition is too narrow. Consider the modal sentence 'Necessarily, nine is greater than seven.' This is true: nine is necessarily greater than seven. Now consider 'Necessarily, the number of planets is greater than seven.' This is false, for the number of planets is a contingent matter. Yet nine is the number of planets. The substitution of co-referential terms has altered the truth-value of the sentence, and the context is intensional. But the intensionality is created by the modal operator 'necessarily,' not by any psychological verb. The second condition fails. The definition has identified intensionality with the behaviour of belief-contexts and desire-contexts, overlooking the modal, counterfactual, and temporal contexts that exhibit precisely the same logical feature.

definition: The conventional meaning of an expression is fixed if and only if (i) the meaning is established by explicit agreement among speakers, (ii) the agreement can be identified at a determinate point in the history of the language, and (iii) the meaning does not change once the convention is established.
conditions: meaning established by explicit agreement
agreement identifiable at determinate historical point
meaning does not change once convention established
counterexample: The English word 'nice' once meant foolish or ignorant (from the Latin nescius). Its current meaning of pleasant or agreeable emerged gradually over several centuries through patterns of use, not through any explicit agreement among speakers. The first and second conditions both fail: there was no identifiable moment of convention-setting and no explicit agreement. Yet the current conventional meaning of 'nice' is perfectly determinate.
missing_condition: The definition models linguistic convention upon deliberate stipulation, but the conventional meanings of most words arise through gradual, unplanned shifts in usage rather than through any identifiable act of collective agreement.
passage: One might hold that the conventional meaning of an expression is fixed if and only if the meaning is established by explicit agreement among speakers, the agreement can be identified at a determinate point in the history of the language, and the meaning does not change once the convention is established. This account treats linguistic meaning as something like a social contract: the speakers come together, agree on what their words shall mean, and the matter is settled. Yet the history of language tells a very different story. Consider the English word 'nice.' In the fourteenth century it meant foolish or ignorant, reflecting its Latin origin in nescius, meaning not knowing. Over the subsequent centuries it shifted through a series of senses — precise, delicate, refined — until by the eighteenth century it had acquired its present meaning of pleasant or agreeable. At no point was there an explicit agreement among speakers to change the word's meaning. The shift occurred gradually, through patterns of usage that no one planned or directed. The first and second conditions both fail. Yet the current conventional meaning of 'nice' is as determinate as that of any word in the language. The definition has confused convention with stipulation, when in truth the conventions of a living language are established and altered by the inarticulate, collective practice of its speakers.

definition: A sign has natural meaning if and only if (i) there is a causal connection between the sign and what it signifies, (ii) the connection holds independently of human intention, and (iii) the sign is a physical event or state.
conditions: causal connection between sign and signified
connection holds independently of human intention
sign is physical event or state
counterexample: Tree rings signify the age of a tree. Each ring corresponds to a year of growth. The rings are a physical state, the connection is independent of human intention, and there is a causal relation between the passage of seasons and the formation of rings. Yet the rings also carry information about climatic conditions — a wide ring signifies a favourable growing season — and this meaning is not simply causal but involves a complex correlation between multiple environmental variables. The sign means more than a single causal chain accounts for.
missing_condition: The definition reduces natural meaning to a simple causal connection, but natural signs often carry information through complex correlations with multiple factors, and their meaning may be richer than any single causal pathway would explain.
passage: A familiar account holds that a sign has natural meaning if and only if there is a causal connection between the sign and what it signifies, the connection holds independently of human intention, and the sign is a physical event or state. Smoke means fire, and the meaning rests upon a causal connection: fire produces smoke. This account captures the standard cases. Yet the simplicity of the causal model proves misleading when applied to more complex natural signs. Consider the rings of a tree. A cross-section of the trunk reveals concentric rings, each corresponding to a year of growth. The rings naturally mean the tree's age, and the causal connection is straightforward. But the rings also signify the climatic conditions of each growing season: a wide ring indicates ample rainfall and warmth; a narrow ring indicates drought or cold. The ring width correlates with temperature, precipitation, soil conditions, and the presence or absence of competing vegetation. The natural meaning of the ring pattern is not exhausted by a single causal connection but involves a complex web of correlational information. The definition, which requires a causal connection in the singular, understates the richness of natural meaning, which often depends upon the sign's correlation with multiple factors rather than upon a single causal chain.

definition: A noun phrase bears a semantic role if and only if (i) it stands in a definite thematic relation to the verb, (ii) each noun phrase bears exactly one role, and (iii) each role is filled by exactly one noun phrase.
conditions: stands in definite thematic relation to verb
each noun phrase bears exactly one role
each role filled by exactly one noun phrase
counterexample: In the sentence 'John rolled the ball down the hill,' John is both the agent (the initiator of the action) and, in a secondary sense, the cause of the ball's motion. Depending on the analysis, John may bear two thematic roles simultaneously. The second condition, requiring that each noun phrase bear exactly one role, fails.
missing_condition: The definition assumes a strict one-to-one mapping between noun phrases and roles, but certain constructions allow a single noun phrase to bear multiple thematic roles, and the mapping between syntactic positions and semantic roles is more flexible than the definition permits.
passage: A standard account in the philosophy of language and in linguistics holds that a noun phrase bears a semantic role if and only if it stands in a definite thematic relation to the verb, each noun phrase bears exactly one role, and each role is filled by exactly one noun phrase. This account, inspired by the theta-criterion in certain syntactic theories, provides a tidy picture: each participant in the event described by the verb occupies one and only one thematic slot. Yet the picture proves too rigid. Consider the sentence 'John rolled the ball down the hill.' John is the agent of the rolling — he is the one who initiates and controls the action. But John is also, in an important sense, the instrument or cause of the ball's change of location. On a careful analysis, John bears two thematic relations to the verb: that of agent and that of cause. The second condition, which requires that each noun phrase bear exactly one semantic role, fails. The definition has imposed a strict one-to-one correspondence between noun phrases and roles that natural language does not always observe, for the relationships between participants and actions are often richer and more layered than a single thematic label can capture.

definition: A term denotes an object if and only if (i) the term picks out exactly one object in the domain, (ii) the object exists, and (iii) the denotation is fixed by the descriptive content associated with the term.
conditions: term picks out exactly one object
object exists
denotation fixed by descriptive content
counterexample: Kripke argued that proper names denote their bearers not by virtue of any descriptive content but by virtue of a causal-historical chain linking current uses of the name to an initial baptism. The name 'Aristotle' denotes the philosopher not because he satisfies some cluster of descriptions — teacher of Alexander, author of the Metaphysics — but because the name was conferred upon him and transmitted through a chain of speakers. The third condition fails.
missing_condition: The definition assumes a descriptivist theory of reference, but the causal theory holds that the denotation of a proper name is fixed by a historical chain of transmission rather than by any descriptive content the speaker associates with the name.
passage: One might hold that a term denotes an object if and only if the term picks out exactly one object in the domain, the object exists, and the denotation is fixed by the descriptive content associated with the term. This definition captures the Fregean picture of reference: a name refers to an object by virtue of the sense it expresses, and the sense is a condition that the object uniquely satisfies. Yet Kripke's work on naming has challenged the third condition. Consider the name 'Aristotle.' On the descriptivist view, the name denotes the man who satisfies some associated description — the teacher of Alexander, the author of the Metaphysics, the student of Plato. But Kripke argued that a speaker might associate the wrong descriptions with the name — perhaps he thinks Aristotle was a playwright — and yet still succeed in referring to the correct individual, because the name was conferred upon the philosopher in an initial act of naming and has been transmitted from speaker to speaker through a causal-historical chain. The third condition fails: denotation is not fixed by descriptive content but by causal history. The definition has assumed that reference is always mediated by description, when the causal theory shows that names may refer directly, without the intermediation of any descriptive sense.

definition: Predication occurs if and only if (i) a property is ascribed to a subject, (ii) the subject is a particular individual, and (iii) the ascription is either true or false.
conditions: property ascribed to subject
subject is particular individual
ascription is either true or false
counterexample: The sentence 'Gold is a metal' ascribes a property (being a metal) to a subject (gold). But the subject is not a particular individual; it is a kind or a substance. The second condition fails, yet the sentence is a paradigmatic instance of predication — indeed, it is the sort of subject-predicate sentence that Aristotle took as the basis of his logic.
missing_condition: The definition restricts subjects of predication to particular individuals, but predication extends to kinds, masses, and abstract entities, which are not individuals in the ordinary sense yet serve as legitimate subjects of property-ascription.
passage: A standard account holds that predication occurs if and only if a property is ascribed to a subject, the subject is a particular individual, and the ascription is either true or false. This account captures the simple cases: 'Socrates is wise' ascribes wisdom to the individual Socrates, and the sentence is either true or false. Yet the definition is too restrictive in its conception of what may serve as a subject. Consider the sentence 'Gold is a metal.' A property — being a metal — is ascribed to a subject — gold. The ascription is either true or false (it is true). But the subject is not a particular individual. Gold is not a person or a thing that can be pointed to; it is a kind of substance, an abstract category under which particular lumps and nuggets fall. The second condition fails. Yet the sentence is a perfectly intelligible piece of predication; it is, indeed, the sort of generic sentence upon which a great deal of scientific and philosophical discourse depends. The definition has modelled predication upon the case of singular sentences about individuals and has neglected the predications that take kinds, masses, and abstract entities as their subjects.

definition: Linguistic communication succeeds if and only if (i) the speaker encodes a thought in a sentence, (ii) the hearer decodes the sentence to recover the thought, and (iii) the thought recovered by the hearer is identical to the thought encoded by the speaker.
conditions: speaker encodes thought in sentence
hearer decodes sentence to recover thought
thought recovered identical to thought encoded
counterexample: When a speaker says 'Can you pass the salt?' the thought he encodes, taken literally, is a question about the hearer's ability. But what the hearer recovers is a request: the speaker wants the salt passed to him. The thought recovered is not identical to the thought literally encoded. The third condition fails, yet the communication is entirely successful — indeed, more successful than it would have been had the hearer answered 'Yes, I can' without passing the salt.
missing_condition: The definition models communication as the transmission of a fixed code, but successful communication routinely depends upon the hearer's pragmatic inference of the speaker's intended meaning, which may diverge substantially from the literal content of the sentence uttered.
passage: A natural model of linguistic communication holds that communication succeeds if and only if the speaker encodes a thought in a sentence, the hearer decodes the sentence to recover the thought, and the thought recovered by the hearer is identical to the thought encoded by the speaker. This code model, as it has been called, treats language as a conduit through which thoughts are transmitted from mind to mind. Yet the model fails to account for the pervasive role of pragmatic inference. Consider the utterance 'Can you pass the salt?' The sentence, taken literally, is a question about the hearer's physical ability to move a salt cellar. If the hearer decodes the sentence according to its literal content, he will recover the thought that the speaker is enquiring about his capacities. But the speaker is not making such an enquiry; he is requesting the salt. The hearer understands this perfectly well and passes the salt without comment. The thought recovered — that the speaker wants the salt — is not identical to the thought literally encoded — that the speaker is asking whether the hearer is able to pass it. The third condition fails. Yet the communication has succeeded completely. The definition has treated communication as the mechanical transmission of literal content, when in practice communication depends upon the hearer's capacity to infer the speaker's intended meaning from context, convention, and conversational purpose.

definition: A hearer understands a sentence if and only if (i) he can parse its syntactic structure, (ii) he knows the meaning of each word, and (iii) he can state the proposition the sentence expresses.
conditions: can parse syntactic structure
knows meaning of each word
can state proposition expressed
counterexample: A competent English speaker who hears the sentence 'Time flies like an arrow' can parse its syntax and knows the meaning of each word. He can state its apparent proposition: that time passes swiftly, as an arrow flies swiftly. Yet the sentence is syntactically ambiguous — it could also mean that a certain species of fly, called time flies, is fond of an arrow — and the hearer's understanding of the sentence depends on pragmatic context, not merely on parsing and word knowledge. The conditions are met for one reading, yet the hearer may fail to understand the sentence as intended.
missing_condition: The definition equates understanding with the ability to derive a proposition from syntax and lexicon, but understanding a sentence in context requires pragmatic competence — the ability to select the intended reading from among multiple possibilities and to grasp the speaker's communicative purpose.
passage: One might suppose that a hearer understands a sentence if and only if he can parse its syntactic structure, he knows the meaning of each word, and he can state the proposition the sentence expresses. This definition captures the compositional picture of understanding: the hearer's grammatical and lexical competence suffices for comprehension. Yet the definition overlooks the role of pragmatic context. Consider the sentence 'Time flies like an arrow.' A competent speaker can parse the sentence and knows the meaning of every word. He may state its proposition: that time passes with the swiftness of an arrow's flight. But the sentence is syntactically ambiguous. It could also be parsed as an imperative: time the flies in the manner that an arrow would time them. Or as a statement about a species of fly that is fond of an arrow. These alternative readings are grammatically available, and the hearer's ability to select the intended interpretation depends not on syntax and lexicon alone but on his grasp of the context in which the sentence is uttered. The conditions are satisfied for the most salient reading, yet the hearer does not fully understand the sentence unless he can determine which reading the speaker intends, and this determination requires pragmatic competence that the definition does not mention.

definition: The literal meaning of a sentence is its basic semantic content such that (i) it is determined entirely by the sentence's lexical and syntactic properties, (ii) it is invariant across contexts of utterance, and (iii) it is the meaning a competent speaker would assign to the sentence in isolation.
conditions: determined by lexical and syntactic properties
invariant across contexts
meaning assigned in isolation by competent speaker
counterexample: The sentence 'I am here now' has a literal meaning that varies with every context of utterance: the word 'I' picks out different persons, 'here' picks out different places, and 'now' picks out different times. The second condition fails, for the literal meaning of the sentence is not invariant but shifts with each occasion of use.
missing_condition: The definition requires context-invariance, but sentences containing indexical expressions — 'I,' 'here,' 'now,' 'this' — have literal meanings that are essentially context-dependent, and the literal/non-literal distinction must be drawn within a context rather than prior to it.
passage: A natural account holds that the literal meaning of a sentence is its basic semantic content, determined entirely by the sentence's lexical and syntactic properties, invariant across contexts of utterance, and identical to the meaning a competent speaker would assign to the sentence in isolation. This account serves the purpose of distinguishing what a sentence literally says from what a speaker might mean by it on a particular occasion. Yet the requirement of context-invariance proves untenable for a large class of sentences. Consider the sentence 'I am here now.' Its lexical and syntactic properties are fixed: it consists of a pronoun, a copula, an adverb of place, and an adverb of time. But the literal meaning of the sentence changes with every utterance. When spoken by Jones in London on Tuesday, the sentence literally says that Jones is in London on Tuesday. When spoken by Smith in Edinburgh on Wednesday, it literally says that Smith is in Edinburgh on Wednesday. The second condition fails: the literal meaning is not invariant but shifts with the identity of the speaker, his location, and the time of utterance. The definition has assumed that literal meaning is a fixed, decontextualised property of the sentence, when in truth the literal meaning of any sentence containing indexical expressions is essentially context-dependent.

definition: A sentence S is meaningful if and only if (i) S is composed of words each of which has an independently specifiable sense, (ii) these senses combine according to the syntactic rules of the language to yield a composite sense, and (iii) the composite sense determines a definite set of conditions under which S would be true.
conditions: S is composed of words each of which has an independently specifiable sense
these senses combine according to the syntactic rules of the language to yield a composite sense
the composite sense determines a definite set of conditions under which S would be true
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'The nothing nothings.' Heidegger and certain of his translators have treated this sentence as meaningful — indeed, as expressing something of philosophical importance. Yet it is far from clear that 'nothing,' functioning as a verb, has an independently specifiable sense in any standard lexicon, and the composite sense of the sentence does not determine a definite set of truth-conditions in the ordinary way. The first and third conditions appear to fail, yet the sentence is not mere gibberish; it is discussed, debated, and treated as meaningful by a significant philosophical tradition.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that meaningfulness requires compositional determination of truth-conditions from independently specifiable word-senses, but it neglects the possibility that sentences may acquire meaning through their role in a broader philosophical or literary discourse, even when standard compositional mechanisms are strained or absent.
passage: It is natural to suppose that a sentence is meaningful if and only if it is composed of words each possessing an independently specifiable sense, these senses combine according to the syntactic rules of the language to yield a composite sense, and this composite sense determines a definite set of conditions under which the sentence would be true. The proposal has the considerable virtue of explaining how speakers of a language are able to understand sentences they have never previously encountered: the meaning of the whole is built up, in a regular and predictable fashion, from the meanings of the parts. A sentence such as 'The cat is on the mat' is meaningful because 'cat,' 'mat,' and 'on' each have specifiable senses, and these senses combine, via the syntactic structure of the sentence, to yield a proposition whose truth-conditions are perfectly determinate. One might suppose that this compositional account exhausts the notion of sentential meaning. Yet one must attend to certain sentences that seem to resist this analysis. The sentence 'The nothing nothings,' which appears in Heidegger's writings and has been the subject of extensive philosophical discussion, does not obviously satisfy the proposed conditions. It is unclear that 'nothing,' employed as a verb, possesses an independently specifiable sense in any standard dictionary or semantic theory, and the composite sense of the sentence — if it has one — does not determine truth-conditions in any straightforward manner. Nevertheless, the sentence is not treated as nonsense by a substantial tradition of philosophical commentary; it is discussed, interpreted, and taken to express something of importance about the nature of negation and being. The definition, in requiring that each word have an independent sense and that the whole determine definite truth-conditions, may be too restrictive, for it excludes sentences whose meaning is constituted not by standard compositional mechanisms but by their role within a larger discourse.

definition: A singular term t refers to an object o if and only if (i) t is associated with a definite description that uniquely picks out o, (ii) the speaker who uses t intends to pick out the object satisfying that description, and (iii) o is the unique object satisfying the description in the actual world.
conditions: t is associated with a definite description that uniquely picks out o
the speaker who uses t intends to pick out the object satisfying that description
o is the unique object satisfying the description in the actual world
counterexample: Consider a case in which a speaker uses the name 'Aristotle' intending to refer to the philosopher who tutored Alexander the Great. Suppose that the description the speaker associates with the name is 'the teacher of Alexander who wrote the Nicomachean Ethics.' In fact, Aristotle satisfies this description. But now suppose that the speaker is mistaken about one element: the speaker also believes that Aristotle was born in Athens, and if pressed would include 'born in Athens' in the cluster of descriptions associated with the name. Aristotle was born in Stagira, not Athens. If the associated description must uniquely and correctly pick out the referent, the inclusion of this false element threatens to undermine the reference relation, yet the speaker plainly succeeds in referring to Aristotle.
