missing_condition: The definition requires that the associated description correctly and uniquely identify the referent, but it fails to accommodate the fact that speakers may associate partially incorrect descriptions with a name and yet succeed in referring, provided a sufficient core of the associated information is accurate or the name is linked to the referent through a causal-historical chain independent of any description.
passage: One might propose that a singular term refers to an object if and only if the term is associated with a definite description that uniquely picks out that object, the speaker intends to pick out the object satisfying the description, and the object is the unique satisfier of the description in the actual world. This account has a certain elegance: it explains how names manage to single out particular individuals by tying reference to descriptive content in the speaker's mind. When one uses the name 'Aristotle,' one has in mind certain descriptions — 'the author of the Metaphysics,' 'the teacher of Alexander,' 'the founder of the Lyceum' — and the name refers to whichever individual uniquely satisfies these descriptions. The proposal appears to work well for many ordinary cases. Yet difficulties arise when one considers that speakers frequently associate partially inaccurate descriptions with names they use successfully. A speaker may use the name 'Aristotle' whilst believing, among other things, that Aristotle was born in Athens. This is false; Aristotle was born in Stagira. If the description the speaker associates with the name includes the condition 'born in Athens,' then no individual satisfies all the conditions simultaneously — for the actual Aristotle fails the birthplace condition. On a strict reading of the definition, the speaker's use of the name would fail to refer, or would refer to no one at all. Yet plainly the speaker does refer to Aristotle, and is understood by all interlocutors to be doing so. The definition is too demanding in requiring that every element of the associated description be satisfied; what seems to be wanted is some weaker condition that permits reference even when portions of the associated descriptive content are erroneous.

definition: A proper name N names an individual x if and only if (i) there exists an initial act of baptism in which N was bestowed upon x, (ii) there is an unbroken chain of communicative uses of N linking each subsequent user back to that initial baptism, and (iii) each user in the chain intends to use N with the same reference as the person from whom the user acquired it.
conditions: there exists an initial act of baptism in which N was bestowed upon x
there is an unbroken chain of communicative uses of N linking each subsequent user back to that initial baptism
each user in the chain intends to use N with the same reference as the person from whom the user acquired it
counterexample: Consider the name 'Madagascar.' According to historical evidence, the name was originally applied by Marco Polo (or his sources) not to the large island off the southeast coast of Africa but to a portion of the African mainland — possibly the region of Mogadishu. Through a series of cartographic errors and misunderstandings, the name came to be applied to the island instead. The chain of communicative uses is unbroken, but at some point in the chain the reference shifted: a user applied the name to a different geographical entity than the one to which the previous user had applied it. The third condition — that each user intends the same reference — fails at the point of the shift, yet 'Madagascar' now names the island, and this usage is entirely standard.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that referential intention is preserved throughout the chain, but it fails to account for the possibility of systematic referential drift, in which a name may gradually or abruptly come to name a different entity through widespread communal misapplication, and the new reference becomes the accepted one.
passage: It is tempting to hold that a proper name names an individual if and only if there exists an initial act of baptism in which the name was bestowed upon that individual, there is an unbroken chain of communicative uses linking each subsequent user of the name back to the original baptism, and each user in the chain intends to preserve the reference of the person from whom the name was acquired. This causal-chain account has considerable appeal, for it explains how names can refer across generations and across vast stretches of time without requiring that every user of the name possess the same descriptive information about the bearer. One may know very little about Homer and yet refer to Homer successfully, provided one's use of the name is linked, through a chain of prior uses, to an original act of naming. Yet one must consider the name 'Madagascar.' Historical scholarship suggests that the name was originally applied not to the great island off the coast of Africa but to a region of the mainland — perhaps the area around Mogadishu. Through cartographic error, the name was transferred to the island. The chain of uses is unbroken in the relevant sense — each mapmaker took the name from a prior source — but at some point the referential intention shifted: a cartographer applied 'Madagascar' to the island, not to the mainland region to which the previous source had applied it. The third condition fails at that juncture, for the user did not intend the same reference as the source. Yet 'Madagascar' now unquestionably names the island, and no competent speaker of English would take it to name anything else. The definition fails to accommodate such cases of referential drift, in which the established reference of a name shifts through communal misapplication and the new reference becomes the accepted standard.

definition: A translation T of a sentence S from language L1 into language L2 is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-conditions of S, (ii) T preserves the logical form of S, and (iii) for every term in S, there is a corresponding term in T that has the same extension.
conditions: T preserves the truth-conditions of S
T preserves the logical form of S
for every term in S, there is a corresponding term in T that has the same extension
counterexample: Consider the German sentence 'Es regnet' and its standard English translation 'It is raining.' The translation is universally regarded as adequate. Yet the logical forms of the two sentences differ in subtle but philosophically significant respects: the German sentence lacks the expletive subject 'it' that English grammar requires, and the tense and aspect systems of the two languages do not align precisely. Moreover, it is not clear that 'regnet' and 'is raining' have exactly the same extension, for what counts as raining may be subject to slight differences in the conventional boundaries drawn by speakers of the two languages. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are at best imperfectly satisfied, yet the translation is adequate by any reasonable standard.
missing_condition: The definition demands exact preservation of logical form and term-by-term extensional correspondence, but adequate translation may require only approximate preservation of truth-conditions and communicative equivalence, since the syntactic structures and lexical boundaries of different languages rarely align with the precision the definition assumes.
passage: One might propose that a translation of a sentence from one language into another is adequate if and only if it preserves the truth-conditions of the original, preserves its logical form, and provides for every term in the original a corresponding term in the translation with the same extension. The proposal captures the intuition that a good translation says the same thing as the original — that the two sentences are true under exactly the same circumstances and that their internal structures correspond in the relevant respects. For certain pairs of closely related languages and for certain classes of simple sentences, this account works tolerably well. But one must consider the translation of the German sentence 'Es regnet' into English as 'It is raining.' This translation is regarded as entirely adequate; no competent translator would quarrel with it. Yet difficulties emerge upon closer examination. The German sentence contains no expletive subject corresponding to the English 'it'; the syntactic structures of the two sentences differ, and with them, arguably, their logical forms. Furthermore, the aspectual systems of the two languages are not perfectly aligned: the English progressive 'is raining' marks an ongoing process in a way that the German simple present does not precisely replicate. And it is not obvious that the extension of 'regnet' is identical to that of 'is raining,' for the conventional boundaries of what counts as raining — as opposed to drizzling, misting, or sleeting — may differ subtly between linguistic communities. The second and third conditions are imperfectly satisfied at best, yet the translation is plainly adequate. What this suggests is that the definition sets the bar too high: adequate translation requires not exact structural and extensional correspondence but a more flexible relation of communicative equivalence that allows for the inevitable divergences between the grammatical and lexical resources of different languages.

definition: An utterance U is metaphorical if and only if (i) U contains a term used in a sense that departs from its literal or standard meaning, (ii) the departure is signalled by a semantic tension or apparent falsehood in the literal reading, and (iii) the speaker intends the hearer to recognise the non-literal sense by perceiving the tension.
conditions: U contains a term used in a sense that departs from its literal or standard meaning
the departure is signalled by a semantic tension or apparent falsehood in the literal reading
the speaker intends the hearer to recognise the non-literal sense by perceiving the tension
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'The chairman ploughed through the agenda.' This is standardly classified as metaphorical — 'ploughed' is being used figuratively. Yet the sentence is not obviously false on its literal reading; it is merely odd or unusual. There is no clear semantic tension of the kind one finds in 'Juliet is the sun,' where the literal reading ascribes celestial identity to a human being. A chairman might, in some extended but not outlandish sense, plough through items. The second condition — that the departure be signalled by an apparent falsehood — is not clearly satisfied, yet the utterance is metaphorical.
missing_condition: The definition requires that metaphor be signalled by semantic tension or apparent falsehood, but many metaphors — particularly conventional or 'dead' metaphors and cases of catachresis — produce no felt tension at all, and the hearer processes the non-literal sense without ever entertaining the literal reading as false.
passage: It is commonly held that an utterance is metaphorical if and only if it contains a term used in a sense departing from its literal meaning, this departure is signalled by a semantic tension or apparent falsehood in the literal reading, and the speaker intends the hearer to recognise the non-literal sense by perceiving this tension. The account has intuitive appeal. When Shakespeare writes 'Juliet is the sun,' the literal reading — that a young woman is identical with a celestial body — is plainly false, and this falsehood signals to the reader that a non-literal interpretation is required. The reader perceives the tension and searches for the intended figurative meaning: that Juliet is radiant, life-giving, the centre of Romeo's world. The three conditions appear to be satisfied, and the analysis seems illuminating. Yet one must attend to a different class of cases. The sentence 'The chairman ploughed through the agenda' is metaphorical: 'ploughed' is not being used in its literal agricultural sense. But is there a felt semantic tension or apparent falsehood? The literal reading is not obviously false in the way that 'Juliet is the sun' is false; it is merely unusual. One can imagine, without great strain, a use of 'ploughed' that hovers between the literal and the figurative, and many hearers will process the sentence without ever pausing to register a tension between literal and intended meaning. The metaphor is sufficiently conventional that the figurative sense is grasped directly, without the intermediate step of perceiving a literal falsehood. The second condition, which demands that the departure be signalled by apparent falsehood, is not clearly satisfied. What this suggests is that the definition captures one prominent mechanism by which novel metaphors are understood but fails to accommodate the large class of conventional metaphors in which the figurative sense is processed without any felt tension at all.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker S expresses an intention to perform some future act A, (ii) S intends U to place S under an obligation to perform A, and (iii) the hearer H recognises and accepts that S has thereby undertaken the obligation.
conditions: the speaker S expresses an intention to perform some future act A
S intends U to place S under an obligation to perform A
the hearer H recognises and accepts that S has thereby undertaken the obligation
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man, under duress, says to his captor: 'I promise I shall pay the ransom.' The man expresses an intention to perform a future act, and the captor recognises the utterance as a promise. Yet the man does not genuinely intend his utterance to place himself under an obligation — he speaks only to secure his release and regards the promise as void the moment he is free. The second condition fails. Nevertheless, many would hold that a promise has been made, albeit one extracted under duress; the social and linguistic conventions of promising attach to the utterance regardless of the speaker's private reservations.
missing_condition: The definition locates the binding force of a promise in the speaker's sincere intention, but it fails to account for the conventional or institutional character of promising, whereby the utterance of certain words in certain circumstances constitutes a promise irrespective of the speaker's private mental states.
passage: One might propose that an utterance constitutes a promise if and only if the speaker expresses an intention to perform some future act, the speaker intends the utterance to place the speaker under an obligation to perform that act, and the hearer recognises and accepts the obligation thus undertaken. This account captures much of what is involved in paradigmatic cases of promising. When one says to a friend, 'I promise to return your book by Friday,' one expresses an intention to return the book, one intends to bind oneself to doing so, and the friend recognises and accepts the commitment. All three conditions are satisfied, and the promise is made. Yet one must consider a less comfortable case. A man, held captive, says to his captor: 'I promise I shall pay the ransom.' The utterance has the grammatical form of a promise; the captor understands it as a promise and acts upon it. But the man who speaks does not genuinely intend to bind himself to the obligation. He speaks under duress, with the sole aim of securing his release, and he regards the words as empty the moment he is free. The second condition — that the speaker intend the utterance to place himself under an obligation — fails. Yet there is a strong intuition, shared by many philosophers and by the conventions of ordinary discourse, that a promise has nonetheless been made. The man promised, even if the promise was extracted by force and even if the moral force of such a promise is debatable. The linguistic and social conventions of promising attach to the utterance by virtue of its form and its context, regardless of the speaker's private mental reservations. The definition, in requiring sincere intention as a necessary condition, fails to accommodate the conventional or institutional dimension of speech acts, in which the performance of the act may depend upon the public character of the utterance rather than upon the speaker's inner states.

definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) there exist borderline cases to which it is indeterminate whether F applies, (ii) F admits of sorites-susceptible gradations — that is, there is a dimension along which small incremental changes never seem sufficient to alter the applicability of F, and (iii) F lacks any precise boundary separating its positive extension from its negative extension.
conditions: there exist borderline cases to which it is indeterminate whether F applies
F admits of sorites-susceptible gradations
F lacks any precise boundary separating its positive extension from its negative extension
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is a prime number.' There are no borderline cases of primality; every natural number greater than one is either prime or composite, and there is a precise and decidable boundary separating the two classes. The predicate does not admit of sorites-susceptible gradations in any natural sense, for there is no dimension along which small incremental changes threaten to turn a prime into a composite. Yet the predicate 'prime' is, in a certain sense, indeterminate in its application to very large numbers — not because of vagueness, but because of computational intractability. For a sufficiently large number, it may be practically impossible to determine whether the predicate applies. This is not vagueness as the definition intends it, yet it shows that the definition does not adequately distinguish vagueness from other forms of indeterminacy in the application of predicates.
missing_condition: The definition conflates vagueness with indeterminacy of application in general. What it lacks is a condition specifying that the indeterminacy must be semantic in character — arising from the meaning of the predicate itself — rather than from practical, computational, or other non-semantic sources of uncertainty about extension.
passage: It is widely held that a predicate is vague if and only if there exist borderline cases to which it is indeterminate whether the predicate applies, the predicate admits of sorites-susceptible gradations along some dimension, and the predicate lacks any precise boundary separating its positive from its negative extension. The account appears to capture the essential features of vagueness. 'Tall' is vague because there are borderline heights, because a series of small increments in height never seems to take one from the non-tall to the tall at any single step, and because there is no precise height that marks the boundary. 'Red' is vague for analogous reasons. But one ought to consider a rather different sort of predicate. 'Is a prime number' is not, by any standard account, a vague predicate. Every natural number greater than one is determinately either prime or composite, and the boundary between the two classes is perfectly precise. Yet for very large numbers — numbers with millions of digits — it may be practically impossible to determine whether the predicate applies. No known algorithm may complete the computation in any feasible time. There is, in a practical sense, an indeterminacy in the application of the predicate to such numbers: we cannot say whether they are prime or not. This is plainly not vagueness as the definition intends it; it is computational intractability, not semantic indeterminacy. But the definition, as stated, does not clearly exclude such cases. It speaks of indeterminacy, of the absence of a precise boundary, and of sorites susceptibility, but it does not specify that the indeterminacy must arise from the meaning of the predicate rather than from extrinsic practical difficulties. What is wanted is an additional condition distinguishing semantic vagueness from other sources of indeterminacy in the application of predicates.

definition: Two sentences S1 and S2 are synonymous if and only if (i) S1 and S2 have the same truth-conditions, (ii) S1 and S2 are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value, and (iii) a competent speaker who understands S1 would, upon reflection, assent to S2, and conversely.
conditions: S1 and S2 have the same truth-conditions
S1 and S2 are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value
a competent speaker who understands S1 would, upon reflection, assent to S2, and conversely
counterexample: Consider the sentences 'All unmarried men are unmarried' and 'All bachelors are unmarried.' Both are true under exactly the same conditions — indeed, both are necessarily true. They are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value. And a competent speaker would assent to both. Yet the two sentences differ in cognitive significance: the first is a trivial logical truth, recognisable as such by anyone who grasps the meanings of the individual words, whereas the second, though also analytic, conveys substantive lexical information about the relationship between 'bachelor' and 'unmarried.' The three conditions are all satisfied, yet the sentences are not, in the fullest sense, synonymous — they differ in informativeness and cognitive role.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the sentences possess the same cognitive significance — that they make the same contribution to thought and reasoning — rather than merely sharing truth-conditions and extensional intersubstitutability.
passage: One might hold that two sentences are synonymous if and only if they share truth-conditions, they are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value, and any competent speaker who understands the one would assent to the other upon reflection. The proposal captures a natural and widespread intuition about sameness of meaning: two sentences mean the same thing if they say the same thing about the same situations. 'It is raining' and 'Rain is falling' appear to satisfy these conditions, and the judgement that they are synonymous seems secure. But one ought to consider a more delicate pair. Take 'All unmarried men are unmarried' and 'All bachelors are unmarried.' Both sentences are true, indeed necessarily so; they are intersubstitutable in extensional contexts; and a competent speaker of English would assent to both. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet the two sentences differ in their cognitive significance. The first is a trivial logical truth — a tautology of the form 'All things that are F and G are F' — recognisable as true by any speaker who grasps the logical words, regardless of any knowledge of the English lexicon beyond the most elementary. The second, though also analytic, is informative in a way the first is not: it tells the hearer something about the meaning of the word 'bachelor,' namely, that being unmarried is part of what it is to be a bachelor. The difference is not one of truth-conditions, for both are true in all possible circumstances. Nor is it one of extensional intersubstitutability. It is a difference in the kind of understanding they convey, in the role they play in reasoning, in their informativeness. The definition, in attending only to truth-conditions and extensional equivalence, fails to capture this finer-grained dimension of meaning that distinguishes a trivial tautology from a substantive analytic truth.

definition: A definite description 'the F' refers to an object o if and only if (i) there exists exactly one object that is F, (ii) o is that unique object, and (iii) the speaker uses 'the F' with the intention of picking out whichever object uniquely satisfies F.
conditions: there exists exactly one object that is F
o is that unique object
the speaker uses 'the F' with the intention of picking out whichever object uniquely satisfies F
counterexample: Consider a case in which two colleagues are in a room and one says to the other, 'The table is covered with papers.' There are, in the world at large, many millions of tables; the description 'the table' does not uniquely pick out any one of them. The first condition fails when the domain of quantification is taken to be unrestricted. Yet the speaker succeeds in referring — both speaker and hearer understand perfectly well which table is meant, namely the one in the room before them. The reference is achieved not by uniqueness of satisfaction in an unrestricted domain but by the contextual salience of the particular table.
missing_condition: The definition requires global uniqueness of satisfaction, but in ordinary discourse definite descriptions typically refer by exploiting contextual restrictions on the domain — the salience of the object in the conversational situation — rather than by uniquely satisfying the predicate across all objects in the world.
passage: It is natural to hold that a definite description 'the F' refers to an object if and only if there exists exactly one thing that is F, that object is the thing in question, and the speaker uses the description intending to pick out whichever object uniquely satisfies the predicate. The account provides a tidy explanation of how descriptions function: 'the author of Waverley' refers to Scott because Scott is the unique individual who wrote Waverley, and the speaker intends to pick out whoever satisfies that condition. When uniqueness holds, the mechanism operates smoothly. But one must consider ordinary uses of definite descriptions in everyday speech. Two colleagues sit in a room, and one says to the other: 'The table is covered with papers.' Now, there are many millions of tables in the world. The description 'the table' does not, on an unrestricted reading of the domain, uniquely pick out any single object; the first condition fails spectacularly. Yet no misunderstanding occurs. The hearer knows immediately which table is meant — the table that stands between them, the table upon which the papers are strewn. The speaker succeeds in referring, and the hearer succeeds in identifying the referent, because the conversational context restricts the domain of discourse to the objects in the room. The table is salient; it is the only table that matters for the purposes of the exchange. The definition, in requiring that the predicate be satisfied by exactly one object, fails to accommodate the pervasive role of contextual domain restriction in the ordinary use of definite descriptions. What is wanted is a condition that acknowledges the contribution of conversational context to the determination of reference.

definition: A metaphor M is apt if and only if (i) M draws a comparison between two things belonging to different semantic domains, (ii) the comparison highlights a genuine and significant resemblance between the two things, and (iii) the resemblance is one that a competent speaker would recognise upon reflection.
conditions: M draws a comparison between two things belonging to different semantic domains
the comparison highlights a genuine and significant resemblance between the two things
the resemblance is one that a competent speaker would recognise upon reflection
counterexample: Consider the metaphor 'Time is money.' This is standardly regarded as an apt metaphor, and it draws a comparison between two things from different domains — time and currency. Yet it is far from clear that there is a genuine resemblance between time and money; the aptness of the metaphor lies not in a pre-existing resemblance that the metaphor reveals but in the way the metaphor structures our understanding of time by imposing upon it the conceptual framework of monetary exchange — spending, saving, wasting, investing. The second condition, which requires a pre-existing resemblance, is not satisfied in any straightforward sense; the metaphor creates a way of seeing rather than pointing to an antecedent likeness.
missing_condition: The definition treats metaphorical aptness as a matter of revealing pre-existing resemblances, but it fails to accommodate metaphors whose aptness consists in creating new ways of conceptualising a subject — structuring thought about one domain in terms of another — rather than highlighting likenesses that were already there to be perceived.
passage: One might propose that a metaphor is apt if and only if it draws a comparison between two things from different semantic domains, highlights a genuine and significant resemblance between them, and the resemblance is one that a competent speaker would recognise upon reflection. The proposal accords well with certain paradigmatic metaphors. 'The fog comes on little cat feet' is apt because fog and a cat do genuinely share a certain quality of silent, soft, stealthy approach, and a competent speaker of English, upon reflecting on the comparison, would recognise this resemblance. The three conditions are satisfied, and the analysis seems adequate. Yet one must consider a different sort of metaphor. 'Time is money' is among the most pervasive metaphors in English, shaping not merely poetic language but the everyday discourse of practical life. We speak of spending time, saving time, wasting time, investing time, budgeting time. The metaphor is apt — it structures an entire domain of thought and action. But does it highlight a genuine resemblance between time and money? Time is not a medium of exchange; it cannot be deposited, withdrawn, or lent at interest. The aptness of the metaphor does not lie in a pre-existing likeness between the two domains but in the way the metaphor imposes the conceptual structure of monetary exchange upon our understanding of time, enabling us to think about time in ways we otherwise could not. The second condition — which requires a genuine resemblance — is not satisfied in any straightforward sense, for the metaphor does not so much reveal a resemblance as create a conceptual mapping. The definition, in treating aptness as a matter of highlighting pre-existing likenesses, fails to accommodate metaphors whose power consists in the novel structuring of thought rather than in the recognition of antecedent similarities.

definition: A proper name N is meaningful if and only if (i) N abbreviates a definite description or cluster of descriptions, (ii) these descriptions constitute the cognitive content associated with N by competent speakers, and (iii) two names are synonymous just in case they abbreviate the same descriptions.
conditions: N abbreviates a definite description or cluster of descriptions
these descriptions constitute the cognitive content associated with N by competent speakers
two names are synonymous just in case they abbreviate the same descriptions
counterexample: Consider the names 'Hesperus' and 'Phosphorus.' Both name the planet Venus; the astronomical discovery that the evening star and the morning star are the same celestial body was a significant empirical finding. On the proposed account, the two names abbreviate different clusters of descriptions — 'the bright celestial body visible in the evening' and 'the bright celestial body visible in the morning' — and are therefore not synonymous. This much the definition handles. But consider further: if the meaning of a name is exhausted by the descriptions it abbreviates, then 'Hesperus is Phosphorus' should be analytic for anyone who knows the relevant astronomical fact, since for such a speaker the descriptions converge. Yet the sentence was an empirical discovery, not an analytic truth; it enlarged our understanding of the world in a way that no mere synonym-substitution could. The definition cannot account for the informative character of identity statements involving co-referential names.
missing_condition: The definition treats the meaning of a name as wholly descriptive, but it fails to acknowledge that names may have a semantic function — rigid designation of a particular individual across possible circumstances — that is not captured by any cluster of contingent descriptions, and that explains why identity statements involving co-referential names are informative rather than trivially analytic.
passage: A prominent view holds that a proper name is meaningful if and only if it abbreviates a definite description or cluster of descriptions, these descriptions constitute the cognitive content that competent speakers associate with the name, and two names are synonymous just in case they abbreviate the same descriptions. The view explains, among other things, why names seem to carry cognitive content: when one hears the name 'Aristotle,' one thinks of the pupil of Plato, the teacher of Alexander, the author of the Nicomachean Ethics, and these descriptions are, on this account, what the name means. The proposal also seems to explain the apparent meaningfulness of identity statements: 'Cicero is Tully' is informative because the two names abbreviate different clusters of descriptions. Yet the explanation, on closer inspection, is unsatisfying. Consider 'Hesperus' and 'Phosphorus.' These names refer to the same celestial body — the planet Venus. The discovery that the evening star and the morning star are identical was a substantive astronomical achievement. On the proposed account, 'Hesperus' abbreviates descriptions such as 'the bright celestial body visible in the evening sky,' whilst 'Phosphorus' abbreviates 'the bright celestial body visible in the morning sky.' The names abbreviate different descriptions and are therefore not synonymous; so far the definition appears to work. But consider what happens once the astronomical identity is known. For a speaker who knows that the evening star is the morning star, the descriptive clusters converge — the speaker now associates both names with the full set of Venus-descriptions. On the definition, the two names should now be synonymous for that speaker, and the sentence 'Hesperus is Phosphorus' should be analytic. Yet it is not; it remains an empirical truth about the world. The definition, in identifying the meaning of a name with a cluster of descriptions, cannot account for the persistent informativeness of such identity statements.

definition: A word w in language L1 is translatable into language L2 if and only if (i) there exists a word w* in L2 whose extension is identical to that of w, (ii) w* occupies the same position in the semantic field of L2 as w occupies in L1, and (iii) w and w* are intersubstitutable in corresponding sentences without alteration of truth-value.
conditions: there exists a word w* in L2 whose extension is identical to that of w
w* occupies the same position in the semantic field of L2 as w occupies in L1
w and w* are intersubstitutable in corresponding sentences without alteration of truth-value
counterexample: Consider the Portuguese word 'saudade,' which denotes a deep emotional state of nostalgic longing for something or someone absent, mingled with the bittersweet recognition that the object of longing may never return. There is no single English word whose extension is identical: 'nostalgia,' 'longing,' 'yearning,' and 'homesickness' each capture some aspect but none covers the full range. The first condition fails, and the second fails with it, for there is no single English word occupying the same position in the semantic field. Yet 'saudade' is not untranslatable in any absolute sense; it can be rendered into English by means of paraphrase, circumlocution, or extended description.
missing_condition: The definition requires word-for-word extensional equivalence, but it fails to account for the fact that translatability may be achieved at the level of phrases, paraphrases, or extended descriptions rather than single-word correspondences, and that the absence of a lexical equivalent does not entail the absence of the means to express the same content.
passage: One might propose that a word in one language is translatable into another if and only if there exists a word in the target language whose extension is identical, that word occupies the same position in the semantic field of the target language, and the two words are intersubstitutable in corresponding sentences without alteration of truth-value. The proposal embodies a tidy picture: translation is, at bottom, a matter of finding lexical equivalents — words that mean the same thing across languages. For many common words the proposal works well enough. The English word 'dog' and the French word 'chien' have the same extension, occupy analogous positions in their respective semantic fields, and are intersubstitutable in corresponding sentences. But one must consider less accommodating cases. The Portuguese word 'saudade' denotes a distinctive emotional state — a deep nostalgic longing for something or someone absent, suffused with the bittersweet awareness that what is longed for may never be recovered. No single English word captures this complex of feeling. 'Nostalgia' is too narrow; 'longing' too general; 'yearning' too active; 'homesickness' too specific. There is no English word whose extension coincides with that of 'saudade,' and consequently the first and second conditions cannot be satisfied. Yet 'saudade' is not, in any reasonable sense, untranslatable. English speakers can be made to understand the concept through paraphrase, illustration, and extended description. The content expressed by 'saudade' is accessible to English speakers; what is lacking is a single lexical item. The definition, in requiring word-for-word equivalence, mistakes a feature of the most convenient translations for a necessary condition of translatability itself. What is wanted is a broader notion that acknowledges translation at the level of phrases and descriptions, not merely at the level of individual words.

definition: A term T is vague if and only if (i) T admits of borderline cases, (ii) the borderline region is narrow — that is, the clear positive and clear negative cases vastly outnumber the borderline ones, and (iii) the vagueness of T can in principle be eliminated by stipulating a precise boundary.
conditions: T admits of borderline cases
the borderline region is narrow
the vagueness of T can in principle be eliminated by stipulating a precise boundary
counterexample: Consider the term 'religion.' It admits of borderline cases: it is unclear whether certain practices — ancestor veneration, secular Buddhism, Confucianism as a way of life — fall within the extension of the term. But the borderline region is not narrow; it is vast. The clear cases of religion (Christianity, Islam, Judaism) and the clear non-cases (stamp-collecting, arithmetic) are separated by an enormous and heterogeneous borderline zone encompassing spiritual movements, ethical systems, folk practices, and syncretic traditions. The second condition fails. Moreover, it is doubtful that the vagueness of 'religion' could be eliminated by stipulating a precise boundary, for the difficulty lies not in the absence of a sharp cut-off but in the multidimensional and family-resemblance character of the concept. The third condition is likewise doubtful.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that vagueness is always a minor imprecision at the margins, eliminable by stipulation, but it fails to accommodate terms whose vagueness is structural and pervasive — arising from family-resemblance or cluster-concept character — such that no single stipulation could render them precise without distorting their conceptual role.
passage: It is sometimes supposed that a term is vague if and only if it admits of borderline cases, the borderline region is narrow relative to the clear cases, and the vagueness can in principle be eliminated by stipulating a precise boundary. On this view, vagueness is a minor imprecision at the margins — a slight fuzziness of the sort that affects terms like 'tall' or 'red,' where the vast majority of cases are clear and only a few intermediate ones are uncertain. And the imprecision is, in principle, remediable: one could stipulate that 'tall' applies to anyone over six feet and thereby resolve every borderline case. The definition seems plausible for such predicates. But one must consider a different sort of term. 'Religion' admits of borderline cases — this much is uncontroversial. It is unclear whether ancestor veneration, secular Buddhism, Confucianism as a system of ethics, or certain New Age spiritualities count as religions. But the borderline region is not narrow; it is enormous. Between the paradigmatic religions — Christianity, Islam, Hinduism — and the paradigmatic non-religions — stamp-collecting, mathematics, plumbing — there lies a vast territory of spiritual movements, ethical systems, folk practices, and syncretic traditions whose classification is genuinely uncertain. The second condition is not satisfied: the borderline zone does not consist of a thin band of marginal cases but of a wide and heterogeneous domain. Furthermore, it is far from clear that a stipulated boundary would resolve the vagueness without distorting the concept beyond recognition. 'Religion' is not vague in the way 'tall' is vague; its vagueness arises from the family-resemblance character of the concept, from the fact that the various things called religions share overlapping similarities rather than a common essence. The definition, in treating vagueness as a narrow and eliminable imprecision, fails to accommodate terms whose vagueness is structural and pervasive.

definition: A word w has meaning if and only if (i) w stands in a conventional association with a mental image or idea in the minds of speakers, (ii) the mental image is substantially the same across competent speakers of the language, and (iii) the communication of meaning consists in the speaker's evoking, by means of w, the associated image in the mind of the hearer.
conditions: w stands in a conventional association with a mental image or idea in the minds of speakers
the mental image is substantially the same across competent speakers of the language
the communication of meaning consists in the speaker evoking the associated image in the mind of the hearer
counterexample: Consider the word 'if.' It is meaningful — indeed, it is essential to the functioning of the language — yet it does not stand in conventional association with any mental image. One cannot form a mental picture of 'if'; the word expresses a logical relation, not a perceptible object or quality. The first condition fails. Similarly, words such as 'but,' 'nevertheless,' 'although,' and 'or' are meaningful without being associated with mental images. The definition cannot account for the meaning of logical connectives and discourse particles.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that meaning consists in the evocation of mental imagery, but it fails to accommodate the large class of words — logical connectives, prepositions, discourse particles — whose meaning is constituted by their role in the construction of propositions and the articulation of logical relations, not by association with any mental image.
passage: A venerable account of meaning holds that a word has meaning if and only if it stands in conventional association with a mental image in the minds of speakers, this image is substantially the same across competent speakers, and communication consists in the speaker's evoking the associated image in the hearer's mind. The account has a certain intuitive plausibility. When one hears the word 'horse,' one forms a mental picture of a horse; when one hears 'red,' one summons an image of redness. The word calls up the image, and the image is the meaning. For many concrete nouns and simple adjectives, the account seems tolerably adequate. Yet one must consider a word of a very different kind. The word 'if' is indisputably meaningful. It is one of the most frequently used words in English; without it, the language would be crippled. It appears in every conditional sentence, every hypothetical reasoning, every expression of contingency. But what mental image is associated with 'if'? One cannot form a picture of 'if' in the way one forms a picture of a horse or a sunset. The word does not denote any object, quality, or event that can be pictured in the mind's eye; it expresses a logical relation between propositions. The first condition fails entirely: there is no mental image conventionally associated with 'if,' and consequently the second and third conditions are inapplicable. The same difficulty arises for a wide range of words: 'but,' 'or,' 'nevertheless,' 'although,' 'every,' 'some.' These words are meaningful, and their meanings are precise and learnable, yet they resist assimilation to the image theory. The definition, in tying meaning to mental imagery, captures at best one species of linguistic meaning and fails entirely to account for the logical and structural vocabulary upon which the expressive power of language depends.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a command if and only if (i) the speaker S occupies a position of authority over the hearer H, (ii) S uses U to direct H to perform some action A, and (iii) H is under an obligation, by virtue of S's authority, to perform A.
conditions: the speaker S occupies a position of authority over the hearer H
S uses U to direct H to perform some action A
H is under an obligation, by virtue of S's authority, to perform A
counterexample: Consider a case in which a child says to a parent, 'Give me the ball!' The child occupies no position of authority over the parent; the parent is under no obligation, by virtue of the child's authority, to comply. Yet the utterance is a command — it has the grammatical form, the illocutionary force, and the communicative intent of a command. The first and third conditions fail, yet a command has been issued, even if it is one the parent may choose to ignore.
missing_condition: The definition locates the command-making force of an utterance in the authority relation between speaker and hearer, but it fails to recognise that commanding is a speech act that can be performed by anyone regardless of authority — what authority contributes is not the existence of the command but the speaker's entitlement to obedience.
passage: One might hold that an utterance constitutes a command if and only if the speaker occupies a position of authority over the hearer, the speaker uses the utterance to direct the hearer to perform some action, and the hearer is under an obligation, deriving from the speaker's authority, to comply. The account captures the paradigmatic case of commanding. A military officer says to a subordinate, 'Advance to the bridge,' and the utterance is a command: the officer has authority, the utterance directs an action, and the subordinate is obligated to obey. The three conditions are all satisfied, and the analysis seems correct. Yet one must consider a rather different situation. A child of four says to a parent, in a tone of unmistakable imperative force, 'Give me the ball!' The child occupies no position of authority over the parent; the conventions of the family do not place the child in command. The parent is under no obligation, by virtue of the child's authority, to hand over the ball. The first and third conditions fail. Yet the child has issued a command. The utterance has the grammatical form of a command — the imperative mood — and the illocutionary force of a command: it is an attempt to direct the hearer's action. The parent may refuse to comply, but the refusal is a refusal to obey a command, not a failure to understand one. The definition, in requiring authority as a necessary condition, confuses two things: the speech act of commanding and the entitlement to obedience. A command may be issued by anyone; what authority contributes is not the existence of the command but the legitimacy of the demand for compliance. The child commands without authority, and the command is none the less a command for that.

definition: A demonstrative expression 'that F' refers to an object o if and only if (i) the speaker accompanies the utterance with a pointing gesture or other ostensive indication directed at o, (ii) o is an F, and (iii) o is perceptually available to both speaker and hearer at the time of utterance.
conditions: the speaker accompanies the utterance with a pointing gesture or other ostensive indication directed at o
o is an F
o is perceptually available to both speaker and hearer at the time of utterance
counterexample: Consider a case in which two friends are reminiscing about a holiday, and one says, 'That little café where we had breakfast — do you remember it?' The speaker uses the demonstrative 'that little café' to refer to a particular café, but the café is not perceptually available to either speaker or hearer; they are sitting in a drawing-room in London, and the café is in Paris. Nor does the speaker accompany the utterance with any pointing gesture. The first and third conditions fail, yet the demonstrative expression succeeds in referring, for the shared memory of the holiday provides sufficient context for the hearer to identify the intended referent.
missing_condition: The definition requires perceptual availability and ostensive indication, but it fails to accommodate the widespread use of demonstratives in contexts of shared memory, discourse reference, and deferred ostension, where the referent is identified not by perception and pointing but by shared background or prior discourse.
passage: One might propose that a demonstrative expression 'that F' refers to an object if and only if the speaker accompanies the utterance with a pointing gesture directed at the object, the object is an F, and the object is perceptually available to both speaker and hearer. The account captures the most straightforward cases of demonstrative reference. One points at a bird in a tree and says, 'That robin is singing beautifully'; the bird is visible, the gesture singles it out, and the hearer identifies the referent without difficulty. The conditions are satisfied, and the reference succeeds. But one must consider a less straightforward case. Two friends sit in a drawing-room in London, reminiscing about a journey they once made together. One says to the other: 'That little café where we had breakfast — do you remember it?' The speaker uses the demonstrative 'that little café' and succeeds in referring to a particular café in Paris — a café both friends visited years ago. But the café is not perceptually available; it is hundreds of miles away. The speaker makes no pointing gesture; there is nothing in the room to point at. The first and third conditions both fail. Yet the reference succeeds, and the hearer identifies the intended café without the slightest difficulty, drawing upon shared memories of the journey. The demonstrative functions here not by perception and pointing but by evoking a shared experience that supplies the context needed for identification. The definition, in requiring ostension and perceptual availability, captures only one mode of demonstrative reference and fails to accommodate the many cases in which demonstratives refer to objects that are absent, remembered, or merely talked about.

definition: A sentence S is meaningful if and only if (i) S can be verified or falsified by some possible observation or set of observations, (ii) the method of verification is specifiable in advance, and (iii) if S cannot be so verified, S is either a tautology of logic or a definition.
conditions: S can be verified or falsified by some possible observation or set of observations
the method of verification is specifiable in advance
if S cannot be so verified, S is either a tautology of logic or a definition
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'Every event has a cause.' This sentence is neither a tautology of logic nor a mere definition; it makes a substantive claim about the structure of the world. Yet it cannot be verified or falsified by any particular observation or set of observations, for it is a universal claim of unrestricted generality — no finite body of evidence could establish it, and no single observation could refute it without auxiliary assumptions. The first condition fails, and the sentence is not a tautology or definition, so the third condition's exception does not apply. Yet the sentence is meaningful; it has been debated by philosophers for centuries and has clear implications for scientific practice.
missing_condition: The definition ties meaningfulness to verifiability, but it fails to accommodate sentences that are meaningful by virtue of their role in the organisation of enquiry, their inferential connections to other sentences, or their function in theoretical reasoning, even when no specifiable observation could decisively verify or falsify them.
passage: A stringent account of meaning holds that a sentence is meaningful if and only if it can be verified or falsified by some possible observation, the method of verification is specifiable in advance, and any sentence that cannot be so verified is meaningful only if it is a tautology of logic or a definition. The account embodies a powerful idea: that the meaning of a sentence is bound up with the conditions under which it would be confirmed or disconfirmed by experience, and that sentences which float free of all possible observation are devoid of content. For many sentences in the natural sciences, the account works admirably. 'Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius at standard pressure' is meaningful because one can specify the observation that would verify it — one heats water at sea level and measures the boiling point. But one must consider the sentence 'Every event has a cause.' This is a principle of great philosophical and scientific importance; it has been discussed, defended, and attacked by philosophers from Aristotle to Hume and beyond. It is plainly meaningful — it makes a claim about the structure of the world, and its acceptance or rejection has far-reaching consequences for the conduct of enquiry. Yet it cannot be verified or falsified by any particular observation or finite set of observations. It is a universal claim of unrestricted scope: every event, past, present, and future, without exception, has a cause. No body of evidence, however large, could establish it conclusively; no single counter-instance could refute it without contentious auxiliary assumptions. And it is not a tautology of logic, for its denial is not self-contradictory; nor is it a mere definition. All three conditions fail. The definition, in restricting meaningfulness to verifiable sentences plus tautologies and definitions, excludes a class of sentences that are central to philosophical and scientific reasoning.

definition: An expression E is a dead metaphor if and only if (i) E was originally used metaphorically, with a living sense of the figurative transfer, (ii) through repeated use E has lost all trace of its metaphorical origin, and (iii) competent speakers process E as a literal expression without any awareness of the original figure.
conditions: E was originally used metaphorically, with a living sense of the figurative transfer
through repeated use E has lost all trace of its metaphorical origin
competent speakers process E as a literal expression without any awareness of the original figure
counterexample: Consider the expression 'the leg of a table.' This is standardly cited as a dead metaphor — 'leg,' originally denoting a bodily limb, has been extended to furniture. Through long use, the expression has become conventional. Yet it has not lost all trace of its metaphorical origin. English speakers retain a dim awareness that tables do not literally have legs in the way animals do; the word retains a faint resonance of the original bodily sense. The expression is neither fully alive nor fully dead — it is, one might say, dormant. The second condition, which requires the complete loss of all trace, is too absolute; the metaphorical origin lingers at the margins of awareness.
missing_condition: The definition draws a binary distinction between live and dead metaphors, but it fails to accommodate the large intermediate class of dormant or faded metaphors in which the figurative origin is not fully present to consciousness but has not entirely vanished — expressions that hover between the literal and the metaphorical.
passage: It is commonly held that an expression is a dead metaphor if and only if it was originally used metaphorically with a living sense of figurative transfer, through repeated use it has lost all trace of its metaphorical origin, and competent speakers process it as a literal expression without any awareness of the original figure. The account draws a sharp line between live metaphors, in which the figurative transfer is felt by the speaker, and dead ones, in which the transfer has been forgotten entirely. 'The mouth of a river' is often cited as an example: speakers use the expression without thinking of a human mouth; the word 'mouth' has simply acquired a secondary literal sense meaning 'opening' or 'point of discharge.' The three conditions seem to be satisfied. But one ought to consider 'the leg of a table.' This expression is conventional and longstanding; no one hesitates over it in ordinary speech. It is standardly classified as a dead metaphor. Yet a moment's reflection reveals that the metaphorical origin has not entirely vanished. English speakers retain a dim but recoverable awareness that tables do not literally have legs in the biological sense — that the word 'leg' is being extended from its primary application to the limbs of animals. The expression occupies an intermediate zone: it is not a living metaphor, for speakers do not experience the shock of figurative recognition that accompanies 'Juliet is the sun'; but neither has it lost all trace of its origin, for the bodily resonance of 'leg' persists at the margins of consciousness. The second condition, which requires the total extinction of all metaphorical awareness, is too demanding. What the case reveals is that the boundary between live and dead metaphors is not sharp but gradual, and that there exists a large class of expressions — dormant or fading metaphors — that the binary definition cannot accommodate.

definition: A name N may be bestowed upon an individual x if and only if (i) x exists at the time of naming, (ii) the person bestowing N is in a position to identify x by some perceptual or descriptive means, and (iii) the bestowal is a public act witnessed or recorded in a manner accessible to the linguistic community.
conditions: x exists at the time of naming
the person bestowing N is in a position to identify x by some perceptual or descriptive means
the bestowal is a public act witnessed or recorded in a manner accessible to the linguistic community
counterexample: Consider the naming of a future child. Expectant parents commonly choose and bestow a name — 'Charlotte,' say — upon a child who has not yet been born and therefore does not, in the fullest sense, exist as a separate individual at the time of naming. The parents cannot identify the child perceptually; they have not seen or met the person they are naming. And the bestowal is often a private decision between two individuals, not a public act in any formal sense. All three conditions fail or are only partially satisfied, yet the name is genuinely bestowed and will function as the child's name from birth.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the named individual exist and be identifiable at the time of naming, but it fails to accommodate the widespread practice of naming future or anticipated entities — children not yet born, ships not yet launched, institutions not yet founded — where the name is bestowed in advance and awaits its bearer.
passage: One might propose that a name may be bestowed upon an individual if and only if the individual exists at the time of naming, the person bestowing the name is in a position to identify the individual by perceptual or descriptive means, and the bestowal is a public act accessible to the linguistic community. The account captures many paradigmatic cases of naming. A child is born, the parents perceive the child, and the name is bestowed in a ceremony — a christening, a registration, a public announcement — that makes the naming accessible to the wider community. The conditions are all satisfied. But one must consider a different case. Expectant parents, some months before the birth of their child, choose the name 'Charlotte.' They refer to the expected child by this name in conversation; they write it on cards and tell their friends. The name is bestowed, in every meaningful sense, upon an individual who does not yet exist. The parents cannot identify Charlotte by perceptual means, for Charlotte has not yet been born; they identify her only as 'the child we are expecting,' a description that does not yet have a determinate bearer. And the bestowal is a private decision between two persons, not a public act in any formal or institutional sense. Yet no one would deny that the child, upon being born, bears the name Charlotte, and that this name was chosen and bestowed in advance of her existence. The definition, in requiring the present existence of the named individual and the possibility of perceptual identification, fails to accommodate the pervasive practice of naming in anticipation — a practice that extends not only to unborn children but to ships not yet launched, institutions not yet founded, and projects not yet begun.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a question if and only if (i) U has interrogative grammatical form, (ii) the speaker S does not know the answer to the question expressed by U, and (iii) S desires to obtain the answer from the hearer H.
conditions: U has interrogative grammatical form
the speaker S does not know the answer to the question expressed by U
S desires to obtain the answer from the hearer H
counterexample: Consider the case of a barrister who asks a witness in cross-examination, 'Were you not, in fact, at the public house on the evening of the twelfth?' The barrister knows the answer perfectly well — has documentary evidence that the witness was indeed at the public house — and does not desire to obtain the answer from the witness so much as to compel the witness to state it before the jury. The second condition fails, and arguably the third fails in its standard interpretation. Yet the utterance is unquestionably a question; it has the form and the illocutionary force of a question, and the witness is expected to answer.
missing_condition: The definition treats questions as expressions of genuine ignorance and a desire for information, but it fails to accommodate the large class of questions — rhetorical questions, examination questions, cross-examination questions, Socratic questions — in which the speaker already knows the answer and the purpose of asking is not to acquire information but to elicit a response for some other conversational or institutional purpose.
passage: One might propose that an utterance constitutes a question if and only if it has interrogative grammatical form, the speaker does not know the answer, and the speaker desires to obtain the answer from the hearer. The account captures the simple case of information-seeking. A traveller asks a stranger, 'What time does the next train depart?' The utterance has interrogative form, the traveller does not know the answer, and the traveller wishes to learn it from the stranger. All three conditions are satisfied, and the utterance is plainly a question. Yet one must consider the barrister in cross-examination. The barrister, addressing a witness, asks: 'Were you not, in fact, at the public house on the evening of the twelfth?' The utterance has interrogative form; it is, grammatically and pragmatically, a question. But the barrister knows the answer. The barrister has documentary evidence — a receipt, a witness statement — establishing that the witness was at the public house. The purpose of the question is not to acquire information the barrister lacks but to compel the witness to state, before the jury, a fact the barrister already possesses. The second condition fails; the barrister is not ignorant of the answer. And the third condition is satisfied, if at all, only in a strained sense: the barrister desires not so much to obtain the answer as to elicit it publicly. The definition, in tying the question to the speaker's ignorance and desire for information, fails to accommodate the many contexts in which questions are asked by speakers who know the answers — in courtrooms, in classrooms, in philosophical dialogues, and in the rhetoric of everyday persuasion.

definition: A sentence S is precise if and only if (i) every predicate in S has a sharp boundary separating its extension from its anti-extension, (ii) the truth-value of S is determinately true or determinately false in every possible state of affairs, and (iii) no competent speaker would hesitate in assigning a truth-value to S in any given case.
conditions: every predicate in S has a sharp boundary separating its extension from its anti-extension
the truth-value of S is determinately true or determinately false in every possible state of affairs
no competent speaker would hesitate in assigning a truth-value to S in any given case
counterexample: Consider the sentence '7 is a prime number.' Every predicate in this sentence — 'is a prime number' — has a sharp boundary; every natural number is determinately prime or determinately not prime. The truth-value of the sentence is determinately true. Yet a very young child learning arithmetic might hesitate in assigning a truth-value to the sentence, not because of any vagueness in the predicate but because of incomplete mathematical education. The third condition fails when applied to some speakers, even though the sentence is, by every reasonable standard, precise. The definition conflates the precision of a sentence with the confidence of all possible competent speakers, but competence admits of degrees and domains.
missing_condition: The definition requires that no competent speaker would hesitate, but competence is itself a vague and domain-relative notion; what is needed is a condition specifying that the precision of a sentence is a feature of its semantic properties — the sharpness of its predicates and the determinacy of its truth-conditions — rather than a psychological fact about the responses of speakers.
passage: One might hold that a sentence is precise if and only if every predicate in it has a sharp boundary separating its extension from its anti-extension, the sentence has a determinate truth-value in every possible state of affairs, and no competent speaker would hesitate to assign a truth-value to the sentence in any given case. The account seems to capture the notion of precision as the absence of vagueness: a precise sentence is one that draws sharp lines, admits of no borderline cases, and occasions no uncertainty in competent speakers. The sentence '2 + 2 = 4' satisfies all three conditions admirably: the predicates are sharp, the truth-value is determinate, and no speaker hesitates. But consider the sentence '7 is a prime number.' The predicate 'is a prime number' has a perfectly sharp boundary; every natural number is determinately prime or determinately not. The truth-value of the sentence is determinately true. The sentence is precise by any reasonable standard. Yet the third condition — that no competent speaker would hesitate — is not unambiguously satisfied. A child of six, learning arithmetic, may hesitate when asked whether 7 is prime; the child may not yet have mastered the concept of primality. Is the child a competent speaker? The child is a competent speaker of English in many domains but not, perhaps, in the domain of number theory. The definition, in requiring universal absence of hesitation among competent speakers, introduces an unwanted dependency on the notion of competence, which is itself vague and domain-relative. What the case reveals is that precision is a semantic property of sentences — a matter of the sharpness of their predicates and the determinacy of their truth-conditions — and ought not to be defined in terms of the psychological responses of speakers, however competent.

definition: A general term F refers to a natural kind K if and only if (i) F is associated with a set of observable properties that are jointly sufficient for membership in K, (ii) these properties are possessed by all and only the members of K, and (iii) any competent speaker can determine whether an object belongs to K by checking for these properties.
conditions: F is associated with a set of observable properties that are jointly sufficient for membership in K
these properties are possessed by all and only the members of K
any competent speaker can determine whether an object belongs to K by checking for these properties
counterexample: Consider the term 'water.' One might suppose that water is identified by its observable properties: it is a colourless, odourless, tasteless liquid that quenches thirst, fills lakes and rivers, and falls as rain. But these properties are not, in fact, jointly sufficient for membership in the kind water, for there might exist a substance — call it XYZ — that shares all these observable properties yet differs in its underlying chemical composition. XYZ would satisfy every observable condition associated with 'water' and yet would not be water. The first and second conditions fail: the observable properties do not uniquely pick out the kind.
missing_condition: The definition ties kind-membership to observable surface properties, but it fails to recognise that natural kinds may be individuated by underlying structural or compositional properties — such as chemical composition — that are not observable by ordinary speakers and that may diverge from the observable properties used in everyday identification.
passage: One might propose that a general term refers to a natural kind if and only if the term is associated with a set of observable properties jointly sufficient for kind-membership, these properties are possessed by all and only the members of the kind, and any competent speaker can determine membership by checking for the properties. The account has an appealing simplicity. 'Gold' refers to the kind gold, and one identifies gold by its observable properties: it is yellow, metallic, lustrous, heavy, malleable. A speaker who encounters an object possessing all these properties is entitled to classify it as gold. For practical purposes, the account often works. Yet one must consider the term 'water.' Water is identified in everyday life by a cluster of observable properties: it is a colourless, odourless, tasteless liquid; it quenches thirst; it fills rivers and falls as rain. These properties are jointly sufficient for the practical identification of water in most circumstances. But are they sufficient for kind-membership? Imagine a substance — one may call it XYZ — that is indistinguishable from water in all observable respects: colourless, odourless, tasteless, liquid at room temperature, capable of quenching thirst and sustaining life. Suppose, however, that XYZ differs from water in its underlying chemical composition. XYZ satisfies every observable condition associated with 'water,' and any competent speaker, inspecting XYZ without specialised equipment, would classify it as water. Yet XYZ is not water, for water is H2O and XYZ is not. The first and second conditions fail: the observable properties do not uniquely identify the kind. What the case reveals is that natural-kind terms may be anchored not to observable surface properties but to underlying structural features — chemical composition, microphysical constitution — that are not accessible to ordinary observation and that may come apart from the properties by which speakers ordinarily identify instances of the kind.

definition: A translation T of a text X from language L1 into language L2 is faithful if and only if (i) T preserves the propositional content of every sentence in X, (ii) T preserves the rhetorical structure — the order of arguments, the arrangement of paragraphs, the progression of thought — of X, and (iii) T introduces no content not present in X.
conditions: T preserves the propositional content of every sentence in X
T preserves the rhetorical structure of X
T introduces no content not present in X
counterexample: Consider the translation of a passage of classical Chinese poetry into English. Classical Chinese is a terse, imagistic language with minimal grammatical marking; English requires articles, pronouns, tense, and explicit syntactic structure that the Chinese does not provide. A faithful English translation must supply these elements — must introduce 'the,' 'a,' 'he,' tense markers, and connectives that have no direct counterparts in the original. The third condition fails, for the translation necessarily introduces content — grammatical content, at a minimum — that is not present in the source text. Yet the translation may be entirely faithful in every sense that matters.
missing_condition: The definition requires that no content be introduced beyond what the original contains, but it fails to acknowledge that the grammatical and structural differences between languages make the introduction of certain elements unavoidable in translation, and that faithfulness must be judged relative to the expressive resources of the target language, not by an impossible standard of zero addition.
passage: One might hold that a translation of a text is faithful if and only if it preserves the propositional content of every sentence, preserves the rhetorical structure of the original, and introduces no content not present in the source. The account embodies a natural ideal: a faithful translation says exactly what the original says, in the same order, and adds nothing of its own. For translations between closely related languages — from French into English, say, or from Portuguese into Spanish — the ideal is often approximately achievable, and the three conditions can be substantially satisfied. But one must consider a more demanding case. A translator undertakes to render a passage of classical Chinese poetry into English. The Chinese original is terse in the extreme: it consists of a sequence of images — moon, river, boat, pine — with minimal grammatical apparatus. There are no articles, no explicit pronouns, no tense markers, no conjunctions binding the images together. The English language requires these elements; one cannot write grammatical English without them. The translator must therefore supply articles: 'the moon,' 'a river.' The translator must introduce pronouns: 'he gazes.' The translator must choose a tense: 'the moon shone' or 'the moon shines.' And the translator must, in many cases, introduce connectives — 'and,' 'but,' 'as' — that make explicit the relations between images that the Chinese leaves implicit. The third condition fails: the translation necessarily introduces content not present in the original. Yet this introduction is not a defect of the translation; it is an inevitable consequence of the structural differences between the two languages. The definition, in demanding that nothing be added, sets an impossible standard — one that would render all translation from structurally dissimilar languages unfaithful by definition.

definition: An utterance U is meaningful if and only if (i) U expresses a proposition, (ii) the proposition expressed by U is either true or false, and (iii) the truth or falsity of the proposition is independent of the context in which U is uttered.
conditions: U expresses a proposition
the proposition expressed by U is either true or false
the truth or falsity of the proposition is independent of the context in which U is uttered
counterexample: Consider the utterance 'I am here now.' This utterance is meaningful — indeed, it is trivially true whenever it is uttered. It expresses a proposition, and the proposition is true. But the proposition expressed is not independent of the context of utterance: which proposition is expressed depends entirely on who says 'I,' where 'here' is, and when 'now' is. The same sentence, uttered by different speakers at different times and places, expresses different propositions. The third condition fails, for the truth-value of the proposition depends constitutively on the context. Yet the utterance is meaningful on every occasion of its use.
missing_condition: The definition requires context-independence, but it fails to accommodate the pervasive class of context-dependent or indexical expressions — 'I,' 'here,' 'now,' 'this,' 'that' — whose meaningfulness consists precisely in their capacity to express different propositions in different contexts of utterance.
passage: A tempting account of meaningfulness holds that an utterance is meaningful if and only if it expresses a proposition, the proposition is either true or false, and the truth or falsity of the proposition is independent of the context in which the utterance is made. The account has the attraction of tying meaning firmly to propositional content and truth-value, and it works well for a range of context-free sentences. 'Snow is white' expresses the same proposition regardless of who says it, when, or where, and its truth-value does not vary with context. The three conditions are satisfied, and the utterance is meaningful. But one must consider the utterance 'I am here now.' This sentence is meaningful; any competent speaker of English understands it immediately. It expresses a proposition — namely, that the speaker is located at the place of utterance at the time of utterance — and the proposition is true whenever the sentence is sincerely asserted. The first two conditions are satisfied. But the third condition fails. Which proposition is expressed depends entirely upon the context: upon who is speaking, where they are, and when they speak. 'I am here now,' uttered by one person in London at noon, expresses a different proposition from the same sentence uttered by a different person in Edinburgh at midnight. The truth-value of the proposition, far from being independent of context, is constitutively determined by it. The definition, in requiring context-independence, excludes the entire class of utterances containing indexical expressions — 'I,' 'here,' 'now,' 'this,' 'today,' 'yesterday' — which form a large and indispensable part of meaningful discourse. What is wanted is an account that recognises that meaningfulness may consist precisely in the systematic dependence of propositional content upon context.

definition: A word w has meaning if and only if (i) w is associated with a mental image in the mind of any competent speaker who understands it, (ii) this mental image is qualitatively the same across all competent speakers, and (iii) the meaning of w is exhausted by the content of this image.
conditions: w is associated with a mental image in the mind of any competent speaker who understands it
this mental image is qualitatively the same across all competent speakers
the meaning of w is exhausted by the content of this image
