counterexample: Consider the word 'justice.' It is meaningful — indeed, it is one of the most important words in the vocabulary of any civilised language. Yet it is far from obvious that 'justice' is associated with a mental image in the mind of the speaker. When one thinks of justice, one does not form a picture in the way that one might form a picture when thinking of a red ball or a tall tree. And even if some speakers do associate a vague image with the word — the image of a pair of scales, perhaps, or of a blindfolded figure — it would be rash to suppose that this image is qualitatively the same across all competent speakers. A Roman lawyer and an English barrister might associate quite different images with the word, yet both understand it perfectly well. The first and second conditions appear to fail, yet the word is manifestly meaningful.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that meaning is constituted by mental imagery, but many meaningful words — particularly abstract and evaluative terms — are understood without the mediation of any determinate image. What is wanted is an account of meaning that accommodates the understanding of abstract vocabulary, where meaning may consist in inferential role, conditions of correct application, or connections to other concepts rather than in associated imagery.
passage: One might propose that a word has meaning if and only if it is associated with a mental image in the mind of any competent speaker, this image is qualitatively the same across all speakers, and the meaning of the word is wholly constituted by the content of this image. The proposal has a certain intuitive force for concrete terms. When one hears the word 'horse,' one may well form a mental picture of a horse — a four-legged creature of a certain size and form — and one might suppose that this picture is tolerably similar from speaker to speaker. Likewise, the word 'red' may call up a mental image of a patch of colour, and one might think that this image is what the word means. Yet one must consider the word 'justice.' This word is meaningful; it is used with confidence in legal argument, in moral philosophy, in the daily affairs of civic life. But it is not obviously associated with any mental image. When one thinks of justice, one does not visualise a particular scene or object in the way that one visualises a horse or a patch of red. Some speakers may associate the word with the image of a pair of scales, others with a courtroom, others with no image at all — yet all understand the word. The first condition is not clearly satisfied, and the second condition, requiring qualitative identity of the image across speakers, fails plainly. The meaning of 'justice' is not an image but something else entirely — a network of inferential connections, a set of conditions for correct and incorrect application, a role within a broader evaluative vocabulary. The definition has taken a feature that belongs to certain concrete words and elevated it to a universal requirement, when in truth a great portion of meaningful language operates without the mediation of imagery.

definition: A singular term t refers to an object o if and only if (i) the speaker can provide a definite description that uniquely identifies o, (ii) the speaker can distinguish o from all other objects by means of this description, and (iii) the speaker's referential intention is directed at the object satisfying the description.
conditions: the speaker can provide a definite description that uniquely identifies o
the speaker can distinguish o from all other objects by means of this description
the speaker's referential intention is directed at the object satisfying the description
counterexample: Consider a young child who uses the name 'Mummy' to refer to her mother. The child succeeds in referring — she picks out a particular individual and is understood by all who hear her. Yet the child cannot provide a definite description that uniquely identifies her mother as opposed to all other women. If pressed, the child might say 'the lady who lives in my house,' but this would not uniquely identify the mother if there were, as there might be, an aunt or a grandmother in the same house. The first and second conditions fail, yet the child refers successfully.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the speaker be able to articulate a uniquely identifying description, but speakers — particularly young children, linguistically unsophisticated speakers, and persons in hurried conversation — may refer successfully without possessing any such description, provided the referential link is sustained by causal contact, ostension, or communal practice rather than by descriptive identification.
passage: It is natural to propose that a singular term refers to an object if and only if the speaker can provide a definite description uniquely identifying that object, can distinguish the object from all others by means of this description, and directs her referential intention at the object satisfying the description. The proposal appears to capture the referential competence of a mature, reflective speaker. When a philosopher uses the name 'Frege,' she might offer the description 'the author of the Begriffsschrift' or 'the logician who distinguished between sense and reference,' and this description uniquely picks out the intended individual. Yet one must consider the case of a young child who uses the name 'Mummy' to refer to her mother. The child refers successfully; she picks out a particular person, communicates her desires and observations, and is understood without the slightest difficulty. But the child cannot provide a definite description that uniquely identifies her mother. She has no stock of individuating descriptions at her disposal. Were one to ask her who she means by 'Mummy,' she might say 'the lady who looks after me' or 'the lady in the house,' but these descriptions would not distinguish her mother from an aunt, a nanny, or a grandmother who might equally satisfy them. The first and second conditions fail. The child's successful reference is sustained not by descriptive identification but by something else — by the causal and perceptual contact she has with her mother, by the ostensive situations in which the name was first introduced. The definition has modelled reference upon the capacities of a philosophically articulate adult and has thereby excluded the perfectly competent referential practices of those who lack descriptive sophistication.

definition: A name N is a genuine proper name if and only if (i) N applies to exactly one individual, (ii) N has no descriptive content — that is, N contributes nothing to the meaning of a sentence beyond identifying its bearer, and (iii) N is conferred in a single, datable act of naming.
conditions: N applies to exactly one individual
N has no descriptive content
N is conferred in a single, datable act of naming
counterexample: Consider the name 'John.' It applies not to exactly one individual but to millions of individuals across many countries and centuries. One cannot point to a single bearer of 'John'; the name is shared by countless persons. The first condition fails. Yet 'John' is a paradigmatic proper name — it is used on every occasion as a name, not as a description or a general term, and each use of it in a particular conversational context succeeds in picking out a particular individual.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a proper name apply to exactly one individual, but many proper names are shared by numerous individuals. What distinguishes a proper name from a common noun is not uniqueness of bearer but the manner in which it is used — to identify a particular individual in a given context of utterance — rather than to describe or classify.
passage: One might define a genuine proper name as a word that applies to exactly one individual, has no descriptive content, and is conferred in a single datable act of naming. The definition captures something of the intuitive contrast between proper names and common nouns: 'Socrates' applies to one man, describes nothing about him, and was presumably bestowed upon him at birth or shortly thereafter. 'Horse,' by contrast, applies to many individuals and carries descriptive content. The proposal seems clean and well-motivated. Yet one must attend to the name 'John.' This name is, by any ordinary reckoning, a proper name; it is listed in every register of personal names, it functions syntactically as a proper name in every language that employs it, and speakers use it to identify particular individuals. But it does not apply to exactly one individual. It is borne by millions of persons — kings, apostles, plumbers, schoolchildren — across the whole history of English-speaking civilisation and beyond. The first condition of the definition fails outright. One cannot salvage the definition by saying that each 'John' is a different name that merely shares a phonological form with the others, for speakers treat 'John' as a single name that happens to have many bearers, much as a surname may be shared by many families. The definition has confused a contingent feature of some names — the fact that they happen to be unique to a single individual — with a necessary condition of proper-namehood, when in truth what makes a word a proper name is not the singularity of its bearer but the manner of its employment in discourse.

definition: A translation T of a sentence S from language L1 into language L2 is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-conditions of S — that is, T is true in L2 in exactly those circumstances in which S is true in L1, (ii) T preserves the logical form of S, and (iii) T employs only words in L2 that have exact equivalents in L1.
conditions: T preserves the truth-conditions of S
T preserves the logical form of S
T employs only words in L2 that have exact equivalents in L1
counterexample: Consider the German word 'Schadenfreude,' meaning pleasure derived from another's misfortune. An adequate English translation of a sentence containing 'Schadenfreude' might render it as 'malicious pleasure in another's suffering' or 'delight at another's misfortune.' These translations may well preserve the truth-conditions of the original. But they do not employ a word in English that has an exact equivalent of 'Schadenfreude'; English has no single-word equivalent, and the translator must resort to a periphrasis. The third condition fails, yet the translation may be perfectly adequate for all practical and scholarly purposes.
missing_condition: The definition requires exact lexical equivalence between the two languages, but natural languages differ in their lexical resources, and an adequate translation may employ paraphrases, circumlocutions, or approximate equivalents where no exact match exists. What is wanted is a condition concerning the preservation of communicative content, not the existence of one-to-one lexical correspondences.
passage: Suppose one holds that a translation of a sentence from one language into another is adequate if and only if it preserves the truth-conditions of the original, preserves its logical form, and employs only words that have exact equivalents in the source language. The first two conditions are plausible and widely endorsed; a translation that alters what the sentence says, or that reshapes its logical structure beyond recognition, has failed in its task. The third condition seems a natural addition: one might think that a translation that introduces words with no counterpart in the original is, in some sense, saying something new rather than reproducing what was said. Yet one must consider the case of the German word 'Schadenfreude.' This word, denoting pleasure taken in the misfortune of another, has no single-word equivalent in English. The translator who encounters 'Schadenfreude' in a German text must render it by a phrase — 'malicious delight in another's suffering,' perhaps, or 'pleasure at another person's ill fortune.' Such a phrase may capture the truth-conditions of the original sentence tolerably well. But it does not employ a word in English that is the exact equivalent of 'Schadenfreude'; it employs several words, none of which individually means what 'Schadenfreude' means. The third condition fails. Yet the translation is adequate — it communicates the content of the original, it is understood by the English reader, and it serves its purpose. The definition has demanded a degree of lexical correspondence that natural languages, with their divergent vocabularies and differing ways of carving up experience, simply do not afford. An adequate theory of translation must accommodate the ubiquitous phenomenon of lexical gaps, in which one language possesses a word for which no single equivalent exists in another.

definition: A sentence S is metaphorical if and only if (i) S is literally false, (ii) the speaker of S knows it to be literally false, and (iii) the hearer of S recognises that the speaker does not intend the literal meaning.
conditions: S is literally false
the speaker of S knows it to be literally false
the hearer of S recognises that the speaker does not intend the literal meaning
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'No man is an island,' uttered by a preacher in a sermon. The sentence is literally true — no human being is, in point of geographical fact, an island. Yet the sentence is metaphorical; it is understood by all parties as expressing something about human interdependence and the impossibility of complete self-sufficiency, not as making a trivial geographical observation. The first condition fails, for the sentence is literally true rather than literally false, yet it is a paradigmatic metaphor.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that metaphors are always literally false, but some metaphors are literally true and derive their metaphorical force from the manifest triviality or obviousness of their literal content. What is wanted is a characterisation that accommodates both literally false and literally true metaphors, perhaps by attending to the speaker's intention to communicate something beyond or different from the literal meaning.
passage: One might propose that a sentence is metaphorical if and only if it is literally false, the speaker knows it to be literally false, and the hearer recognises that the speaker does not intend the literal meaning. The definition captures many familiar cases. When one says 'Juliet is the sun,' the sentence is literally false — Juliet is a person, not a celestial body — and both speaker and hearer are aware of this. The metaphorical interpretation is triggered precisely by the recognition that the literal content is false and that the speaker must therefore intend something else. Yet one must attend to the sentence 'No man is an island.' This sentence, which Donne made famous and which has become one of the most widely quoted metaphors in the English language, is literally true. No human being is a body of land surrounded by water. The literal truth of the sentence is so obvious as to be trivial, and it is this very triviality that signals to the hearer that the speaker intends something beyond the literal meaning — that human beings are not self-sufficient, that each person is connected to and dependent upon others. The sentence is metaphorical, and powerfully so, yet it is not literally false. The first condition of the definition fails. The definition has assumed that literal falsity is the mechanism by which metaphorical meaning is activated, but in truth some of the most effective metaphors operate through literal truth whose very obviousness prompts the hearer to search for a deeper significance.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker S asserts that S will perform some future action A, (ii) S intends to perform A, and (iii) the hearer H desires that A be performed.
conditions: the speaker S asserts that S will perform some future action A
S intends to perform A
the hearer H desires that A be performed
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man promises his dying father that he will visit the family grave each year. The father, who is lucid but knows he will shortly die, extracts this promise. The man asserts that he will visit the grave, and he intends to do so. But the father, once dead, will have no desires at all — and even at the moment of the promise, the father's desire is not that the action be performed for his own benefit in any straightforward sense, since he knows he will not be alive to witness it. Suppose, further, a case in which a person promises to repay a debt to a creditor who is indifferent about receiving repayment and does not particularly desire it. The third condition fails in both cases, yet these are genuine promises.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the hearer desire the promised action, but promises may be made to hearers who are indifferent, who will soon cease to exist, or who extract the promise for reasons other than personal desire. What is wanted is a condition concerning the speaker's undertaking of an obligation, not the hearer's psychological attitude toward the promised action.
passage: It is natural to suppose that an utterance constitutes a promise if and only if the speaker asserts that she will perform some future action, intends to perform it, and the hearer desires that the action be performed. The definition captures the prototypical case of promising: one promises a friend to meet her at a particular time, one intends to keep the appointment, and the friend wants one to appear. The three conditions seem jointly to characterise what it is to make a promise. Yet one must consider the case of a man who promises his dying father to visit the family grave each year. The man asserts that he will perform the action and intends to carry it out. But the father will shortly be dead, and the dead have no desires. Even at the moment the promise is extracted, the father's attitude is perhaps better described as a wish for the perpetuation of a familial duty than as a desire that the specific action be performed for his own gratification. Suppose, moreover, that a debtor promises to repay a sum to a wealthy creditor who is genuinely indifferent to whether the money is returned. The creditor does not desire repayment; he has forgotten the debt. Yet the debtor's utterance constitutes a promise, and the debtor is bound by it. The third condition fails in both cases. Promising, it seems, does not require that the hearer desire the promised action; it requires rather that the speaker place herself under an obligation to perform it. The definition has confused a common accompaniment of promising — the hearer's desire — with a constitutive condition of the act itself.

definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) there exist borderline cases to which F neither clearly applies nor clearly fails to apply, (ii) F admits of degrees — objects can be more or less F, and (iii) F lacks any precise boundary separating the things to which it applies from the things to which it does not.
conditions: there exist borderline cases to which F neither clearly applies nor clearly fails to apply
F admits of degrees — objects can be more or less F
F lacks any precise boundary separating its positive from its negative extension
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is a heap.' It admits of borderline cases: when one grain is removed from a heap, at some point one is uncertain whether what remains is still a heap. It lacks a precise boundary. Yet it is not obvious that 'heap' admits of degrees in the way that 'tall' or 'red' does. One does not naturally say that one collection of sand is more a heap than another; a collection of sand is either a heap or it is not, though one may be uncertain which. The second condition appears to fail, yet 'heap' is the paradigmatic example of a vague predicate — it is the very term from which the sorites paradox takes its name.
missing_condition: The definition requires that vague predicates admit of degrees, but some vague predicates — particularly those denoting the presence or absence of a qualitative condition rather than a quantitative magnitude — may resist degree-attribution even as they exhibit borderline cases and lack precise boundaries. What is wanted is a characterisation that does not presuppose that all vagueness is a matter of degree.
passage: One might define a vague predicate as one for which there exist borderline cases, which admits of degrees, and which lacks any precise boundary separating its extension from its anti-extension. The definition appears to capture the central features of familiar vague predicates. 'Tall' has borderline cases — persons of intermediate height to whom the predicate neither clearly applies nor clearly fails to apply — admits of degrees, since one person may be taller than another, and lacks a precise cut-off point separating the tall from the not-tall. 'Red' similarly has borderline cases in the region of orange, admits of degrees of redness, and lacks a sharp boundary. Yet one must consider the predicate 'is a heap,' from which the ancient sorites paradox takes its very name. 'Heap' has borderline cases: as one removes grains from a heap of sand, one arrives at collections about which one is genuinely uncertain whether they constitute a heap. 'Heap' lacks a precise boundary: there is no determinate number of grains at which a heap ceases to be a heap. But it is not evident that 'heap' admits of degrees in any straightforward sense. One does not ordinarily say that one pile of sand is more a heap than another pile of similar size but different shape. The concept of a heap seems to be an all-or-nothing affair — one either has a heap or one does not — even though the boundary between having a heap and not having one is irreducibly unclear. The second condition of the definition fails, yet 'heap' is the paradigmatic vague predicate in the philosophical literature. The definition has assumed that all vagueness is a matter of degree, but some vague predicates resist degree-attribution whilst still exhibiting the hallmarks of vagueness.

definition: Two sentences S1 and S2 have the same meaning if and only if (i) S1 and S2 have the same truth-conditions, (ii) S1 and S2 are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value, and (iii) S1 and S2 produce the same cognitive response in any competent speaker who understands both.
conditions: S1 and S2 have the same truth-conditions
S1 and S2 are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value
S1 and S2 produce the same cognitive response in any competent speaker who understands both
counterexample: Consider the sentences 'The morning star is a planet' and 'The evening star is a planet.' These sentences have the same truth-conditions, for the morning star is the evening star — both are Venus — and so the sentence is true if and only if Venus is a planet. They are intersubstitutable in extensional contexts without change of truth-value. But they do not produce the same cognitive response in a speaker. An ancient astronomer who did not know that the morning star and the evening star are the same body might accept the first sentence and reject the second, or vice versa. The third condition fails, yet the two sentences have the same truth-conditions and are extensionally equivalent.
missing_condition: The definition conflates sameness of truth-conditions with sameness of cognitive significance. Two sentences may share truth-conditions and extensional substitutability whilst differing in the mode of presentation of the object they concern. What is wanted is a distinction between meaning understood as truth-conditional content and meaning understood as sense or cognitive value, recognising that sameness of the former does not guarantee sameness of the latter.
passage: Suppose one holds that two sentences have the same meaning if and only if they have the same truth-conditions, they are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value, and they produce the same cognitive response in any competent speaker. The first two conditions capture what many philosophers have taken to be the core of sentential meaning: if two sentences are true in exactly the same circumstances and can be substituted for one another in all straightforward contexts, it seems reasonable to say that they mean the same thing. The third condition adds a psychological requirement that appears to strengthen the account further. Yet one must attend to the sentences 'The morning star is a planet' and 'The evening star is a planet.' The morning star is the evening star — both expressions designate the planet Venus — and so the two sentences are true in exactly the same circumstances. They are intersubstitutable in extensional contexts without change of truth-value. But they do not produce the same cognitive response. An astronomer of antiquity, unaware that the bright body visible before dawn and the bright body visible after sunset are one and the same planet, might assent to the one sentence and withhold assent from the other. The discovery that the morning star is the evening star was a substantive astronomical achievement, not a triviality. The two sentences differ in what Frege would have called their sense, even though they agree in their reference and their truth-conditions. The third condition of the definition fails. The analysis has run together two notions of meaning that must be kept distinct: meaning as truth-conditional content, which the two sentences share, and meaning as cognitive significance, which they do not.

definition: A definite description 'the F' refers to an object o if and only if (i) there exists exactly one object satisfying the predicate F, (ii) the speaker uses 'the F' with the intention of picking out that unique object, and (iii) the context of utterance is such that 'the F' could not reasonably be taken to pick out any other object.
conditions: there exists exactly one object satisfying the predicate F
the speaker uses 'the F' with the intention of picking out that unique object
the context of utterance is such that 'the F' could not reasonably be taken to pick out any other object
counterexample: Consider a speaker who says, at a dinner party, 'The table is covered with plates.' There is not exactly one table in the world; there are millions. The predicate 'table' is satisfied by a vast multitude of objects. Yet the speaker's utterance succeeds in referring to a particular table — the one at which the dinner is being held. The context of utterance restricts the domain so that 'the table' picks out the salient table, even though the first condition, taken without contextual restriction, fails.
missing_condition: The definition requires that exactly one object satisfy the predicate absolutely, but in ordinary discourse definite descriptions typically succeed in referring by means of contextual restriction of the domain rather than by there being a unique satisfier of the predicate across all of reality. What is wanted is a condition that accommodates the role of context in narrowing the domain of quantification.
passage: One might propose that a definite description 'the F' refers to an object if and only if there exists exactly one object satisfying the predicate F, the speaker intends to pick out that object, and the context is such that no other referent could reasonably be supposed. The analysis has considerable appeal when one considers descriptions such as 'the present Queen of England,' where there is, in fact, exactly one individual satisfying the description, and no ambiguity arises. Yet one must consider the case of a speaker at a dinner party who says 'The table is covered with plates.' There is not exactly one table in the world. There are tables in every house, every restaurant, every classroom, every workshop in every country. The predicate 'table' is satisfied by an astronomically large number of objects. Yet the speaker's utterance refers without difficulty to a particular table — the one at which the guests are seated. Every person present understands which table is meant. The first condition of the definition, if construed as requiring that there be exactly one F in the whole of reality, fails spectacularly. What makes the reference succeed is not the uniqueness of the satisfier of the predicate but the restriction imposed by the conversational context upon the domain within which uniqueness is assessed. The speaker and her hearers tacitly restrict the domain to the objects in the immediate environment, and within that restricted domain there is one and only one table. The definition has overlooked the essential contribution of context to the functioning of definite descriptions in ordinary discourse.

definition: An utterance U is an assertion if and only if (i) the speaker S believes the propositional content of U, (ii) S intends the hearer H to come to believe the propositional content of U, and (iii) S presents U as a straightforward expression of S's belief.
conditions: the speaker S believes the propositional content of U
S intends the hearer H to come to believe the propositional content of U
S presents U as a straightforward expression of S's belief
counterexample: Consider a barrister who, in the course of a trial, asserts 'My client was at home on the evening in question.' The barrister may not believe this — she may have strong private doubts about her client's whereabouts. Yet she asserts it, and the assertion has all the conventional marks of assertion: it is put forward as a claim, it invites the jury to accept it as true, and it has legal and social consequences. The first condition fails, yet the utterance is an assertion.
missing_condition: The definition requires belief on the part of the speaker, but assertions may be made by speakers who do not believe what they assert, as in legal advocacy, diplomatic communication, and other institutional contexts in which the norms governing assertion are determined by the role of the speaker rather than by her private beliefs. What is wanted is an account that accommodates the conventional and institutional dimensions of assertion.
passage: One might hold that an utterance is an assertion if and only if the speaker believes its propositional content, intends the hearer to come to believe it, and presents it as a straightforward expression of belief. The definition captures the ordinary case of sincere assertion tolerably well. When a man says 'It is raining,' he typically believes that it is raining, he intends his hearer to come to accept this, and he presents his utterance as an honest report of how things stand. But one must consider the case of a barrister who, in the course of a criminal trial, asserts before the jury that her client was at home on the evening in question. The barrister may well harbour private doubts about the truth of this claim; she may believe, on the basis of her private knowledge of the case, that her client was elsewhere. Yet she asserts it. She puts the proposition forward with all the conventional force of assertion — she invites the jury to accept it, she presents it as a claim to be weighed alongside the evidence, and the assertion has legal consequences. The utterance bears every outward mark of an assertion. The first condition, however, fails: the barrister does not believe the content of what she says. The definition has modelled assertion upon the norms of sincere everyday communication and has thereby excluded the assertions made in institutional contexts — courtrooms, diplomatic negotiations, parliamentary debate — where the speaker's role, rather than her private convictions, determines what she is entitled and obliged to assert.

definition: A metaphorical utterance U communicates its intended meaning M if and only if (i) U has a literal meaning L that is distinct from M, (ii) the hearer first computes L and then, recognising its inadequacy, derives M by a process of comparison between the literal subject and the metaphorical predicate, and (iii) M can be fully stated as a literal paraphrase.
conditions: U has a literal meaning L distinct from M
the hearer first computes L and then derives M by comparison
M can be fully stated as a literal paraphrase
counterexample: Consider Shakespeare's 'All the world's a stage.' One might attempt to paraphrase this metaphor literally: 'Human life resembles a theatrical performance in that people adopt roles, enter and exit situations, and are observed by others.' But this paraphrase, however careful, does not capture the full force of the metaphor. The metaphor suggests further that life is a scripted affair, that human agency is perhaps an illusion, that there is an audience — and these suggestions proliferate without determinate limit. The third condition fails, for no finite literal paraphrase exhausts the meaning of the metaphor.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that metaphorical meaning can be fully captured by a literal paraphrase, but many metaphors generate an indefinite range of implications and associations that resist complete literal restatement. What is wanted is a recognition that metaphors may be cognitively and semantically irreducible — that they may communicate something that cannot be said in any other way.
passage: Suppose one holds that a metaphorical utterance communicates its intended meaning if and only if the utterance has a literal meaning distinct from the intended metaphorical meaning, the hearer first computes the literal meaning and then derives the metaphorical meaning by a process of comparison, and the metaphorical meaning can be fully stated as a literal paraphrase. The third condition seems especially reasonable: if one cannot say what a metaphor means in plain language, one might wonder whether it means anything at all. And for simple metaphors the paraphrase condition is easily met. 'He is a fox' may be paraphrased as 'He is cunning.' 'She has a heart of stone' may be rendered as 'She is unsympathetic.' Yet one must attend to more complex cases. Shakespeare's metaphor 'All the world's a stage' resists complete paraphrase. One might say that it means human life resembles a theatrical performance, that people adopt roles and are in some sense performers. But the metaphor suggests more: that there is a script, that exits and entrances are predetermined, that there is an audience, that the action is bounded by a beginning and an end that the actors do not control. These implications proliferate, each suggesting further implications, and no finite literal statement captures them all. The third condition fails. The metaphor communicates something that cannot be wholly translated into non-metaphorical language. The definition has assumed that metaphorical meaning is always a detour to a literal destination, but some metaphors are not paraphrasable in principle — they are not ornamental restatements of something that could have been said plainly, but irreducible instruments of thought.

definition: A proper name N has meaning if and only if (i) N can be analysed as an abbreviated definite description, (ii) the description associated with N is the same for all competent speakers, and (iii) the meaning of a sentence containing N can be given by replacing N with the associated description.
conditions: N can be analysed as an abbreviated definite description
the associated description is the same for all competent speakers
the meaning of a sentence containing N can be given by replacing N with the associated description
counterexample: Consider the name 'Aristotle.' One speaker may associate with this name the description 'the teacher of Alexander the Great'; another may associate 'the author of the Nicomachean Ethics'; a third may associate 'the most famous pupil of Plato.' These descriptions are not the same, and there is no canonical description that all competent speakers associate with the name. The second condition fails. Moreover, the sentence 'Aristotle was fond of dogs' does not have the same meaning as 'The teacher of Alexander was fond of dogs' for a speaker who associates the name with the description 'the author of the Nicomachean Ethics.' Yet the name is meaningful and is used successfully by all three speakers.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a single definite description be unanimously associated with the name by all speakers, but speakers typically associate different clusters of descriptions with a name, and successful use of the name does not depend upon agreement on any one description. What is wanted is an account of names that allows for variation in associated descriptive content among speakers, or that grounds the name's meaning in something other than description altogether.
passage: It is sometimes proposed that a proper name has meaning if and only if it can be analysed as an abbreviated definite description, this description is the same for all competent speakers, and the meaning of any sentence containing the name can be given by replacing the name with the description. The proposal has a distinguished pedigree and explains how names might be meaningful despite appearing to lack descriptive content: the name 'Aristotle' would mean 'the teacher of Alexander' or 'the author of the Metaphysics,' and the meaning of any sentence containing 'Aristotle' could be recovered by substituting the appropriate description. Yet one must ask: which description? Different speakers associate different descriptions with the name 'Aristotle.' A student of ancient military history might think of Aristotle primarily as the teacher of Alexander. A student of moral philosophy might think of him as the author of the Nicomachean Ethics. A student of Plato might identify him as Plato's most illustrious pupil. These are different descriptions, and they are not even extensionally equivalent, for there are possible circumstances in which the teacher of Alexander is not the author of the Ethics. The second condition of the definition fails plainly: there is no single description associated with 'Aristotle' by all competent speakers. The proposal that names are abbreviated descriptions founders on the manifest diversity of the descriptions that different speakers associate with the same name. If the meaning of 'Aristotle' were a particular description, then speakers who associated different descriptions would mean different things by the name, and communication would be imperilled. Yet communication proceeds without difficulty.

definition: The meaning of a sentence S is given if and only if (i) one specifies the truth-conditions of S — the state of affairs that must obtain for S to be true, (ii) the specification is compositional — the truth-conditions of S are determined by the meanings of its constituent expressions and the way they are syntactically combined, and (iii) the truth-conditions are stated in a metalanguage that is antecedently understood by the theorist.
conditions: One specifies the truth-conditions of S
The specification is compositional — truth-conditions are determined by meanings of constituents and syntactic combination
The truth-conditions are stated in a metalanguage antecedently understood by the theorist
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'Ouch!' uttered upon stubbing one's toe. It has no truth-conditions. It is not the sort of sentence that can be true or false; it is an exclamation, an expression of pain. Yet it is meaningful — any competent speaker of English understands it immediately. Condition (i) fails, since there is no state of affairs that must obtain for 'Ouch!' to be true. The sentence has meaning but no truth-conditions, and a theory that identifies meaning with truth-conditions cannot account for it.
missing_condition: The definition identifies meaning with truth-conditions, but many meaningful expressions — exclamations, imperatives, performatives — have no truth-conditions, and an adequate theory of meaning must accommodate the meaningful use of language beyond the declarative.
passage: One might propose that the meaning of a sentence is given when one specifies its truth-conditions — the state of affairs that must obtain for it to be true — where this specification is compositional and is stated in a metalanguage antecedently understood by the theorist. This is, broadly, the Davidsonian programme, and it has seemed to many philosophers to offer the most promising framework for a systematic theory of meaning. By assigning truth-conditions to every sentence of a language, one captures what any competent speaker implicitly grasps. Yet one might observe that the account is too narrow. Consider the sentence 'Ouch!' uttered upon stubbing one's toe. It is meaningful; any competent speaker of English understands it immediately and without difficulty. But it has no truth-conditions. It is not the sort of thing that can be true or false. It does not describe a state of affairs; it expresses a sensation. Condition (i) fails entirely. If meaning is truth-conditions, then 'Ouch!' is meaningless — a conclusion that is plainly absurd. And the difficulty is not confined to exclamations. Imperatives ('Shut the door!'), performatives ('I hereby promise to pay'), and questions ('Is it raining?') are all meaningful, and none of them have truth-conditions in the straightforward sense. What the case reveals is that the identification of meaning with truth-conditions captures only one dimension of linguistic meaning — the assertoric — and leaves untouched the vast territory of meaningful language that lies beyond the domain of the true and the false.

definition: A singular term T refers to an object O if and only if (i) the speaker who uses T intends to pick out O, (ii) there is a causal-historical chain connecting the speaker's use of T to an initial act of naming or dubbing in which O was singled out, and (iii) the causal chain has not been broken by any subsequent act of re-dubbing that redirects T to a different object.
conditions: The speaker intends to pick out O
There is a causal-historical chain connecting the use of T to an initial act of naming in which O was singled out
The causal chain has not been broken by re-dubbing that redirects T to a different object
counterexample: Consider a competent English speaker who uses the name 'Hesperus' to refer to a certain bright celestial body visible in the evening sky. As it happens, the original act of dubbing that introduced 'Hesperus' into the language singled out the planet Venus. The speaker intends to refer to Venus and the causal chain is intact. But suppose the speaker believes, quite firmly, that Hesperus is a star, not a planet. The speaker's descriptive beliefs about the referent are false. Now consider: does the name still refer to Venus? It does, on the causal theory, because intention and causal chain are intact. But the case becomes more strained if we consider a community that introduces a name 'Vulcan' for a planet they believe to orbit between Mercury and the Sun. There was an initial act of dubbing, the speakers intend to refer, and no re-dubbing has occurred. Yet there is no such planet. Conditions (i) and (ii) appear to be satisfied — the chain traces back to a dubbing — but O does not exist. The definition does not adequately handle empty reference.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that the initial act of dubbing successfully singles out an existent object, but names may be introduced through acts of dubbing that fail to attach to any real object, and the definition does not account for reference failure or empty names.
passage: One might propose that a singular term refers to an object if and only if the speaker intends to pick out that object, there is a causal-historical chain connecting the speaker's use of the term to an initial act of naming in which the object was singled out, and the chain has not been broken by any subsequent act of re-dubbing. This is, in broad outline, the causal theory of reference, and it has considerable virtues. It explains how reference is preserved across generations of speakers, how it survives changes in the descriptive beliefs associated with a name, and how it is possible for speakers to refer to objects about which they are profoundly ignorant. But one might observe that the definition presupposes something it ought not: that the initial act of dubbing successfully attaches the name to an existent object. Consider the name 'Vulcan,' introduced in the nineteenth century for a planet that astronomers believed to orbit between Mercury and the Sun. There was a perfectly good act of dubbing — Le Verrier introduced the name in 1859. Subsequent speakers used the name with the intention of referring to that planet, and the causal chain from Le Verrier's introduction to their use is intact. No re-dubbing has occurred. Conditions (i), (ii), and (iii) appear to be satisfied. Yet 'Vulcan' does not refer to anything, because there is no such planet. The definition does not accommodate this case. It provides no account of reference failure — of what happens when the causal chain traces back to a dubbing that fails to attach to any real object. What is wanted is an account that allows for the possibility of empty names and explains how a term can satisfy all the conditions for reference and yet refer to nothing.

definition: A proper name N names an individual I if and only if (i) N is associated with a definite description D that uniquely identifies I — that is, I is the one and only object satisfying D, (ii) any competent user of N could, if pressed, supply D or a near equivalent, and (iii) the referent of N is determined by D — that is, N refers to whatever uniquely satisfies D.
conditions: N is associated with a definite description D that uniquely identifies I
Any competent user of N could supply D or a near equivalent
The referent of N is determined by D — N refers to whatever uniquely satisfies D
counterexample: Consider the name 'Feynman.' Most competent users of this name know that Feynman was a physicist. But if pressed, they could not supply a uniquely identifying description. They might say 'the physicist who worked on quantum electrodynamics,' but that description does not uniquely identify Feynman, for Schwinger and Tomonaga also worked on quantum electrodynamics. Condition (ii) fails. Yet the name 'Feynman' successfully refers to Richard Feynman whenever it is used. Moreover, even those who cannot supply any description at all — who know only that 'Feynman' is the name of some scientist — manage to refer to him when they use the name.
missing_condition: The definition requires that competent users associate a uniquely identifying description with a name, but speakers routinely use names successfully while possessing descriptions that are vague, shared among multiple individuals, or altogether absent, and the mechanism of reference does not depend on such descriptive information.
passage: One might propose that a proper name names an individual if and only if the name is associated with a definite description that uniquely identifies the individual, any competent user could supply that description, and the referent is determined by the description — the name refers to whatever uniquely satisfies it. This is, in essence, the descriptivist theory of names, and it has a certain tidiness. It explains how names have cognitive significance — 'Hesperus' and 'Phosphorus' differ in meaning because they are associated with different descriptions — and it fits certain cases well. 'The author of Waverley' uniquely identifies Scott, and one might think that 'Scott' works in just the same way. Yet one might observe that the theory encounters a stubborn difficulty. Consider the name 'Feynman.' Most competent users of this name know that Feynman was a physicist. But if pressed to supply a uniquely identifying description, they would falter. They might say 'the physicist who worked on quantum electrodynamics,' but that description does not uniquely identify Feynman — Schwinger and Tomonaga also made fundamental contributions to the same theory. Condition (ii) fails: competent users cannot supply a uniquely identifying description. And yet the name 'Feynman' successfully refers to Richard Feynman whenever it is used. Moreover, even those who can supply no description at all — who know only that 'Feynman' is the name of some scientist whose field they have forgotten — manage to refer to him by using the name. What the case reveals is that the descriptive theory overestimates the role of descriptions in fixing reference, and that names may refer successfully even when the speaker's descriptive resources are impoverished or inaccurate.

definition: A translation T of a sentence S from language L₁ into language L₂ is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-conditions of S — T is true in L₂ in exactly those circumstances in which S is true in L₁, (ii) T preserves the logical relations of S — if S entails or is entailed by other sentences in L₁, then T bears the corresponding relations to their translations in L₂, and (iii) T is unique — there is exactly one sentence of L₂ that satisfies (i) and (ii).
conditions: T preserves the truth-conditions of S
T preserves the logical relations of S
T is unique — exactly one sentence of L₂ satisfies conditions (i) and (ii)
counterexample: Consider translating the Japanese sentence 'Ame ga futte iru' into English. One might render it as 'It is raining,' or as 'Rain is falling,' or as 'There is rain.' Each of these translations preserves the truth-conditions — each is true in just those circumstances in which the Japanese sentence is true — and each preserves the relevant logical relations. Yet they are distinct sentences of English. Condition (iii) is not satisfied: there is no unique translation. The definition would force us to say that none of these is an adequate translation, which is absurd. All three are perfectly adequate.
missing_condition: The definition demands uniqueness of translation, but natural languages characteristically offer multiple adequate translations of any given sentence, and the requirement of uniqueness conflates adequacy of translation with determinacy of translation.
passage: One might define an adequate translation as a sentence in the target language that preserves the truth-conditions and logical relations of the original and that is unique — the only sentence of the target language to do so. The first two conditions are eminently reasonable. A translation that changes the truth-conditions is not a translation but a paraphrase or a distortion; a translation that disrupts logical relations fails to capture the role of the sentence in the inferential network of the source language. But one might observe that the third condition is too demanding. Consider the task of translating the Japanese sentence 'Ame ga futte iru' into English. One might render it as 'It is raining,' or as 'Rain is falling,' or as 'There is rain falling.' Each of these translations preserves the truth-conditions of the original — each is true in just those circumstances in which the Japanese sentence is true — and each preserves the relevant logical relations. Yet they are distinct sentences of English. Condition (iii) fails: there is no unique translation. If we insist upon uniqueness, we are forced to deny that any of these is an adequate translation, which is plainly absurd. All three are perfectly adequate, and a competent translator might choose among them on grounds of style, register, or context without any sacrifice of fidelity. What the case reveals is that the definition has confused two distinct desiderata — adequacy and uniqueness — and that the former does not entail the latter. Natural languages characteristically offer multiple ways of saying the same thing, and a theory of translation must allow for this multiplicity rather than legislating it away.

definition: An utterance U is metaphorical if and only if (i) U contains a predicate that is applied to a subject to which it does not literally apply — that is, the predicate's standard extension does not include the subject, (ii) the speaker intends the hearer to recognise that the predicate is not being used literally, and (iii) the point of U is to highlight a specific resemblance or analogy between the subject and the things to which the predicate literally applies.
conditions: U contains a predicate applied to a subject to which it does not literally apply
The speaker intends the hearer to recognise that the predicate is not literal
The point of U is to highlight a specific resemblance or analogy between subject and literal extension
counterexample: Consider the utterance 'Juliet is the sun,' spoken by Romeo in Shakespeare's play. The predicate 'is the sun' does not literally apply to Juliet, and the speaker intends the hearer to recognise this. But what is the specific resemblance or analogy that the metaphor highlights? One might say: warmth, brightness, centrality, life-giving power, beauty. But these are many resemblances, not one, and the metaphor seems to work precisely by leaving open which similarities are most salient. Condition (iii) requires that the point be to highlight a specific resemblance, but the power of the metaphor lies in its openness and richness, its refusal to be cashed out as a single comparison. The case suggests that metaphor is not merely abbreviated simile.
missing_condition: The definition reduces metaphor to the highlighting of a specific resemblance, but the most powerful metaphors are characteristically open-ended, inviting the hearer to discover multiple dimensions of similarity and even to find connections that the speaker did not explicitly intend.
passage: One might propose that an utterance is metaphorical if and only if it applies a predicate to a subject to which it does not literally apply, the speaker intends the hearer to recognise the non-literal use, and the point is to highlight a specific resemblance or analogy between the subject and the things to which the predicate literally applies. This is, in effect, the comparison theory of metaphor: a metaphor is an abbreviated simile, and its content can in principle be stated as an explicit comparison. The definition works tolerably well for simple cases. 'Richard is a lion' highlights the resemblance between Richard and lions in respect of courage. But one might observe that the definition falters when confronted with the most interesting and powerful metaphors. Consider the utterance 'Juliet is the sun.' The predicate does not literally apply to Juliet, and the speaker intends the hearer to recognise this. But what is the specific resemblance that the metaphor highlights? Warmth? Brightness? Centrality? Life-giving power? Beauty that dazzles the eye? All of these, perhaps, and more besides. The metaphor works precisely by refusing to be cashed out as a single comparison. Its power lies in its openness, its richness, its capacity to suggest multiple dimensions of similarity simultaneously and to invite the hearer to discover connections that even the speaker may not have explicitly intended. Condition (iii) requires that the point be to highlight a specific resemblance, but the point of this metaphor — and of great metaphors generally — is something far less determinate and far more fertile. What the case reveals is that the comparison theory reduces metaphor to a cognitive shorthand when it is in fact a mode of thought that generates new understanding rather than merely summarising old resemblances.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker S, in uttering U, expresses an intention to perform some future action A, (ii) S intends U to place S under an obligation to perform A, and (iii) the hearer H recognises S's intention and accepts the obligation that U purports to impose.
conditions: The speaker expresses an intention to perform some future action A
The speaker intends the utterance to place herself under an obligation to perform A
The hearer recognises the speaker's intention and accepts the obligation
counterexample: Consider a case in which a person makes a promise under duress. A robber holds a shopkeeper at gunpoint and demands that the shopkeeper promise to hand over the contents of the safe. The shopkeeper says, 'I promise.' The shopkeeper expresses an intention to perform the action, intends the words to be taken as placing herself under an obligation, and the robber recognises and accepts this. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet it is far from clear that the shopkeeper has made a genuine promise. The utterance was coerced, and most competent speakers of the language would say that a promise extracted by force is not binding. The concept of promising seems not to apply, despite the satisfaction of all stated conditions.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the promise be made freely, without coercion or duress, and an adequate account of promising must include a condition specifying that the speaker's expression of intention is voluntary.
passage: One might define a promise as follows: an utterance constitutes a promise if and only if the speaker expresses an intention to perform some future action, intends the utterance to place herself under an obligation to perform that action, and the hearer recognises the speaker's intention and accepts the obligation. The definition captures much of what we ordinarily mean by a promise. When I say to a friend, 'I promise to return your book tomorrow,' I express an intention, I intend to bind myself, and my friend recognises and accepts the commitment. All three conditions are satisfied, and a genuine promise has been made. But one might observe that the definition is too permissive. Consider a case in which a promise is extracted under duress. A robber holds a shopkeeper at gunpoint and demands that the shopkeeper promise to hand over the contents of the safe. The shopkeeper says, 'I promise.' She expresses an intention; she intends the words to be understood as placing her under an obligation; the robber recognises the intention and accepts it. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet most competent speakers would say that this is not a genuine promise — or, if it is a promise in some thin sense, it is not a binding one. A promise obtained by force lacks the voluntary character that we take to be essential to the act of promising. What the case reveals is that the definition has omitted a crucial condition: the speaker's expression of intention must be free from coercion or duress. Without this condition, the definition counts as promises utterances that fail to bind because they lack the element of free consent that gives promising its normative force.

definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) there exist borderline cases of F — objects of which it is unclear whether F applies, (ii) F admits of degrees — objects can be more or less F, and (iii) the borderline cases of F form a sharply bounded region — there is a precise point at which clear cases of F give way to borderline cases.
conditions: There exist borderline cases of F — objects of which it is unclear whether F applies
F admits of degrees — objects can be more or less F
The borderline cases form a sharply bounded region — there is a precise point where clear cases give way to borderline cases
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'tall' as applied to adult humans. There are borderline cases — persons of whom it is genuinely unclear whether they are tall — and the predicate admits of degrees: one person may be taller than another. But condition (iii) requires that the borderline region be sharply bounded, and this seems plainly wrong. There is no precise height at which 'clearly tall' gives way to 'borderline tall.' The transition is itself vague: the boundary of the borderline cases is as indeterminate as the boundary of the predicate itself. This is what is sometimes called higher-order vagueness. The predicate is vague, but condition (iii) is not satisfied.
missing_condition: The definition imposes sharp boundaries on the borderline region, but vague predicates characteristically exhibit higher-order vagueness — the boundaries of the borderline cases are themselves vague — and an adequate account must allow for this phenomenon.
passage: One might propose that a predicate is vague if and only if there exist borderline cases of it, it admits of degrees, and the borderline cases form a sharply bounded region — there is a precise point at which clear cases give way to borderline ones. The first two conditions are uncontroversial. The predicate 'tall' has borderline cases and admits of degrees; so does 'red,' 'bald,' 'rich,' and a great many other predicates of ordinary language. But one might observe that the third condition is deeply problematic. Consider the predicate 'tall' as applied to adult humans. There are persons who are clearly tall, persons who are clearly not tall, and persons in between of whom it is genuinely uncertain whether they are tall. So far, so good. But is there a precise height at which 'clearly tall' gives way to 'borderline tall'? It appears not. A person of six feet two inches is clearly tall; a person of five feet ten is borderline; but where exactly does the transition occur? The boundary of the borderline region is itself vague — it is as indeterminate as the boundary between tall and not-tall. This is the phenomenon of higher-order vagueness: not only is the predicate vague, but the predicate 'borderline case of tall' is itself vague. Condition (iii) is not satisfied — the borderline cases do not form a sharply bounded region — and yet the predicate is paradigmatically vague. What the case reveals is that the definition has attempted to impose a precise structure upon a phenomenon that is essentially imprecise, and that an adequate account of vagueness must allow for vagueness all the way up, without presupposing sharp boundaries at any level.

definition: Two sentences S₁ and S₂ have the same meaning if and only if (i) S₁ and S₂ have the same truth-conditions — they are true in exactly the same possible circumstances, (ii) S₁ and S₂ have the same inferential role — whatever can be inferred from S₁ can be inferred from S₂ and vice versa, and (iii) a competent speaker who understands S₁ is thereby in a position to understand S₂ without acquiring any new information.
conditions: S₁ and S₂ have the same truth-conditions
S₁ and S₂ have the same inferential role
A competent speaker who understands S₁ is thereby in a position to understand S₂
counterexample: Consider the sentences 'Water is wet' and 'H₂O is wet.' They have the same truth-conditions — they are true in exactly the same possible circumstances, since water is H₂O. They have the same inferential role — whatever follows from one follows from the other. But condition (iii) is not satisfied. A competent speaker who understands 'Water is wet' need not be in a position to understand 'H₂O is wet,' for the latter requires acquaintance with chemistry that the former does not. A child who has never heard of molecular formulae understands the first sentence perfectly but cannot understand the second. The sentences differ in meaning despite sharing truth-conditions and inferential role.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the cognitive or informational dimension of meaning — the fact that two sentences may be true in exactly the same circumstances and yet differ in the mode of presentation, the conceptual resources required for comprehension, or the information they convey to a particular speaker.
passage: One might propose that two sentences have the same meaning if and only if they share truth-conditions, share inferential role, and are such that a competent speaker who understands one is thereby in a position to understand the other. The first two conditions are standard and jointly quite strong: sentences that agree in truth-conditions and inferential role are, by most accounts, very close in content. The third condition is intended to rule out cases where sameness of truth-conditions masks a difference in cognitive significance. Yet one might observe that conditions (i) and (ii) can be satisfied while condition (iii) fails, and that this failure points to a genuine difference in meaning. Consider the sentences 'Water is wet' and 'H₂O is wet.' They are true in exactly the same possible circumstances — water just is H₂O, and the identification is metaphysically necessary. They have the same inferential role: whatever follows from one follows from the other. But a child who has never encountered chemistry understands 'Water is wet' perfectly well and cannot understand 'H₂O is wet' at all. The latter requires conceptual resources — acquaintance with molecular theory, familiarity with chemical notation — that the former does not. Condition (iii) is not satisfied. And this seems to point to a genuine difference in meaning. The two sentences present the same state of affairs under different modes of description, and the mode of description is part of what they mean. What the case reveals is that sameness of truth-conditions and inferential role, even taken together, do not suffice for sameness of meaning, and that an adequate criterion must accommodate the cognitive and conceptual dimension of meaning that the truth-conditional approach alone cannot capture.

definition: A definite description 'the F' refers to an object O if and only if (i) there exists exactly one object satisfying F, (ii) O is that unique object, and (iii) the speaker uses 'the F' with the intention of picking out whatever uniquely satisfies F.
conditions: There exists exactly one object satisfying F
O is that unique object
The speaker uses 'the F' with the intention of picking out whatever uniquely satisfies F
counterexample: Consider a speaker at a party who says, 'The man drinking a martini is a philosopher,' gesturing towards a particular person across the room. In fact, the man she is gesturing towards is drinking water from a martini glass, and there is, unbeknown to her, another man in the next room who is the only person actually drinking a martini. Condition (i) is satisfied — there is exactly one man drinking a martini at the party — but the speaker does not intend to refer to that man. She intends to refer to the man she sees, who is not drinking a martini at all. The description 'the man drinking a martini' picks out, by conditions (i) and (ii), the wrong person — the one in the next room — while the speaker's communicative intention is directed at someone else entirely. The definition yields a referent that is not the intended one.
missing_condition: The definition does not distinguish between the semantic referent of a description (fixed by satisfaction of the descriptive content) and the speaker's referent (fixed by communicative intention), and cases of mismatch between the two reveal that an adequate account must accommodate both dimensions of reference.
passage: One might define the reference of a definite description as follows: 'the F' refers to an object if and only if there exists exactly one object satisfying F, that object is the referent, and the speaker uses the description with the intention of picking out whatever uniquely satisfies F. This is, in essence, Russell's theory supplemented with a condition of speaker intention. It works well when the speaker's intention and the descriptive content align — when the man I call 'the author of Waverley' is indeed the one and only author of Waverley, and I intend to refer to whoever that may be. But one might observe that intention and descriptive content may diverge. Consider a speaker at a party who says, 'The man drinking a martini is a philosopher,' gesturing towards a particular person. The man she gestures towards is in fact drinking water from a martini glass. Meanwhile, in another room, there is a man who is the only person genuinely drinking a martini at the party. Conditions (i) and (ii) pick out the man in the other room — he uniquely satisfies the description. But the speaker does not intend to refer to him; she has never seen him. Her communicative intention is directed at the man before her, who does not satisfy the description at all. The definition yields the wrong referent. What the case reveals is that definite descriptions may be used referentially — to pick out a particular individual the speaker has in mind — as well as attributively — to denote whoever happens to satisfy the description — and the definition, by tying reference to satisfaction, cannot accommodate the referential use.

definition: An utterance U is an assertion if and only if (i) the speaker S, in uttering U, presents a proposition as true, (ii) S believes the proposition expressed by U to be true, and (iii) S intends that the hearer come to believe the proposition on the basis of recognising S's intention to communicate it.
conditions: The speaker presents a proposition as true
The speaker believes the proposition to be true
The speaker intends the hearer to come to believe the proposition on the basis of recognising the communicative intention
counterexample: Consider an actor on stage who delivers the line, 'The king is dead.' The actor presents a proposition as true — the dramatic context requires it — and intends the audience to entertain the proposition. But the actor does not believe the proposition to be true; she is performing a role. Condition (ii) fails. Yet her utterance has all the surface features of an assertion — the declarative mood, the indicative syntax, the assertoric force. More troublingly, consider a scientist who asserts a hypothesis she does not yet believe, in order to subject it to scrutiny: 'The speed of light in a vacuum varies with altitude.' She presents the proposition as true, intends her colleagues to consider it, but does not believe it. Again, condition (ii) fails, yet the utterance functions as an assertion in scientific discourse.
missing_condition: The definition requires sincere belief as a condition of assertion, but assertions may be made insincerely, performatively, or provisionally, and the act of presenting a proposition as true does not require that the speaker actually hold it to be true.
passage: One might propose that an utterance is an assertion if and only if the speaker presents a proposition as true, believes the proposition to be true, and intends the hearer to come to believe it on the basis of recognising the speaker's communicative intention. This definition captures the paradigm case: when I say, 'It is raining,' I present a proposition as true, I believe it, and I intend my hearer to come to share my belief. But one might observe that the belief condition is too restrictive. Consider an actor on stage who delivers the line, 'The king is dead.' She presents a proposition as true — the conventions of theatre require her to do so with conviction — and the audience entertains the proposition in the context of the play. But the actor does not believe the king is dead; she is performing a role. Condition (ii) fails. Yet her utterance has every surface feature of an assertion. More troublingly, consider a scientist who asserts a hypothesis that she does not yet believe, presenting it to her colleagues for critical examination: 'The speed of light in a vacuum varies with altitude.' She does not believe this — she thinks it is probably false — but she presents it as true in order to see whether it can withstand scrutiny. This too is an assertion, at least in the sense relevant to scientific discourse, and yet condition (ii) is not satisfied. What the case reveals is that the definition has confused two things: the norm of assertion (one ought to assert only what one believes) and the nature of assertion (what makes an utterance an assertion in the first place). An utterance may be an assertion — may function as one, be taken as one, and have the consequences of one — even when the speaker does not believe what she says.

definition: A word W has meaning if and only if (i) W is associated with a mental image or idea in the mind of the speaker, (ii) the mental image associated with W is shared — that is, it is the same or sufficiently similar in the minds of all competent speakers of the language, and (iii) W can be used in a sentence to make a statement that is either true or false.
conditions: W is associated with a mental image or idea in the speaker's mind
The mental image is shared among competent speakers
W can be used in a sentence to make a true or false statement
counterexample: Consider the word 'if.' It is a perfectly meaningful word — no competent speaker of English would say that 'if' lacks meaning, and its contribution to the sentences in which it appears is precise and indispensable. Yet it is far from clear what mental image or idea is associated with 'if.' One does not picture anything when one hears the word; it is a logical connective, not a name for an object or quality. Condition (i) fails, or at least becomes extremely strained, for logical connectives, prepositions, and other syncategorematic terms that are meaningful without being associated with any identifiable mental image.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that meaning consists in the association of words with mental images, but many meaningful words — logical connectives, prepositions, articles — have no imagistic content. The definition needs a broader account of meaning that accommodates words whose contribution to the sentence is functional rather than referential or imagistic.
passage: One might propose that a word has meaning if and only if it is associated with a mental image or idea in the mind of the speaker, the image is shared among competent speakers, and the word can be used in a sentence to make a true or false statement. This definition reflects a venerable picture of meaning: words are labels for ideas, and communication succeeds because speakers attach the same idea to the same word. The requirement of shared mental images ensures that communication is possible, and the requirement that the word contribute to statements that are true or false ensures that meaning is connected to the world. Yet one might observe that this picture, however natural it seems, does not survive scrutiny. Consider the word 'if.' It is a word of the utmost importance in any language — without it, conditional reasoning would be inexpressible. No one would say that 'if' is meaningless. Yet it is extraordinarily difficult to specify what mental image or idea is associated with 'if.' One does not picture anything upon hearing it; one does not entertain a distinct idea in the way one might entertain the idea of a horse or a colour. The word 'if' contributes to the meaning of the sentences in which it appears not by naming an object or evoking an image but by specifying a logical relation between propositions. The same observation applies to words such as 'not,' 'or,' 'every,' 'the,' and 'of' — words that are indispensable to the expression of thought but that resist assimilation to the image theory of meaning. The definition, it appears, has been framed with nouns and adjectives in mind and has overlooked the large class of words whose meaning consists not in what they picture but in what they do.

definition: A singular term T refers to an object O if and only if (i) T is a well-formed expression of the language that occupies a referring position in a sentence, (ii) the speaker who uses T intends to pick out O and no other object, and (iii) the use of T enables the hearer to identify which object is meant.
conditions: T is a well-formed expression occupying a referring position
The speaker intends to pick out O and no other object
The use of T enables the hearer to identify the object
counterexample: Consider a case in which two identical twins, indistinguishable in appearance, are present at a gathering. A speaker says 'That man is a fine pianist,' intending to refer to the twin who plays the piano. But the hearer, unable to distinguish the twins, identifies the wrong one — the twin who has never touched a piano. The speaker's expression is well-formed and occupies a referring position; the speaker intends to pick out one particular twin; but the hearer identifies the wrong object. Condition (iii) fails, yet it seems wrong to say that the speaker has failed to refer. The speaker has successfully referred to the pianist; it is the hearer who has failed to identify the referent.
missing_condition: The definition makes successful reference depend upon the hearer's ability to identify the referent, but reference may succeed even when the hearer misidentifies the object intended. A distinction between the speaker's act of referring and the hearer's act of identifying is needed; the definition conflates the two.
passage: One might define reference by saying that a singular term refers to an object if and only if the term occupies a referring position in a sentence, the speaker intends to pick out that object and no other, and the use of the term enables the hearer to identify which object is meant. This definition combines elements from both the speaker's side and the hearer's side of the communicative exchange. The speaker must have a definite intention; the hearer must be able to recover the speaker's intention by means of the term used. In straightforward cases, the definition works well: a speaker says 'That man over there,' intending to pick out a particular individual, and the hearer, following the speaker's gesture and the context, identifies the intended individual. Yet one might observe that the definition breaks down when the hearer's identification goes wrong through no fault of the speaker. Consider a gathering at which two identical twins are present. The speaker says 'That man is a fine pianist,' intending to refer to the twin who plays the piano. The hearer, unable to tell the twins apart, takes the speaker to be referring to the other twin — the one who has never played a note. Condition (iii) is not met: the hearer has not identified the intended object. But it would be strange to say that the speaker has failed to refer. The speaker has referred to the pianist; the hearer has merely failed to recognise which twin the speaker meant. The definition, it appears, has confused the act of referring with the act of understanding a reference. What is needed is a distinction between the speaker's referential act — which may succeed independently of the hearer's response — and the hearer's interpretive act, which may fail for reasons that do not impugn the reference itself.

definition: A proper name N names an object O if and only if (i) N was introduced into the language by an act of naming — a baptism, registration, or other ceremony in which N was associated with O, (ii) there is an unbroken causal chain of usage linking the present use of N back to the original act of naming, and (iii) the speaker who uses N intends to use it with the same reference as the person from whom the speaker acquired the name.
conditions: N was introduced by an act of naming associated with O
There is an unbroken causal chain from present use back to the original naming
The speaker intends to preserve the reference of prior users
counterexample: Consider the name 'Madagascar.'
