According to certain historical accounts, the name was originally applied by Marco Polo — or by his sources — to a portion of the mainland of Africa, not to the island that we now call Madagascar. Through a series of misunderstandings and cartographic errors, the name came to be applied to the island instead. The original act of naming associated the name with the mainland; there is an unbroken causal chain of usage stretching from the present day back through centuries of cartography; and each speaker in the chain intended to use the name as they had received it. Yet the name now refers to the island, not to the mainland. All three conditions point to the mainland as the referent, but common usage fixes the reference on the island.
missing_condition: The definition does not account for the possibility that reference may shift over time through accumulated error or convention, so that the name comes to refer to an object other than the one designated in the original act of naming. A condition addressing the role of community usage in stabilising or shifting reference is needed.
passage: One might propose that a proper name names an object if and only if the name was introduced by an act of naming associated with that object, there is an unbroken causal chain of usage linking the present use back to the original naming, and the speaker intends to preserve the reference of prior users. This definition embodies a causal-historical theory of names: the reference of a name is fixed not by any description the speaker associates with it but by its historical origin and the chain of transmission through which it has passed. The definition has considerable appeal. It explains how a name can refer to an object even when the speaker knows very little about that object — what matters is not the speaker's descriptive beliefs but the causal chain connecting the speaker's use to the original baptism. Yet one might observe that the definition does not account for cases in which reference shifts over the course of the causal chain. Consider the name 'Madagascar.' Historical evidence suggests that the name was originally applied not to the island we now call Madagascar but to a region of the African mainland. Through a succession of cartographic errors and misunderstandings, the name migrated from the mainland to the island. Each link in the causal chain preserved the intention to refer as the previous user had referred, and the chain stretches back, unbroken, to the original naming. Yet the name now refers to the island, not to the mainland. The three conditions, if applied rigorously, would yield the result that 'Madagascar' refers to the mainland — a result that conflicts with the settled usage of every speaker of every language in which the name occurs. What the definition lacks is a recognition that community usage may, over time, override the original act of naming and fix the reference on a different object.

definition: A translation T of a sentence S from language L1 into language L2 is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-value of S — that is, T is true in L2 if and only if S is true in L1, (ii) T preserves the logical form of S — that is, if S is a conjunction, T is a conjunction; if S is a conditional, T is a conditional; and so forth, and (iii) T uses vocabulary in L2 that is the conventional equivalent of the vocabulary in S.
conditions: T preserves the truth-value of S
T preserves the logical form of S
T uses conventionally equivalent vocabulary
counterexample: Consider a Japanese sentence that uses an honorific construction to express deference to the person being addressed. A translation into English may preserve the truth-value — the propositional content is the same — and the logical form, and it may use the conventional English equivalents of the Japanese content words. But the honorific dimension of the original — the indication of the social relationship between speaker and addressee — is lost, because English lacks the grammatical resources to express it. All three conditions are met, yet something essential to the meaning of the original has been omitted. The translation is adequate by the stated criteria but inadequate as a rendering of what the sentence communicates.
missing_condition: The definition does not account for the pragmatic, social, and contextual dimensions of meaning that may be expressed by grammatical features — such as honorifics, evidentials, or mood — that have no structural equivalent in the target language. A condition addressing the preservation of pragmatic or communicative force is needed.
passage: One might define adequate translation by requiring that the translation preserve the truth-value of the original, preserve its logical form, and use vocabulary that is the conventional equivalent of the original's vocabulary. This definition captures a great deal of what we expect from a translation: it should say the same thing, say it in the same logical shape, and use the right words. A translation that turned a true sentence into a false one, or that rendered a conjunction as a disjunction, or that used words with entirely different meanings, would plainly be inadequate. Yet one might observe that the three conditions, taken jointly, do not exhaust what is involved in adequate translation. Consider a sentence in Japanese that employs an honorific construction to express the speaker's deference to the addressee. The propositional content of the sentence — what it says about the world — can be preserved in English, and the logical form can be maintained, and the content words can be matched with their conventional English equivalents. Yet the honorific dimension of the original is lost entirely, because English has no grammatical means of expressing the social relation between speaker and addressee that the Japanese construction encodes. The translation is adequate by the three stated criteria, but it fails to convey something that a competent speaker of Japanese would regard as an essential part of what the sentence communicates. What the definition lacks is a condition addressing the pragmatic and social dimensions of meaning — the aspects of communication that go beyond truth-value, logical form, and vocabulary to include the relationships, attitudes, and contextual implications that the grammatical structure of the original language may encode.

definition: A sentence S is metaphorical if and only if (i) S contains a term T that is used in a sense different from its literal or standard meaning, (ii) the non-literal use of T is intended by the speaker and is recognisable as such by the hearer, and (iii) the non-literal use of T is not a case of ambiguity — that is, T does not simply have a second standard meaning that is operative in S.
conditions: S contains a term used non-literally
The non-literal use is intended and recognisable
The non-literal use is not mere ambiguity
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'The committee shot down my proposal.' The word 'shot down' is used non-literally — no firearm was involved. The speaker intends the non-literal use, and the hearer recognises it immediately. And the expression is not a case of ambiguity in the narrow sense; 'shot down' does not have 'rejected' as a second listed meaning in the dictionary. All three conditions are met. Yet one might hesitate to call this a metaphor. The expression 'shot down a proposal' is so thoroughly conventionalised — so much a part of the ordinary stock of English idiom — that it has lost whatever metaphorical vitality it once possessed. It is a dead metaphor, or perhaps merely an idiom, and calling it metaphorical in the same sense as a fresh and illuminating figure of speech obscures an important distinction.
missing_condition: The definition does not distinguish between live metaphors — which produce a fresh and illuminating shift of meaning — and dead metaphors or conventional idioms, in which the non-literal usage has become so entrenched that no imaginative transfer is felt. A condition requiring that the non-literal use involve a living act of semantic creativity or insight is needed.
passage: One might define metaphor by stating that a sentence is metaphorical if and only if it contains a term used non-literally, the non-literal use is intended by the speaker and recognisable by the hearer, and the non-literal use is not a case of ordinary ambiguity. This definition has the merit of distinguishing metaphor from both literal speech and mere equivocation. A term used metaphorically is not being used in its standard sense, and it is not merely taking on a second standard sense that it happens to possess; it is doing something different from both. The definition captures this intermediate status. Yet one might observe that the definition draws no distinction between metaphors that are alive and metaphors that are dead. Consider the sentence 'The committee shot down my proposal.' The expression 'shot down' is non-literal — no weapons were discharged. The speaker intends the non-literal use; the hearer recognises it without difficulty. And the expression is not a case of simple ambiguity. All three conditions are met. But the expression is so thoroughly conventionalised that it has lost whatever metaphorical force it once possessed. When a speaker says that a proposal was 'shot down,' no one pictures a gun, no one experiences a flash of imaginative insight, no one feels the tension between the literal and the figurative that characterises a living metaphor. The expression has become an idiom — a fixed phrase whose non-literal meaning is as familiar and as unremarkable as any literal expression. The definition, as stated, classifies dead metaphors and living metaphors together, without recognising that the distinction between them is philosophically significant. What appears to be needed is a condition that captures the creative or illuminating dimension of metaphor — the sense in which a genuine metaphor produces a new way of seeing rather than merely deploying a conventional idiom.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) U is an assertion by the speaker S that S will perform a specified act A in the future, (ii) S intends, at the time of uttering U, to perform A, and (iii) the utterance of U is recognised by the hearer H as placing S under an obligation to perform A.
conditions: U is an assertion that S will perform a future act A
S intends to perform A at the time of utterance
The hearer recognises U as placing S under an obligation
counterexample: Consider an actor on stage who says to another character, 'I promise I shall return before dawn.' The utterance asserts that the speaker will perform a future act; the actor may even intend, in some attenuated sense, to perform the act within the fiction. And the audience recognises the utterance as one that, within the play, places the character under an obligation. All three conditions are met, yet the actor has not made a promise. No real obligation has been incurred; the utterance is part of a performance, not a genuine speech act. The actor's words have the form and recognisability of a promise without constituting one.
missing_condition: The definition does not require that the utterance be made in earnest — that is, in circumstances in which the speaker genuinely undertakes an obligation rather than merely performing the outward form of a promise. A condition distinguishing serious from non-serious (fictional, theatrical, ironic) utterances is needed.
passage: One might propose that an utterance constitutes a promise if and only if it is an assertion that the speaker will perform a specified future act, the speaker intends at the time to perform the act, and the hearer recognises the utterance as placing the speaker under an obligation. This definition captures the essential elements of promising as it is ordinarily understood: the commitment to future action, the sincerity of the commitment, and the social recognition that the commitment has been made. The hearer's recognition is particularly important, for a promise that is not recognised as such by anyone fails to create the interpersonal bond that is the point of promising. Yet one might observe that the three conditions may be satisfied in circumstances that do not constitute genuine promises. Consider an actor on a stage who says to another character, in the course of a dramatic performance, 'I promise I shall return before dawn.' The utterance has the form of a promise: it asserts a future act. The actor may intend, within the fiction, to perform the act. And the audience — and indeed the other actor — recognises the utterance as one that, within the world of the play, creates an obligation. All three conditions are formally satisfied. But the actor has not promised anything. No real obligation has been created; when the curtain falls, the actor is not bound to go anywhere before dawn. The difficulty is that the definition does not distinguish between serious and non-serious utterances — between utterances made in earnest and utterances made in the context of fiction, play-acting, or irony. What is needed is a condition specifying that the utterance be made in circumstances that the speaker and hearer mutually recognise as appropriate for the genuine undertaking of obligations.

definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) there exist clear cases to which F applies and clear cases to which it does not, (ii) there exist borderline cases — cases in which competent speakers are uncertain whether F applies or not, and (iii) the borderline cases arise because the meaning of F does not determine a sharp boundary between its positive and negative extensions.
conditions: There exist clear positive and clear negative cases
There exist borderline cases of uncertainty among competent speakers
The borderline cases arise from the absence of a sharp semantic boundary
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is an adult' as applied to human beings in a legal jurisdiction that sets the age of majority at eighteen. There are clear cases — a ten-year-old is not an adult, a thirty-year-old is — and there are, one might say, borderline cases in ordinary usage: is a seventeen-year-old an adult? Competent speakers disagree. But the legal definition draws a perfectly sharp boundary at the eighteenth birthday. The predicate, in its legal use, has clear cases, has cases that ordinary speakers find uncertain, but does not lack a sharp boundary — the law provides one. All three conditions appear to be met in ordinary usage, but the predicate is not vague in its legal usage, where the boundary is perfectly determinate. The definition does not distinguish between vagueness inherent in the predicate and uncertainty that arises from the speaker's ignorance of a precise convention.
missing_condition: The definition does not distinguish vagueness from ignorance or indeterminacy about a precise boundary that exists but is unknown to the speaker. A condition clarifying that vagueness is a semantic phenomenon — a feature of the meaning of the predicate rather than of the speaker's state of information — is needed.
passage: One might define vagueness by saying that a predicate is vague if and only if there exist clear cases of application and clear cases of non-application, there exist borderline cases in which competent speakers are uncertain, and the borderline cases arise because the meaning of the predicate does not fix a sharp boundary. This definition captures the intuitive picture of vagueness admirably. A predicate like 'tall' has clear positive cases (a person of seven feet) and clear negative cases (a person of four feet), and between these extremes there is a region of uncertainty that competent speakers navigate with hesitation and disagreement. The definition locates the source of this uncertainty in the semantics of the predicate: the meaning of 'tall' simply does not determine where tallness begins. Yet one might observe that the definition does not adequately distinguish vagueness from other sources of uncertainty. Consider the predicate 'is an adult' as used in a legal context. Ordinary speakers may be uncertain whether a seventeen-year-old is an adult — a borderline case, one might say. But the law draws a sharp boundary at the eighteenth birthday: a person is legally an adult on that day and not before. The predicate, in its legal use, is not vague; it is perfectly precise. The uncertainty that ordinary speakers feel arises not from the absence of a sharp boundary but from their ignorance of, or inattention to, the legal convention that supplies one. The definition, as stated, does not distinguish between genuine semantic indeterminacy — where no boundary exists to be discovered — and mere ignorance of a boundary that does exist. What is needed is a condition that ties vagueness to the semantic structure of the predicate itself, rather than to the psychological state of the speaker.

definition: Two sentences S1 and S2 have the same meaning if and only if (i) S1 and S2 have the same truth-value in every possible circumstance — that is, they are logically equivalent, (ii) S1 and S2 contain terms that are pairwise synonymous, and (iii) a competent speaker who understands S1 would, upon reflection, assent to S2, and vice versa.
conditions: S1 and S2 have the same truth-value in every possible circumstance
S1 and S2 contain pairwise synonymous terms
A competent speaker who understands one would assent to the other
counterexample: Consider the sentences 'All unmarried men are bachelors' and 'All bachelors are unmarried men.' They have the same truth-value in every possible circumstance; they are logically equivalent. Their terms are pairwise synonymous — 'bachelor' and 'unmarried man' being conventionally treated as synonyms. And a competent speaker who assents to one would assent to the other. All three conditions are met, and in this case the definition seems to yield the right result. But now consider 'The morning star is the morning star' and 'The morning star is the evening star.' These sentences have the same truth-value in every possible circumstance — both are necessarily true, since the morning star is the evening star. But condition (ii) fails: 'morning star' and 'evening star' are not synonymous. And condition (iii) is questionable: a speaker who has never learned that the morning star and the evening star are the same object might assent to the first but not the second. The definition correctly identifies a difference in meaning here, but it does so by relying on conditions that are difficult to apply in cases where co-referential terms differ in cognitive significance.
missing_condition: The definition does not adequately handle the distinction between meaning and reference — the fact that two expressions may refer to the same object in every possible circumstance yet differ in the manner in which they present that object. A condition addressing the mode of presentation or cognitive significance of the terms is needed.
passage: One might propose that two sentences have the same meaning if and only if they are logically equivalent, they contain pairwise synonymous terms, and competent speakers who understand one would assent to the other. This definition attempts to capture sameness of meaning through a combination of logical, lexical, and psychological criteria. Logical equivalence ensures that the sentences agree on every question of truth and falsity. Pairwise synonymy ensures that the building blocks of the two sentences are interchangeable. And the assent condition provides a behavioural test. In simple cases, these criteria converge: 'All bachelors are unmarried' and 'All unmarried men are bachelors' satisfy all three conditions and are indeed synonymous. Yet one might observe that the definition struggles with cases involving co-referential terms that differ in what one might call cognitive significance. Consider the sentences 'The morning star is the morning star' and 'The morning star is the evening star.' If both terms refer to the same object — the planet Venus — then the sentences have the same truth-value in every possible circumstance. But 'morning star' and 'evening star' are not synonymous; they present the same object under different aspects. And a speaker who has not learned the astronomical identity might readily assent to the first sentence while withholding assent from the second. The definition correctly identifies a difference in meaning in this case, but it does so by means of the synonymy condition, which raises the further question of what synonymy itself consists in. The deeper difficulty is that the definition does not address the distinction between meaning and reference — the fact that expressions may co-refer without being interchangeable in all linguistic contexts. What is needed is a condition that captures the manner in which an expression presents its referent, not merely the referent itself.

definition: A definite description 'the F' refers to an object O if and only if (i) there exists exactly one object that satisfies the predicate F, (ii) O is that unique object, and (iii) the speaker uses the expression 'the F' with the intention of picking out the object that uniquely satisfies F.
conditions: There exists exactly one object satisfying F
O is that unique object
The speaker intends to pick out the unique satisfier of F
counterexample: Consider a speaker at a dinner party who says 'The man drinking champagne is a famous novelist,' intending to refer to a particular gentleman across the room who appears to be holding a glass of champagne. In fact, the gentleman is drinking sparkling water, not champagne. Furthermore, there is another person at the party who is drinking champagne but who is entirely unknown to the speaker. There is exactly one person drinking champagne — condition (i) is met — but that person is not the one the speaker intends to refer to. The speaker's intention is directed toward the gentleman with the sparkling water, who does not satisfy the description. The definition yields the result that the speaker has referred to the person actually drinking champagne, yet the speaker plainly meant to refer to someone else entirely.
missing_condition: The definition does not accommodate cases in which the speaker's referential intention diverges from the semantic content of the description used. A condition addressing the possibility that a speaker may successfully refer to an object even when the description employed does not accurately characterise that object is needed.
passage: One might define the reference of a definite description by stating that 'the F' refers to an object if and only if there exists exactly one object satisfying F, the object in question is that unique satisfier, and the speaker intends to pick out the unique satisfier. This definition combines the Russellian requirement of uniqueness with the speaker's referential intention, and in many cases it yields intuitively correct results. When a speaker says 'the author of Waverley,' meaning to refer to the unique person who wrote that novel, the three conditions are typically satisfied, and the reference succeeds. Yet one might observe that the definition breaks down in cases where the speaker's intention and the content of the description come apart. Consider a dinner party at which a speaker says 'The man drinking champagne is a famous novelist,' gesturing toward a gentleman across the room who appears to be holding a glass of champagne. In fact, the gentleman is drinking sparkling water. Meanwhile, another guest — unknown to the speaker — is actually drinking champagne. There is exactly one person drinking champagne, satisfying condition (i), and that person is a definite individual, satisfying condition (ii). But the speaker does not intend to refer to that person; the speaker intends to refer to the gentleman with the sparkling water. The definition yields the result that the speaker has referred to the actual champagne-drinker, but the speaker's communicative intention was plainly directed elsewhere. The definition, it appears, has failed to accommodate the gap between what a description semantically determines and whom the speaker means to talk about. What is needed is a richer account of the relation between descriptions and referential intentions.

definition: An utterance U constitutes an assertion if and only if (i) U expresses a proposition — that is, U has a content that is either true or false, (ii) the speaker S presents the proposition as true — that is, S puts forward the proposition as something S believes, and (iii) S intends the hearer to accept the proposition on the basis of S's authority or testimony.
conditions: U expresses a proposition that is true or false
The speaker presents the proposition as true
The speaker intends the hearer to accept the proposition on S's authority
counterexample: Consider a teacher who, in the course of presenting an argument for philosophical discussion, says 'The soul is immortal.' The utterance expresses a proposition; the teacher presents it as true — not as something the teacher personally believes, but as a proposition within the argument being examined. And the teacher intends the students to entertain the proposition, though not on the teacher's authority but as part of a dialectical exercise. Condition (i) is met, and conditions (ii) and (iii) are partially met in a way that the definition does not easily accommodate. The teacher is not asserting that the soul is immortal in the sense of putting forward a personal belief; the teacher is advancing a proposition for the sake of argument. The definition does not distinguish between sincere assertions, in which the speaker vouches for the truth of the proposition, and dialectical or pedagogical uses, in which a proposition is put forward without personal endorsement.
missing_condition: The definition does not distinguish between asserting a proposition — presenting it as something one believes and vouches for — and merely putting forward a proposition for the sake of argument, examination, or discussion. A condition requiring that the speaker be personally committed to the truth of the proposition is needed to distinguish assertion from dialectical or pedagogical uses of declarative sentences.
passage: One might define assertion by requiring that the utterance express a proposition, that the speaker present the proposition as true, and that the speaker intend the hearer to accept it on the speaker's authority. This definition captures the paradigmatic case of assertion: a person who tells another something that the first person believes and wants the second person to believe as well. The requirement that the utterance express a proposition distinguishes assertions from commands, questions, and exclamations. The requirement that the proposition be presented as true distinguishes assertion from supposing or conjecturing. And the requirement of intended acceptance gives assertion its communicative point. Yet one might observe that the definition does not adequately distinguish assertion from certain other speech acts that share its surface features. Consider a philosophy teacher who, in the course of presenting an argument for classroom discussion, utters the sentence 'The soul is immortal.' The utterance expresses a proposition; the teacher presents it as true — within the context of the argument being examined. And the teacher may intend the students to take the proposition seriously, to consider its grounds, and to evaluate its consequences. But the teacher is not asserting, in his or her own voice, that the soul is immortal. The proposition is put forward for the sake of argument, not as an expression of the teacher's personal conviction. The definition, as stated, cannot accommodate this distinction, because it does not require that the speaker be personally committed to the truth of the proposition. What is needed is a condition that captures the element of personal endorsement or commitment that distinguishes genuine assertion from the dialectical or pedagogical deployment of propositions that one does not oneself affirm.

definition: The meaning of a sentence S is the set of conditions under which S is true if and only if (i) S is a declarative sentence, (ii) the truth-conditions of S are determinable by a competent speaker of the language, and (iii) any two sentences with the same truth-conditions have the same meaning.
conditions: S is a declarative sentence
The truth-conditions of S are determinable by a competent speaker of the language
Any two sentences with the same truth-conditions have the same meaning
counterexample: Consider the sentences 'The morning star is bright' and 'The evening star is bright.' Since the morning star and the evening star are in fact the same celestial body (Venus), these two sentences have exactly the same truth-conditions: both are true if and only if Venus is bright. Condition (iii) therefore requires that they have the same meaning. Yet a competent speaker might sincerely assert one while denying the other, which strongly suggests that they differ in meaning.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the distinction between sense and reference. Two sentences may share truth-conditions while differing in the mode of presentation by which their common referent is given. A condition requiring identity of sense — not merely identity of truth-conditions — is needed for identity of meaning.
passage: One might propose that the meaning of a sentence is the set of conditions under which it is true, provided that the sentence is declarative, its truth-conditions are determinable by a competent speaker, and any two sentences with the same truth-conditions have the same meaning. This truth-conditional theory of meaning has much to recommend it: it connects meaning to the world in a perspicuous way and provides a clear criterion of synonymy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by a famous example from the philosophy of astronomy. The sentences 'The morning star is bright' and 'The evening star is bright' have, as a matter of astronomical fact, precisely the same truth-conditions, since the morning star and the evening star are the same object — the planet Venus. On the proposed account, these sentences have the same meaning. But a competent speaker of English may sincerely affirm the one whilst denying the other, not knowing that the morning star is the evening star. This suggests that the two sentences differ in meaning despite sharing truth-conditions. What is missing from the definition is a recognition that meaning involves not merely what conditions must obtain for a sentence to be true, but how those conditions are presented or conceived by the speaker — a distinction between sense and reference that the truth-conditional account, as stated, does not draw.

definition: A proper name N refers to an object O if and only if (i) there exists a definite description D that the speaker associates with N, (ii) D uniquely identifies O, and (iii) the speaker intends to use N to pick out the object satisfying D.
conditions: There exists a definite description D that the speaker associates with N
D uniquely identifies O
The speaker intends to use N to pick out the object satisfying D
counterexample: Consider a community that uses the name 'Jonah' to refer to the biblical prophet. Most members of the community associate with the name some cluster of descriptions — 'the man who was swallowed by a whale,' 'the prophet who preached to Nineveh,' and so on. Suppose that historical research reveals that no single individual satisfies all or even most of these descriptions, yet the community's use of 'Jonah' was originally grounded in a naming ceremony performed in the presence of a particular historical person. On the proposed account, 'Jonah' either fails to refer or refers to whichever individual satisfies the associated description, which may be the wrong person or no person at all. Yet intuitively the name refers to the individual who was the original bearer.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate cases in which reference is secured not by a description the speaker associates with the name but by a causal-historical chain linking the speaker's use of the name to an original act of naming. A condition permitting reference to be transmitted through such chains is needed.
passage: A natural account of how proper names refer holds that a name N refers to an object O if and only if there exists a definite description that the speaker associates with N, this description uniquely identifies O, and the speaker intends to use N to pick out the object satisfying the description. This description theory captures many ordinary cases: when I use the name 'Aristotle,' I associate with it some description — 'the teacher of Alexander,' perhaps, or 'the author of the Metaphysics' — and my use of the name succeeds in referring to Aristotle because the description uniquely picks him out. Yet the account encounters difficulties when we consider how names are used across a community over time. Consider the name 'Jonah.' Most speakers associate with it descriptions drawn from the biblical narrative: the man swallowed by a whale, the reluctant prophet sent to Nineveh. Suppose historical research reveals that no single individual satisfies these descriptions. On the proposed account, the name would fail to refer, or would refer to some individual other than the one we intend. But if the use of 'Jonah' was originally grounded in a naming ceremony performed upon a particular person, and the name has been passed down from speaker to speaker since that time, it seems that the name refers to that original bearer regardless of what descriptions later speakers associate with it. The definition must be broadened to admit causal-historical chains of reference-transmission.

definition: An act of naming is successful if and only if (i) a speaker utters a name N in the presence of an object O with the intention of conferring N upon O, (ii) the relevant linguistic community accepts the conferral, and (iii) subsequent uses of N by members of the community are intended to pick out O.
conditions: A speaker utters a name N in the presence of an object O with the intention of conferring N upon O
The relevant linguistic community accepts the conferral
Subsequent uses of N by members of the community are intended to pick out O
counterexample: Consider the naming of a ship. A dignitary smashes a bottle against the hull and declares, 'I name this ship Fortitude.' The ship is not present in the sense of being a sentient object that can be ostended to in the usual way — it is a large material object at which one gestures. The community accepts the conferral, and subsequent uses of 'Fortitude' are intended to pick out the ship. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. But now suppose that, through a bureaucratic error, the wrong ship is in the dock — the ship before the dignitary is actually the one scheduled to be named 'Resilience.' The dignitary is in the presence of an object upon which she confers the name, but the object is not the one she intends to name.
missing_condition: The definition does not require that the speaker correctly identify the object upon which the name is conferred. A condition ensuring that the speaker's identifying intention matches the object actually present is needed to handle cases of misidentification at the moment of naming.
passage: One might suppose that an act of naming is successful if and only if a speaker utters a name in the presence of an object with the intention of conferring the name upon it, the relevant linguistic community accepts the conferral, and subsequent uses of the name are intended to pick out the object. This account captures the basic structure of baptismal ceremonies: a person stands before an object, declares it to bear a certain name, and the community takes up the name in reference to that object. Yet the account is vulnerable to cases of misidentification at the moment of naming. Consider a ship-naming ceremony in which a dignitary declares, 'I name this ship Fortitude,' while smashing a bottle against the hull. The community accepts the conferral and subsequently uses the name to refer to the vessel. But suppose that, through a bureaucratic error, the wrong ship has been brought to the dock — the vessel before the dignitary is not the one scheduled for naming but a different ship altogether. The dignitary is in the presence of an object and confers a name upon it with full intention, yet her identifying intention — to name a particular ship — does not match the object actually before her. The definition, which requires only presence and intention, cannot distinguish between the ship the dignitary intends to name and the ship she actually names. A further condition is needed to secure the alignment of identifying intention with the identity of the object present.

definition: A sentence S2 in language L2 is a correct translation of a sentence S1 in language L1 if and only if (i) S2 has the same truth-conditions as S1, (ii) S2 preserves the logical form of S1, and (iii) a bilingual speaker would assent to S2 in all and only those circumstances in which she would assent to S1.
conditions: S2 has the same truth-conditions as S1
S2 preserves the logical form of S1
A bilingual speaker would assent to S2 in all and only those circumstances in which she would assent to S1
counterexample: Consider a Japanese sentence that is customarily used as a greeting in contexts where an English speaker would say 'How are you?' The Japanese sentence, taken literally, may have different truth-conditions and a different logical form from the English sentence. Yet a bilingual speaker moves fluently between the two, assenting to each in precisely the same social circumstances. Condition (iii) is satisfied while conditions (i) and (ii) fail. Conversely, one might construct a sentence in French that matches the truth-conditions and logical form of an English sentence but carries connotations that would prevent a bilingual speaker from using the two interchangeably.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the pragmatic and connotative dimensions of meaning that are essential to translation. A condition requiring that S2 preserve not only truth-conditions and logical form but also the communicative force and connotative associations of S1 is needed.
passage: A plausible first analysis of correct translation holds that a sentence in one language correctly translates a sentence in another if and only if the two sentences share truth-conditions, the translation preserves the logical form of the original, and a bilingual speaker would assent to each in precisely the same circumstances. This definition is attractive in its rigour: it combines a semantic requirement (truth-conditions), a structural requirement (logical form), and a pragmatic test (bilingual assent). Yet the definition proves both too restrictive and too permissive. It is too restrictive because many successful translations fail to preserve logical form. A Japanese greeting that functions as the equivalent of 'How are you?' may have a quite different logical structure, yet the translation serves its communicative purpose admirably. It is too permissive because two sentences may share truth-conditions and logical form whilst differing in connotation — one may be formal and the other colloquial, one may carry an implication of irony and the other none — differences that a competent translator would regard as rendering the one an incorrect translation of the other. It appears that the definition must be supplemented with a condition addressing the pragmatic and connotative aspects of meaning that truth-conditions and logical form alone do not capture.

definition: An utterance U is metaphorical if and only if (i) the literal meaning of U is plainly false or absurd in the context of utterance, (ii) there exists a salient resemblance between the literal subject and the metaphorical subject, and (iii) a competent speaker of the language recognises the intended non-literal meaning.
conditions: The literal meaning of U is plainly false or absurd in the context of utterance
There exists a salient resemblance between the literal subject and the metaphorical subject
A competent speaker of the language recognises the intended non-literal meaning
counterexample: Consider the utterance 'No man is an island,' spoken in a philosophical discussion. The literal meaning is not plainly absurd — indeed, taken literally, the sentence is obviously true: no human being is a body of land surrounded by water. Condition (i) therefore fails. Yet the utterance is unmistakably metaphorical. It uses the concept of an island to express the thought that no person is entirely self-sufficient or isolated from others. The definition wrongly excludes metaphors whose literal content is true.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that metaphor requires the literal meaning to be false or absurd, but many metaphors are literally true. A condition that distinguishes metaphor from literal speech by reference to the speaker's communicative intention — the intention to convey something beyond or other than the literal content — is needed in place of the falsity requirement.
passage: One might analyse metaphor as follows: an utterance is metaphorical if and only if its literal meaning is plainly false or absurd in context, there exists a salient resemblance between the literal and metaphorical subjects, and a competent speaker recognises the intended non-literal meaning. This analysis captures a wide range of standard cases. When one says 'Juliet is the sun,' the literal meaning is plainly false — no human being is a star — there is a salient resemblance between the warmth and centrality of the sun and the qualities attributed to Juliet, and competent speakers readily grasp the intended meaning. Yet the analysis stumbles upon an important class of cases. Consider the utterance 'No man is an island.' Taken literally, this sentence is obviously true: no person is a body of land surrounded by water. Condition (i) therefore fails, for the literal meaning is neither false nor absurd. Yet the utterance is unambiguously metaphorical; it employs the image of an island to express the thought that no person is wholly self-contained. The definition, by tying metaphor to the falsity or absurdity of the literal meaning, cannot accommodate metaphors that happen to be literally true. What is needed is a condition referring to the speaker's intention to communicate something that goes beyond or departs from the literal content, regardless of whether that literal content is true or false.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker S, in uttering U, expresses an intention to perform some future action A, (ii) S intends that the hearer H recognise this expression of intention, and (iii) S believes that H would prefer A to be performed rather than not.
conditions: The speaker S, in uttering U, expresses an intention to perform some future action A
S intends that the hearer H recognise this expression of intention
S believes that H would prefer A to be performed rather than not
counterexample: Consider a man who says to his neighbour, 'I shall mow your lawn on Saturday.' He expresses an intention to perform a future action, he intends his neighbour to recognise this expression of intention, and he believes his neighbour would prefer the lawn to be mowed. All three conditions are met. Yet if the man utters the sentence merely as a statement of his plans — informing his neighbour of what he intends to do, without any sense that he is placing himself under an obligation — the utterance does not constitute a promise. It is a prediction about his own future behaviour, not a commitment.
missing_condition: The definition omits the crucial condition that the speaker intend, by the utterance, to place himself under an obligation to perform the action. Without this condition of self-imposed obligation, a mere statement of intention satisfies the definition, which is too permissive.
passage: One might propose that an utterance constitutes a promise if and only if the speaker expresses an intention to perform some future action, intends the hearer to recognise this expression of intention, and believes the hearer would prefer the action to be performed. This definition picks out many genuine cases of promising: when I say 'I promise to return your book by Friday,' I express an intention, I intend my hearer to recognise it, and I believe he would prefer to have his book back. Yet the definition is too broad. Consider a man who says to his neighbour, 'I shall mow your lawn on Saturday.' He expresses an intention to perform a future action, intends his neighbour to recognise this, and believes the neighbour would prefer the lawn mowed. All three conditions are satisfied. But if the man utters the sentence merely to inform his neighbour of his plans — as one might say 'I shall be in London next week' — without any sense that he is undertaking an obligation, the utterance is not a promise. It is a statement of intention, nothing more. What distinguishes a promise from a mere announcement of one's plans is the speaker's intention to bind himself — to place himself under an obligation to perform the action. The definition, as it stands, does not capture this essential feature of promissory speech.

definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) there exist borderline cases to which it is unclear whether F applies, (ii) F admits of degrees, and (iii) F lacks a precise boundary separating its positive extension from its negative extension.
conditions: There exist borderline cases to which it is unclear whether F applies
F admits of degrees
F lacks a precise boundary separating its positive extension from its negative extension
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is a child.' There exist borderline cases — is a person of fifteen a child? — and the predicate lacks a sharp boundary between its positive and negative extensions. But it is doubtful that 'is a child' admits of degrees in the way that 'is tall' or 'is red' does. One does not naturally say that a three-year-old is more of a child than a ten-year-old; both are plainly children. Conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied, but condition (ii) is not, yet the predicate appears to be vague.
missing_condition: The definition is too restrictive in requiring that a vague predicate admit of degrees. Some predicates are vague — possessing borderline cases and lacking sharp boundaries — without being gradable. The condition requiring degrees should be replaced or weakened to accommodate non-gradable vague predicates.
passage: A common analysis of vagueness holds that a predicate is vague if and only if there exist borderline cases to which it is unclear whether the predicate applies, the predicate admits of degrees, and the predicate lacks a precise boundary separating its positive from its negative extension. This analysis fits many paradigmatic cases of vagueness admirably. The predicate 'is tall' has borderline cases (is a man of five feet ten inches tall?), admits of degrees (one person can be taller than another), and lacks a sharp boundary. Similarly for 'is bald,' 'is red,' and 'is hot.' Yet the analysis proves too narrow when one considers predicates that appear to be vague without admitting of degrees. Consider 'is a child.' There are borderline cases — a fifteen-year-old is neither clearly a child nor clearly not a child — and there is no precise boundary between childhood and adulthood. But 'is a child' does not naturally admit of degrees. We do not say that a three-year-old is more of a child than an eight-year-old; both are simply children. The predicate is vague, possessing borderline cases and lacking a sharp boundary, yet it fails the degree condition. The definition, by insisting upon gradability, excludes a class of predicates that one would wish to count as vague.

definition: A word W is meaningful if and only if (i) W is associated with a mental image in the mind of the speaker, (ii) this mental image is consistently evoked across occasions of the word's use, and (iii) the hearer, upon hearing W, forms a relevantly similar mental image.
conditions: W is associated with a mental image in the mind of the speaker
This mental image is consistently evoked across occasions of the word's use
The hearer, upon hearing W, forms a relevantly similar mental image
counterexample: Consider the word 'justice.' It is plainly meaningful — competent speakers use it with great regularity and mutual comprehension. Yet it is far from clear that the word is associated with any mental image at all. One does not, upon hearing the word 'justice,' form a picture in one's mind in the way that one might form a picture upon hearing the word 'horse.' Condition (i) fails, yet the word is meaningful.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires that meaning consist in the association of words with mental images. Many meaningful words — abstract terms such as 'justice,' 'although,' 'if,' and 'nevertheless' — are not associated with mental images. A condition grounding meaning in use, convention, or inferential role rather than in mental imagery is needed.
passage: An initially attractive theory of meaning holds that a word is meaningful if and only if it is associated with a mental image in the mind of the speaker, this image is consistently evoked across occasions of use, and the hearer forms a relevantly similar image upon hearing the word. The theory has an intuitive appeal: when I use the word 'horse,' I seem to form a mental picture of a horse, and I expect my hearer to do the same. The communication succeeds, on this view, because speaker and hearer share relevantly similar images. Yet the theory founders upon a large and important class of cases. Consider the word 'justice.' It is unquestionably meaningful — it figures in legal codes, political debates, and moral arguments, and competent speakers use it with full mutual comprehension. But what mental image does the word evoke? One does not, upon hearing 'justice,' form a picture in one's mind in any straightforward sense. The same is true of words such as 'although,' 'if,' 'nevertheless,' and 'therefore.' These words are meaningful but imageless. The definition, by tying meaning to mental imagery, cannot account for the meaningfulness of abstract and logical vocabulary. An adequate account of meaning must look beyond imagery to the role that words play in the practices and inferences of a linguistic community.

definition: A definite description 'the F' refers to an object O if and only if (i) there exists exactly one object satisfying the predicate F, (ii) the speaker uses 'the F' with the intention of picking out the unique satisfier, and (iii) the context of utterance does not include any other object satisfying F.
conditions: There exists exactly one object satisfying the predicate F
The speaker uses 'the F' with the intention of picking out the unique satisfier
The context of utterance does not include any other object satisfying F
counterexample: Consider a speaker at a dinner party who says, 'The author of Waverley was a remarkable man.' There exists exactly one author of Waverley — Sir Walter Scott — the speaker intends to pick out that unique individual, and the context includes no other object satisfying the description. All three conditions are met. Now consider a speaker who says, 'The table is covered with books,' in a room containing two tables. There is more than one table, so condition (i) fails. Yet the speaker may succeed in referring to a particular table — the one nearest to her, perhaps — by exploiting contextual salience. The definition rules this out.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the way in which context can restrict the domain of quantification, allowing a definite description to refer even when its predicate is satisfied by more than one object in the world at large. A condition permitting the domain to be contextually restricted is needed.
passage: A standard account of definite descriptions holds that 'the F' refers to an object if and only if there exists exactly one thing satisfying the predicate F, the speaker intends to pick out this unique satisfier, and the context includes no other object satisfying F. This Russellian analysis works well for descriptions such as 'the author of Waverley,' where there is in fact exactly one satisfier and no ambiguity arises. Yet the analysis encounters difficulties with the commonest uses of definite descriptions in everyday speech. Consider a speaker at a dinner party who says, 'The table is covered with books.' The room may contain two tables, and the world certainly contains a great many more. On the proposed definition, the description fails to refer, since there is not exactly one table. But the speaker plainly succeeds in referring — she means the table nearest to her, or the table at which the conversation is taking place, and her hearers understand her without difficulty. What makes this possible is that the context of utterance implicitly restricts the domain of quantification to a single relevant table. The definition, by ignoring the role of contextual domain restriction, fails to accommodate a vast class of perfectly successful referential uses of definite descriptions.

definition: Two proper names N1 and N2 are synonymous if and only if (i) N1 and N2 refer to the same object, (ii) N1 and N2 are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value, and (iii) a competent speaker who understands both names would assent to the identity statement 'N1 is N2.'
conditions: N1 and N2 refer to the same object
N1 and N2 are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value
A competent speaker who understands both names would assent to the identity statement 'N1 is N2'
counterexample: Consider a competent English speaker who knows the names 'Mark Twain' and 'Samuel Clemens' but is unaware that they name the same man. The names refer to the same object and are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value. But the speaker would not assent to 'Mark Twain is Samuel Clemens,' for she does not know this to be the case. Condition (iii) therefore fails, and on the proposed definition the names are not synonymous. Yet it seems wrong to deny their synonymy on grounds of a single speaker's ignorance.
missing_condition: The definition makes synonymy of names contingent upon the state of a speaker's information, which confuses a semantic property (synonymy) with a psychological one (the speaker's awareness of an identity). The third condition should be replaced by one that characterises synonymy in terms of the names' semantic properties independently of any particular speaker's beliefs.
passage: One might define the synonymy of proper names as follows: two names are synonymous if and only if they refer to the same object, are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value, and a competent speaker who understands both names would assent to the identity statement linking them. The first two conditions are straightforwardly semantic: they require coreference and extensional intersubstitutability. The third introduces a test involving the linguistic competence of speakers. Yet this third condition is problematic. Consider a competent English speaker who knows the names 'Mark Twain' and 'Samuel Clemens' but does not know that they denote the same individual. The names refer to the same object and are intersubstitutable in extensional contexts without altering truth-values. But the speaker, being ignorant of the identity, would not assent to 'Mark Twain is Samuel Clemens.' On the proposed definition, the names are not synonymous. This result is unsatisfactory, for it makes the synonymy of names hostage to the contingent state of a particular speaker's information. Synonymy is a relation between linguistic expressions, not between expressions and the beliefs of individual speakers. The definition must be revised to characterise synonymy in purely semantic terms, without appeal to what any particular speaker happens to know.

definition: The meaning of a sentence S is the set of conditions under which S is true if and only if (i) S expresses a proposition, (ii) the proposition is determinately true or false in every possible state of affairs, and (iii) competent speakers of the language can in principle determine whether those conditions obtain.
conditions: S expresses a proposition
The proposition is determinately true or false in every possible state of affairs
Competent speakers can in principle determine whether the truth-conditions obtain
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'There are infinitely many twin primes.' This sentence expresses a proposition. If the twin prime conjecture is true, the proposition is true in every possible state of affairs; if false, it is false in all. Yet competent speakers of English — even competent mathematicians — cannot at present determine whether the truth-conditions obtain. The conjecture is unresolved. The third condition is not met, and yet the sentence is not meaningless.
missing_condition: The definition requires that truth-conditions be in principle determinable by competent speakers, but this conflates meaning with verifiability and excludes sentences whose meaning is perfectly clear even though their truth-value is beyond current human capacity to ascertain.
passage: A truth-conditional account of meaning holds that the meaning of a sentence is given by the conditions under which it is true, provided the sentence expresses a proposition, the proposition is determinate in every possible state of affairs, and competent speakers can in principle determine whether the conditions obtain. This last requirement — the determinability condition — is meant to ensure that meaning is not divorced from the capacities of actual language users. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this requirement is provided by certain mathematical propositions. Consider the sentence 'There are infinitely many twin primes.' The sentence expresses a clear proposition. If the twin prime conjecture is true, the proposition is necessarily true; if false, necessarily false. The proposition is determinately true or false. Yet no competent speaker, and indeed no mathematician now living, can determine whether the truth-conditions obtain. The conjecture has resisted proof for over a century. The third condition is not met. Yet the sentence is not meaningless — every competent speaker of English with a modicum of mathematical understanding grasps what it asserts. The definition, in tying meaning to the determinability of truth-conditions, conflates the meaning of a sentence with our capacity to verify it, and thereby excludes sentences whose meaning is perfectly transparent but whose truth-value is unknown.

definition: A proper name N refers to an object O if and only if (i) there is a definite description D associated with N by competent speakers, (ii) O uniquely satisfies D, and (iii) competent speakers intend to refer to O by N in virtue of O's satisfying D.
conditions: There is a definite description associated with N by competent speakers
O uniquely satisfies that description
Competent speakers intend to refer to O in virtue of O's satisfying the description
counterexample: Consider the name 'Aristotle.' One speaker associates the description 'the teacher of Alexander the Great'; another associates 'the author of the Nicomachean Ethics'; a third associates 'the student of Plato.' These descriptions pick out the same individual, but they are different descriptions. There is no single definite description that all competent speakers associate with the name. The first condition is not met in its singular form, and yet the name 'Aristotle' successfully refers.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that a single definite description is associated with each name, but fails to account for the fact that different speakers may associate different descriptions with the same name, and that reference may succeed through a causal-historical chain rather than through description-satisfaction.
passage: A descriptivist account of reference holds that a name refers to the object that uniquely satisfies a definite description associated with the name by competent speakers. This account has a venerable history and captures something important about our use of names: when we use the name 'Aristotle,' we appear to have in mind a set of properties — perhaps that he was a philosopher, a student of Plato, the teacher of Alexander, the author of certain treatises. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis emerges when we notice the diversity of descriptions that different speakers associate with the same name. Ask one scholar what 'Aristotle' means to her, and she will say 'the author of the Metaphysics.' Ask another, and he will say 'the founder of the Lyceum.' Ask a third, and she will say 'the teacher of Alexander.' These descriptions, though they happen to pick out the same individual, are quite different. There is no single definite description that all competent speakers associate with the name. If the definition requires that there be such a description, it is not met. And if we weaken the requirement to allow different speakers to associate different descriptions, we face the question of what unifies their reference — for it is the same individual to whom they all refer. What the definition lacks is the recognition that reference may be secured not by a description at all but by a causal-historical connection linking current uses of the name to an original act of naming.

definition: An act of naming is successful if and only if (i) a speaker introduces a new name N by ostension or stipulation in the presence of an object O, (ii) the speaker intends N to designate O, and (iii) the linguistic community adopts N as the conventional designation for O.
conditions: A speaker introduces N by ostension or stipulation in the presence of O
The speaker intends N to designate O
The linguistic community adopts N as the conventional designation
counterexample: Consider the naming of the planet Neptune. The name was introduced not in the presence of the planet but on the basis of mathematical prediction — the planet had been postulated to account for perturbations in the orbit of Uranus. The speaker was not in the presence of the object; Neptune had not yet been observed. The first condition is unmet. Yet the naming was entirely successful: the community adopted 'Neptune,' and the name refers to the planet to this day.
missing_condition: The definition requires that naming occur in the presence of the object, but fails to accommodate cases in which a name is successfully introduced for an object that is merely posited, predicted, or otherwise not available for ostension.
passage: A natural account of naming holds that a name is successfully introduced when a speaker ostends or stipulates in the presence of the object, intends the name to designate that object, and the community adopts the name. This account fits many paradigmatic cases: the naming of a child at baptism, the naming of a ship at its christening, the naming of a newly discovered island by its explorer. In each case, the namer is in the presence of the object. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the naming of theoretical or predicted entities. Consider the name 'Neptune.' The planet was named before it was ever observed. Its existence was inferred from gravitational perturbations in the orbit of Uranus, and the name was assigned on the basis of mathematical prediction. The namer was not in the presence of the planet; the planet had not yet been seen by any human eye. The first condition is entirely unmet. Yet the naming was successful by every standard: the community adopted the name, and 'Neptune' now refers to the eighth planet of the solar system. The definition's insistence on the presence of the object excludes a significant class of successful namings — those in which the object is posited, predicted, or otherwise beyond the reach of direct ostension at the moment of naming.

definition: A translation T of a sentence S from language L1 into language L2 is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-conditions of S, (ii) T is grammatically well-formed in L2, and (iii) T employs vocabulary in L2 that corresponds to the vocabulary of S in L1.
conditions: T preserves the truth-conditions of S
T is grammatically well-formed in L2
T employs corresponding vocabulary
counterexample: Consider the French sentence 'Il pleut.' A truth-conditionally adequate translation into English is 'It is raining.' The truth-conditions are preserved, the English sentence is grammatical, and the vocabulary corresponds. Now consider the German sentence 'Gemutlichkeit herrscht im Raum.' The truth-conditions might be preserved by the English sentence 'There is a cosy, warm, convivial atmosphere in the room.' But the single German word 'Gemutlichkeit' has no single English equivalent; the translation requires a circumlocution. The third condition — vocabulary correspondence — is not met, yet the translation may be perfectly adequate.
missing_condition: The definition requires vocabulary correspondence, but fails to recognise that adequate translation may require circumlocution, paraphrase, or the use of expressions that lack any one-to-one correspondence with the source vocabulary.
passage: One might define an adequate translation as one that preserves truth-conditions, is grammatically well-formed, and employs corresponding vocabulary. This definition captures the desiderata of accuracy, fluency, and fidelity. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises from the well-known phenomenon of lexical gaps between languages. Consider the German word 'Gemutlichkeit,' which denotes a particular quality of cosiness, warmth, conviviality, and comfortable belonging. There is no single English word that corresponds to it. An adequate translation of a sentence containing 'Gemutlichkeit' must resort to circumlocution or paraphrase — perhaps 'a cosy and convivial warmth' — but no single item in the English vocabulary corresponds to the German term. The third condition, which requires vocabulary correspondence, is not met. Yet the circumlocutory translation may be perfectly adequate: it preserves the truth-conditions, it is grammatically well-formed, and it conveys the meaning of the original with tolerable fidelity. The definition, in insisting on vocabulary correspondence, imposes a constraint that cannot be satisfied when languages carve up the world differently at the lexical level. What is needed is a weaker condition — perhaps that the translation convey the content of the original by whatever lexical means are available, without requiring that each word find a counterpart.

definition: An utterance U is metaphorical if and only if (i) U is literally false, (ii) the speaker does not intend U to be taken literally, and (iii) U invites the hearer to perceive a resemblance between the literal subject and the figurative predicate.
conditions: U is literally false
The speaker does not intend U to be taken literally
U invites perception of a resemblance between subject and figurative predicate
counterexample: Consider the utterance 'No man is an island,' spoken in the course of a sermon. The sentence is literally true — no human being is, in fact, an island. The first condition is not met. Yet the utterance is plainly metaphorical: the speaker does not intend the remark as a geographical observation, and the hearer is invited to perceive a resemblance between isolation and island-existence. A literal truth may function metaphorically.
missing_condition: The definition requires literal falsity, but fails to recognise that metaphorical force may attach to sentences that are literally true, where the metaphorical content is carried by the invitation to perceive a resemblance that goes beyond what the literal truth-conditions convey.
passage: A common account of metaphor holds that a metaphorical utterance is one that is literally false, not intended literally, and invites the perception of a resemblance between subject and figurative predicate. This account works well for paradigm cases: 'Juliet is the sun' is literally false, not intended as a claim in astronomy, and invites the hearer to perceive a resemblance between Juliet and the sun in respect of radiance or centrality. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by metaphors that are literally true. Consider the sentence 'No man is an island,' uttered in a sermonic context. The sentence is literally true: no human being is, in geographical fact, an island. The first condition of the definition is not met. Yet the utterance is unmistakably metaphorical. The speaker does not intend the remark as a contribution to physical geography, and the hearer is invited to perceive a resemblance between the condition of being isolated and the condition of being surrounded by water. The metaphorical force is entirely present despite the literal truth of the sentence. The definition, by requiring literal falsity, excludes an important class of metaphors — those whose power derives precisely from the tension between what is literally said and what is metaphorically conveyed, even when what is literally said happens to be true.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker S expresses an intention to perform a future act A, (ii) S intends the utterance to place S under an obligation to perform A, and (iii) the hearer H recognises S's intention to undertake the obligation.
conditions: S expresses an intention to perform a future act A
S intends the utterance to place S under an obligation
H recognises S's intention to undertake the obligation
counterexample: Consider a hostage who, under duress, utters the words 'I promise to pay the ransom.' She expresses an intention to perform a future act, she appears to intend the utterance as obligation-creating, and the captors recognise her intention. All three conditions are formally satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to say that a genuine promise has been made, for the utterance was coerced and the speaker's apparent intention was extracted by threat rather than freely given.
missing_condition: The definition omits the requirement that the speaker's expression of intention be freely undertaken, without coercion or duress, and thus cannot distinguish genuine promises from forced declarations that mimic the form of promising.
passage: One might analyse a promise as an utterance in which the speaker expresses an intention to perform a future act, intends the utterance to be obligation-creating, and is recognised as so intending by the hearer. This analysis captures the core structure of promising: the forward-looking intention, the self-imposed obligation, and the mutual understanding between speaker and hearer. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider the effect of coercion. Suppose a hostage, held at gunpoint, utters the words 'I promise to pay the ransom.' She expresses an intention to perform a future act — the payment. She appears to intend the utterance as obligation-creating — the captors certainly take it so. And the captors recognise her intention. All three conditions are formally met. Yet it seems deeply wrong to say that she has made a genuine promise. The utterance was extracted by threat, and whatever obligation-like appearance it possesses is vitiated by the coercion under which it was produced. A promise, properly so called, must be freely given. The definition, by attending only to the expression of intention, the creation of obligation, and mutual recognition, fails to require that the speaker act under conditions of freedom. What is missing is the condition that the expression of intention be voluntary and uncoerced.

definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) there exist borderline cases — objects of which it is unclear whether F applies, (ii) F admits of degrees, and (iii) F lacks a sharp boundary separating its positive extension from its negative extension.
conditions: There exist borderline cases of F
F admits of degrees
F lacks a sharp boundary between positive and negative extension
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is a prime number.' There are no borderline cases: every natural number is determinately either prime or not prime. The predicate does not admit of degrees: a number cannot be more or less prime. And the boundary between the primes and the composites is perfectly sharp. Now consider the predicate 'is a small number.' There are borderline cases — is 47 small? — and the predicate lacks a sharp boundary. But it is unclear that 'small' admits of degrees in the relevant sense: one does not ordinarily say that 3 is more small than 5 in the way one says that a man of 5'11" is more tall than a man of 5'9". The second condition may not be met for all vague predicates.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the predicate admit of degrees, but some vague predicates — such as 'is a heap' — have borderline cases and lack sharp boundaries without clearly admitting of scalar gradation, suggesting that degree-theoretic structure is not a necessary feature of vagueness.
passage: A standard analysis holds that a predicate is vague when it has borderline cases, admits of degrees, and lacks a sharp boundary. This analysis is attractive because it explains the characteristic features of paradigm vague predicates such as 'tall,' 'red,' and 'rich.' These predicates all have borderline cases, come in degrees, and lack sharp boundaries. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the degree condition is worth examining. Consider the predicate 'is a heap.' Certainly there are borderline cases — is five grains a heap? — and certainly the predicate lacks a sharp boundary. But it is not obvious that 'heap' admits of degrees in the manner that 'tall' does. One does not ordinarily say that a mound of a thousand grains is more a heap than a mound of five hundred; both are heaps, and the larger one is simply a larger heap. The degree-theoretic structure that is so natural for 'tall' — where we can order individuals along a scale — does not obviously apply to 'heap' in the same way. Yet 'heap' is the paradigm case of a vague predicate, being the very term from which the sorites paradox takes its name. If the definition requires that all vague predicates admit of degrees, it threatens to exclude the most celebrated example of vagueness in the philosophical literature.

definition: Two sentences S1 and S2 are synonymous if and only if (i) S1 and S2 have the same truth-conditions, (ii) S1 and S2 are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value, and (iii) competent speakers would assent to S1 in exactly those circumstances in which they would assent to S2.
conditions: S1 and S2 have the same truth-conditions
S1 and S2 are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value
Competent speakers would assent to S1 in exactly the circumstances of assenting to S2
counterexample: Consider the sentences 'All creatures with a heart are creatures with a heart' and 'All creatures with a heart are creatures with a kidney.' These sentences have the same truth-value in every extensional context, since every creature with a heart does in fact have a kidney. They are intersubstitutable in extensional contexts without change of truth-value. And a competent speaker who accepted the biological generalisation would assent to both under the same circumstances. Yet they are plainly not synonymous: the first is a logical truth, while the second is an empirical generalisation that might have been false.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the identity of truth-conditions hold not merely in the actual world but across all possible worlds, thereby conflating coextensiveness with synonymy.
passage: One might propose that synonymy consists in sameness of truth-conditions, intersubstitutability in extensional contexts, and identity of assent-conditions among competent speakers. This proposal is carefully constructed: it goes beyond mere coextensiveness by adding the assent condition. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency can be constructed from a well-known example in the philosophy of biology. Consider the sentences 'All creatures with a heart are creatures with a heart' and 'All creatures with a heart are creatures with a kidney.' As it happens, every creature with a heart does possess a kidney, and vice versa. The two sentences have the same truth-value, and they are intersubstitutable in extensional contexts without affecting truth-value. A competent speaker who is aware of this biological correlation would assent to both under the same circumstances. All three conditions are met. Yet the sentences are manifestly not synonymous. The first is a logical truth — it could not be otherwise — while the second is an empirical generalisation that is true but might have been false. In a possible world where heartless organisms possess kidneys, the second sentence would be false while the first would remain true. The definition fails because it considers only the actual world: it checks whether speakers actually assent under the same circumstances but does not check whether they would do so under all possible circumstances. What is missing is the modal dimension — the requirement that sameness of truth-conditions hold across all possible states of affairs.

definition: A demonstrative expression D (such as 'this' or 'that') refers to an object O if and only if (i) the speaker accompanies D with a gesture or act of pointing directed at O, (ii) O is perceptually salient to both speaker and hearer, and (iii) the hearer identifies O as the target of the gesture.
conditions: The speaker accompanies D with a pointing gesture directed at O
O is perceptually salient to both speaker and hearer
The hearer identifies O as the target of the gesture
counterexample: Consider a speaker who, during a telephone conversation, says 'That was a remarkable performance' in reference to a concert both parties attended the previous evening. There is no pointing gesture, and the object of reference — the performance — is not perceptually present to either party. Yet both speaker and hearer understand perfectly well what 'that' refers to. The first and second conditions are unmet, and yet demonstrative reference succeeds.
missing_condition: The definition restricts demonstrative reference to cases involving perceptual presence and pointing, but fails to accommodate deferred or anaphoric uses of demonstratives in which the referent is identified through shared context, memory, or prior discourse rather than through perception and gesture.
passage: A natural analysis of demonstrative reference requires a pointing gesture, perceptual salience, and hearer identification.
