This analysis captures the paradigmatic case: a speaker points at an object in the shared visual field and says 'that,' and the hearer follows the gesture to identify the referent. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is furnished by the many non-perceptual uses of demonstratives. Consider two friends speaking by telephone the day after attending a concert. One says, 'That was a remarkable performance.' There is no gesture — the speaker is unseen by the hearer. The performance is not perceptually present to either party; it occurred the previous evening. Yet neither speaker nor hearer has the slightest difficulty understanding the reference. 'That' refers to the concert performance, and its reference is secured not by ostension or perceptual salience but by the shared context of the preceding conversation and the common experience of the concert. The definition, in requiring a pointing gesture and perceptual salience, excludes a large class of perfectly successful demonstrative references — those made in discourse about absent, past, or abstract objects, where shared context rather than shared perception does the referential work.

definition: A name N is meaningful if and only if (i) N is associated with a cluster of properties P1, ..., Pn such that the bearer of N is believed to possess a sufficient number of the Pi, (ii) the cluster is shared among competent speakers, and (iii) the meaning of N is exhausted by this cluster — that is, N contributes nothing to the proposition expressed beyond the descriptive content of the cluster.
conditions: N is associated with a cluster of properties believed to be possessed by its bearer
The cluster is shared among competent speakers
N's meaning is exhausted by the cluster
counterexample: Consider the name 'Hesperus.' Competent speakers associate it with the cluster: 'the bright celestial body visible in the evening sky.' The name 'Phosphorus' is associated with a different cluster: 'the bright celestial body visible in the morning sky.' If the meaning of each name is exhausted by its cluster, then the sentence 'Hesperus is Phosphorus' should be trivially true or obviously false, depending on whether the clusters overlap. Yet the discovery that Hesperus is Phosphorus was a substantive astronomical finding. The sentence is informative in a way that 'Hesperus is Hesperus' is not, which is inexplicable if the meaning of each name is nothing more than its associated cluster.
missing_condition: The definition claims that a name's meaning is exhausted by its descriptive cluster, but fails to account for the informative character of identity statements, which suggests that names contribute something to propositions — perhaps their referent directly — that is not captured by any cluster of descriptions.
passage: A cluster-description theory of names holds that a name is meaningful in virtue of being associated with a shared cluster of properties, and that the name's meaning is exhausted by this cluster. The theory improves upon the single-description account by allowing for a plurality of associated properties, thereby accommodating the diversity of descriptions that different speakers associate with a name. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the classic puzzle of informative identity statements. Consider the names 'Hesperus' and 'Phosphorus.' The first is associated with the cluster 'the bright celestial body visible in the evening sky,' the second with 'the bright celestial body visible in the morning sky.' If the meaning of each name is exhausted by its respective cluster, then the sentence 'Hesperus is Phosphorus' ought to be analysable as the claim that the evening star is the morning star — and this analysed form should be either trivially evident from the descriptions or plainly false. But the discovery that Hesperus is Phosphorus was a genuine astronomical achievement, an informative finding that could not have been made from the armchair. The sentence 'Hesperus is Phosphorus' says something that 'Hesperus is Hesperus' does not, and yet the cluster theory can offer no account of this difference, for on its analysis both sentences express the same proposition. What the theory misses is that names may contribute their referent directly to the proposition, rather than contributing a package of descriptive content.

definition: The meaning of a word W is the object or class of objects to which W refers — that is, W means what it denotes, and two words have the same meaning if and only if (i) they refer to the same object or class, (ii) they are intersubstitutable in all declarative sentences without change of truth-value, and (iii) a competent speaker who understands one will thereby understand the other.
conditions: They refer to the same object or class
They are intersubstitutable without change of truth-value
A competent speaker who understands one thereby understands the other
counterexample: Consider the words 'the Morning Star' and 'the Evening Star,' both of which refer to the planet Venus. Condition (i) is satisfied: they denote the same object. Yet the sentence 'The ancient Babylonians knew that the Morning Star is the Morning Star' is trivially true, while 'The ancient Babylonians knew that the Morning Star is the Evening Star' is false — they did not know this. The two expressions are not intersubstitutable in all declarative sentences without change of truth-value, so condition (ii) is violated. Furthermore, a speaker may understand 'the Morning Star' without understanding that it refers to the same object as 'the Evening Star,' so condition (iii) is also violated.
missing_condition: The definition identifies meaning with reference and thereby cannot account for the fact that co-referential expressions may differ in the mode by which they present their referent, a difference that affects truth-value in certain sentential contexts.
passage: One might propose that the meaning of a word is simply the object or class of objects to which it refers, and that two expressions have the same meaning when they refer to the same thing, are intersubstitutable in all sentences without change of truth-value, and are such that understanding one entails understanding the other. This referential theory of meaning has an attractive simplicity: it grounds meaning in the publicly accessible relation between words and the world. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis has been recognised since the earliest investigations into the logic of identity. The expressions 'the Morning Star' and 'the Evening Star' refer to the same celestial body — the planet Venus. Condition (i) is satisfied. Yet the sentence 'The ancient Babylonians knew that the Morning Star is the Morning Star' is a trivial truth, while 'The ancient Babylonians knew that the Morning Star is the Evening Star' is false, for this identification was an astronomical discovery. The two expressions are not intersubstitutable in all contexts without change of truth-value. Condition (ii) fails. Moreover, a competent speaker may perfectly well understand the expression 'the Morning Star' — knowing that it refers to a bright celestial object visible at dawn — without realising that it refers to the same body as 'the Evening Star.' Condition (iii) also fails. One might observe that the definition identifies meaning with reference and cannot accommodate the fact that expressions may refer to the same object while differing in the manner in which they present that object, a difference that is semantically consequential in contexts involving propositional attitudes.

definition: A singular term T refers to an individual X if and only if (i) the speaker who uses T intends to pick out X, (ii) T is associated with a description that X uniquely satisfies, and (iii) a competent hearer can identify X on the basis of the description associated with T.
conditions: The speaker intends to pick out X
T is associated with a description X uniquely satisfies
A competent hearer can identify X via the associated description
counterexample: Consider a speaker at a party who points across the room and says, 'That man drinking champagne is a famous novelist.' In fact, the man is drinking sparkling water, not champagne. The description associated with the term — 'the man drinking champagne' — is not uniquely satisfied by anyone in the room, since no one is drinking champagne. Condition (ii) is violated. Yet the speaker has successfully referred to a particular individual: both the speaker and the hearer know perfectly well which person is being discussed. The reference succeeds despite the falsity of the associated description.
missing_condition: The definition makes reference depend upon the accuracy of an associated description, but reference may succeed through demonstrative context, perceptual salience, or shared attention even when the description the speaker associates with the term is false of the referent.
passage: One might propose that a singular term refers to an individual when the speaker intends to pick out that individual, the term is associated with a description that the individual uniquely satisfies, and a hearer can identify the individual on the basis of the description. This proposal ties reference to a descriptive mechanism: the term reaches its referent via a description that the referent satisfies. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises from the common phenomenon of misdescription. A speaker at a gathering points across the room and says, 'That man drinking champagne is a famous novelist.' The man in question is in fact drinking sparkling water. The description associated with the demonstrative — 'the man drinking champagne' — is false of its intended referent. Condition (ii) is violated: no one uniquely satisfies the description, or if the description is taken loosely, it fails to discriminate the intended individual. Yet the reference plainly succeeds: both speaker and hearer know exactly which person is being discussed. The hearer identifies the referent not by satisfying the description but by following the speaker's gesture and attending to the perceptually salient individual. One might observe that the definition assigns too great a role to descriptive content and too little to demonstrative and perceptual mechanisms of identification, and that reference frequently succeeds not because the associated description is accurate but because contextual and perceptual cues secure the referent independently of any description.

definition: An utterance U is metaphorical if and only if (i) U says something that is literally false, (ii) the speaker intends U to be understood non-literally, and (iii) a competent hearer can identify the intended non-literal meaning by recognising a similarity between the literal subject and the metaphorical predicate.
conditions: U is literally false
The speaker intends non-literal understanding
A competent hearer identifies the meaning via a recognised similarity
counterexample: Consider the utterance 'No man is an island,' spoken in the course of a sermon. The literal content — that no human being is a body of land surrounded by water — is not false but trivially true. Condition (i) is not satisfied. Yet the utterance is unquestionably metaphorical: the speaker intends, and the hearer understands, that no person is entirely self-sufficient or disconnected from the rest of humanity. The metaphor works not because the literal statement is false but because the literal truth is so trivially obvious that the hearer is compelled to seek a more substantial meaning.
missing_condition: The definition requires literal falsity, but some metaphors — particularly those of the 'No man is an island' type — are literally true and function metaphorically precisely because their literal truth is too obvious to be the point.
passage: One might propose that an utterance is metaphorical when it is literally false, the speaker intends it to be taken non-literally, and the hearer can identify the intended meaning by perceiving a similarity between the literal subject and the metaphorical predicate. This account captures many familiar metaphors: 'Juliet is the sun' is literally false, Shakespeare intends a non-literal reading, and the hearer perceives a similarity between Juliet and the sun in respect of radiance and centrality. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises with metaphors that are literally true. When John Donne writes 'No man is an island,' the literal content — that no human being is a body of land surrounded by water — is not false but trivially and obviously true. No one could mistake it for a controversial geographical claim. Condition (i) is not met. Yet the utterance is as thoroughly metaphorical as any in the language. The hearer recognises that the trivial literal truth is not the point; the metaphor works by inviting the hearer to consider what it would mean for a person to be like an island — isolated, self-contained, cut off from the mainland of human fellowship. One might observe that the definition has identified metaphor with literal falsity, but that some metaphors exploit literal truth rather than literal falsity, relying upon the very obviousness of the literal content to signal that a non-literal interpretation is required.

definition: Two expressions E1 and E2 are synonymous if and only if (i) E1 and E2 have the same extension — they apply to the same objects, (ii) E1 and E2 are interchangeable in all linguistic contexts without altering the meaning of the containing sentence, and (iii) a competent speaker who is asked what E1 means will produce E2, or vice versa.
conditions: E1 and E2 have the same extension
E1 and E2 are interchangeable in all contexts without altering meaning
A competent speaker asked for the meaning of one will produce the other
counterexample: Consider the expressions 'creature with a heart' and 'creature with kidneys.' As a matter of biological fact, these expressions have the same extension — every creature with a heart also has kidneys, and vice versa. Condition (i) is satisfied. Yet no competent speaker asked what 'creature with a heart' means would reply 'creature with kidneys.' The two expressions are not synonymous, because they characterise their common extension by entirely different properties. Condition (iii) is violated, but more fundamentally, the case reveals that sameness of extension is insufficient for synonymy.
missing_condition: The definition relies on extensional equivalence as a necessary condition, but synonymy requires sameness of intension — the expressions must not merely apply to the same objects but must do so by virtue of the same properties or criteria.
passage: One might define synonymy by requiring that two expressions have the same extension, be interchangeable in all contexts without change of meaning, and be such that a speaker asked for the meaning of one would produce the other. This definition attempts to capture synonymy from three angles — extensional, substitutional, and speaker-based. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis, at least with respect to condition (i), is not far to seek. The expressions 'creature with a heart' and 'creature with kidneys' happen, as a matter of biological fact, to have the same extension: every creature possessing a heart also possesses kidneys, and conversely. Condition (i) is satisfied. Yet the expressions are plainly not synonymous. They characterise their common extension by entirely different anatomical properties, and a speaker who understands one may have no idea that it applies to exactly the same creatures as the other. Condition (iii) fails: no competent speaker asked what 'creature with a heart' means would answer 'creature with kidneys.' The case reveals that extensional equivalence is too weak a condition for synonymy. Two expressions may apply to exactly the same objects and yet differ in meaning because they pick out those objects by different criteria. One might observe that synonymy requires not merely sameness of extension but sameness of intension — the expressions must specify the same property or criterion of application, not merely happen to apply to the same things.

definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) there are clear cases to which F applies, (ii) there are clear cases to which F does not apply, and (iii) there are borderline cases for which it is indeterminate whether F applies or not.
conditions: There are clear positive cases
There are clear negative cases
There are borderline cases of indeterminacy
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is a prime number.' There are clear cases to which it applies (2, 3, 5, 7), clear cases to which it does not apply (4, 6, 8, 9), and — one might argue — no borderline cases whatsoever, since every natural number is determinately either prime or not. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied in the trivial sense that the first two hold, but condition (iii) is not. The predicate is not vague. Now consider a different case: the predicate 'is a heap.' There are clear cases (a thousand grains), clear non-cases (a single grain), and borderline cases (thirty grains). But the distinctive feature of the vagueness of 'heap' is not merely that borderline cases exist but that the boundary between the positive cases and the borderline cases is itself indeterminate — there is no sharp line between clear heaps and borderline heaps. The definition captures the existence of borderline cases but not the higher-order vagueness that is characteristic of genuinely vague predicates.
missing_condition: The definition captures first-order vagueness — the existence of borderline cases — but omits higher-order vagueness, the phenomenon whereby the boundaries between clear cases and borderline cases are themselves indeterminate.
passage: One might propose that a predicate is vague when there are clear positive cases, clear negative cases, and borderline cases for which application is indeterminate. This appears to be a clean and intuitive characterisation: it distinguishes vague predicates like 'tall' and 'red' from precise predicates like 'even number' by pointing to the existence of borderline cases. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis emerges when we press upon the boundaries of the borderline region itself. Take the predicate 'is a heap.' There are clear heaps — a thousand grains piled together — and clear non-heaps — a single grain. And there are borderline cases — perhaps thirty grains, or forty. The definition captures this much. But the distinctive difficulty of vagueness is that the boundary between the clear cases and the borderline cases is itself indeterminate. Is fifty grains a clear case of a heap, or a borderline case? There seems to be no determinate answer, and the indeterminacy recurs at every level. The definition, by specifying only that borderline cases exist, treats the regions of clear application and clear non-application as though they had sharp boundaries with the borderline region, which is precisely the kind of sharpness that vague predicates resist. One might observe that genuine vagueness is characterised not only by first-order borderline cases but by the absence of sharp boundaries at every level — a phenomenon of higher-order vagueness that the definition does not capture.

definition: A speaker S performs a speech act of promising if and only if (i) S utters a sentence that expresses the intention to perform a future action A, (ii) S intends the utterance to place himself under an obligation to perform A, and (iii) the hearer H recognises S's intention to undertake the obligation.
conditions: S utters a sentence expressing intention to perform a future action
S intends the utterance to place himself under obligation
H recognises S's intention to undertake the obligation
counterexample: Consider an actor on stage who utters, in the course of a play, 'I promise to return before dawn.' The sentence expresses the intention to perform a future action. The hearer — the audience — recognises that the character is undertaking an obligation. Yet no promise has been made: the actor does not intend to place himself under any obligation, and the audience does not take him to have done so. Condition (ii) is not satisfied, since the actor is performing a role rather than expressing his own intention. But now consider a subtler case: a person says to a friend, 'I promise I'll be there,' fully intending to fulfil the commitment, yet both parties understand that the utterance is merely an emphatic expression of intention rather than a binding promise — 'I promise' here functions as a rhetorical intensifier. All three conditions appear to be met, yet neither party would say that a promise has been made in the full sense.
missing_condition: The definition does not require that the utterance be made in circumstances that both speaker and hearer recognise as appropriate for the creation of a genuine obligation, as opposed to circumstances in which the form of promising is used for emphasis, dramatic performance, or other non-obligating purposes.
passage: One might propose that a speaker performs the speech act of promising when he utters a sentence expressing the intention to perform a future action, intends the utterance to place himself under obligation, and the hearer recognises this intention. This definition captures the core intuition: a promise is a commitment voluntarily undertaken and mutually understood. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises from the common use of promissory language in contexts where no genuine obligation is created. Consider the familiar expression 'I promise I'll try to make it,' uttered by a friend who is uncertain whether he can attend a gathering. The sentence expresses the intention to perform a future action. The speaker may even intend, in some attenuated sense, to obligate himself. The hearer recognises the intention. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet both speaker and hearer understand that this is not a binding promise but an emphatic expression of intention — the word 'promise' is being used as a rhetorical intensifier rather than as a performative that creates an obligation. If the speaker fails to attend, neither party would say that a promise has been broken. One might observe that the definition fails to require that the utterance be made in circumstances that both parties recognise as appropriate for the creation of genuine obligations — circumstances that exclude dramatic performance, rhetorical emphasis, and the various conventional uses of promissory language in which the form of a promise is present but its binding force is absent.

definition: A sentence S presupposes a proposition P if and only if (i) the truth of S requires the truth of P, (ii) the falsity of S also requires the truth of P, and (iii) a competent speaker who utters S takes P for granted as part of the common ground.
conditions: The truth of S requires the truth of P
The falsity of S also requires the truth of P
A competent speaker takes P for granted
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'Even John passed the examination.' This sentence presupposes that John was among the least likely to pass. A competent speaker takes this for granted, and the presupposition persists under negation: 'Even John didn't pass' still conveys that John was among the least likely candidates. But now consider: 'The truth of S requires the truth of P' — is it true that the sentence 'Even John passed the examination' requires, for its truth, that John was among the least likely to pass? A strict logician might say no: the sentence is true if and only if John passed, regardless of how likely or unlikely this was. The presupposition is a pragmatic feature of the utterance rather than a truth-conditional requirement. Condition (i) is not clearly satisfied, yet the presupposition is robust and real.
missing_condition: The definition treats presupposition as a truth-conditional relation, but many presuppositions — particularly those triggered by focus particles like 'even' — are pragmatic in character and persist in use without being requirements for the truth of the sentence.
passage: One might define presupposition as a relation whereby both the truth and the falsity of a sentence require the truth of a presupposed proposition, and the speaker takes the presupposition for granted. This account has the virtue of distinguishing presupposition from entailment: an entailed proposition follows from the truth of a sentence but not from its falsity, whereas a presupposed proposition survives negation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by the focus particle 'even.' The sentence 'Even John passed the examination' carries the presupposition that John was among the least likely to pass. This presupposition persists under negation: 'Not even John passed' still conveys the same background assumption. Condition (iii) is satisfied — a competent speaker takes it for granted. But condition (i) raises a difficulty. Is it a requirement for the truth of the sentence that John was among the least likely to pass? In the strict truth-conditional sense, the sentence is true if and only if John passed the examination. The proposition that John was among the least likely candidates is not a condition of the sentence's truth but a pragmatic accompaniment of its use — a piece of information that the speaker conveys by choosing 'even' rather than a neutral form of expression. One might observe that the definition has assumed that all presuppositions are truth-conditional, whereas a significant class of presuppositions — those triggered by focus particles, intonation, and word order — are pragmatic in character, arising from the conventions governing the use of particular linguistic forms rather than from the truth-conditions of the sentences in which they appear.

definition: The meaning of a complex expression E is compositional if and only if (i) E is composed of meaningful sub-expressions, (ii) the meaning of E is wholly determined by the meanings of its sub-expressions and the syntactic rules by which they are combined, and (iii) the meaning of E is invariant under substitution of synonymous sub-expressions.
conditions: E is composed of meaningful sub-expressions
E's meaning is determined by sub-expression meanings and syntax
E's meaning is invariant under substitution of synonyms
counterexample: Consider the expression 'kick the bucket' used idiomatically to mean 'to die.' The expression is composed of meaningful sub-expressions — 'kick,' 'the,' and 'bucket' all have conventional meanings. But the meaning of the whole — dying — is not determined by the meanings of these parts and their syntactic arrangement. If we substitute a synonym for 'bucket' — say, 'pail' — we get 'kick the pail,' which does not mean 'to die.' Conditions (ii) and (iii) are both violated. Yet the expression is a perfectly legitimate part of the language, understood by every competent speaker.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that all meaningful complex expressions are compositional, but natural languages contain a substantial class of idiomatic expressions whose meanings are conventional wholes not derivable from the meanings of their parts.
passage: One might propose that the meaning of a complex expression is compositional when it is composed of meaningful sub-expressions, its meaning is wholly determined by the meanings of those sub-expressions and the syntactic rules combining them, and its meaning is invariant under substitution of synonymous parts. This principle of compositionality has extraordinary explanatory power: it accounts for our ability to understand sentences we have never encountered before, for the productivity of natural language, and for the systematicity of meaning. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis — or at least the limits of its application — is provided by the ubiquitous phenomenon of idiomatic expression. The phrase 'kick the bucket,' meaning 'to die,' is composed of meaningful words: 'kick' means to strike with the foot, 'the' is a definite article, and 'bucket' means a cylindrical container. The syntactic structure is that of a standard verb phrase. Yet the meaning of the whole — dying — bears no compositional relation to the meanings of the parts. Substituting a synonym for any part destroys the idiom: 'strike the bucket,' 'kick the pail,' 'kick the container' all fail to convey the meaning of the original. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are both violated. One might observe that the principle of compositionality captures a fundamental feature of how language works in general, but that every natural language contains a substantial stock of idiomatic expressions that function as unanalysed wholes, and that a theory of meaning must accommodate both the compositional and the idiomatic dimensions of linguistic competence.

definition: A speaker S makes an assertion by uttering a sentence U if and only if (i) S presents U as true, (ii) S believes U to be true, and (iii) S intends the hearer to accept U on the basis of S's testimony.
conditions: S presents U as true
S believes U to be true
S intends the hearer to accept U on S's testimony
counterexample: Consider a barrister in court who says, 'My client was at home on the evening in question.' The barrister presents this as true, intends the jury to accept it, and — let us suppose — the proposition is indeed the client's testimony. But the barrister may not believe the statement to be true; professional duty requires him to present his client's case regardless of his private opinion. Condition (ii) is violated, yet the barrister has performed a recognisable act of assertion within the institutional context of the courtroom. The definition excludes a familiar and important class of assertions made in professional or institutional roles.
missing_condition: The definition requires personal belief, but assertions may be made in institutional or professional roles in which the speaker presents a proposition as true and invites acceptance without necessarily endorsing it as his own belief.
passage: One might define assertion as the act of presenting a sentence as true, believing it to be true, and intending the hearer to accept it on the basis of one's testimony. This definition captures the ordinary case in which a speaker who says 'It is raining' presents the proposition as true, believes it, and intends the hearer to take it as so. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis is provided by professional and institutional speech. A barrister who says in court, 'My client was at home on the evening of the twenty-third,' presents this as true and intends the jury to accept it. The speech act has all the outward marks of assertion. But the barrister may harbour no personal belief in the truth of the statement; professional ethics require him to present his client's account with full vigour regardless of his private assessment. Condition (ii) is violated: the barrister does not believe what he asserts. Yet the act is recognisably an assertion — it is not a question, not a supposition, not a piece of fiction. The jury treats it as an assertion, the court records it as one, and the legal consequences that follow depend upon its assertoric character. One might observe that the definition has been modelled on the case of sincere personal testimony and does not accommodate the class of assertions made from within institutional roles, where the speaker's personal belief is irrelevant and the assertoric force derives from the role rather than from the individual.

definition: A proper name N refers to an individual X if and only if (i) there is a definite description D that is associated with N in the minds of competent speakers, (ii) X uniquely satisfies D, and (iii) competent speakers use N with the intention of referring to whoever satisfies D.
conditions: A definite description D is associated with N by competent speakers
X uniquely satisfies D
Speakers intend to refer to whoever satisfies D
counterexample: Consider the name 'Aristotle.' Different competent speakers associate different descriptions with this name: some think of 'the teacher of Alexander,' others of 'the author of the Metaphysics,' still others of 'the greatest philosopher of antiquity.' These descriptions, taken singly, might in principle be satisfied by different individuals — a tutor who taught Alexander but wrote nothing, or a philosopher who wrote the Metaphysics but never met Alexander. Yet all these speakers succeed in referring to the same individual. Condition (i) is not uniquely satisfied — there is no single description associated with the name — yet reference is secured nonetheless.
missing_condition: The definition requires that a unique description be associated with each name, but proper names typically function without any single associated description, and reference is secured by historical and causal chains of transmission rather than by descriptive content.
passage: One might propose that a proper name refers to an individual by virtue of a definite description associated with it in the minds of competent speakers, such that the referent is whoever uniquely satisfies that description, and speakers intend to refer to whoever does so. This descriptive theory of names has a natural appeal: it explains how names manage to pick out particular individuals by tying them to identifying properties. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises from the diversity of descriptions that speakers associate with a single name. Consider the name 'Aristotle.' Some speakers associate with it the description 'the teacher of Alexander the Great'; others, 'the author of the Nicomachean Ethics'; others, 'the founder of the Lyceum'; still others, 'the most influential philosopher of antiquity.' These descriptions are not equivalent — they pick out the same individual as a matter of historical fact, but they might in principle have come apart. A tutor of Alexander who never wrote a word of philosophy would satisfy the first description but not the others. There is no single definite description that all competent speakers associate with the name 'Aristotle,' and condition (i) is therefore not satisfied in the way the definition requires. Yet all these speakers refer, by the name 'Aristotle,' to the same individual, and they do so without difficulty. One might observe that names function not by encoding a description but by being transmitted through a historical chain of usage from an initial act of naming, and that the definition confuses the identifying knowledge that speakers happen to possess with the semantic mechanism by which names refer.

definition: A word W means a property F if and only if (i) competent speakers of the language use W to refer to things that have F, (ii) competent speakers would withhold W from things that lack F, and (iii) the extension of W — the class of things to which W applies — is determined entirely by F.
conditions: Competent speakers use W to refer to things that have F
Competent speakers withhold W from things that lack F
The extension of W is determined entirely by F
counterexample: Consider the word 'water.' Competent English speakers use it to refer to the clear, potable liquid found in rivers and lakes, and they withhold it from other liquids. Yet the extension of 'water' is not determined entirely by the observable properties that speakers use to identify water in ordinary practice. It is determined by the chemical composition H2O. A liquid that perfectly mimicked every observable property of water but had a different chemical structure would not, we should say, be water, even though speakers would apply the word to it on the basis of its observable properties.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the extension of a natural-kind term may be determined by underlying structural or compositional properties that are not available to ordinary speakers, so that the meaning of the word outruns the identifying criteria that competent speakers employ.
passage: One might define the meaning of a word as a property such that competent speakers use the word to refer to things possessing the property, withhold the word from things lacking it, and such that the extension of the word is determined entirely by this property. This account captures the meaning of many ordinary terms: the word 'red,' for instance, is used by speakers to refer to red things, withheld from non-red things, and its extension is determined by the property of redness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises with natural-kind terms such as 'water.' Competent English speakers use the word 'water' to refer to the clear, potable, odourless liquid found in rivers, lakes, and rain, and they withhold it from other substances. The identifying property they employ is a cluster of observable features — clarity, potability, a certain taste and feel. Yet the extension of 'water' is not determined by these observable properties. It is determined by the chemical composition H2O. A liquid from another world that perfectly mimicked every observable property of water but had an entirely different molecular structure would not be water, and we should be reluctant to apply the word to it once the structural difference was disclosed. One might observe that the definition, by tying the extension of a word to the property speakers use in identifying its instances, fails to accommodate cases in which the true determinant of extension is a hidden or theoretical property that goes beyond ordinary speakers' identifying capacities.

definition: A proper name N refers to an individual x if and only if (i) there is a set of descriptions D associated with N in the minds of competent speakers, (ii) x uniquely satisfies the descriptions in D, and (iii) speakers intend to refer to the individual satisfying D when they use N.
conditions: There is a set of descriptions associated with N in the minds of competent speakers
x uniquely satisfies the descriptions in D
Speakers intend to refer to the individual satisfying D when they use N
counterexample: Consider the name 'Aristotle.' The descriptions commonly associated with this name include 'the teacher of Alexander the Great,' 'the author of the Metaphysics,' and 'the founder of the Lyceum.' Now suppose, through some extraordinary historical discovery, that the individual who taught Alexander was not the same person who wrote the Metaphysics. No single individual uniquely satisfies all the descriptions. Yet speakers who use the name 'Aristotle' would not conclude that the name fails to refer. They would revise their descriptions to accommodate the discovery, and the name would continue to refer to some particular historical individual.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the reference of a proper name may be fixed not by a set of descriptions that the referent uniquely satisfies but by an initial act of naming — a baptism or introduction — and a subsequent chain of communicative use that preserves the link to the original individual.
passage: One might propose that a proper name refers to an individual in virtue of a set of descriptions associated with the name in the minds of competent speakers, where the individual uniquely satisfies those descriptions and speakers intend to refer to whoever satisfies them. This proposal captures an intuitive picture of how names work: we use the name 'Aristotle' because we associate with it such descriptions as 'the teacher of Alexander' and 'the author of the Metaphysics,' and the name refers to the individual who satisfies these descriptions. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account arises when we imagine that historical research uncovers a startling fact: the individual who taught Alexander the Great was not the same person who wrote the Metaphysics or founded the Lyceum. In such a scenario, no single individual uniquely satisfies all the descriptions commonly associated with the name 'Aristotle.' Condition (ii) of the definition fails. Yet speakers who use the name would not conclude that 'Aristotle' fails to refer. They would adjust their descriptions, and the name would continue to refer to some particular historical figure — presumably the one from whom the name has been transmitted through a chain of communicative use. One might observe that the reference of the name appears to be preserved by a causal-historical chain of transmission rather than by the satisfaction of a description, and that the definition, by tying reference entirely to descriptive content, cannot accommodate this resilience.

definition: An utterance U is metaphorical if and only if (i) U contains a term T that is applied to a subject S to which T does not literally apply, (ii) there is a recognised resemblance between S and the things to which T literally applies, and (iii) a competent speaker can recover the intended meaning of U by identifying this resemblance.
conditions: U contains a term applied to a subject to which it does not literally apply
There is a recognised resemblance between the subject and the things to which the term literally applies
A competent speaker can recover the intended meaning by identifying this resemblance
counterexample: Consider the metaphor 'Juliet is the sun.' Romeo applies the term 'sun' to Juliet, to whom it does not literally apply. A competent speaker grasps that Juliet is being characterised as radiant, life-giving, and central to Romeo's existence. Yet it would be a considerable impoverishment to say that the meaning is recovered by identifying a resemblance between Juliet and the sun. The metaphor does not merely assert a likeness — it invites the hearer to see Juliet through the lens of the sun, generating an indefinite range of associations that cannot be captured by any single paraphrase.
missing_condition: The definition reduces metaphor to implicit comparison, failing to recognise that metaphorical utterances characteristically generate new meaning by inviting the hearer to view the subject through the lens of the metaphorical term, producing an open-ended range of implications that no literal paraphrase can exhaust.
passage: One might define a metaphorical utterance as one in which a term is applied to a subject to which it does not literally apply, where there is a recognised resemblance between the subject and the things to which the term literally applies, and where a competent speaker recovers the meaning by identifying this resemblance. This account captures something important about how metaphors function: when we say that a man is a fox, we rely upon the hearer's recognition of a resemblance between the man's behaviour and the cunning traditionally attributed to foxes. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises with metaphors of greater richness and complexity. Consider Romeo's declaration that Juliet is the sun. A competent speaker grasps something of the intended meaning — that Juliet is radiant, vital, and the centre of Romeo's world. Yet to say that the meaning is recovered by identifying a resemblance between Juliet and the sun is to impoverish the metaphor almost beyond recognition. The metaphor does not merely assert that Juliet resembles the sun in certain specifiable respects. It invites the hearer to see Juliet through the lens of everything we associate with the sun — warmth, light, the source of life, the centre around which other things revolve — and this process generates an indefinite range of implications and associations that no literal paraphrase can exhaust. One might observe that the definition, by reducing metaphor to the identification of resemblance, fails to capture the generative and open-ended character of metaphorical meaning.

definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) there exist borderline cases to which competent speakers are uncertain whether F applies, (ii) the boundaries of F's extension are not precisely fixed by linguistic convention, and (iii) the uncertainty in borderline cases arises from the speaker's incomplete information about the object in question.
conditions: There exist borderline cases to which competent speakers are uncertain whether F applies
The boundaries of F's extension are not precisely fixed by linguistic convention
The uncertainty in borderline cases arises from the speaker's incomplete information about the object
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'tall' as applied to adult men. There exist borderline cases — a man of five feet ten inches, say — to which competent speakers are uncertain whether 'tall' applies. The boundaries of the predicate's extension are not precisely fixed by convention. Yet the uncertainty does not arise from incomplete information about the man. We may know his height to the nearest millimetre and still be uncertain whether to call him tall. The uncertainty is intrinsic to the predicate itself, not to our information about the object.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly locates the source of vagueness in the speaker's incomplete information, when in fact the uncertainty characteristic of vague predicates persists even when all relevant information about the object is available, because the uncertainty is a feature of the predicate's meaning rather than of our factual situation.
passage: One might define a vague predicate as one for which there exist borderline cases generating uncertainty among competent speakers, whose boundaries are not precisely fixed by convention, and whose uncertainty in borderline cases arises from the speaker's incomplete information about the object. This definition attempts to assimilate vagueness to a familiar kind of uncertainty — the uncertainty that arises when we lack relevant facts. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this assimilation concerns the predicate 'tall' as applied to adult men. There plainly exist borderline cases: a man of five feet ten inches may or may not be called tall, and competent speakers will disagree. The boundaries of the predicate are not fixed by convention — no linguistic rule specifies the precise height at which a man becomes tall. Yet the uncertainty does not arise from incomplete information about the man. We may know his height to the nearest millimetre. We may know his weight, his build, and the average height of the population to which he belongs. All relevant factual information may be available, and the uncertainty persists. One might observe that this persistence reveals something important: the uncertainty attaching to vague predicates is not a species of factual ignorance but is intrinsic to the predicate itself. The predicate 'tall' does not draw a precise boundary that we have failed to discover; it simply draws no precise boundary at all. The definition, by attributing the uncertainty to incomplete information, misidentifies the source of vagueness.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker S expresses an intention to perform a future action A, (ii) S intends that the hearer H take the utterance as placing S under an obligation to perform A, and (iii) S sincerely intends to perform A.
conditions: The speaker expresses an intention to perform a future action
The speaker intends that the hearer take the utterance as placing the speaker under an obligation
The speaker sincerely intends to perform the action
counterexample: Consider a person who, under duress, promises a kidnapper that she will deliver a sum of money, fully intending from the moment she speaks never to deliver it and to contact the police instead. She expresses an intention to perform a future action, and she intends that the kidnapper take her utterance as placing her under an obligation. Condition (iii) — sincerity — is not satisfied. Yet in the social and legal context, the utterance functions as a promise: the kidnapper relies upon it, and the speaker has placed herself under a commitment that she may subsequently be held to have made. It would be odd to say that no promise was made merely because the speaker was insincere.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that promising is a conventional social act whose force depends upon the utterance's conformity to social conventions of commitment-making rather than upon the speaker's sincere intention, so that insincere promises are still promises — though dishonest ones.
passage: One might define a promise as an utterance in which the speaker expresses an intention to perform a future action, intends that the hearer take the utterance as placing the speaker under an obligation, and sincerely intends to perform the action. This definition ties the act of promising to the speaker's genuine intentions and captures the paradigmatic case in which a person promises in good faith and means what she says. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider insincere promises. Suppose a person, held captive by a kidnapper, promises to deliver a ransom, fully intending from the moment she speaks to contact the police and deliver nothing. She expresses an intention to perform a future action and intends that the kidnapper take her utterance as creating an obligation. Condition (iii) is plainly unsatisfied: she does not sincerely intend to perform the action. Yet in the social context, the utterance functions as a promise. The kidnapper relies upon it. Others who learn of the utterance would describe it as a promise — a broken one, perhaps, or a dishonest one, but a promise none the less. One might observe that the act of promising appears to be a conventional social act whose force is determined by the utterance's conformity to established conventions of commitment-making. The speaker who goes through the forms of promising has promised, whether or not she is sincere. The definition, by requiring sincerity, conflates the conditions for an honest promise with the conditions for a promise as such.

definition: Two expressions E1 and E2 are synonymous if and only if (i) E1 and E2 have the same extension — they apply to exactly the same objects, (ii) E1 and E2 can be substituted for each other in any sentence without altering the truth-value of that sentence, and (iii) competent speakers regard E1 and E2 as interchangeable.
conditions: E1 and E2 have the same extension
E1 and E2 can be substituted for each other in any sentence without altering truth-value
Competent speakers regard E1 and E2 as interchangeable
counterexample: Consider the expressions 'creature with a heart' and 'creature with kidneys.' As a matter of biological fact, every creature that has a heart also has kidneys, and vice versa. The two expressions have the same extension. Competent speakers might well regard them as interchangeable in many contexts. Yet they are plainly not synonymous. The sentence 'Necessarily, every creature with a heart has a heart' is true, but the sentence 'Necessarily, every creature with a heart has kidneys' is not — the co-extension is contingent, not necessary. Condition (ii) fails in modal contexts, revealing that co-extensiveness is insufficient for synonymy.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the co-extension of the two expressions be necessary rather than contingent, so that expressions which merely happen to apply to the same objects are conflated with expressions that share their meaning.
passage: One might define synonymy as the relation between two expressions that have the same extension, that can be substituted for each other in any sentence without altering truth-value, and that competent speakers regard as interchangeable. This definition captures an intuitive notion of sameness of meaning and has the merit of combining extensional with attitudinal criteria. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the expressions 'creature with a heart' and 'creature with kidneys.' As a matter of biological fact, these expressions are co-extensive: every creature that possesses the one organ also possesses the other. In many ordinary sentences, they can be substituted without altering truth-value: 'The cat is a creature with a heart' and 'The cat is a creature with kidneys' are both true. Competent speakers might well regard them as interchangeable in everyday discourse. Yet they are not synonymous. The sentence 'Necessarily, every creature with a heart has a heart' is true, but the sentence 'Necessarily, every creature with a heart has kidneys' is not, for there is no necessity in the biological connection between the two organs. The co-extension is contingent — a product of evolutionary history rather than of meaning. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only co-extension rather than necessary co-extension, cannot distinguish genuine synonymy from the accidental coincidence of extensions.

definition: A speaker S asserts a proposition P if and only if (i) S utters a declarative sentence expressing P, (ii) S presents P as true, and (iii) S believes that P is true.
conditions: S utters a declarative sentence expressing P
S presents P as true
S believes that P is true
counterexample: Consider an actor performing a role in a play who utters the declarative sentence 'The king is dead' in a tone of earnest conviction. The actor utters a declarative sentence, and in the context of the dramatic performance, the character presents the proposition as true. Yet the actor does not assert that the king is dead. She is performing, not asserting. All the surface features of assertion are present — the declarative form, the presentation of a proposition as true — yet no assertion occurs, because the utterance is governed by the conventions of dramatic performance rather than those of sincere communication.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the utterance be governed by the conventions of serious discourse rather than by the conventions of fiction, performance, or other non-assertoric modes, so that utterances in dramatic or literary contexts are wrongly classified as assertions.
passage: One might define assertion as the act in which a speaker utters a declarative sentence expressing a proposition, presents the proposition as true, and believes it to be true. This definition captures the paradigmatic case of assertion in everyday communication: a person who says 'It is raining' in the course of ordinary conversation, believing it to be raining, is plainly asserting that it is raining. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises in the context of dramatic performance. An actor who, in the course of performing Hamlet, utters the sentence 'The king is dead' does so with all the surface features of assertion. She utters a declarative sentence, and in the dramatic context she presents the proposition as true — her character sincerely announces the death of the king. Yet the actor is not asserting that the king is dead. She is performing a role, and her utterance is governed by the conventions of theatrical performance rather than by the conventions of serious communication. One might observe that no one in the audience takes the actor to be committing herself to the truth of the proposition. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the form of the utterance and the apparent sincerity of the speaker, fails to distinguish between genuine assertions, which are governed by the norms of serious discourse, and utterances that merely mimic the form of assertion within non-assertoric contexts such as fiction, performance, and play.

definition: A speaker S implicates a proposition Q by uttering a sentence expressing P if and only if (i) S does not say Q explicitly, (ii) the hearer H can infer Q from the fact that S has said P together with general principles of cooperative communication, and (iii) S intends H to infer Q.
conditions: S does not say Q explicitly
H can infer Q from P together with general principles of cooperative communication
S intends H to infer Q
counterexample: Consider a conversation in which one party asks whether a certain colleague is a good philosopher, and the other replies, 'He has very neat handwriting.' The hearer infers, by cooperative principles, that the speaker means to suggest that the colleague is not a good philosopher — for if she had something favourable to say about his philosophical abilities, she would have said it. The speaker intends this inference. All three conditions are met. Now consider a case in which a speaker, asked the same question, replies identically but without any intention of conveying a negative assessment — she has simply misheard the question and believes she was asked about the colleague's penmanship. The hearer infers the same negative implicature. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but (iii) is not. Yet the hearer's inference is not unreasonable, and in ordinary practice we should say the utterance implicates the negative assessment whether or not the speaker intended it.
missing_condition: The definition makes implicature depend entirely upon the speaker's intention, failing to recognise that implicatures may arise from the objective features of the conversational context and the reasonable inferences available to the hearer, regardless of the speaker's actual communicative intention.
passage: One might define implicature as the relation in which a speaker, by uttering a sentence expressing one proposition, conveys another proposition that is not explicitly stated, where the hearer can infer the conveyed proposition from general principles of cooperative communication and the speaker intends the hearer to draw this inference. This definition captures many familiar cases: when a speaker, asked for a letter of recommendation, writes only that the candidate is punctual, the reader infers that the speaker has nothing favourable to say about the candidate's abilities, and the speaker intends this inference. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition arises when the speaker's intention is absent. Suppose a person is asked whether a colleague is a good philosopher and replies, 'He has very neat handwriting.' The hearer, relying upon cooperative principles, infers that the speaker means to suggest the colleague is no good. But suppose the speaker has misheard the question and genuinely believes she was asked about the colleague's penmanship. She has no intention of conveying a negative assessment. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied — the negative proposition is not stated explicitly, and the hearer can infer it from cooperative principles — but condition (iii) fails. Yet the hearer's inference is entirely reasonable, and one might observe that in ordinary practice we should say the utterance carries the negative implicature whether or not the speaker intended it. It appears that the definition, by making the speaker's intention a necessary condition, cannot accommodate implicatures that arise from the objective conversational situation.

definition: A sentence S presupposes a proposition P if and only if (i) the truth of P is taken for granted by anyone who asserts S, (ii) the truth of P is equally taken for granted by anyone who denies S, and (iii) if P is false, then S is neither true nor false.
conditions: The truth of P is taken for granted by anyone who asserts S
The truth of P is equally taken for granted by anyone who denies S
If P is false then S is neither true nor false
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'John has stopped smoking.' This presupposes that John formerly smoked. Anyone who asserts the sentence takes it for granted that John smoked, and anyone who denies it — by saying 'John has not stopped smoking' — equally takes this for granted. If John never smoked, the presupposition fails. Yet it is not obvious that the sentence becomes neither true nor false when the presupposition fails. One might argue that if John never smoked, the sentence 'John has stopped smoking' is simply false. He has not stopped smoking because he never started. The truth-value gap predicted by condition (iii) is at best controversial.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that presupposition failure invariably produces a truth-value gap, when in fact many competent speakers judge that sentences with failed presuppositions are straightforwardly false, suggesting that presupposition is a pragmatic rather than a semantic phenomenon that need not affect truth-value in the manner the definition requires.
passage: One might define presupposition as the relation in which a sentence takes for granted a proposition that is equally presupposed whether the sentence is asserted or denied, and where the failure of the presupposed proposition renders the sentence neither true nor false. This definition captures a familiar pattern: the sentence 'The present king of France is bald' presupposes that there is a present king of France, and this presupposition survives negation — 'The present king of France is not bald' presupposes the same thing. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of condition (iii) arises with the sentence 'John has stopped smoking.' The sentence presupposes that John formerly smoked. Anyone who asserts it takes this for granted, and anyone who denies it does likewise. But suppose John has never smoked. The definition predicts that the sentence is neither true nor false — that it falls into a truth-value gap. Yet many competent speakers, confronted with this situation, are strongly inclined to say that the sentence is simply false. John has not stopped smoking, they would say, because he never started. One might observe that this reaction suggests that presupposition failure does not invariably produce a gap in truth-value, and that the phenomenon of presupposition may be better understood as a pragmatic feature of the conversational context than as a semantic relation that governs truth-value. The definition, by building the truth-value gap into the very notion of presupposition, may be too strong.

definition: A sentence S is analytic if and only if (i) S is true, (ii) the truth of S is determined solely by the meanings of the terms occurring in S, and (iii) the denial of S is self-contradictory.
conditions: S is true
The truth of S is determined solely by the meanings of its terms
The denial of S is self-contradictory
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'Nothing is both red and green all over at the same time.' This sentence is true, and its denial appears to be not merely false but in some sense absurd. Yet it is far from clear that its truth is determined solely by the meanings of the terms 'red' and 'green.' The sentence seems to express a substantive truth about the nature of colour — it reflects the incompatibility of red and green, which appears to be a fact about colour itself rather than about the meanings of colour words. A person who fully grasped the meanings of 'red' and 'green' might still require experience of colour to see that nothing can be both red and green all over.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the existence of necessary truths whose necessity derives not from the meanings of terms alone but from the nature of the properties those terms designate, so that sentences may be necessary without being analytic in the sense the definition requires.
passage: One might propose that a sentence is analytic when it is true, its truth is determined solely by the meanings of its constituent terms, and its denial is self-contradictory. This characterisation captures the paradigmatic examples: 'All bachelors are unmarried' is true in virtue of what 'bachelor' and 'unmarried' mean, and its denial — that there exists a married bachelor — is self-contradictory. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account arises when one considers sentences expressing colour incompatibilities. The sentence 'Nothing is both red and green all over at the same time' is true, and its denial appears absurd — not merely false, but in some sense impossible. It might seem, therefore, to satisfy all three conditions. Yet one should hesitate to say that its truth is determined solely by the meanings of 'red' and 'green.' The meanings of these words — the linguistic conventions governing their use — do not by themselves entail that the properties they designate are incompatible. A person who had mastered the meanings of both words but had never perceived colours might not be in a position to see that nothing can be both red and green all over. The impossibility appears to flow from the nature of the colours themselves rather than from the meanings of the words. One might observe that the sentence expresses a necessary truth, but a necessity that is grounded in the character of colour rather than in linguistic convention. The definition, by tying analyticity exclusively to meaning, cannot accommodate necessary truths whose source lies in the nature of the properties designated rather than in the definitions of the terms.

definition: The meaning of a declarative sentence S is the set of possible worlds in which S is true — that is, S means that P if and only if (i) S is true in exactly those possible worlds in which P obtains, (ii) competent speakers who understand S are thereby in a position to determine the truth-value of S in any possible world, and (iii) two sentences have the same meaning if and only if they are true in exactly the same possible worlds.
conditions: S is true in exactly those possible worlds in which P obtains
Competent speakers who understand S are thereby in a position to determine the truth-value of S in any possible world
Two sentences have the same meaning if and only if they are true in exactly the same possible worlds
counterexample: Consider the sentences 'Two plus two equals four' and 'Every even number greater than two is the sum of two primes.' Both are true in all possible worlds if Goldbach's conjecture is true, and false in none. By condition (iii), they have the same meaning. Yet a competent speaker of English would not regard them as synonymous: the first is a trivial arithmetic identity, while the second is a deep, unproven conjecture. They differ radically in cognitive significance.
missing_condition: The definition identifies meaning with truth-conditions across possible worlds, and thereby cannot distinguish the meanings of necessarily co-extensional sentences — sentences true in exactly the same possible worlds — that differ in cognitive significance, informativeness, or conceptual content.
passage: A prominent proposal in the philosophy of language identifies the meaning of a sentence with the set of possible worlds in which it is true. This proposal has the merit of providing a precise, compositional semantics: the meaning of a complex expression is determined by the meanings of its parts and the mode of combination. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal arises when we consider necessarily true sentences. If Goldbach's conjecture is true, then 'Two plus two equals four' and 'Every even number greater than two is the sum of two primes' are both true in every possible world. By condition (iii), they have the same meaning. Yet no competent speaker would regard these sentences as synonymous. The first is a trivial truth grasped immediately by any numerate person; the second is a profound conjecture that has resisted proof for centuries. They differ in cognitive significance, in informativeness, and in the mathematical understanding required to grasp them. One might observe that the possible-worlds account, by identifying meaning with truth-conditions, obliterates these distinctions. It appears that the definition cannot discriminate between sentences that, though necessarily co-extensional, differ in every other semantic respect that matters to competent speakers.

definition: A singular term T refers to an object O if and only if (i) T is associated with a definite description that uniquely identifies O, (ii) the speaker who uses T intends to pick out O by means of that description, and (iii) O satisfies the definite description associated with T.
conditions: T is associated with a definite description that uniquely identifies O
The speaker who uses T intends to pick out O by means of that description
O satisfies the definite description associated with T
counterexample: Consider a speaker at a party who sees a man drinking from a martini glass and says, 'The man drinking the martini is interesting.' Unbeknown to the speaker, the glass contains water, not a martini. The man the speaker intends to pick out does not satisfy the associated description 'the man drinking the martini,' for he is not drinking a martini. Yet we should not hesitate to say that the speaker successfully referred to that man — every person present would understand whom the speaker meant.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the intended referent satisfy the associated description, and thereby cannot account for cases of referential use in which the speaker successfully refers to an object by means of a description that the object does not in fact satisfy.
passage: A traditional account of reference holds that a singular term refers to an object by being associated with a definite description that the object uniquely satisfies, where the speaker intends to pick out the object by means of that description. This account has the merit of explaining how reference is secured: the description serves as a criterion that singles out one object from all others. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account arises when the associated description fails to apply. Suppose that a speaker at a gathering sees a man drinking from a martini glass and says, 'The man drinking the martini is interesting.' Unknown to the speaker, the glass contains water. The man does not satisfy the description 'the man drinking the martini.' Condition (iii) is violated. Yet the speaker has plainly referred to the man — every person present knows whom the speaker means. The reference succeeds despite the falsity of the associated description. One might observe that the definition, by requiring satisfaction of the description, conflates two functions: the descriptive function (saying something true about the referent) and the referential function (picking out the referent). In cases of referential use, the latter function may succeed even when the former fails. It appears that the analysis cannot accommodate this duality.

definition: A proper name N names an individual I if and only if (i) there exists a chain of communication linking the current use of N back to an initial baptism in which I was dubbed N, (ii) each speaker in the chain intends to use N to refer to the same individual as the speaker from whom they acquired the name, and (iii) the initial baptism was conducted in the perceptual presence of I.
conditions: There exists a chain of communication linking the current use of N back to an initial baptism in which I was dubbed N
Each speaker in the chain intends to use N to refer to the same individual as the speaker from whom they acquired the name
The initial baptism was conducted in the perceptual presence of I
counterexample: Consider the name 'Neptune.' It was introduced by astronomers who had never perceived the planet but who inferred its existence from perturbations in the orbit of Uranus. The initial baptism was not conducted in the perceptual presence of Neptune. Yet 'Neptune' indisputably names Neptune. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but condition (iii) is not.
missing_condition: The definition requires perceptual presence at the initial baptism and thereby cannot accommodate cases in which a name is introduced for an entity whose existence is inferred rather than perceived — a common practice in science and other domains.
passage: A causal-historical account of naming holds that a proper name names an individual if there is a chain of communication from current use back to an initial baptism, each speaker intends referential continuity, and the baptism was conducted in the perceptual presence of the named individual. This analysis captures many ordinary cases: a child is named in the presence of family, and the name passes from speaker to speaker, each intending to refer to the same child. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the analysis arises with names introduced by theoretical inference. The name 'Neptune' was introduced before the planet was ever observed. Astronomers noted perturbations in the orbit of Uranus and inferred the existence of a hitherto unperceived planet, which they named 'Neptune.' The initial baptism was conducted not in the perceptual presence of the planet but in its absence — the planet was a theoretical posit whose existence was confirmed only later by telescopic observation. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied: there is a chain of communication, and each speaker intends to refer to the same planet. But condition (iii) is violated: the initial baptism was not conducted in perceptual presence. One might observe that the definition, by requiring perceptual acquaintance at the moment of naming, cannot accommodate the common scientific practice of naming entities whose existence is inferred from theory.

definition: A translation T of a sentence S from language L1 into language L2 is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-conditions of S — that is, T is true in all and only those circumstances in which S is true, (ii) T employs only expressions of L2 whose meanings are independently established, and (iii) a competent bilingual speaker would accept T as a rendering of S.
conditions: T preserves the truth-conditions of S — that is, T is true in all and only those circumstances in which S is true
T employs only expressions of L2 whose meanings are independently established
A competent bilingual speaker would accept T as a rendering of S
counterexample: Consider the French sentence 'Il pleut' and two English translations: 'It is raining' and 'Rain is falling.' Both preserve the truth-conditions of the French sentence, both employ independently established English expressions, and a competent bilingual speaker would accept either as a rendering. Yet the two translations differ in grammatical structure, emphasis, and stylistic register. The definition, satisfying all three conditions for both, provides no basis for preferring one over the other, even when contextual or stylistic considerations clearly favour one.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the translation preserve features beyond truth-conditions — such as register, connotation, emphasis, or syntactic parallelism — so that translations which are truth-conditionally equivalent but stylistically or pragmatically divergent are treated as equally adequate.
passage: One might analyse adequate translation as the preservation of truth-conditions using independently established expressions, endorsed by competent bilingual speakers. This analysis captures the central demand that a translation say the same thing as the original. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when we consider translations that are truth-conditionally identical but pragmatically divergent. The French sentence 'Il pleut' may be rendered into English as 'It is raining' or as 'Rain is falling.' Both translations are true in exactly the same circumstances as the French original. Both employ well-established English expressions. A competent bilingual speaker might accept either. All three conditions are satisfied by both translations. Yet the two renderings differ in emphasis, register, and syntactic character: the first is colloquial and natural, while the second is more formal and somewhat stilted. In certain contexts — a novel, a weather report, a poem — the choice between them matters greatly. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to truth-conditions and bilingual endorsement, cannot distinguish translations that are pragmatically apt from those that, whilst truth-conditionally adequate, fail to capture the tone, emphasis, or stylistic character of the original.

definition: An utterance U is metaphorical if and only if (i) the literal meaning of U is false or absurd when applied to the intended subject, (ii) U invites the hearer to transfer features from a secondary subject to a primary subject by way of analogy, and (iii) the hearer can identify a set of salient resemblances between the two subjects that renders the transferred features intelligible.
conditions: The literal meaning of U is false or absurd when applied to the intended subject
U invites the hearer to transfer features from a secondary subject to a primary subject by way of analogy
The hearer can identify a set of salient resemblances between the two subjects that renders the transferred features intelligible
counterexample: Consider the utterance 'No man is an island.' Taken literally, this is true — no human being is a body of land surrounded by water. The literal meaning is neither false nor absurd. Yet the utterance is plainly metaphorical: it invites the hearer to transfer features of insularity — isolation, self-sufficiency — from the geographical concept to the human condition. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied, but condition (i) is not, since the literal meaning is straightforwardly true.
missing_condition: The definition requires that the literal meaning of the metaphorical utterance be false or absurd, and thereby cannot accommodate metaphors whose literal content is true but whose metaphorical import consists in inviting the hearer to attend to features that transcend the literal truth.
passage: A natural analysis of metaphor holds that an utterance is metaphorical when its literal meaning is false or absurd, when it invites analogical transfer of features, and when salient resemblances render the transfer intelligible. This analysis accommodates many familiar metaphors: 'Juliet is the sun' is literally false, and the hearer transfers features of warmth and radiance from the sun to Juliet. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises with metaphors whose literal content is true. Consider the utterance 'No man is an island.' Taken literally, this is uncontroversially true: no human being is a geographical feature. Condition (i) is not satisfied — the literal meaning is neither false nor absurd. Yet the utterance is paradigmatically metaphorical. It invites the hearer to consider human beings in the light of insular isolation and to recognise that no person is wholly self-sufficient. One might observe that the definition, by requiring literal falsity as a necessary condition, cannot accommodate metaphors whose power lies precisely in the truistic character of their literal content. It appears that the trigger for metaphorical interpretation is not always literal falsity but sometimes a recognition that the literal truth, though undeniable, fails to exhaust what the speaker intends to communicate.
