definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker S, in uttering U, expresses an intention to perform a future action A, (ii) S believes that A is something the hearer H wants S to do, and (iii) S intends U to place S under an obligation to perform A.
conditions: The speaker S, in uttering U, expresses an intention to perform a future action A
S believes that A is something the hearer H wants S to do
S intends U to place S under an obligation to perform A
counterexample: Consider a mafioso who says to a shopkeeper, 'I promise to visit your shop every week.' The mafioso expresses an intention to perform a future action (visiting the shop). The mafioso believes the hearer wants the action performed — if only out of fear. The mafioso intends the utterance to create an obligation. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that this is not a promise but a threat: the mafioso's 'visits' are acts of intimidation, and the shopkeeper's 'want' is coerced rather than genuine.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the hearer's desire for the promised action be genuine and uncoerced, so that threats disguised as promises satisfy all the stated conditions.
passage: One might analyse promising as the expression of an intention to perform a future action that the hearer wants performed, accompanied by the speaker's intention to create an obligation. This analysis captures the paradigmatic features of promising: the future-directedness, the hearer's interest, and the self-imposed obligation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider insincere or coercive uses of the promissory form. Suppose a mafioso addresses a shopkeeper: 'I promise to visit your shop every week.' The mafioso expresses an intention to perform a future action — visiting the shop. The mafioso believes the shopkeeper wants the visits to occur, at least in the sense that the shopkeeper prefers visits to the unpleasant alternative. The mafioso intends the utterance to create an obligation. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that this utterance is not a promise but a thinly veiled threat. The shopkeeper's 'desire' for the visits is not a genuine desire but a preference extracted under duress. It appears that the definition, by failing to require that the hearer's desire be freely and genuinely held, cannot distinguish promises from threats. The line between the two is drawn not merely by the speaker's intentions and the formal structure of the utterance but by the conditions under which the hearer's desire is formed.

definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) there exist borderline cases to which neither F nor its negation can be confidently applied, (ii) F admits of degrees — that is, F can apply to objects to a greater or lesser extent, and (iii) F is susceptible to the sorites paradox: a series of pairwise indistinguishable cases leads from a clear instance of F to a clear non-instance.
conditions: There exist borderline cases to which neither F nor its negation can be confidently applied
F admits of degrees — that is, F can apply to objects to a greater or lesser extent
F is susceptible to the sorites paradox: a series of pairwise indistinguishable cases leads from a clear instance of F to a clear non-instance
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is a prime number.' There exist borderline cases in a practical sense — very large numbers whose primality is unknown — to which neither 'is a prime number' nor 'is not a prime number' can be confidently applied given current computational resources. Yet 'is a prime number' does not admit of degrees: a number either is or is not prime. And it is not susceptible to the sorites paradox. The predicate is not vague; it is merely indeterminate owing to our ignorance for certain cases. Condition (i) appears satisfied, but conditions (ii) and (iii) are not.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between genuine semantic vagueness, in which the borderline cases arise from the meaning of the predicate itself, and mere indeterminacy arising from ignorance, in which the borderline cases arise from our ignorance of determinate facts.
passage: One might characterise a vague predicate as one that admits of borderline cases, comes in degrees, and is susceptible to the sorites paradox. This characterisation captures predicates like 'tall,' 'bald,' and 'red,' which exhibit all three features. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis arises when we consider predicates that are merely indeterminate owing to ignorance rather than genuinely vague. Consider the predicate 'is a prime number.' For very large numbers — numbers beyond the reach of current computational methods — we may be unable to determine whether the predicate applies. These are borderline cases in a practical sense: neither the predicate nor its negation can be confidently applied. Yet 'is a prime number' does not admit of degrees — a number is either prime or it is not — and it is not susceptible to the sorites paradox. The indeterminacy arises not from the meaning of the predicate but from the limits of our computational capacity. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to the phenomenology of borderline cases, cannot distinguish the semantic vagueness of 'tall' from the mere indeterminacy-from-ignorance of 'is a prime number.' It appears that a satisfactory account must locate the source of the borderline cases in the meaning of the predicate rather than in the limitations of those who apply it.

definition: A word W means M if and only if (i) speakers of the language regularly use W to convey M, (ii) hearers who understand the language regularly interpret W as conveying M, and (iii) the regularity described in (i) and (ii) is sustained by a convention — a mutually recognised practice that speakers and hearers expect one another to follow.
conditions: Speakers of the language regularly use W to convey M
Hearers who understand the language regularly interpret W as conveying M
The regularity described in (i) and (ii) is sustained by a convention — a mutually recognised practice that speakers and hearers expect one another to follow
counterexample: Consider the word 'nice,' which in contemporary English is regularly used to convey something like 'pleasant' or 'agreeable.' This regularity is sustained by convention. All three conditions are met. Yet in earlier centuries, 'nice' meant 'foolish' or 'ignorant.' The conventional meaning shifted over time without any explicit decision or agreement. The definition gives no account of how meaning changes: at the transitional stage, when some speakers used 'nice' in the old sense and others in the new, the regularity condition was not clearly satisfied, yet the word was not meaningless.
missing_condition: The definition presupposes a stable convention and cannot accommodate periods of semantic transition in which a word's meaning is shifting, so that no single convention governs its use and neither the old nor the new meaning fully satisfies the regularity conditions.
passage: A conventionalist account of meaning holds that a word means what speakers regularly use it to convey, hearers regularly interpret it as conveying, and the regularity is sustained by mutual convention. This account has the merit of grounding meaning in actual linguistic practice. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider semantic change. The word 'nice' in contemporary English is used by convention to mean 'pleasant' or 'agreeable.' All three conditions are satisfied. But in the fourteenth century, 'nice' meant 'foolish' — a meaning sustained by a different convention. Between these two stable states, there was a period of transition during which some speakers used 'nice' in the older sense and others in the newer. During this period, neither usage constituted a clear regularity, and the convention was fractured. The definition, requiring regularity sustained by convention, cannot accommodate this transitional stage. One might observe that the word was not meaningless during the transition — speakers understood one another, albeit sometimes imperfectly — yet the definition provides no resources for describing a meaning that is in flux. It appears that the conventionalist analysis, by requiring a settled convention, cannot account for the pervasive phenomenon of semantic drift.

definition: A general term T refers to a natural kind K if and only if (i) T is typically applied to samples of K on the basis of observable characteristics shared by those samples, (ii) the extension of T is determined by the underlying nature common to paradigmatic samples rather than by the observable surface features, and (iii) competent speakers defer to experts in determining the precise boundaries of K.
conditions: T is typically applied to samples of K on the basis of observable characteristics shared by those samples
The extension of T is determined by the underlying nature common to paradigmatic samples rather than by the observable surface features
Competent speakers defer to experts in determining the precise boundaries of K
counterexample: Consider the term 'jade,' which is applied to green, hard, translucent stones. The extension of 'jade' is determined by observable surface features — colour, hardness, lustre — that are shared by two chemically distinct minerals, jadeite and nephrite. There is no single underlying nature common to all jade samples; the two minerals have different molecular structures. Competent speakers defer to experts. Yet conditions (i) and (iii) are satisfied while (ii) is violated: the underlying nature does not unify the extension, because there are two underlying natures grouped under one term.
missing_condition: The definition assumes that every natural kind term picks out samples unified by a single underlying nature, and thereby cannot accommodate cases in which a single term, through historical accident or surface resemblance, groups together samples belonging to distinct natural kinds.
passage: A natural kind theory of reference holds that a general term refers to a kind whose extension is determined by the underlying nature common to paradigmatic samples, even though speakers initially apply the term on the basis of surface features, and defer to experts for precise boundary-drawing. This account captures the reference of terms like 'water' and 'gold,' where a single underlying nature — molecular or atomic structure — unifies the extension. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account arises with the term 'jade.' Ordinary speakers apply 'jade' to green, hard, translucent stones on the basis of observable characteristics. They defer to experts for precise classification. But the experts discover that 'jade' encompasses two chemically distinct minerals: jadeite (a pyroxene) and nephrite (an amphibole). There is no single underlying nature common to all jade samples. Condition (ii) is violated: the extension is not determined by one underlying nature but by the disjunction of two. One might observe that the definition presupposes a one-to-one correspondence between natural kind terms and underlying natures. It appears that this presupposition fails for terms like 'jade,' where historical practice has grouped together samples that, at the level of underlying structure, belong to distinct kinds.

definition: A proper name N is meaningful if and only if (i) N is associated with a cluster of descriptions that speakers would use to identify the bearer of N, (ii) the bearer of N satisfies a weighted majority of the descriptions in the cluster, and (iii) no other individual satisfies the same weighted majority of descriptions.
conditions: N is associated with a cluster of descriptions that speakers would use to identify the bearer of N
The bearer of N satisfies a weighted majority of the descriptions in the cluster
No other individual satisfies the same weighted majority of descriptions
counterexample: Consider a case in which the name 'Jonah' is associated with the cluster of descriptions: the Hebrew prophet who was swallowed by a great fish, who preached in Nineveh, and who was the son of Amittai. Suppose historical research reveals that no individual satisfies even one of these descriptions — the story is entirely legendary. The bearer of the name satisfies none of the descriptions in the cluster, violating condition (ii). Yet speakers would not say that 'Jonah' is meaningless; they would say that Jonah did not exist, which presupposes that the name has meaning sufficient to support an existential denial.
missing_condition: The definition cannot accommodate names whose associated descriptions are wholly unsatisfied, for it treats the meaning of a name as dependent upon the existence of a bearer who satisfies the cluster, and thereby cannot explain how a name can be meaningful even when its bearer does not exist.
passage: A cluster theory of names holds that a proper name is meaningful if it is associated with a cluster of descriptions, the bearer satisfies a weighted majority of them, and no other individual satisfies the same majority. This analysis improves upon the simple description theory by allowing flexibility: the bearer need not satisfy every description, only a sufficient proportion. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider names from legend or myth. The name 'Jonah' is associated with a cluster of descriptions: the Hebrew prophet who was swallowed by a great fish, who preached repentance in Nineveh, and who was the son of Amittai. Suppose that scholarly investigation reveals no historical individual who satisfies even one of these descriptions — the entire narrative is legendary. Condition (ii) is violated: no bearer satisfies a weighted majority of the cluster. Yet speakers would not say that 'Jonah' is meaningless. On the contrary, they would say that Jonah did not exist — a claim that presupposes 'Jonah' has sufficient meaning to support an existential denial. One might observe that the definition, by tying the meaningfulness of a name to the existence of a bearer who satisfies the associated descriptions, cannot account for the manifest meaningfulness of names whose bearers are fictional or legendary.

definition: A word W means M if and only if (i) competent speakers of the language use W to convey M, (ii) a hearer who understands the language can recover M from an utterance of W in context, and (iii) the association between W and M is governed by a convention shared among the speakers of the language.
conditions: Competent speakers of the language use W to convey M
A hearer who understands the language can recover M from an utterance of W in context
The association between W and M is governed by a convention shared among the speakers of the language
counterexample: Suppose a speaker, wishing to convey to a companion that they should leave a tedious gathering, says 'What a lovely evening this has been' in a tone of unmistakable irony. The hearer understands perfectly that the speaker means 'We should leave.' But the conventional meaning of the sentence is an expression of enjoyment, not a proposal to depart. The speaker has used the words to convey something, the hearer has recovered it, but the association between the words and what is conveyed is not governed by convention. All three conditions are met only if we take M to be the conventional meaning, but the speaker's actual meaning is something quite different.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between what a word or sentence conventionally means and what a speaker means by using it on a particular occasion, thereby conflating linguistic meaning with speaker meaning.
passage: One might characterise meaning as the content that competent speakers use a word to convey, that hearers can recover from an utterance, and that is governed by linguistic convention. This characterisation captures the standard cases with admirable economy: the word 'red' means the colour red because speakers use it to convey that colour, hearers recover that content, and the association is conventional. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal arises in the phenomenon of verbal irony. Suppose a speaker, trapped at a tedious gathering, says to a companion, 'What a lovely evening this has been,' in a tone that makes perfectly clear that the remark is ironic. The speaker uses the words to convey not enjoyment but its opposite — perhaps a suggestion that they should leave. The hearer, understanding the irony, recovers this meaning without difficulty. One might say that conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied with respect to the ironically conveyed meaning. But condition (iii) is not: there is no convention in English that associates the sentence 'What a lovely evening this has been' with the proposal to leave a gathering. The ironic meaning is produced by the speaker's exploitation of the conventional meaning, not by convention itself. It appears that the definition cannot accommodate the divergence between what the words conventionally mean and what the speaker means by them on a particular occasion. One might observe that this divergence is not a marginal phenomenon but a pervasive feature of linguistic communication, arising not only in irony but in metaphor, understatement, and indirect speech generally.

definition: A singular term T refers to an object O if and only if (i) T is associated with a description or set of properties that uniquely identifies O, (ii) O satisfies the description or possesses the properties associated with T, and (iii) competent speakers who use T intend to pick out the object that satisfies the associated description.
conditions: T is associated with a description or set of properties that uniquely identifies O
O satisfies the description or possesses the properties associated with T
Competent speakers who use T intend to pick out the object that satisfies the associated description
counterexample: Suppose that speakers commonly associate the name 'Aristotle' with the description 'the teacher of Alexander the Great.' Aristotle does satisfy this description. But now suppose that historical research reveals that Alexander's actual teacher was a different philosopher entirely, and that Aristotle never taught Alexander. When speakers used the name 'Aristotle,' they were referring to the man born in Stagira who wrote the Metaphysics — not to whoever taught Alexander. The description fails to identify the intended referent, yet speakers were referring to Aristotle all along.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the possibility that the descriptions speakers associate with a name may be incorrect, and that a name may refer to an individual not because the individual satisfies the associated description but because the name has been transmitted through a causal chain of usage originating in an initial act of naming.
passage: One might propose that a singular term refers to an object in virtue of being associated with a uniquely identifying description that the object satisfies and that speakers intend to apply. This proposal captures a natural picture of how reference works: we attach descriptions to names, and the names pick out whatever fits the descriptions. 'Aristotle' refers to Aristotle because Aristotle is the one who satisfies the descriptions we associate with the name — the teacher of Alexander, the author of the Metaphysics, the philosopher born in Stagira. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal arises when we consider the possibility that the associated descriptions might be false. Suppose it were discovered that the individual we call 'Aristotle' never in fact taught Alexander. The most commonly associated description would then fail to identify anyone who is Aristotle. On the proposed definition, the name 'Aristotle' would refer to whoever did teach Alexander — some entirely different person. But this seems plainly wrong. Speakers who used the name 'Aristotle' were not referring to Alexander's actual teacher, whoever that may have been; they were referring to the philosopher who wrote certain books and held certain views, regardless of whether he taught Alexander. One might observe that the definition makes reference hostage to the accuracy of associated descriptions, when in practice reference seems to be secured by a chain of usage stretching back to the original introduction of the name, rather than by the correctness of any particular description.

definition: Two expressions E₁ and E₂ are synonymous if and only if (i) E₁ and E₂ can be substituted for each other in any sentence without altering the truth-value of that sentence, (ii) E₁ and E₂ evoke the same mental associations in competent speakers, and (iii) E₁ and E₂ belong to the same register and carry the same connotations.
conditions: E₁ and E₂ can be substituted for each other in any sentence without altering the truth-value of that sentence
E₁ and E₂ evoke the same mental associations in competent speakers
E₁ and E₂ belong to the same register and carry the same connotations
counterexample: Consider the expressions 'ophthalmologist' and 'eye doctor.' They can be substituted for each other in extensional contexts without altering truth-value. They evoke, one might argue, the same mental associations — both bring to mind a physician who treats ailments of the eye. But they do not belong to the same register: 'ophthalmologist' is formal and technical, whilst 'eye doctor' is colloquial. Condition (iii) fails. Yet in any context where register is irrelevant, the expressions are perfectly synonymous. The definition, by requiring identity of register, excludes pairs of expressions that are synonymous in meaning even when they differ in stylistic level.
missing_condition: The definition fails to distinguish between synonymy of meaning and identity of stylistic or pragmatic properties, and thereby requires more than sameness of meaning for two expressions to count as synonymous.
passage: One might characterise synonymy as the relation that holds between two expressions when they are intersubstitutable without change of truth-value, evoke the same mental associations, and share the same register and connotations. This characterisation aims at a comprehensive account of sameness of meaning that includes not only denotation but also the pragmatic and stylistic dimensions of language. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this approach arises in the most ordinary vocabulary. Consider the expressions 'ophthalmologist' and 'eye doctor.' They denote the same class of medical practitioners. They can be substituted for each other in straightforward assertions without changing truth-values: 'Jones visited an ophthalmologist' and 'Jones visited an eye doctor' are true under exactly the same conditions. A competent speaker who hears either expression forms the same conception of the kind of physician in question. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. But the expressions differ in register: 'ophthalmologist' belongs to the technical vocabulary of medicine, whilst 'eye doctor' belongs to everyday colloquial speech. One would not use 'eye doctor' in a medical journal, nor 'ophthalmologist' in casual conversation with a child. Condition (iii) fails. The definition therefore denies that the expressions are synonymous. Yet one might observe that they mean precisely the same thing, and that the difference between them is a matter of style rather than of meaning. It appears that the definition, by incorporating register and connotation into the requirements for synonymy, demands more than sameness of meaning and thereby renders synonymy rarer than it in fact is.

definition: An utterance U is metaphorical if and only if (i) U asserts something that is literally false, (ii) the literal falsity of U is manifest to both speaker and hearer, and (iii) the hearer can recover the speaker's intended meaning by identifying a relevant resemblance between the literal subject and the metaphorical predicate.
conditions: U asserts something that is literally false
The literal falsity of U is manifest to both speaker and hearer
The hearer can recover the speaker's intended meaning by identifying a relevant resemblance between the literal subject and the metaphorical predicate
counterexample: Consider the utterance 'No man is an island.' Taken literally, this is true: no human being is a body of land surrounded by water. Condition (i) fails, since the utterance is literally true rather than literally false. Yet the utterance is paradigmatically metaphorical — it uses the image of an island to convey something about human interdependence. The definition excludes it because it is not literally false.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate metaphors that are literally true, where the metaphorical meaning is conveyed not by the falsity of the literal assertion but by the incongruity or unexpectedness of making a literally true assertion in the given context.
passage: One might propose that metaphor consists in asserting something literally false, where the falsity is manifest and the hearer recovers the intended meaning by identifying a resemblance between subject and predicate. This proposal captures a large class of metaphors. When one says 'Juliet is the sun,' the literal assertion is false — Juliet is not a celestial body — and the hearer grasps the intended meaning by recognising a resemblance between Juliet's radiance and the sun's. The manifest falsity serves as a signal that the utterance is not to be taken at face value. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal arises when we consider metaphors that are literally true. 'No man is an island' is literally true: no human being is a body of land surrounded by water. The literal truth is so obvious as to be trivial, and it is precisely this triviality that signals the presence of metaphor. The hearer understands that the speaker is not making a geographical observation but conveying something about human connectedness and dependence. Condition (i) fails, however, because the utterance is literally true. The definition is compelled to deny that 'No man is an island' is a metaphor. One might observe that this is an unacceptable result, for the utterance is among the most celebrated metaphors in the English language. It appears that literal falsity, though common in metaphor, is not essential to it, and that what signals metaphorical intent may be the incongruity of a trivially true assertion rather than the manifest falsity of a literally false one.

definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) there exist borderline cases to which the application of F is neither clearly correct nor clearly incorrect, (ii) F admits of degrees — that is, objects can be more or less F, and (iii) the boundaries of F's extension are not sharply defined.
conditions: There exist borderline cases to which the application of F is neither clearly correct nor clearly incorrect
F admits of degrees
The boundaries of F's extension are not sharply defined
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is a prime number.' A natural number either is or is not prime; there are no borderline cases, and the predicate does not admit of degrees. It is not vague. But now consider the predicate 'is a heap.' It plainly has borderline cases and lacks sharp boundaries. However, one might question whether it admits of degrees: is a collection of one thousand grains more of a heap than a collection of five hundred? One might say that both are equally heaps, and that the difference between them is one of size rather than of heap-ness. Condition (ii) is doubtful, yet the predicate is paradigmatically vague.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for predicates that are vague in virtue of having borderline cases and lacking sharp boundaries but that do not clearly admit of degrees, thereby imposing a scalar structure that not all vague predicates possess.
passage: One might characterise vagueness as the possession of borderline cases, the admission of degrees, and the absence of sharp boundaries. This characterisation captures the most discussed examples: 'tall,' 'bald,' 'red,' and the like. These predicates have borderline cases — persons who are neither clearly tall nor clearly not tall — admit of degrees — one person can be taller than another — and lack sharp boundaries — there is no precise height at which tallness begins. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises when we consider the predicate 'is a heap.' It is among the most ancient examples of vagueness, figuring in the sorites paradox attributed to Eubulides. It has borderline cases: a collection of, say, thirty grains of sand is neither clearly a heap nor clearly not a heap. Its boundaries are not sharply defined: there is no precise number of grains at which a collection becomes a heap. But does 'heap' admit of degrees? One might observe that it is unclear whether a collection of one thousand grains is more of a heap than a collection of five hundred. Both are heaps; the difference between them seems to be a difference in size rather than a difference in the degree to which they are heaps. If 'heap' does not admit of degrees, then condition (ii) fails, and the definition is compelled to classify it as not vague. Yet 'heap' is the paradigmatic case of a vague predicate. It appears that the definition, by requiring the admission of degrees, imposes a scalar structure that some vague predicates may lack.

definition: A sentence S presupposes a proposition P if and only if (i) the truth of P is required for S to have a determinate truth-value, (ii) the negation of S also requires the truth of P for a determinate truth-value, and (iii) P is not part of what S asserts but is rather a background condition taken for granted by the speaker.
conditions: The truth of P is required for S to have a determinate truth-value
The negation of S also requires the truth of P for a determinate truth-value
P is not part of what S asserts but is rather a background condition taken for granted by the speaker
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'Jones has stopped beating his horse.' This presupposes that Jones has a horse and was previously beating it. The negation — 'Jones has not stopped beating his horse' — equally presupposes that Jones has a horse. But now suppose Jones says, in an argument with a neighbour, 'I have stopped beating my horse,' precisely in order to assert that he once beat the horse. The proposition that he once beat the horse is not merely a background condition; it is precisely what he intends to communicate. Condition (iii) fails, even though the proposition is, by standard tests, a presupposition of the sentence.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate cases in which a speaker deliberately exploits the presuppositional structure of a sentence in order to assert the presupposed content, thereby collapsing the distinction between what is presupposed and what is asserted.
passage: One might propose that presupposition is the relation that holds when the truth of a background proposition is required for a sentence and its negation alike to have determinate truth-values, and when that proposition is not part of what the sentence asserts. This proposal captures the standard cases. 'The present king of France is bald' presupposes that there is a present king of France: both the sentence and its negation require the existence of such a king for a determinate truth-value, and the existence claim is a background condition rather than an assertion. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the third condition arises in strategic communication. Suppose a man says to his neighbour, 'I have stopped beating my horse.' On the standard analysis, this sentence presupposes that the man has a horse and that he was previously beating it. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. But suppose the man utters the sentence precisely in order to convey that he once beat the horse — this is the information he wishes to communicate, and he chooses the presuppositional form to make it appear to be already accepted rather than newly asserted. In this case, the presupposed content is part of what the speaker intends to assert, and condition (iii) fails. One might observe that this strategic exploitation of presupposition is far from unusual: speakers regularly use presuppositional constructions to smuggle controversial claims into the background of discourse. It appears that the definition, by requiring that presupposed content not be asserted, cannot accommodate the pragmatic flexibility with which speakers actually deploy presupposition.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker S, in uttering U, expresses an intention to perform some future action A, (ii) S intends U to place S under an obligation to perform A, and (iii) the hearer H recognises S's intention and accepts the obligation thereby created.
conditions: The speaker S expresses an intention to perform some future action A
S intends U to place S under an obligation to perform A
The hearer H recognises S's intention and accepts the obligation thereby created
counterexample: Suppose a man, held at gunpoint, says to his captor, 'I promise to give you all my money.' He expresses an intention to perform a future action. He intends the utterance to place himself under an obligation. The captor recognises and accepts the obligation. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to say that the man has made a genuine promise, for his utterance was coerced rather than voluntary. Promises extracted by force or duress are not binding in the way that freely given promises are.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the speaker's utterance be voluntary and free from coercion, and thereby counts as genuine promises utterances that are extracted by force and that lack the moral authority characteristic of freely undertaken obligations.
passage: One might characterise a promise as an utterance by which a speaker expresses an intention to perform a future action, intends thereby to place himself under an obligation, and is recognised and accepted by the hearer. This characterisation identifies three central features of promising: the expression of intention, the creation of obligation, and the mutual recognition of speaker and hearer. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider the conditions under which the utterance is made. Suppose a man, confronted by an armed assailant, says, 'I promise to give you all my money if you let me go.' He expresses an intention to perform a future action — the handing over of money. He intends the utterance to create an obligation, at least in the sense that he expects the assailant to hold him to it. The assailant recognises the intention and accepts the obligation. Every condition of the definition is satisfied. Yet one might observe that the man has not, in any morally significant sense, made a promise. His utterance was coerced; it was extracted by force rather than freely given. A promise given under duress lacks the moral weight of a promise given voluntarily. It appears that the definition, by attending to the expression of intention, the creation of obligation, and mutual recognition, neglects the requirement that the speaker's commitment be voluntary. Without this requirement, the definition cannot distinguish a genuine promise from a forced capitulation.

definition: A speaker S makes an assertion in uttering a sentence U if and only if (i) S presents the propositional content of U as true, (ii) S believes the propositional content of U to be true, and (iii) S intends the hearer to accept the propositional content of U on the basis of S's presentation.
conditions: S presents the propositional content of U as true
S believes the propositional content of U to be true
S intends the hearer to accept the propositional content of U on the basis of S's presentation
counterexample: Consider an actor on stage who utters the sentence 'Denmark is a prison.' He presents the propositional content as true — he delivers the line with conviction, as though affirming it. He may even believe it, if he happens to hold that view. And the audience, in a certain sense, accepts the content within the framework of the play. Yet the actor is not making an assertion. He is performing a role, and his utterance is understood by all parties as a contribution to a dramatic fiction rather than as a claim about the world.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the speaker present the propositional content as true in a context of genuine communicative commitment rather than in a context of pretence, fiction, or performance, and thereby cannot distinguish assertions from the utterances of actors, storytellers, and other practitioners of non-assertive discourse.
passage: One might propose that assertion consists in presenting propositional content as true, believing it to be true, and intending the hearer to accept it. This proposal identifies assertion with a certain combination of presentation, belief, and communicative intention. It correctly classifies the standard cases: when a scientist reports her findings, when a witness testifies in court, when a friend tells you the time, each presents content as true, believes it, and intends acceptance. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal arises in the theatre. An actor, delivering Hamlet's lines, says with every appearance of conviction, 'Denmark is a prison.' He presents this content as true — his performance is indistinguishable, in its surface features, from a sincere assertion. He may even believe it, if he happens to share the sentiment. And the audience, engaged in the play, accepts the content within the dramatic framework. Conditions (i), (ii), and (iii) may all be satisfied. Yet no one would say that the actor has made an assertion. His utterance is understood by all parties as a contribution to a fiction, not as a claim about the state of Denmark. One might observe that what the definition lacks is a requirement that the presentation occur within a context of genuine communicative commitment — a context in which the speaker undertakes to stand behind the content and to answer for its truth. The actor undertakes no such commitment. It appears that the definition, by attending to presentation, belief, and intention without specifying the appropriate context, cannot distinguish assertion from performance.

definition: A speaker S implicates a proposition Q by uttering a sentence U if and only if (i) S does not say Q directly — Q is not part of the literal content of U, (ii) the hearer H can infer Q from U together with background assumptions about rational communication, and (iii) S could cancel the implication of Q without contradiction by adding a further clause to U.
conditions: S does not say Q directly
The hearer H can infer Q from U together with background assumptions about rational communication
S could cancel the implication of Q without contradiction by adding a further clause to U
counterexample: Consider a speaker who says, 'Jones has three children,' in response to the question 'Does Jones qualify for the family tax benefit, which requires at least three children?' The hearer infers that Jones has exactly three children. This is not said directly — the literal content is compatible with Jones having more. The inference arises from background assumptions about rational communication. And the speaker could cancel it: 'Jones has three children — in fact, he has five.' All three conditions are satisfied. But now suppose the implication is a conventional one — arising from the meaning of a word rather than from pragmatic reasoning. Consider 'Jones is poor but honest.' The word 'but' implies a contrast between poverty and honesty, yet this implication is not cancellable without oddity: 'Jones is poor but honest — and there is no contrast between the two' sounds contradictory. Condition (iii) fails for conventional implications, even though they are clearly cases of implication.
missing_condition: The definition restricts implicature to conversational implicature by requiring cancellability, and thereby excludes conventional implicatures that arise from the meanings of specific words and that cannot be cancelled without contradiction or oddity.
passage: One might characterise implicature as a proposition that is not said directly, that the hearer can infer from background assumptions about rational communication, and that the speaker could cancel without contradiction. This characterisation captures the phenomenon of conversational implicature with considerable precision. When a speaker says 'Jones has three children,' the hearer typically infers that Jones has exactly three, though the literal content is compatible with more. The speaker could cancel this inference — 'Jones has three children; in fact he has five' — without contradiction. All three conditions are met. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation arises with what might be called conventional implication. Consider the sentence 'Jones is poor but honest.' The word 'but' carries an implication of contrast: it suggests that poverty and honesty are in some tension with each other. This implication is not part of the truth-conditional content of the sentence — condition (i) is satisfied, for 'Jones is poor and honest' has the same truth-conditions. And the hearer can infer it — condition (ii) is satisfied. But the implication is not cancellable: one cannot say 'Jones is poor but honest, though I do not mean to suggest any contrast between the two' without producing an utterance that strikes competent speakers as contradictory or at least deeply odd. Condition (iii) fails. The definition therefore excludes conventional implications from the class of implicatures. One might observe that this exclusion is too severe, for the contrast conveyed by 'but' is genuinely implied rather than said, and any adequate account of implication should accommodate it.

definition: To understand a sentence S is to grasp its truth-conditions if and only if (i) one can specify the conditions under which S would be true and the conditions under which S would be false, (ii) one's specification is compositional — that is, it derives the truth-conditions of S from the meanings of S's parts and the way they are combined, and (iii) one's grasp of the truth-conditions suffices for understanding S in every context of use.
conditions: One can specify the conditions under which S would be true and the conditions under which S would be false
One's specification is compositional
One's grasp of the truth-conditions suffices for understanding S in every context of use
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'That is heavy.' One can specify its truth-conditions compositionally: it is true if and only if the object demonstrated is heavy. But this specification does not suffice for understanding the sentence in every context, because 'that' requires a demonstration to fix its reference, and 'heavy' is relative to a standard that varies with context — a heavy feather is not heavy by the standard applied to suitcases. One can grasp the compositional truth-conditions of the sentence and still fail to understand what is being said on a particular occasion of use. Condition (iii) fails.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that for sentences containing demonstratives, indexicals, or context-sensitive predicates, a grasp of compositional truth-conditions must be supplemented by contextual information in order to yield full understanding, and that truth-conditions alone may underdetermine what is communicated.
passage: One might propose that understanding a sentence consists in grasping its truth-conditions, where this grasp is compositional and sufficient for understanding in every context. This proposal embodies the central thesis of truth-conditional semantics: to understand a sentence is to know what the world would have to be like for the sentence to be true. The requirement of compositionality ensures that understanding is systematic — it explains how speakers can understand sentences they have never encountered before, by deriving truth-conditions from the meanings of parts and their mode of combination. The requirement of sufficiency ensures that truth-conditions exhaust what there is to understand. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this proposal arises in the analysis of context-sensitive language. Consider the sentence 'That is heavy.' Its truth-conditions, compositionally specified, state that it is true if and only if the demonstrated object is heavy. But this specification, considered in the abstract, does not suffice for understanding the sentence on any particular occasion. The demonstrative 'that' requires a contextual act of demonstration to fix its reference. The predicate 'heavy' is relative to a standard that varies with context: a heavy feather weighs a fraction of what a heavy suitcase weighs. One may grasp the compositional truth-conditions perfectly and still not understand what is being said, because the truth-conditions themselves are indeterminate until contextual parameters are supplied. It appears that the definition, by requiring that a grasp of truth-conditions suffice for understanding in every context, makes a demand that context-sensitive sentences cannot satisfy, and that a supplementary account of contextual determination is needed.

definition: A word W means an object or property M if and only if (i) competent speakers of the language use W to refer to or predicate M, (ii) the use of W to designate M is governed by a rule or convention within the linguistic community, and (iii) any competent speaker, upon learning the rule, would be able to apply W correctly to new instances of M.
conditions: Competent speakers of the language use W to refer to or predicate M
The use of W to designate M is governed by a rule or convention within the linguistic community
Any competent speaker, upon learning the rule, would be able to apply W correctly to new instances of M
counterexample: Consider the word 'game' as it functions in English. Competent speakers apply it to chess, football, solitaire, ring-a-ring-o'-roses, and countless other activities. Condition (i) is satisfied. But what rule or convention governs this use? There is no single feature common to all games — some are competitive, others are not; some involve skill, others pure chance; some are played alone, others in groups. No rule can be stated that would enable a speaker to determine, for any arbitrary activity, whether it is a game. Condition (iii) fails, for the word's application cannot be captured by a rule that generates correct verdicts in all new cases. Yet 'game' is a perfectly meaningful word.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate words whose meaning is constituted not by a rule specifying necessary and sufficient conditions for application but by a network of overlapping similarities among instances, none of which need be shared by all.
passage: One might propose that a word means what it does in virtue of a rule governing its use within a linguistic community — a rule that any competent speaker can learn and apply to new cases. This proposal combines the social dimension of meaning, grounded in shared convention, with the productive dimension, grounded in the capacity to extend the word to fresh instances. It explains how speakers can communicate successfully and how children can learn language: they acquire rules and apply them. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the analysis of ordinary vocabulary. Consider the English word 'game.' Competent speakers apply it without hesitation to a vast range of activities: board games, card games, ball games, Olympic games, war games, word games. The word is not vague in any troublesome sense; speakers generally agree about whether a given activity is a game. Condition (i) is satisfied. But when one attempts to state the rule that governs this application, one finds that no such rule can be formulated. Board games involve a board, but ball games do not. Some games are competitive, but patience is not. Some require skill, but roulette requires only luck. Some are played by groups, but solitaire is played alone. There is no single feature, or conjunction of features, present in all games and absent from all non-games. The activities we call games are united not by a common essence but by a network of overlapping and criss-crossing similarities. One might observe that this phenomenon is not peculiar to the word 'game.' It arises with 'language,' 'number,' 'tool,' and many other perfectly meaningful terms. The definition, by requiring a rule that generates correct application to new cases, presupposes that every meaningful word has a specifiable essence. Where no such essence exists, the definition must declare the word meaningless — a conclusion that is plainly absurd.

definition: A name N refers to an object O if and only if (i) N is associated with a definite description D that uniquely identifies O, (ii) a competent speaker who uses N intends to designate whatever satisfies D, and (iii) O in fact satisfies D.
conditions: N is associated with a definite description D that uniquely identifies O
A competent speaker who uses N intends to designate whatever satisfies D
O in fact satisfies D
counterexample: Consider the name 'Aristotle.' Different speakers associate different descriptions with it: 'the teacher of Alexander,' 'the author of the Metaphysics,' 'the most famous student of Plato.' Suppose a speaker associates with 'Aristotle' the description 'the teacher of Alexander' and uses the name on this basis. If it were discovered that Aristotle never taught Alexander, the speaker would not conclude that 'Aristotle' fails to refer; she would revise the associated description. The name seems to refer to the man regardless of which description the speaker has in mind. Condition (i) fails, for there is no single definite description that all competent users associate with the name.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that proper names may refer to their bearers directly, in virtue of a historical chain of use linking current speakers to an original act of naming, rather than in virtue of any description the speaker associates with the name.
passage: One might maintain that a name refers to its bearer in virtue of an associated definite description: 'Aristotle' refers to the individual who satisfies whatever description the speaker associates with the name — perhaps 'the teacher of Alexander' or 'the author of the Nicomachean Ethics.' This view has the advantage of explaining how names are meaningful: they are, in effect, abbreviated descriptions, and their meaning consists in the descriptive content the speaker attaches to them. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the ordinary use of proper names. Consider the name 'Aristotle' as it is used among educated English speakers. Different speakers associate quite different descriptions with it. One speaker thinks of Aristotle as the teacher of Alexander; another as the philosopher who wrote the Metaphysics; a third as the most celebrated student of Plato. These descriptions pick out the same individual, but they do so by different routes and emphasise different features. Now suppose it were discovered that Aristotle never in fact taught Alexander — that the historical tradition on this point were mistaken. The speaker who associated 'Aristotle' with the description 'the teacher of Alexander' would not conclude that the name fails to refer. She would not say, 'There is no Aristotle, since no one satisfies my description.' She would say, 'I was wrong about Aristotle — he did not teach Alexander after all.' The name continues to refer to the man, regardless of the falsity of the associated description. One might observe that what secures the reference of the name is not any particular description but a historical chain connecting current uses of the name back to an initial act of naming the individual. The definition, by grounding reference in description, gets the order of explanation backwards.

definition: The meaning of a declarative sentence S is given by its truth-conditions if and only if (i) S has a determinate set of conditions under which it is true, (ii) understanding S consists in grasping these conditions, and (iii) two sentences with identical truth-conditions have the same meaning.
conditions: S has a determinate set of conditions under which it is true
Understanding S consists in grasping these conditions
Two sentences with identical truth-conditions have the same meaning
counterexample: Consider the sentences 'Hesperus is a planet' and 'Phosphorus is a planet.' Since Hesperus is Phosphorus — both names designate Venus — the two sentences are true under exactly the same conditions: both are true if and only if Venus is a planet. Their truth-conditions are identical. Yet the sentences differ in meaning, as is shown by the fact that an ancient astronomer might assent to one whilst denying the other, not realising that the morning star and the evening star are the same celestial body. Condition (iii) fails: identical truth-conditions do not guarantee sameness of meaning.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the cognitive or informational dimension of meaning — the fact that two sentences may be true in exactly the same circumstances yet differ in the way they present their subject matter, such that a rational agent may take different attitudes toward them.
passage: One might propose that the meaning of a declarative sentence consists in its truth-conditions: to understand a sentence is to grasp the conditions under which it would be true, and sentences with the same truth-conditions have the same meaning. This proposal is elegant and powerful. It connects meaning to the world in a perspicuous way: to know what a sentence means is to know what the world must be like for the sentence to be true. It also promises a compositional theory of meaning, for the truth-conditions of a complex sentence can be derived from the truth-conditions of its parts. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the phenomenon of informative identity statements. Consider the two sentences 'Hesperus is a planet' and 'Phosphorus is a planet.' Hesperus and Phosphorus are the same celestial body — Venus — observed at different times: one in the evening, the other in the morning. The two sentences are therefore true under exactly the same conditions. Both are true if and only if Venus is a planet, and false otherwise. Their truth-conditions are identical, and condition (iii) therefore entails that they have the same meaning. But they do not have the same meaning, as the following consideration shows. An ancient astronomer who had not yet discovered that the morning star and the evening star are one and the same body might perfectly well accept 'Hesperus is a planet' on the basis of evening observations whilst remaining agnostic about 'Phosphorus is a planet,' having made no morning observations. The astronomer understands both sentences; his grasp of their truth-conditions, at the level relevant to his conduct, is adequate. Yet he rationally takes different attitudes toward them. One might observe that the difference in meaning between the two sentences lies not in the conditions under which they are true but in the manner in which they present their subject matter — in what one might call their sense as opposed to their reference.

definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) there exist borderline cases to which neither F nor not-F can be determinately applied, (ii) F admits of a sorites series — a sequence of cases such that adjacent members are indiscriminable in respect of F yet the endpoints differ, and (iii) the vagueness of F is eliminable by stipulating precise boundaries for its application.
conditions: There exist borderline cases to which neither F nor not-F can be determinately applied
F admits of a sorites series — a sequence of cases such that adjacent members are indiscriminable in respect of F yet the endpoints differ
The vagueness of F is eliminable by stipulating precise boundaries for its application
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is a heap.' It admits of borderline cases and a sorites series: one grain of sand is not a heap; adding one grain to a non-heap does not make a heap; yet ten thousand grains constitute a heap. One might attempt to eliminate the vagueness by stipulating that a collection of five hundred or more grains constitutes a heap. But the stipulated boundary is arbitrary and fails to capture the meaning of 'heap.' Speakers will not accept 499 grains as determinately not a heap and 500 as determinately a heap. The predicate resists the imposition of precise boundaries, and any stipulation distorts its meaning. Condition (iii) fails: the vagueness is not eliminable without altering the concept.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that the vagueness of many predicates is a constitutive feature of their meaning rather than a deficiency that can be remedied by stipulation, and that enforcing precision would change the concept rather than clarify it.
passage: One might characterise vagueness as a property of predicates that admit borderline cases and sorites series, where the vagueness is in principle eliminable through the stipulation of precise boundaries. The first two conditions capture the phenomena most closely associated with vagueness: the existence of hard cases and the susceptibility to paradoxical reasoning by small increments. The third condition reflects the thought that vagueness is a deficiency of language — an imprecision that could, with sufficient care, be repaired. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this third condition may be drawn from the analysis of ordinary predicates. Consider the predicate 'is a heap.' It satisfies conditions (i) and (ii) beyond any question. There are borderline cases: is a collection of forty grains of sand a heap? Neither 'yes' nor 'no' seems determinately correct. And the sorites series is easily constructed: remove one grain from a heap and it remains a heap; continue until only one grain remains, and it is no longer a heap. But can the vagueness be eliminated by stipulation? Suppose one decrees that a heap is any collection of five hundred or more grains. This stipulation introduces precision, but at the cost of distorting the concept. Competent speakers will not accept that 499 grains arranged in a mound-shaped pile fail to constitute a heap whilst 500 grains scattered in a thin layer do constitute one. One might observe that the problem is not merely practical but conceptual. The predicate 'heap' is essentially vague: its capacity to admit borderline cases is part of what it means to be a heap-concept rather than a precise-cardinality concept. A language in which 'heap' had sharp boundaries would contain a different concept, not a clarified version of the original one. The definition, by supposing that vagueness is eliminable, treats as accidental what is in fact essential.

definition: An utterance U is a metaphor if and only if (i) U contains a term or phrase that is used in a sense other than its literal sense, (ii) the non-literal sense is recoverable by the audience through a comparison between the literal and the intended meaning, and (iii) U can be paraphrased without loss of content by a literal statement that makes the comparison explicit.
conditions: U contains a term or phrase that is used in a sense other than its literal sense
The non-literal sense is recoverable by the audience through a comparison between the literal and the intended meaning
U can be paraphrased without loss of content by a literal statement that makes the comparison explicit
counterexample: Consider the metaphor 'Juliet is the sun.' One may attempt a literal paraphrase: 'Juliet is like the sun in that she is radiant, warm, life-giving, and the centre of Romeo's existence.' But this paraphrase does not capture the full content of the metaphor. The metaphor suggests further that Romeo's world revolves around Juliet, that her absence would plunge him into darkness, that she illuminates everything she touches — and these suggestions proliferate without determinate limit. No finite literal paraphrase can exhaust the metaphor's content. Condition (iii) fails: the metaphor resists paraphrase without loss.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that metaphors characteristically possess an open-ended surplus of meaning that cannot be fully captured by any literal paraphrase, and that this inexhaustibility is not a deficiency but a central feature of metaphorical expression.
passage: One might propose that a metaphor is an utterance in which a term is used non-literally, where the non-literal sense is recoverable through comparison and expressible in a literal paraphrase. This proposal makes metaphor a species of indirect communication: the speaker says one thing, means another, and the audience recovers the intended meaning by discerning the relevant comparison. The requirement of paraphrasability ensures that the metaphor's content is fully specifiable in literal terms. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this requirement may be drawn from literary analysis. Consider Romeo's declaration: 'Juliet is the sun.' What is the literal paraphrase? One might attempt: 'Juliet resembles the sun in being bright, warm, and central to the speaker's life.' This captures something, but not everything. The metaphor also suggests that Juliet dispels darkness, that she sustains life, that she rises and sets — that Romeo's emotional world has the same relation to Juliet that the physical world has to the sun. And even this expanded paraphrase does not exhaust the metaphor, for further comparisons can always be drawn: that one cannot look directly at Juliet, that she is at an immense remove, that she is indifferent to the world she illuminates. The metaphor is, as it were, cognitively productive: it generates new comparisons the more one attends to it. One might observe that this open-endedness is not an accidental property of a few especially rich metaphors but a characteristic feature of metaphor as such. Even simple metaphors — 'the leg of the table,' 'a warm voice' — resist exhaustive paraphrase, for the comparison they invoke extends along dimensions that no finite literal statement can enumerate. The definition, by requiring paraphrasability without loss, misrepresents metaphor as a decorative device for conveying literal content and fails to recognise the distinctive cognitive work that metaphor performs.

definition: Two expressions E1 and E2 are synonymous if and only if (i) E1 and E2 can be substituted for each other in any sentence without altering the truth-value of that sentence, (ii) E1 and E2 have the same extension — they apply to exactly the same objects or classes, and (iii) the sameness of extension in (ii) holds necessarily, not merely as a matter of contingent fact.
conditions: E1 and E2 can be substituted for each other in any sentence without altering the truth-value of that sentence
E1 and E2 have the same extension — they apply to exactly the same objects or classes
The sameness of extension in (ii) holds necessarily, not merely as a matter of contingent fact
counterexample: Consider the expressions 'creature with a heart' and 'creature with kidneys.' As a matter of biological fact, every creature with a heart has kidneys and vice versa: their extensions are identical. And this coextensiveness is arguably necessary, given the biological constraints on organ systems. Conditions (ii) and (iii) may be satisfied. Yet the expressions are plainly not synonymous: 'creature with a heart' draws attention to the circulatory system, while 'creature with kidneys' draws attention to the excretory system. Substitution in certain sentences alters their cognitive significance: 'The physician examined the creature with a heart' and 'The physician examined the creature with kidneys' convey different information about the physician's focus. Condition (i) fails in such contexts.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that synonymous expressions share not merely extension, even necessarily, but also the same mode of presentation or cognitive content — that they characterise their referents in the same way.
passage: One might characterise synonymy as the relation that obtains between two expressions that are necessarily coextensive and intersubstitutable in all sentential contexts without change of truth-value. This characterisation combines the extensional requirement — that the expressions apply to the same objects — with the intensional requirement — that this coextensiveness hold of necessity — and adds an operational test: substitutability salva veritate. Together these conditions seem strong enough to ensure genuine sameness of meaning. Yet a case which reveals their insufficiency may be drawn from the relation between biological predicates. Consider the expressions 'creature with a heart' and 'creature with kidneys.' Biology teaches us that these predicates are coextensive: every creature possessing the one organ possesses the other. One might argue that this coextensiveness is not merely contingent but arises from deep biological necessities governing the co-evolution of organ systems. If so, conditions (ii) and (iii) are satisfied. But the expressions are not synonymous. They present the creatures they classify under different aspects: one highlights the circulatory function, the other the excretory function. This difference manifests in substitution failures. Consider the sentence 'The veterinarian was particularly interested in creatures with hearts because of her research on blood circulation.' Substituting 'creatures with kidneys' alters the intelligibility of the sentence: the connection between the classification and the research interest is severed. One might observe that what is lacking in the definition is any requirement concerning the manner in which the expressions characterise their referents. Necessary coextensiveness ensures that the expressions track the same objects across all possible circumstances, but it does not ensure that they do so in the same way — and it is sameness of manner, not merely sameness of tracking, that constitutes synonymy.

definition: A speaker S makes an assertion in uttering a sentence U if and only if (i) S presents the propositional content of U as true, (ii) S believes the propositional content of U to be true, and (iii) S intends the audience to accept the propositional content of U on the basis of S's presentation.
conditions: S presents the propositional content of U as true
S believes the propositional content of U to be true
S intends the audience to accept the propositional content of U on the basis of S's presentation
counterexample: Consider an actor on stage who delivers the line 'The king is dead' with full conviction, presenting it as true within the dramatic context and intending the audience to accept it as true within the play. Condition (i) is met — the content is presented as true. Condition (iii) is met — the actor intends the audience to accept it. But condition (ii) is problematic: the actor does not believe that any actual king has died; she is performing. Nevertheless, there is a sense in which the utterance functions exactly like an assertion within its context. Conversely, consider a liar who utters 'I was at home all evening.' He presents the content as true and intends the audience to accept it, satisfying (i) and (iii), yet he does not believe it, violating (ii). We should nonetheless say he asserted something — a false assertion, but an assertion all the same.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that assertion is constituted by the public act of presenting a proposition as true and undertaking a commitment to its truth, rather than by the private mental state of believing it — for liars assert, and their assertions are none the less assertions for being insincere.
passage: One might propose that assertion consists in presenting a propositional content as true, believing it to be true, and intending the audience to accept it. This definition unites a public element — the act of presentation — with a private element — the state of belief — and a communicative element — the intention to secure acceptance. It explains why mere recitation or quotation does not constitute assertion: the speaker who recites a passage from a book does not present its content as her own belief. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the belief requirement may be drawn from the phenomenon of lying. Suppose a witness testifies in court: 'I was at home all evening.' The witness presents the propositional content as true — condition (i) is satisfied. The witness intends the court to accept this content — condition (iii) is satisfied. But the witness is lying: she was not at home, and she does not believe that she was. Condition (ii) fails. On the proposed definition, the witness has not made an assertion. But this is plainly wrong. The witness has asserted that she was at home all evening; she has asserted something false, and she has asserted it insincerely, but she has asserted it. The law treats her utterance as an assertion — she may be prosecuted for perjury. Ordinary speakers treat it as an assertion — they would say she asserted a falsehood. One might observe that the concept of assertion is essentially public and normative: to assert is to present a proposition as true and thereby to undertake a commitment to its truth, regardless of one's private beliefs. The definition, by requiring actual belief, confuses the sincerity condition — which governs the ethics of assertion — with the constitutive conditions — which determine what assertion is.

definition: The meaning of a complex expression E is compositional if and only if (i) E is composed of meaningful sub-expressions arranged according to syntactic rules, (ii) the meaning of E is wholly determined by the meanings of its sub-expressions and the syntactic rules by which they are combined, and (iii) a grasp of the meanings of the sub-expressions and the syntactic rules is sufficient for understanding E.
conditions: E is composed of meaningful sub-expressions arranged according to syntactic rules
The meaning of E is wholly determined by the meanings of its sub-expressions and the syntactic rules by which they are combined
A grasp of the meanings of the sub-expressions and the syntactic rules is sufficient for understanding E
counterexample: Consider the English expression 'kick the bucket,' meaning to die. The expression is composed of meaningful sub-expressions — 'kick,' 'the,' and 'bucket' — arranged according to standard syntactic rules. Condition (i) is satisfied. But conditions (ii) and (iii) fail: the meaning of the idiom is not determined by the meanings of 'kick,' 'the,' and 'bucket' combined according to syntactic rules, and a speaker who grasps the meanings of these words and the syntax of English may entirely fail to understand the expression. The idiomatic meaning is conventional and unrecoverable from the parts.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate idiomatic expressions, whose meanings are conventionally assigned to whole phrases rather than compositionally derived from their parts, and which constitute a pervasive feature of natural language.
passage: One might maintain that the meaning of a complex expression is determined by the meanings of its parts and the syntactic rules governing their combination. This principle of compositionality has enormous explanatory power: it accounts for the productivity of language — our capacity to understand sentences we have never encountered before — and for its systematicity — the fact that the ability to understand one sentence confers the ability to understand structurally related sentences. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of a strict compositional account may be drawn from the phenomenon of idiomatic expressions. Consider the English phrase 'kick the bucket.' The sub-expressions are familiar: 'kick' means to strike with the foot, 'the' is a definite article, and 'bucket' means a cylindrical container. The syntactic structure is a simple verb phrase: verb, determiner, noun. A speaker who grasps the meanings of the sub-expressions and the syntax of the construction will derive the compositional meaning: to strike a cylindrical container with one's foot. But this is not what the expression means. It means to die. The idiomatic meaning is not derivable from the parts; it is conventionally associated with the phrase as a whole. One might observe that idiomatic expressions are not a marginal phenomenon in natural language but a pervasive one. Every language contains thousands of idioms whose meanings cannot be computed from their constituents. This does not refute the principle of compositionality as a general tendency of language, but it does reveal the insufficiency of any definition that treats compositionality as exceptionless. The definition, by asserting that meaning is wholly determined by parts and syntax, cannot accommodate a class of expressions that are among the most commonly used in everyday speech.

definition: A sentence S presupposes a proposition P if and only if (i) S can be neither true nor false unless P is true, (ii) the negation of S also presupposes P, and (iii) a speaker who utters S in a context where P is not established commits an infelicity rather than stating a falsehood.
conditions: S can be neither true nor false unless P is true
The negation of S also presupposes P
A speaker who utters S in a context where P is not established commits an infelicity rather than stating a falsehood
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'The King of France is bald,' uttered today when there is no King of France. On one influential analysis, this sentence is simply false — there is no King of France, so it is false that he is bald. On this analysis, condition (i) fails: the sentence does have a truth-value (namely, false) even though the presupposition (that there exists a King of France) is not satisfied. Yet most speakers, when told 'The King of France is bald,' do not respond 'That is false' but rather 'There is no King of France' — suggesting that the presupposition has failed rather than that a falsehood has been asserted. The two competing analyses reveal that the definition's requirement in (i) is itself contentious.
missing_condition: The definition assumes without argument that presupposition failure results in truth-value gaps rather than in falsity, thereby adopting one contested analysis of presupposition whilst excluding the equally defensible view that presupposition failure renders the sentence false.
passage: One might characterise presupposition as a relation between sentences and propositions such that the sentence can be neither true nor false unless the presupposed proposition holds, the presupposition survives negation, and failure of the presupposition results in infelicity rather than falsehood. This characterisation captures several features of the phenomenon: the sentence 'John has stopped smoking' presupposes that John once smoked, and this presupposition persists whether the sentence is affirmed or negated. If John never smoked, the utterance is odd rather than straightforwardly false. Yet a case which reveals the instability of the definition's foundational assumption may be drawn from the analysis of definite descriptions. Consider the sentence 'The King of France is bald,' uttered in the present day. There is no King of France. According to the definition, the sentence should be neither true nor false, for the presupposed proposition — that there exists a King of France — is not satisfied. But an alternative and highly influential analysis holds that the sentence is simply false: it asserts, among other things, that there exists a unique King of France, and since this assertion is false, the whole sentence is false. On this analysis, the sentence has a truth-value even though the existence presupposition fails. Condition (i) is contradicted. One might observe that ordinary speakers exhibit divided intuitions. Some say the sentence is false; others say it is neither true nor false; still others say it is somehow inappropriate or beside the point. The definition, by insisting upon truth-value gaps as the consequence of presupposition failure, adopts one side of an unresolved analytical dispute and treats a contested philosophical thesis as a definitional requirement. What is needed is a characterisation of presupposition that is neutral between the gap and the falsity analyses.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker S expresses an intention to perform a future action A, (ii) S intends the utterance to place S under an obligation to perform A, and (iii) the audience recognises S's intention to undertake this obligation.
conditions: The speaker S expresses an intention to perform a future action A
S intends the utterance to place S under an obligation to perform A
The audience recognises S's intention to undertake this obligation
counterexample: Consider a person who, under duress — say, at gunpoint — utters the words 'I promise to hand over all my money tomorrow.' She expresses an intention to perform a future action, she recognises that the utterance places her under a prima facie obligation, and the audience (the robber) recognises the obligation-creating intention. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet it appears doubtful that a genuine promise has been made. The coercion vitiates the voluntary character that we ordinarily require of promises. We should hesitate to say that the victim is morally obligated to deliver the money, even though the formal conditions of the definition are met.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the speaker's expression of intention be voluntary and uncoerced, thereby admitting as promises utterances that are extracted under duress and that lack the moral authority we associate with genuine promissory commitment.
passage: One might define a promise as an utterance in which the speaker expresses an intention to perform a future action, intends to place herself under an obligation to perform it, and secures the audience's recognition of this obligation-creating intention. This definition captures the three dimensions that seem essential to promising: the prospective content (a future action), the normative force (an obligation), and the communicative character (mutual recognition). It explains why a mere prediction is not a promise — the speaker does not intend to create an obligation — and why a promise made in a language the audience does not understand fails — the recognition condition is unsatisfied. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition may be drawn from the phenomenon of coerced utterances.
