Suppose a traveller is accosted by a highwayman who demands, at the point of a pistol, that the traveller promise to deliver a sum of money to a specified location by the following morning. The traveller, fearing for his life, says: 'I promise to bring the money tomorrow.' The traveller has expressed an intention to perform a future action. He is aware that the utterance, in its normal functioning, creates an obligation. And the highwayman recognises the obligation-creating character of the utterance. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet most persons of sound moral judgement would deny that a genuine promise has been made — or, at least, would deny that the traveller is bound by it. The coercion deprives the utterance of the voluntary character that is, one might observe, essential to the moral force of a promise. One might give one's word under threat, but the resulting 'promise' lacks the authority that a freely given promise possesses. The definition, by attending to expression, intention, and recognition whilst neglecting the conditions under which these are produced, cannot distinguish genuine promises from extorted declarations.

definition: A word W means an object O if and only if (i) competent speakers of the language use W to refer to O, (ii) W and O stand in a conventional association established by the linguistic community, and (iii) the meaning of W can be specified by pointing to O or to instances of the kind O exemplifies.
conditions: Competent speakers of the language use W to refer to O
W and O stand in a conventional association established by the linguistic community
The meaning of W can be specified by pointing to O or to instances of the kind O exemplifies
counterexample: Consider the word 'justice.' Competent English speakers use it meaningfully, and it stands in a conventional association established by the linguistic community. Yet one cannot specify the meaning of 'justice' by pointing to justice or to instances that exemplify it in the way one can point to instances of 'red' or 'horse.' Justice is not the sort of thing to which one can direct a finger. The third condition is violated, yet 'justice' is as meaningful as any word in the language.
missing_condition: The definition restricts meaning to ostensively specifiable referents and thereby fails to accommodate abstract terms, logical connectives, and other expressions whose meaning cannot be captured by pointing but must be grasped through use, definition, or inferential role.
passage: One might propose that the meaning of a word consists in its referential relation to an object, established by conventional association and specifiable by ostension. This account captures the meaning of many common nouns with considerable plausibility: one can specify the meaning of 'horse' by pointing to a horse, and the meaning of 'red' by pointing to red things. Yet a case which tells against this definition may be drawn from abstract vocabulary. Consider the word 'justice.' Competent speakers of English use it with great frequency and mutual comprehension. It stands in a conventional association established by centuries of linguistic practice. Yet one cannot specify the meaning of 'justice' by pointing — not to a particular object, nor to instances that exemplify it in the way particular horses exemplify the concept 'horse.' Justice is not the sort of thing towards which one can direct a finger or a gesture. The third condition is plainly violated. Yet 'justice' is as meaningful as any word in the language. It appears that the definition, by making ostensive specifiability a necessary condition of meaningfulness, restricts meaning to those words whose referents are concrete and perceptible. It cannot accommodate the vast class of abstract terms — duty, number, validity, causation — whose meanings must be grasped through definition, inferential role, or use rather than through pointing.

definition: A proper name N refers to an individual I if and only if (i) N is associated with a definite description that I uniquely satisfies, (ii) competent speakers intend to refer to I when using N, and (iii) I exists.
conditions: N is associated with a definite description that I uniquely satisfies
Competent speakers intend to refer to I when using N
I exists
counterexample: Consider the name 'Homer,' used to refer to the author of the Iliad and the Odyssey. Competent speakers intend to refer to a particular ancient poet. The name is associated with the description 'the author of the Iliad and the Odyssey.' Yet it is by no means certain that a single individual satisfying this description existed — some scholars hold that the poems are the work of multiple authors. If no single individual satisfies the description, condition (i) fails and condition (iii) may fail, yet the name 'Homer' continues to function referentially in scholarly and ordinary discourse.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for cases in which a name can function referentially even when the associated description is not uniquely satisfied or the existence of the referent is uncertain — it ties reference too tightly to the satisfaction of a description and the existence of a single corresponding individual.
passage: One might define the reference of a proper name as a relation requiring an associated definite description uniquely satisfied by an individual, the intention of competent speakers to refer to that individual, and the individual's existence. This descriptivist account captures many ordinary cases of naming with apparent success. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from the name 'Homer.' Competent speakers of English and scholars of classical literature use 'Homer' with the intention of referring to a particular ancient Greek poet. The name is associated with the definite description 'the author of the Iliad and the Odyssey.' Yet it is by no means certain that a single individual satisfying this description ever existed. A venerable scholarly tradition holds that the two poems are the work of different poets, or that they are compilations from multiple oral traditions. If no single individual uniquely satisfies the associated description, condition (i) fails. If the referent does not exist, condition (iii) fails. Yet the name 'Homer' continues to function referentially in scholarly and ordinary discourse alike. It appears that the definition, by requiring the existence of a unique individual satisfying the associated description, cannot accommodate cases in which reference operates despite uncertainty about the satisfaction of the identifying description or the existence of the referent.

definition: A naming ceremony N successfully bestows the name 'X' upon an individual I if and only if (i) N is performed by a person with the recognised authority to name, (ii) the name 'X' is uttered in the presence of I, and (iii) there is a community that subsequently uses 'X' to refer to I.
conditions: N is performed by a person with the recognised authority to name
The name 'X' is uttered in the presence of I
There is a community that subsequently uses 'X' to refer to I
counterexample: Consider a child born during a difficult voyage at sea. The captain, who possesses recognised authority, bestows the name 'Adelaide' upon the infant in the presence of the crew. But upon arrival at port, the parents — disliking the name — register the child as 'Margaret,' and the community that subsequently uses a name to refer to the child uses 'Margaret' exclusively. The captain's ceremony satisfied conditions (i) and (ii) but not (iii); the parents' choice satisfies (iii) but arguably not (i) or (ii) in the original sense. Yet the child's name is Margaret.
missing_condition: The definition fails to specify whose authority takes precedence when naming ceremonies conflict, and fails to recognise that the effective name is ultimately determined by sustained communal usage rather than by any single ceremony, however authoritative.
passage: One might propose that a naming ceremony successfully bestows a name when it is performed by a person with recognised authority, the name is uttered in the presence of the individual, and a community subsequently uses the name referentially. This characterisation captures the paradigmatic cases of baptism and civil registration. Yet a case which tells against it may be constructed from a conflict of naming authorities. Suppose a child is born during a difficult voyage at sea. The captain of the vessel, who possesses recognised authority for shipboard ceremonies, bestows the name 'Adelaide' upon the infant in the presence of the assembled crew. The first two conditions are clearly met. But upon arrival at port, the child's parents, disliking the name chosen by the captain, register the child under the name 'Margaret' at the civil registry. The community that subsequently uses a name to refer to the child — family, neighbours, schoolfellows — uses 'Margaret' exclusively. The captain's ceremony satisfied the first two conditions but not the third; the parents' registration satisfies the third but arguably not the first two in the original sense. Yet the child's name is Margaret. It appears that the definition, by failing to specify whose authority takes precedence in cases of conflict, and by failing to recognise that effective naming is ultimately a matter of sustained communal usage, cannot adjudicate between competing ceremonies.

definition: A translation T of a sentence S from language L₁ into language L₂ is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-conditions of S, (ii) T employs grammatically correct expressions of L₂, and (iii) a competent speaker of L₂ who reads T grasps the same proposition as a competent speaker of L₁ who reads S.
conditions: T preserves the truth-conditions of S
T employs grammatically correct expressions of L₂
A competent speaker of L₂ who reads T grasps the same proposition as a competent speaker of L₁ who reads S
counterexample: Consider translating a line of Japanese poetry that employs a particular seasonal word (kigo) carrying rich cultural associations — the word for a certain autumn insect, for instance, which connotes transience, loneliness, and the passing of life. A translation that preserves the truth-conditions — perhaps 'cricket' in English — and is grammatically correct may nonetheless fail to convey the same proposition, for the English word carries none of the same cultural resonance. Conversely, a freer translation that adds explanatory phrases may convey the proposition more faithfully but alter the truth-conditions.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the cultural, connotative, and associative dimensions of meaning that cannot be captured by truth-conditional equivalence alone — it treats translation as a purely semantic relation and neglects the pragmatic and cultural context in which sentences acquire their full significance.
passage: One might define an adequate translation as one that preserves truth-conditions, employs grammatically correct expressions, and enables the reader to grasp the same proposition. This characterisation captures the aspiration of translators to convey what is said faithfully and correctly. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from the translation of poetry between distant cultures. Consider a line of Japanese verse employing a particular seasonal word — the name of a certain autumn insect, perhaps — which carries rich cultural associations of transience, loneliness, and the passing of life. An English translation that renders this as 'cricket' may preserve the truth-conditions: both sentences refer to the same kind of insect. The English is grammatically correct. Yet the English reader, lacking the cultural associations that the Japanese word carries, does not grasp the same proposition in its full significance. The word 'cricket' in English connotes summer evenings and country gardens rather than existential solitude. One might attempt a freer translation, adding explanatory phrases to convey the cultural resonance, but this would alter the truth-conditions. It appears that the definition, by treating translation as a purely semantic relation governed by truth-conditional equivalence, cannot accommodate the cultural, connotative, and associative dimensions of meaning that are essential to the adequacy of translation between distant traditions.

definition: A sentence S is metaphorical if and only if (i) S is literally false, (ii) the speaker does not intend S to be taken literally, and (iii) S conveys a meaning that can be fully paraphrased by a literal statement.
conditions: S is literally false
The speaker does not intend S to be taken literally
S conveys a meaning that can be fully paraphrased by a literal statement
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'No man is an island.' This is literally true — no human being is, in fact, an island. The first condition is violated. Yet the sentence is paradigmatically metaphorical: it conveys that no person is entirely self-sufficient or isolated from others. Furthermore, many philosophers have argued that the meaning conveyed by a rich metaphor resists full literal paraphrase; something is always lost in the translation from figurative to literal speech.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate metaphors that are literally true, and it wrongly assumes that the cognitive content of a metaphor can be exhaustively captured by a literal paraphrase — it overlooks the irreducible character of metaphorical meaning.
passage: One might define a metaphorical sentence as one that is literally false, not intended to be taken literally, and paraphrasable by a literal statement. This account captures a familiar picture: the metaphor says something false on the surface and conveys a truth beneath. Yet a case which tells against this definition may be drawn from well-known examples. Consider the sentence 'No man is an island.' Taken literally, this is true — no human being is, as a matter of geographical fact, an island. The first condition is violated. Yet the sentence is metaphorical by any reasonable standard: it conveys that no person is entirely self-sufficient or isolated from the web of human relationships. The literal truth of the surface statement does not cancel its metaphorical force. Furthermore, the third condition faces a distinct challenge. Many philosophers have argued that the cognitive content of a rich metaphor resists full literal paraphrase. When one says 'Juliet is the sun,' the paraphrase 'Juliet is radiant and life-giving' captures something of the meaning but loses the vividness, the surprise, and the associative richness of the original. It appears that the definition both wrongly excludes literally true metaphors and wrongly assumes that metaphorical meaning is always reducible to literal meaning.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker S sincerely intends to perform the action described in U, (ii) the hearer H understands U as a commitment by S to perform that action, and (iii) S's utterance of U is voluntary.
conditions: The speaker S sincerely intends to perform the action described in U
The hearer H understands U as a commitment by S to perform that action
S's utterance of U is voluntary
counterexample: Consider an actor on stage who, in the course of a play, utters the words 'I promise to return your money by Friday' to another actor. The utterance is voluntary. The audience and the fellow actor understand the words as a commitment within the fictional context. The actor may even, in a certain sense, sincerely intend to utter the words convincingly. Yet no promise has been made. The actor is not committed to returning anyone's money. The conditions appear to be met in a surface reading, but no genuine promise exists because the utterance occurs within a recognised non-serious context.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the utterance occur in a context recognised by both parties as one in which commitments are genuinely undertaken — it omits the institutional and contextual conditions that distinguish serious speech acts from performances, jokes, and other non-serious uses of promissory language.
passage: One might characterise a promise as a voluntary utterance in which the speaker sincerely intends to perform the described action and the hearer understands the utterance as a commitment. This analysis captures the central features of promising as they appear in ordinary transactions. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be drawn from the theatre. Suppose an actor on stage, in the course of performing a play, utters the words 'I promise to return your money by Friday' to a fellow actor. The utterance is voluntary — no one has compelled the actor to speak. The fellow actor and the audience understand the words as expressing a commitment within the context of the performance. The actor may even sincerely intend to deliver the line convincingly. The three conditions appear, on a certain reading, to be satisfied. Yet no genuine promise has been made. The actor is under no obligation to return anyone's money upon leaving the theatre. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the psychological states of speaker and hearer and the voluntariness of the utterance, fails to require that the utterance occur within a context recognised by both parties as one in which genuine commitments are undertaken. The institutional and contextual conditions of promising — what one might call the seriousness conditions — are absent from the analysis.

definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) there exist borderline cases to which neither F nor its negation clearly applies, (ii) F admits of degrees, and (iii) F lacks a precise boundary separating its positive extension from its negative extension.
conditions: There exist borderline cases to which neither F nor its negation clearly applies
F admits of degrees
F lacks a precise boundary separating its positive extension from its negative extension
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is a natural number greater than or equal to one million.' This predicate has a perfectly precise boundary: the number one million. It does not admit of degrees — a number either satisfies the condition or it does not. Yet one might argue that the predicate 'is a large number' does satisfy all three conditions. The case that tells against the definition, however, arises with predicates like 'is a heap.' This predicate satisfies conditions (i) and (iii) — there are borderline cases and no precise boundary — but it is doubtful whether it admits of degrees in the ordinary sense. A collection of grains is a heap or it is not; we do not ordinarily say that one heap is more of a heap than another. Condition (ii) is not clearly met, yet 'heap' is a paradigm of vagueness.
missing_condition: The definition wrongly requires that all vague predicates admit of degrees, failing to recognise that some vague predicates — such as 'heap' or 'chair' — exhibit vagueness through borderline cases and the absence of sharp boundaries without being naturally gradable.
passage: One might define a vague predicate as one that admits of borderline cases, allows for degrees, and lacks a precise boundary. This characterisation fits many familiar examples — 'tall,' 'bald,' 'rich' — which exhibit all three features transparently. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this tripartite analysis may be drawn from a predicate that is vague by universal acknowledgement yet does not straightforwardly admit of degrees. Consider the predicate 'is a heap.' It plainly satisfies the first condition: there are borderline cases — collections of grains for which neither 'heap' nor 'not a heap' clearly applies. It satisfies the third condition: there is no precise number of grains at which a collection becomes a heap. But does it admit of degrees in the sense required by the second condition? We do not ordinarily say that one heap is more of a heap than another, as we might say that one person is taller than another. 'Heap' appears to be what grammarians call a non-gradable predicate. Yet it is a paradigm of vagueness — the sorites paradox is named after it. It appears that the definition, by requiring that vague predicates admit of degrees, imposes a condition that excludes certain paradigmatic instances of the very phenomenon it seeks to characterise.

definition: Two sentences S₁ and S₂ are synonymous if and only if (i) S₁ and S₂ have the same truth-conditions, (ii) substituting S₁ for S₂ in any context preserves truth-value, and (iii) a competent speaker would assent to 'S₁ means the same as S₂.'
conditions: S₁ and S₂ have the same truth-conditions
Substituting S₁ for S₂ in any context preserves truth-value
A competent speaker would assent to 'S₁ means the same as S₂'
counterexample: Consider 'The morning star is visible' and 'The evening star is visible.' These sentences have different truth-conditions — the first is typically true at dawn, the second at dusk — yet the terms 'the morning star' and 'the evening star' refer to the same object (Venus). Now consider 'All unmarried men are bachelors' and 'All bachelors are unmarried men.' These have the same truth-conditions and a competent speaker would assent to their synonymy. Yet substitution in the context 'John wondered whether all unmarried men are bachelors' may alter the truth-value, for John might entertain the question about one formulation without entertaining it about the other.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the opacity of intensional contexts — contexts involving propositional attitudes, modal operators, or quotation — in which substitution of truth-conditionally equivalent sentences may alter truth-value, revealing that synonymy requires more than extensional equivalence.
passage: One might define synonymy as the relation between sentences that share truth-conditions, permit substitution in any context without change of truth-value, and whose equivalence a competent speaker would affirm. This characterisation aims to combine semantic, logical, and intuitive criteria. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the combined conditions may be constructed from the behaviour of sentences in intensional contexts. Consider the sentences 'All bachelors are unmarried men' and 'All unmarried men are bachelors.' They share truth-conditions; a competent speaker would assent to their synonymy. Yet consider the context 'John wondered whether all bachelors are unmarried men.' Substituting the second sentence yields 'John wondered whether all unmarried men are bachelors.' It is possible for the first attribution to be true and the second false — for John might have pondered the question in one formulation without considering the other. The substitution does not preserve truth-value. Condition (ii) is violated in this intensional context, though (i) and (iii) are satisfied. It appears that the definition, by requiring substitutability in every context, is too demanding if taken strictly — for intensional contexts create well-known difficulties — yet without condition (ii), mere sameness of truth-conditions is too permissive.

definition: A definite description 'the F' refers successfully if and only if (i) there exists exactly one object satisfying the predicate F, (ii) the speaker uses 'the F' with the intention of picking out that object, and (iii) the context makes clear which object is intended.
conditions: There exists exactly one object satisfying the predicate F
The speaker uses 'the F' with the intention of picking out that object
The context makes clear which object is intended
counterexample: Consider a speaker who says 'the table' in a room containing two tables. There is no unique object satisfying the predicate 'table' in the room, so condition (i) is violated. Yet the speaker's gesture, gaze, or prior conversational context may make perfectly clear which table is intended, and the hearer has no difficulty identifying the referent. The definite description refers successfully despite the failure of uniqueness.
missing_condition: The definition's uniqueness requirement is too strict — it fails to account for the role of contextual salience, demonstration, and conversational pragmatics in securing reference when the literal descriptive content does not uniquely determine a referent.
passage: One might define the successful reference of a definite description as requiring the existence of exactly one object satisfying the predicate, the speaker's intention to pick out that object, and contextual clarity. The uniqueness condition reflects a Russellian insight: 'the F' presupposes that there is one and only one F. Yet a case which tells against this strict requirement may be drawn from ordinary conversational practice. Suppose a speaker says 'Would you pass me the table?' in a room containing two tables. There is no unique object satisfying the predicate 'table' in this context; condition (i) is violated. Yet the speaker's accompanying gesture — a glance towards the nearer table, or the orientation of an outstretched hand — makes perfectly clear which table is intended. The hearer identifies the referent without hesitation and passes the requested object. The definite description has referred successfully. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon the uniqueness of the satisfier, cannot accommodate the pervasive phenomenon of contextually secured reference. In ordinary discourse, speakers routinely use definite descriptions in situations where the descriptive content alone does not single out a unique referent, relying instead upon salience, demonstration, and shared conversational background to complete the referential act.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a command if and only if (i) the speaker S occupies a position of authority over the hearer H, (ii) S intends H to perform the action described in U, and (iii) U is expressed in the imperative mood.
conditions: The speaker S occupies a position of authority over the hearer H
S intends H to perform the action described in U
U is expressed in the imperative mood
counterexample: Consider a subordinate soldier who says to his commanding officer, 'You might want to take cover, sir,' in a tone of urgent insistence during an artillery bombardment. The soldier does not occupy a position of authority over the officer; the utterance is not in the imperative mood but in the declarative. Yet the utterance functions as a command — or at the very least as a directive with the force of a command — and both parties understand it as such. The situation's urgency confers upon the utterance a directive force that overrides the usual hierarchical and grammatical conditions.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the way situational urgency, practical necessity, or moral authority can confer directive force upon utterances that lack the formal marks of commands — it ties the commanding function too rigidly to institutional hierarchy and grammatical mood.
passage: One might propose that a command is an utterance by an authority figure, directed at a subordinate, expressed in the imperative mood and intended to produce action. This characterisation captures the paradigmatic military order and the employer's instruction. Yet a case which tells against it may be drawn from situations of extreme urgency. Suppose a subordinate soldier, during an artillery bombardment, says to his commanding officer, 'You might want to take cover, sir.' The utterance is not in the imperative mood; it is phrased as a suggestion in the declarative. The soldier does not occupy a position of authority over the officer — quite the reverse. Yet the utterance functions as a directive with all the practical force of a command, and both parties understand it as such. The officer would be foolish to treat the utterance as a mere observation about what he might want to do. The urgency of the situation confers upon the soldier's words a directive force that overrides the usual hierarchical and grammatical conditions. It appears that the definition, by requiring authority, imperative mood, and a particular institutional relation, cannot accommodate the numerous cases in which commands are issued by persons without formal authority, expressed in non-imperative forms, and understood as binding by virtue of situational necessity rather than institutional hierarchy.

definition: A word W has meaning if and only if (i) W is associated with a mental image in the mind of the speaker, (ii) the speaker can use W to communicate with others, and (iii) W occurs in a natural language that is spoken by a community.
conditions: W is associated with a mental image in the mind of the speaker
The speaker can use W to communicate with others
W occurs in a natural language spoken by a community
counterexample: Consider the word 'justice.' A speaker uses this word fluently in conversation, communicates successfully with others about matters of fairness and rights, and the word occurs in English, a natural language spoken by a vast community. All three conditions are met. Yet it is doubtful that the speaker, or indeed any speaker, associates a single determinate mental image with 'justice.' The word is meaningful without being imagistic; its meaning resides in its role in discourse about norms, institutions, and obligations, not in any picture before the mind's eye.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for meaning that is constituted by a word's inferential or functional role within a language — its systematic connections to other words, its conditions of correct application, and its place in patterns of reasoning — rather than requiring an accompanying mental image, which many meaningful words lack.
passage: One might propose that a word has meaning when it is associated with a mental image, can be used for communication, and belongs to a natural language spoken by a community. This imagistic account has a long pedigree, and it captures the intuition that meaningful words are connected to something in the mind. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the vocabulary of moral and political life. Consider the word 'justice.' A competent English speaker uses this word with confidence, discusses questions of justice at length, and communicates effectively about matters of fairness, rights, and obligations. The word belongs to a natural language spoken by hundreds of millions. Each of the three conditions is thus satisfied. Yet it is far from clear that the speaker associates any determinate mental image with 'justice.' What would such an image look like? A blindfolded figure holding scales, perhaps, but this is a conventional symbol rather than a representation of the concept's content. The meaning of 'justice' resides not in any picture before the mind's eye but in the word's systematic connections to other concepts — fairness, desert, rights, obligations — and in its conditions of correct application within moral and political discourse. It appears that the definition, by requiring a mental image as a condition of meaningfulness, excludes or mischaracterises the meaning of abstract terms whose significance is constituted by their inferential and functional role in language rather than by any associated imagery.

definition: A proper name N refers to an object O if and only if (i) N was introduced to designate O, (ii) there exists a causal chain of communication linking the current use of N back to the original act of naming, and (iii) the speaker intends to use N to refer to O.
conditions: N was introduced to designate O
There exists a causal chain of communication linking the current use back to the original naming
The speaker intends to use N to refer to O
counterexample: Suppose a historian reads about a medieval monk named 'Brother Anselm' who is credited with copying a famous manuscript. There was indeed an original act of naming in which the monk was christened 'Anselm.' A causal chain of communication links the historian's use of the name to that original naming through centuries of records. The historian intends to refer to the monk. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose that through a series of scribal errors in the thirteenth century, the records the historian relies upon actually describe the deeds of a different monk, Brother Cuthbert, while attributing them to Anselm. The historian's use of 'Anselm' is causally connected to the naming of one individual, but the descriptive content the historian associates with the name belongs to another. The definition cannot settle whether the historian refers to Anselm or to Cuthbert.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the causal chain be informationally faithful — that the descriptive content transmitted along the chain accurately track the properties of the originally named individual, so that the name's reference is not systematically corrupted by errors in the transmission of identifying information.
passage: One might define reference for proper names by requiring that the name was introduced to designate a particular object, that a causal chain of communication links the current use to the original naming, and that the speaker intends to refer to that object. This causal-chain account captures the intuition that names are anchored to their bearers through a historical process of transmission. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from historical scholarship. Suppose a historian reads about a medieval monk named 'Brother Anselm,' credited with copying a celebrated manuscript. The monk was duly christened 'Anselm' at birth — the original act of naming is secure. A causal chain of communication, running through centuries of monastic records and scholarly citations, links the historian's use of the name to that christening. The historian intends to refer to the monk so named. Each condition is satisfied. Yet suppose that in the thirteenth century, a scribal error transposed the deeds of a different monk, Brother Cuthbert, attributing them to Anselm. Every subsequent record perpetuated this error. The historian, reading these records, associates with the name 'Anselm' a body of descriptive information that actually belongs to Cuthbert. The causal chain traces back to Anselm's naming, but the information carried along that chain belongs to Cuthbert. The definition cannot adjudicate whether the historian refers to Anselm, the bearer of the name, or to Cuthbert, the bearer of the properties. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the existence of a causal chain, an original naming, and speaker intention, fails to require that the chain faithfully transmit identifying information about the originally named individual.

definition: An act of naming is successful if and only if (i) a speaker utters a name N in the presence of an object O with the intention of baptising O as N, (ii) the relevant community accepts N as the name of O, and (iii) N has not previously been assigned to a different object within the same community.
conditions: A speaker utters N in the presence of O with the intention of baptising O as N
The relevant community accepts N as the name of O
N has not previously been assigned to a different object within the same community
counterexample: Suppose explorers arrive at a previously uncharted island and the expedition leader, standing on its shore, declares, 'I name this island Elysium.' The utterance is made in the presence of the island with the explicit intention of baptising it. The community of explorers accepts the name. And suppose no other island in their records bears this name. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the explorers have landed not on one island but on two closely adjacent islands separated by a narrow channel obscured by vegetation, which they have mistaken for a single landmass. The act of naming, though it satisfies all stated conditions, is defective because the object baptised — a single island — does not exist as the speaker conceives it; there are in fact two distinct objects, neither of which is the unified entity the speaker intended to name.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the object O be correctly individuated by the speaker — that the speaker's conception of the boundaries and identity of O correspond to how O actually exists, so that the name is attached to a genuine single object rather than to a mistaken amalgamation of distinct objects.
passage: One might define a successful act of naming as one in which a speaker utters a name in the presence of an object with the intention of baptising it, the community accepts the name, and the name has not been previously assigned to another object. This characterisation captures the social, intentional, and conventional dimensions of naming. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of geographical exploration. Suppose a party of explorers reaches a previously uncharted landmass, and the expedition leader, standing upon its shore, declares with ceremony, 'I name this island Elysium.' The utterance is made in the object's presence with the explicit intention of baptism. The community of explorers unanimously accepts the name. And no other island in their records has been called 'Elysium.' All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the explorers have been deceived by the local geography. What appears to be a single island is in fact two distinct islands separated by a narrow channel, hidden from view by dense vegetation. The speaker intended to name one island, but the object so conceived does not exist. There are two islands, neither of which is identical to the unified landmass the speaker believed to be naming. The act of naming, though it meets every stated condition, is defective in a fundamental respect. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the speaker's intention, community acceptance, and novelty of the name, fails to require that the speaker correctly individuate the object — that the speaker's conception of the object's boundaries and identity correspond to how the object actually exists in the world.

definition: A translation T of a sentence S from language L1 into language L2 is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-conditions of S, (ii) T is grammatically well-formed in L2, and (iii) T uses only vocabulary available in L2.
conditions: T preserves the truth-conditions of S
T is grammatically well-formed in L2
T uses only vocabulary available in L2
counterexample: Suppose the French sentence 'Il fait beau' is translated into English as 'It makes beautiful.' This translation does not satisfy the conditions and is clearly inadequate. But now consider a better case. Suppose a Japanese sentence employing an honorific construction that encodes the speaker's social relation to the addressee is translated into English in a way that preserves the truth-conditions perfectly — the same state of affairs is described — and is grammatically correct and uses only English vocabulary. All three conditions are met. Yet the translation strips away the entire dimension of social register and relational nuance encoded in the original, producing a sentence that, while truth-conditionally equivalent, fails to convey what a competent speaker of Japanese would understand the sentence to communicate.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that T preserve the pragmatic, social, or connotative dimensions of meaning that are encoded in the linguistic form of S — aspects of meaning that go beyond truth-conditions, such as register, politeness levels, and the social relations presupposed by the speaker's choice of expression.
passage: One might propose that an adequate translation preserves the truth-conditions of the original, is grammatically well-formed in the target language, and employs only vocabulary available in that language. This account captures the idea that translation should produce a sentence that is true under exactly the same circumstances as the original, expressed in correct and idiomatic target-language terms. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the translation of Japanese into English. Suppose a Japanese sentence employs an elaborate honorific construction that simultaneously describes a state of affairs and encodes the speaker's precise social relation to the addressee — a relation of deference, for instance, or of intimate familiarity. Now suppose a translator renders this sentence into English in a way that perfectly preserves the truth-conditions: the same event is described, the same agents are identified, and the sentence is true under exactly the same circumstances as the original. The English sentence is grammatically impeccable and uses only standard English vocabulary. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet the translation has stripped away the entire dimension of social register and relational nuance that was encoded in the Japanese original. A competent speaker of Japanese would recognise that the original communicated not only a description of an event but also the speaker's position within a social hierarchy, a dimension entirely absent from the English rendering. It appears that the definition, by requiring only truth-conditional preservation, grammaticality, and lexical availability, fails to demand that the translation preserve pragmatic and social dimensions of meaning that are integral to what the original sentence communicates.

definition: An utterance U is a metaphor if and only if (i) U applies a term to a subject to which it does not literally apply, (ii) the speaker intends the audience to recognise the non-literal application, and (iii) U invites a comparison between the literal and metaphorical subjects.
conditions: U applies a term to a subject to which it does not literally apply
The speaker intends the audience to recognise the non-literal application
U invites a comparison between the literal and metaphorical subjects
counterexample: Suppose a speaker, exasperated by a colleague's obstinacy, says 'You are a brick wall.' The term 'brick wall' does not literally apply to the colleague. The speaker intends the audience to recognise the non-literal usage. And the utterance invites a comparison between brick walls and the colleague. All three conditions are satisfied, and this is indeed a metaphor. Now consider a speaker who says 'My lawyer is a shark.' The same three conditions are met. But suppose the speaker utters this not to illuminate any quality of the lawyer but merely as a dead cliché, used reflexively without any intention to draw the audience's attention to specific points of comparison. The speaker has no particular features of sharks in mind and does not intend the audience to contemplate the comparison; the phrase functions as a stock expression equivalent to 'My lawyer is aggressive.' The utterance satisfies the definition but has lost the characteristic cognitive force of genuine metaphor.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the metaphorical utterance generate a novel or cognitively productive understanding — that the comparison invited by U yield fresh insight or an altered perception of the subject, rather than functioning as a conventionalised stock expression whose metaphorical origin no longer engages the audience's imaginative faculties.
passage: One might define metaphor as an utterance that applies a term non-literally, with the speaker intending the audience to recognise this, and that invites a comparison between the literal and metaphorical subjects. This tripartite analysis captures the semantic deviation, the communicative intention, and the comparative structure that seem characteristic of metaphorical speech. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the currency of everyday language. Consider the utterance 'My lawyer is a shark.' The term 'shark' does not literally apply to a human being. The speaker intends the audience to recognise the non-literal usage. And the utterance invites a comparison between sharks and lawyers. Each condition is satisfied. Yet suppose the speaker utters this phrase not to illuminate any particular quality of the lawyer but merely out of linguistic habit — as a dead cliché used reflexively in the way one might say 'aggressive' or 'ruthless.' The speaker has no specific features of sharks in mind and does not intend the audience to contemplate any detailed comparison. The phrase functions as a stock expression whose metaphorical origin has been worn away by repetition. One might observe that the utterance, though it satisfies all three conditions of the definition, has lost the characteristic cognitive force of genuine metaphor — its capacity to produce fresh insight or an altered perception of the subject. It appears that the definition, by requiring only non-literal application, speaker intention, and an invitation to comparison, fails to demand that the metaphor be cognitively productive, generating a novel understanding rather than merely recycling a conventionalised formula.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker S expresses an intention to perform a future action A, (ii) S intends the utterance to place S under an obligation to perform A, and (iii) the hearer H understands U as committing S to perform A.
conditions: The speaker S expresses an intention to perform a future action A
S intends the utterance to place S under an obligation to perform A
The hearer H understands U as committing S to perform A
counterexample: Suppose a kidnapper tells a hostage, 'I promise to release you tomorrow if you cooperate.' The kidnapper expresses an intention to perform a future action — the release. The kidnapper intends the utterance to be taken as placing the kidnapper under an obligation, for the kidnapper genuinely means to keep the promise if cooperation is forthcoming. The hostage understands the utterance as committing the kidnapper to the release. All three conditions are met. Yet one might question whether a promise extracted under such conditions of duress — where the hearer's only alternative is continued captivity or worse — constitutes a genuine promise, since the hearer's acceptance is not freely given but coerced.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the hearer's acceptance of the commitment be freely given — that H be in a position to accept or decline the speaker's commitment without coercion, so that the promissory relation is entered into under conditions of mutual autonomy rather than domination.
passage: One might define a promise as an utterance in which the speaker expresses an intention to perform a future action, intends the utterance to create an obligation, and is understood by the hearer as making such a commitment. This characterisation captures the intentional, obligatory, and communicative dimensions of promising. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be constructed from circumstances of coercion. Suppose a kidnapper addresses a hostage with the words, 'I promise to release you tomorrow if you cooperate.' The kidnapper expresses a genuine intention to perform a future action — the release of the hostage. The kidnapper intends the utterance to place the kidnapper under an obligation to carry out this action, and indeed means to fulfil the commitment if cooperation is forthcoming. The hostage understands the utterance as a commitment to release. Each of the three conditions of the proposed definition is satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to regard this as a genuine promise. The hostage is in no position to accept or decline the kidnapper's undertaking freely. The so-called promise is issued within a relationship of domination, and the hearer's apparent acceptance is extracted under duress. A genuine promise, one might argue, presupposes a degree of mutual autonomy: the hearer must be free to accept or refuse the speaker's commitment. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the expression of intention, the intention to create obligation, and hearer understanding, fails to demand that the promissory exchange occur under conditions of freedom, where the hearer's acceptance is genuine rather than coerced.

definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) there exist borderline cases to which it is unclear whether F applies, (ii) F admits of degrees, and (iii) F is susceptible to a sorites sequence — a series of cases differing by imperceptible increments along the relevant dimension.
conditions: There exist borderline cases to which it is unclear whether F applies
F admits of degrees
F is susceptible to a sorites sequence
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is a legal adult in jurisdiction J,' where J sets the age of majority at exactly eighteen years. There appear to be no borderline cases: a person either has or has not reached the age of eighteen. The predicate does not admit of degrees; one cannot be more or less a legal adult. And no sorites sequence threatens, since the transition is sharp. None of the conditions are met, and the predicate is clearly not vague. Now consider, by contrast, the predicate 'is a prime number.' One would not ordinarily call this predicate vague. Yet suppose a philosopher constructs a sequence of integers — 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, ... — and notes that for very large numbers, it becomes computationally intractable to determine whether a given number is prime. There exist cases where it is genuinely unclear (given current computational resources) whether the predicate applies. One might argue that primality admits of degrees in a computational sense (more or less easily verified). A sequence of adjacent integers can be constructed. All three conditions could appear to be met. Yet 'is a prime number' is not vague; it is perfectly precise, and the difficulty is merely practical.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the borderline cases, the degree-admitting character, and the sorites susceptibility arise from a genuine semantic indeterminacy in the predicate's meaning — from the absence of a sharp boundary in the concept itself — rather than from merely practical or computational limitations in applying a predicate whose meaning is in fact fully determinate.
passage: One might propose that a predicate is vague when it has borderline cases, admits of degrees, and is susceptible to a sorites sequence. This analysis captures the characteristic features that philosophers have identified in predicates like 'tall,' 'bald,' and 'heap.' Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from an unexpected domain. Consider the predicate 'is a prime number.' For small integers, the application of this predicate is straightforward. But for very large integers — numbers with millions of digits — it may be genuinely unclear, given available computational resources, whether the predicate applies. One might argue that there exist borderline cases in the sense that current methods cannot determine the predicate's application. One might further argue that the predicate admits of degrees in a computational sense: some numbers are easily verified as prime, others less so. And a sequence of adjacent large integers constitutes a series differing by unit increments along which the predicate's applicability is uncertain. All three conditions of the proposed definition could appear to be satisfied. Yet 'is a prime number' is not a vague predicate. It is perfectly precise: every integer either is or is not prime, and the boundary is sharp. The difficulty is entirely practical, residing in our limited computational powers rather than in any semantic indeterminacy in the concept of primality. It appears that the definition, by requiring only borderline cases, degree-admission, and sorites susceptibility, fails to distinguish genuine semantic vagueness — the absence of a sharp boundary in the concept's meaning — from mere practical or computational difficulty in applying a concept that is in itself fully determinate.

definition: Two sentences S1 and S2 have the same meaning if and only if (i) S1 and S2 have the same truth-conditions, (ii) a competent speaker would assent to S1 in exactly those circumstances in which the speaker would assent to S2, and (iii) S1 and S2 belong to the same natural language.
conditions: S1 and S2 have the same truth-conditions
A competent speaker would assent to S1 in exactly the circumstances in which the speaker would assent to S2
S1 and S2 belong to the same natural language
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'Water is H2O' and the sentence 'Water is the predominant liquid on the Earth's surface.' A competent speaker, in ordinary circumstances, would assent to both — both are commonly accepted truths. Both sentences have the same truth-value, and if one takes their truth-conditions to be specified by their actual-world extensions, they may even share truth-conditions in a rough sense. Both are English sentences. All three conditions may appear to be met. Yet the two sentences do not have the same meaning. The first expresses a claim about the molecular composition of water, while the second expresses a geographical fact. A world in which water is not the predominant liquid on the Earth's surface, yet water remains H2O, is readily conceivable, revealing a difference in meaning that the definition cannot capture.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that S1 and S2 share their truth-conditions across all possible circumstances, not merely actual ones — that they be true in the same possible worlds — so that sameness of meaning reflects intensional equivalence rather than a mere coincidence of truth-value and assent patterns in the actual world.
passage: One might define sameness of meaning between two sentences by requiring identical truth-conditions, identical patterns of speaker assent, and membership in the same natural language. This account unites a semantic condition, a behavioural condition, and a linguistic condition in what appears to be a comprehensive characterisation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the relation between scientific and geographical claims. Consider the English sentences 'Water is H2O' and 'Water is the predominant liquid on the Earth's surface.' A competent English speaker, in ordinary circumstances, assents to both sentences with equal readiness; both are widely accepted truths. If one characterises truth-conditions in terms of actual-world extensions, both sentences share the same truth-value and may appear to share truth-conditions. Both belong to the same natural language. Each of the three conditions is thus satisfied. Yet the two sentences plainly do not mean the same thing. The first states a fact about the molecular composition of water, while the second states a geographical fact about the distribution of liquids on our planet. One can readily conceive of circumstances — a world in which some other liquid covers the Earth's surface while water retains its chemical structure — in which the sentences diverge in truth-value. It appears that the definition, by requiring only actual-world truth-conditions, actual assent patterns, and linguistic membership, fails to demand that the sentences share their truth-conditions across all possible circumstances, and thus mistakes a coincidence of actual-world truth for genuine sameness of meaning.

definition: An utterance U constitutes an assertion if and only if (i) the speaker S presents a proposition P as true, (ii) S believes P to be true, and (iii) S intends the hearer to form the belief that P.
conditions: The speaker S presents a proposition P as true
S believes P to be true
S intends the hearer to form the belief that P
counterexample: Suppose a barrister, during cross-examination, says to the court, 'The defendant was at the railway station at nine o'clock on the evening in question.' The barrister presents this proposition as true. The barrister, having reviewed the evidence, genuinely believes it. And the barrister intends the jury to form the belief that the defendant was present at the station. All three conditions are met. Yet one might question whether this constitutes an assertion in the full sense, for the barrister's utterance is governed not by the ordinary norms of sincere communication but by the adversarial conventions of legal proceedings, in which each advocate is expected to present one side of the case. The barrister would make the same utterance regardless of personal conviction, provided it served the client's interests.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the speaker's presentation of P as true be governed by the constitutive norm of sincerity that characterises assertion proper — that S present P as true because S takes truthfulness to be the operative standard, rather than presenting P within an institutional framework whose norms may override or replace the ordinary norm of sincere assertion.
passage: One might define assertion as an utterance in which the speaker presents a proposition as true, believes it, and intends the hearer to adopt the belief. This characterisation captures the declarative form, the sincerity, and the communicative purpose that we associate with asserting. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the courtroom. Suppose a barrister, during cross-examination, states to the jury, 'The defendant was at the railway station at nine o'clock on the evening in question.' The barrister presents this proposition as true. Having carefully reviewed the evidence, the barrister genuinely believes it. And the barrister intends the jury to form the corresponding belief. Each of the three conditions of the proposed definition is satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that the barrister's utterance is not an assertion in the full philosophical sense. It is made within the adversarial framework of legal proceedings, in which each advocate is expected to advance the case most favourable to the client, regardless of personal conviction. The barrister would have made the same utterance had there been reason to doubt the proposition, provided it served the defence. The utterance is governed not by the norm of sincerity that characterises genuine assertion but by the institutional norms of legal advocacy. It appears that the definition, by requiring only the presentation of a proposition as true, belief, and communicative intention, fails to demand that the utterance be governed by the constitutive norm of assertion — the norm that one presents a proposition as true because one takes truthfulness, rather than strategic advantage, to be the operative standard.

definition: A definite description 'the F' refers to an object O if and only if (i) there exists exactly one object satisfying the predicate F, (ii) O is that unique object, and (iii) the speaker uses 'the F' with the intention of picking out O.
conditions: There exists exactly one object satisfying the predicate F
O is that unique object
The speaker uses 'the F' with the intention of picking out O
counterexample: Suppose a guest at a dinner party says, 'The author of Waverley was a fascinating man.' There exists exactly one author of Waverley — Sir Walter Scott. Scott is that unique object. The speaker intends to pick out Scott. All three conditions are met, and reference succeeds. Now consider a variation. Suppose the same guest says, 'The tallest person in this room is wearing a blue jacket.' There is exactly one tallest person in the room, and suppose this is a man named Mr. Davies, who is indeed wearing a blue jacket. The speaker intends to pick out Mr. Davies. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose Mr. Davies is wearing what he believes to be a blue jacket but which, under the peculiar lighting of the dining room, appears unmistakably green to every other observer. The speaker, relying on visual appearance, takes the jacket to be green and has in fact confused Mr. Davies with another guest. The description uniquely identifies Davies, and the speaker intends to refer to Davies by description, yet the speaker's identifying information is systematically mistaken.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the speaker's grounds for identifying the referent be accurate — that the speaker's reasons for taking O to be the unique satisfier of F correspond to genuine features of O, so that the reference is not undermined by the speaker's mistaken identification of the referent's properties.
passage: One might define reference for definite descriptions by requiring a unique satisfier of the predicate, the identity of the referent with that satisfier, and the speaker's intention to pick out that object. This analysis captures the semantic core of definite descriptions and incorporates the speaker's communicative purpose. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the vicissitudes of perception. Suppose a guest at a dinner party says, 'The tallest person in this room is wearing a blue jacket.' Suppose there is indeed exactly one tallest person — a man named Mr. Davies — and Mr. Davies is indeed wearing a blue jacket. The speaker intends to pick out Mr. Davies. Each condition is satisfied. However, suppose the dining room's unusual lighting makes the jacket appear green to every observer present, including the speaker. The speaker, perceiving the jacket as green, has in fact confused Mr. Davies with another guest who appears to be wearing blue under the same lighting conditions. The speaker identifies Mr. Davies through the description, yet the speaker's perceptual grounds for attributing the property of wearing a blue jacket are entirely mistaken — the speaker sees the jacket as green and has arrived at the intention to refer to Mr. Davies through a chain of confused perceptual identifications. The description happens to pick out the right person, and the speaker happens to intend to refer to that person, but the speaker's grounds for the identification are systematically erroneous. It appears that the definition, by requiring only uniqueness, identity, and intention, fails to demand that the speaker's identifying grounds be accurate, leaving reference vulnerable to cases in which the right object is picked out for entirely wrong reasons.

definition: A sentence S means proposition P if and only if (i) competent speakers of the language use S to convey P, (ii) S is conventionally associated with P within the linguistic community, and (iii) a hearer who understands the language can recover P from S without additional contextual information.
conditions: Competent speakers of the language use S to convey P
S is conventionally associated with P within the linguistic community
A hearer who understands the language can recover P from S without additional contextual information
counterexample: Consider the sentence 'I am here now.' Competent speakers use this sentence to convey various propositions — for instance, that John is in London on the fifth of March, or that Mary is in Paris on the tenth of June. The sentence is conventionally associated within the linguistic community with the proposition that the speaker is at the place of utterance at the time of utterance. Yet a hearer cannot recover any specific proposition from the sentence without knowing who is speaking, where, and when. All three conditions fail to be jointly satisfiable: the sentence is associated with a propositional schema rather than a determinate proposition, and the hearer requires contextual information to identify which proposition is being conveyed.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate indexical and context-dependent expressions by requiring that meaning be recoverable without contextual information, thereby excluding the large class of sentences whose propositional content is partially determined by the circumstances of utterance.
passage: One might propose that a sentence means a proposition when competent speakers use the sentence to convey that proposition, the sentence is conventionally associated with the proposition, and a hearer who understands the language can recover the proposition without contextual information. This characterisation captures the use-based, conventional, and self-sufficient aspects of linguistic meaning. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the behaviour of indexical expressions. Consider the sentence 'I am here now.' Competent speakers of English regularly use this sentence, and it is conventionally associated with a certain kind of content — roughly, that the speaker is located at the place of utterance at the time of utterance. But this association does not fix a determinate proposition. When John utters the sentence in London on the fifth of March, it conveys the proposition that John is in London on that date. When Mary utters it in Paris on the tenth of June, it conveys an entirely different proposition. The sentence is associated not with a single proposition but with a propositional schema whose completion depends upon features of the context of utterance. A hearer who understands English perfectly well cannot recover any specific proposition from the sentence without knowing who is speaking, where, and when. One might observe that the third condition of the proposed definition — that the hearer can recover the proposition without contextual information — fails for all sentences containing indexical or demonstrative expressions. The definition, by insisting on context-independence, excludes a vast and central class of meaningful sentences from its purview. It appears that a satisfactory account of sentence meaning must accommodate the systematic dependence of propositional content upon the circumstances of utterance.

definition: A singular term T refers to an object O if and only if (i) T is used by a speaker with the intention of picking out O, (ii) the speaker possesses a description that uniquely identifies O, and (iii) O satisfies the description possessed by the speaker.
conditions: T is used by a speaker with the intention of picking out O
The speaker possesses a description that uniquely identifies O
O satisfies the description possessed by the speaker
counterexample: Suppose a lecturer, intending to refer to the philosopher who wrote the Nicomachean Ethics, says 'Aristotle' while believing that Aristotle was born in Athens and was the founder of the Lyceum. In fact, Aristotle was born in Stagira, not Athens. The lecturer's identifying description — 'the philosopher born in Athens who founded the Lyceum' — is not uniquely satisfied by Aristotle, since no philosopher born in Athens founded the Lyceum. Condition (iii) fails. Yet the lecturer's use of 'Aristotle' plainly succeeds in referring to Aristotle. The audience understands whom the lecturer means, and the philosophical discussion proceeds without difficulty. Reference succeeds despite the failure of the identifying description.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for the possibility that reference may succeed through a causal or historical chain of transmission connecting the speaker's use of the term to the original object, independently of whether the speaker possesses a correct identifying description.
passage: One might analyse reference by proposing that a singular term refers to an object when the speaker intends to pick out the object, possesses a description that uniquely identifies it, and the object satisfies that description. This characterisation captures the intentional, descriptive, and verifying components commonly invoked in accounts of reference. Yet a case which tells against this analysis may be drawn from ordinary academic discourse. Suppose a lecturer, intending to discuss the philosopher who composed the Nicomachean Ethics, says 'Aristotle' in the course of a lecture. The lecturer believes that Aristotle was born in Athens and was the founder of the Lyceum. In fact, Aristotle was born in Stagira, not Athens. The identifying description possessed by the lecturer — 'the philosopher born in Athens who founded the Lyceum' — is not satisfied by any single individual: no philosopher born in Athens founded the Lyceum. Condition (iii) of the proposed definition fails. Yet we should not conclude that the lecturer has failed to refer to anyone. The audience understands perfectly well that the lecturer is speaking about Aristotle, the discussion of the Nicomachean Ethics proceeds without impediment, and no confusion arises. Reference has succeeded despite the falsity of the identifying description. One might observe that the lecturer's use of the name 'Aristotle' is connected to the historical Aristotle through a chain of linguistic transmission: the lecturer learnt the name from a teacher, who learnt it from another, in a chain stretching back to individuals who were acquainted with Aristotle himself. It appears that the definition, by requiring that the speaker possess a correct identifying description, fails to account for the possibility that reference may be secured by a causal or historical chain of transmission rather than by the accuracy of the speaker's beliefs about the referent.

definition: A proper name N names an individual I if and only if (i) N was introduced into the language by an act of baptism or stipulation directed at I, (ii) N has been transmitted from the original baptiser to subsequent users through a continuous chain of communication, and (iii) each user of N intends to use it to refer to the same individual as the person from whom he learnt the name.
conditions: N was introduced into the language by an act of baptism or stipulation directed at I
N has been transmitted from the original baptiser to subsequent users through a continuous chain of communication
Each user of N intends to use it to refer to the same individual as the person from whom he learnt the name
counterexample: Suppose that in the seventeenth century, a group of scholars, upon reading garbled translations of Arabic texts, attribute certain mathematical discoveries to a figure they call 'Magister Domus.' The name is introduced into scholarly usage by a specific act of citation, and it is transmitted through a continuous chain of academic communication over centuries. Each scholar who uses the name intends to refer to the same person as his predecessor. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose there never was any individual named Magister Domus — the discoveries were made by several unrelated mathematicians, and the name arose from a mistranslation. The name has been transmitted through a flawless causal chain and used with the right intentions, but it fails to name anyone because no individual was the genuine target of the original baptism.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the original act of baptism or stipulation be directed at a genuine, existing individual rather than at a figure conjured by error, thereby allowing names that arise from mistranslation or scholarly confusion to satisfy all stated conditions despite naming no one.
passage: One might analyse the naming relation by proposing that a proper name names an individual when the name was introduced by an act of baptism directed at that individual, transmitted through a continuous chain of communication, and used at each stage with the intention of co-referring with the previous user. This causal-chain account captures the historical and intentional structure by which names are ordinarily propagated. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the history of scholarship. Suppose that in the seventeenth century, a group of European scholars, working from garbled translations of Arabic mathematical texts, attributes a series of important discoveries to a figure whom they call 'Magister Domus.' The name is introduced by a specific act of scholarly citation, and over the following centuries it is transmitted through a continuous chain of academic communication: each scholar who writes about Magister Domus has learnt the name from a predecessor, and each intends to refer to the same individual. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, suppose that no individual corresponding to Magister Domus ever existed. The mathematical discoveries in question were made by several unrelated scholars, and the name arose from a mistranslation that fused their contributions into the biography of a single fictitious person. The chain of communication is unbroken, and the referential intentions are impeccable, yet the name fails to name anyone. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only that the baptism be directed at an individual and the chain be properly transmitted, does not ensure that the original baptismal act was directed at a genuine, existing individual rather than at a phantom created by scholarly error. It appears that a satisfactory account must require that the original act of naming be grounded in contact with, or acquaintance with, an actual individual, and not merely with a figure conjured by misunderstanding.

definition: A translation T of a sentence S from language L1 into language L2 is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-conditions of S, (ii) T employs only expressions of L2 that are part of the standard lexicon and grammar, and (iii) a competent speaker of L2 would assent to T in the same circumstances in which a competent speaker of L1 would assent to S.
conditions: T preserves the truth-conditions of S
T employs only expressions of L2 that are part of the standard lexicon and grammar
A competent speaker of L2 would assent to T in the same circumstances in which a competent speaker of L1 would assent to S
counterexample: Suppose S is a line of French poetry by Baudelaire that employs a specific metre, a pattern of internal rhyme, and an allusion to a figure from French literary tradition. A translator produces T in English that preserves the truth-conditions perfectly: T says exactly what S says regarding the scene described. T employs only standard English. And a competent English speaker would assent to T in the same circumstances as a French speaker would assent to S. All three conditions are met. Yet the translation has lost the metre, the internal rhyme, and the allusion, all of which are essential to the literary force and meaning of the original. We should hesitate to call this an adequate translation of the poem.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that T preserve the stylistic, connotative, and literary properties of S — the features of rhythm, register, allusion, and tone that contribute to the meaning of S beyond its truth-conditional content.
passage: One might propose that a translation is adequate when it preserves the truth-conditions of the original sentence, employs standard expressions of the target language, and would receive assent from competent speakers of the target language in the same circumstances as the original. This characterisation captures the semantic, grammatical, and pragmatic desiderata most commonly invoked in discussions of translation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from literary translation. Suppose a translator undertakes to render into English a celebrated line of French verse by Baudelaire. The original employs a specific alexandrine metre, a pattern of internal rhyme that reinforces the emotional tenor of the passage, and an allusion to a figure from the French literary tradition whose resonance depends upon the reader's familiarity with that tradition. The translator produces an English sentence that preserves the truth-conditions of the original with perfect accuracy: the scene described, the objects mentioned, and the relations asserted are all faithfully captured. The translation employs only standard English vocabulary and grammar. And a competent English speaker, confronted with the same scene, would assent to the English sentence in the same circumstances in which a French speaker would assent to the original. All three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied. Nevertheless, we should hesitate to regard the translation as adequate. The metre is lost, the internal rhyme has vanished, and the literary allusion, being specific to the French tradition, finds no equivalent in the English rendering. What remains is a prosaic sentence that communicates the same facts but none of the poetic force. One might observe that the definition, by attending only to truth-conditions, standard usage, and assent, fails to require the preservation of stylistic, connotative, and literary properties that are essential to the meaning of the original in any sense richer than the purely truth-conditional.

definition: An utterance U is a metaphor if and only if (i) U contains an expression used in a sense that deviates from its literal meaning, (ii) a competent hearer can identify the intended non-literal meaning through the recognition of a relevant similarity between the literal and intended referents, and (iii) U is not a case of dead metaphor, idiom, or conventional figurative speech.
conditions: U contains an expression used in a sense that deviates from its literal meaning
A competent hearer can identify the intended non-literal meaning through the recognition of a relevant similarity between the literal and intended referents
U is not a case of dead metaphor, idiom, or conventional figurative speech
counterexample: Suppose a poet writes 'The chairman of the meeting ploughed through the agenda.' The verb 'ploughed' deviates from its literal meaning of turning soil. A competent hearer can recognise a similarity between forcefully turning soil and forcefully advancing through a list of items. And the expression is not yet a dead metaphor or an established idiom in the language. All three conditions are met. Yet consider a second case: a poet writes 'The silence was a green thought in a green shade.' This utterance deviates from literal meaning, but no straightforward similarity between silence and a green thought can be identified. A competent hearer grasps the metaphorical force not through recognising a similarity but through an imaginative engagement that creates new connections. The definition, by requiring similarity recognition, excludes creative metaphors whose force depends upon the generation of novel associations rather than the detection of pre-existing resemblances.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate metaphors whose cognitive effect depends upon the imaginative creation of novel associations rather than the recognition of pre-existing similarities between literal and intended referents.
passage: One might analyse metaphor by proposing that an utterance is metaphorical when it contains an expression used non-literally, the intended meaning can be recovered through the recognition of a similarity between literal and intended referents, and the expression is not a dead metaphor or established idiom.
