This account captures the deviation, intelligibility, and vitality that we associate with living metaphorical language. Yet a case which tells against this analysis may be drawn from poetry. Suppose a poet writes 'The silence was a green thought in a green shade.' The expression 'green thought' deviates from its literal meaning: thoughts are not literally coloured, and the shade is described in a way that resists straightforward paraphrase. A competent reader, confronted with this utterance, does not identify the metaphorical meaning by recognising a pre-existing similarity between silence and greenness, or between thoughts and colours. Rather, the reader's understanding proceeds through an imaginative engagement that creates new connections — associations between the tranquillity of silence, the coolness of shade, and the vitality suggested by the colour green — connections that did not exist prior to the encounter with the metaphor. One might observe that the definition, by requiring the recognition of a relevant similarity, presupposes that the cognitive work of metaphor consists in detecting a resemblance that is already there. Yet the most creative and powerful metaphors do not exploit pre-existing similarities; they generate new ways of seeing by bringing together domains that were not previously associated. The definition, by insisting upon similarity recognition as the mechanism of metaphorical comprehension, excludes precisely those metaphors whose force depends upon the creation of novel associations rather than the detection of familiar ones.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker S, in uttering U, expresses an intention to perform a future action A, (ii) S intends the utterance to place him under an obligation to perform A, and (iii) the hearer H recognises that S intends to bind himself to the performance of A.
conditions: The speaker S, in uttering U, expresses an intention to perform a future action A
S intends the utterance to place him under an obligation to perform A
The hearer H recognises that S intends to bind himself to the performance of A
counterexample: Suppose an actor, during a theatrical performance, delivers the line 'I promise to return your ring by Tuesday' to another actor on stage. The actor expresses an intention to perform a future action. By the conventions of the script, he intends the utterance to place his character under an obligation. And the audience, as well as the other actor, recognises that the character intends to bind himself to the performance. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet no genuine promise has been made: no one is obligated to return any ring, and no one expects any ring to be returned. The utterance occurs within a conventional framework — the theatre — that suspends the normal force of promissory speech.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the utterance be made in circumstances that are not conventionally understood to suspend the normal obligations of speech — that the context of utterance be one in which promissory force is genuinely operative rather than merely performed or represented.
passage: One might propose that an utterance constitutes a promise when the speaker expresses an intention to perform a future action, intends the utterance to create an obligation, and the hearer recognises this intention. This characterisation captures the intentional, obligatory, and communicative dimensions of promising. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the theatre. Suppose an actor, in the course of a performance, delivers the line 'I promise to return your ring by Tuesday.' In the context of the play, the actor expresses an intention to perform a future action — the return of a ring. The conventions of dramatic performance dictate that the actor, in character, intends the utterance to create an obligation upon his character. And the audience, along with the other actor on stage, recognises that the character intends to bind himself to the performance of the promised action. One might suppose that all three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied: an intention is expressed, an obligation is intended, and the intention is recognised. Nevertheless, no genuine promise has been made. The actor is under no obligation to return any ring. The audience expects no ring to be returned. The utterance occurs within a conventional framework — the theatre — that is understood by all parties to suspend the ordinary force of promissory speech. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only the expression of intention, the intention to obligate, and the recognition of that intention, fails to require that these acts occur in a context that does not conventionally suspend the normal force of speech acts. It appears that a satisfactory account of promising must attend to the conditions under which promissory force is genuinely operative, rather than merely represented or performed within a framework that all parties understand to be non-binding.

definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) there exist borderline cases to which it is indeterminate whether F applies, (ii) F admits of degrees, and (iii) F gives rise to a sorites paradox when applied along a dimension of continuous variation.
conditions: There exist borderline cases to which it is indeterminate whether F applies
F admits of degrees
F gives rise to a sorites paradox when applied along a dimension of continuous variation
counterexample: Suppose we consider the predicate 'is a prime number.' There are no borderline cases: for every natural number, it is determinate whether the number is prime or not. The predicate does not admit of degrees: a number is not more or less prime. And it does not give rise to a sorites paradox along any dimension of continuous variation. None of the three conditions is met, and indeed the predicate is not vague. But now consider the predicate 'is a small prime number.' There may be borderline cases — is 31 a small prime? — and the predicate appears to admit of degrees. Yet it does not give rise to a sorites paradox in the standard sense, because the primes do not form a continuous sequence: the gaps between successive primes prevent the construction of a sorites series in which each adjacent pair differs by a negligibly small amount. Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, but condition (iii) is not. Yet the predicate is plainly vague.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for predicates that are vague despite not giving rise to a sorites paradox, because the domain of application lacks the continuous variation required for sorites reasoning. Vagueness can arise in discrete domains where borderline indeterminacy exists without a sorites series.
passage: One might characterise vagueness by proposing that a predicate is vague when it has borderline cases, admits of degrees, and gives rise to a sorites paradox along a dimension of continuous variation. This account captures the indeterminacy, gradability, and paradox-generating character that we associate with vague language. Yet a case which tells against this analysis may be constructed by considering predicates applied to discrete domains. Suppose we consider the predicate 'is a small prime number.' There are plausible borderline cases: one might hesitate over whether 23 or 31 is a small prime. The predicate admits of degrees in a natural sense: 3 is a smaller prime than 29, and there is a range over which the attribution of smallness becomes progressively less certain. Conditions (i) and (ii) of the proposed definition are satisfied. Yet condition (iii) is not straightforwardly satisfied, for the prime numbers do not form a continuous sequence. The gaps between successive primes — sometimes small, sometimes large — prevent the construction of a sorites series in which each adjacent pair of cases differs by a negligibly small amount along a single dimension of continuous variation. One cannot proceed grain by grain from a clear case of a small prime to a clear case of a large prime in the manner required by the classical sorites argument. Nevertheless, the predicate is plainly vague: competent speakers disagree about its application in borderline cases, and no sharp boundary separates the small primes from the non-small primes. It appears that the definition, by requiring that the predicate give rise to a sorites paradox along a continuous dimension, excludes predicates that are vague in discrete domains — domains in which borderline indeterminacy exists without the continuous variation that sorites reasoning demands.

definition: Two expressions E1 and E2 are synonymous if and only if (i) E1 and E2 can be substituted for one another in all declarative sentences without altering the truth-value of those sentences, (ii) competent speakers regard E1 and E2 as interchangeable in ordinary discourse, and (iii) E1 and E2 share the same definition in a standard dictionary of the language.
conditions: E1 and E2 can be substituted for one another in all declarative sentences without altering the truth-value of those sentences
Competent speakers regard E1 and E2 as interchangeable in ordinary discourse
E1 and E2 share the same definition in a standard dictionary of the language
counterexample: Consider the expressions 'bachelor' and 'unmarried man.' They share the same dictionary definition. Competent speakers regard them as interchangeable in ordinary discourse. And substituting one for the other in declarative sentences typically preserves truth-value. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet in the sentence 'He earned a bachelor's degree,' replacing 'bachelor' with 'unmarried man' produces 'He earned an unmarried man's degree,' which is nonsensical. The substitution fails in contexts where 'bachelor' is used in a sense unrelated to its primary dictionary definition, revealing that truth-value preservation does not hold in all declarative sentences.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that E1 and E2 share not only their primary dictionary sense but all their senses, including secondary, technical, and collocational uses, so that substitution preserves truth-value across every context rather than merely in the majority of ordinary contexts.
passage: One might propose that two expressions are synonymous when they can be substituted for one another in all declarative sentences without altering truth-value, competent speakers regard them as interchangeable, and they share the same dictionary definition. This characterisation captures the extensional, social, and lexicographic aspects of synonymy. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the polysemy of common English words. Consider the expressions 'bachelor' and 'unmarried man.' They share the same definition in standard dictionaries of English. Competent speakers regard them as interchangeable in ordinary conversation: one may say either 'John is a bachelor' or 'John is an unmarried man' without any change of meaning or communicative effect. And in most declarative sentences, substituting one for the other preserves truth-value. All three conditions of the proposed definition appear satisfied. Yet consider the sentence 'He earned a bachelor's degree.' If we substitute 'unmarried man' for 'bachelor,' we obtain 'He earned an unmarried man's degree,' which is either nonsensical or means something entirely different from the original sentence. The truth-value is not preserved — indeed, the resulting sentence scarcely has a truth-value at all. One might observe that the failure arises because 'bachelor' has a secondary sense — pertaining to an academic degree — that 'unmarried man' does not share. The dictionary definition captures only the primary sense, and the condition of universal truth-value preservation is violated by the secondary sense. It appears that the definition, by requiring shared dictionary definitions and universal substitutability, fails to account for the multiple senses that expressions carry, and by attending only to the primary sense it overlooks the many contexts in which a secondary or collocational sense blocks substitution.

definition: A definite description 'the F' refers to an object O if and only if (i) O is the unique object satisfying the predicate F, (ii) the speaker who uses 'the F' presupposes that there is exactly one F, and (iii) the context of utterance makes it clear which object is intended.
conditions: O is the unique object satisfying the predicate F
The speaker who uses 'the F' presupposes that there is exactly one F
The context of utterance makes it clear which object is intended
counterexample: Suppose a speaker in a room containing two tables says 'The table is covered with books,' gesturing towards one of them. The predicate 'table' is satisfied by two objects in the room, not uniquely. The speaker does not presuppose that there is exactly one table — she can see both tables. Yet the context of utterance, together with the gesture, makes it clear which table is intended, and the utterance succeeds in referring to the indicated table. Condition (i) fails, condition (ii) fails, but the reference is accomplished. The definition requires uniqueness and presupposition of uniqueness, yet reference by definite description frequently succeeds in the absence of both.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate the pragmatic mechanisms — gesture, salience, and contextual restriction of the domain — by which definite descriptions succeed in referring even when the predicate is not uniquely satisfied in an unrestricted domain and the speaker does not presuppose uniqueness.
passage: One might propose that a definite description 'the F' refers to an object when that object uniquely satisfies the predicate F, the speaker presupposes that there is exactly one F, and the context makes clear which object is intended. This characterisation captures the uniqueness, presuppositional, and contextual elements commonly associated with definite reference. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from everyday conversation. Suppose a speaker is in a room containing two tables, one by the window and one near the door. She says 'The table is covered with books,' gesturing towards the table by the window. The predicate 'table' is satisfied by two objects, not one, so condition (i) fails: there is no unique object satisfying the predicate in the unrestricted domain. The speaker does not presuppose that there is exactly one table — she is perfectly aware that the room contains two — so condition (ii) also fails. Yet the context of utterance, together with the speaker's gesture and the relative salience of the two tables, makes it entirely clear which table is intended, and the utterance succeeds in referring to the table by the window. All parties to the conversation understand which table is meant. One might observe that the definition, by requiring both uniqueness of satisfaction and presupposition of uniqueness, imposes conditions that are frequently absent in successful uses of definite descriptions. Speakers routinely use 'the F' in domains where multiple objects satisfy F, relying upon gesture, conversational salience, and pragmatic restriction of the domain to secure reference. It appears that a satisfactory account must accommodate these pragmatic mechanisms rather than insisting upon a strict uniqueness condition.

definition: An utterance U constitutes an assertion if and only if (i) the speaker S presents the propositional content of U as true, (ii) S intends the hearer to form the belief that the propositional content of U is true, and (iii) S takes responsibility for the truth of the propositional content.
conditions: The speaker S presents the propositional content of U as true
S intends the hearer to form the belief that the propositional content of U is true
S takes responsibility for the truth of the propositional content
counterexample: Suppose a teacher, in a philosophy seminar, states 'The earth is at the centre of the universe' while presenting the Ptolemaic worldview for purposes of exposition. The teacher presents the propositional content as true within the context of the exposition: she speaks in the declarative mood with no ironic inflection. She intends the students to understand that, within the Ptolemaic framework, this proposition was held to be true — and in that sense, she intends them to entertain the belief. And she takes responsibility for the accuracy of her exposition. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet the teacher is not asserting that the earth is at the centre of the universe; she is expounding a historical view without endorsing it.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the speaker S endorse the propositional content of U as true from her own perspective, rather than merely presenting it as true within a framework, for purposes of exposition, or on behalf of another viewpoint.
passage: One might analyse assertion by proposing that an utterance constitutes an assertion when the speaker presents its propositional content as true, intends the hearer to form the corresponding belief, and takes responsibility for the truth of that content. This characterisation captures the presentational, communicative, and committal aspects of assertoric speech. Yet a case which tells against this analysis may be drawn from pedagogical practice. Suppose a philosophy teacher, in the course of a seminar on the history of astronomy, states 'The earth is at the centre of the universe' while presenting the Ptolemaic system. She speaks in the declarative mood, with no ironic inflection or qualifying phrase. She intends her students to understand the propositional content and to appreciate that, within the Ptolemaic framework, it was regarded as true. And she takes responsibility for the accuracy of her exposition: if she had misstated the Ptolemaic view, she would accept correction. One might suppose that all three conditions of the proposed definition are satisfied: the content is presented as true, the hearer is intended to form a corresponding belief, and the speaker accepts responsibility. Nevertheless, the teacher has not asserted that the earth is at the centre of the universe. She does not endorse the proposition from her own perspective; she is expounding a historical view for educational purposes without committing herself to its truth. One might observe that the definition, by requiring only that the speaker present the content as true and intend the hearer to believe it, fails to distinguish between genuinely endorsing a proposition and merely presenting it as the content of another framework or viewpoint. It appears that a satisfactory account of assertion must require that the speaker endorse the propositional content from her own perspective, rather than merely presenting it as something held to be true by others.

definition: The meaning of a sentence S is the set of conditions under which S is true, such that (i) two sentences have the same meaning if and only if they are true under exactly the same conditions, (ii) knowing the meaning of S consists in knowing the conditions under which S is true, and (iii) the meaning of S is determined entirely by the meanings of its constituent words and their syntactic arrangement.
conditions: Two sentences have the same meaning if and only if they are true under exactly the same conditions
Knowing the meaning of S consists in knowing the conditions under which S is true
The meaning of S is determined entirely by the meanings of its constituent words and their syntactic arrangement
counterexample: Suppose one considers the sentences 'The morning star is bright' and 'The evening star is bright.' Since the morning star and the evening star are in fact the same celestial body, namely Venus, these two sentences are true under exactly the same conditions. By the first condition, they have the same meaning. Yet an ancient astronomer might know the meaning of the first sentence without knowing the meaning of the second, for he might not know that the morning star and the evening star are identical. The two sentences differ in what one might call their cognitive significance: they present the same state of affairs under different modes of presentation. The definition, by identifying meaning with truth-conditions, cannot account for this difference.
missing_condition: The definition fails to include a component of meaning that captures the mode of presentation or cognitive significance of a sentence, so that sentences with identical truth-conditions but different modes of presenting their subject matter are wrongly classified as having the same meaning.
passage: One might propose that the meaning of a sentence is the set of conditions under which it is true, that two sentences share meaning just when they share truth-conditions, that to know a sentence's meaning is to know its truth-conditions, and that meaning is compositionally determined by the meanings of constituent words and their syntactic arrangement. This characterisation has considerable theoretical elegance and captures an important aspect of linguistic competence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the phenomenon of co-referential expressions. Consider the sentences 'The morning star is bright' and 'The evening star is bright.' Since the morning star and the evening star are the same celestial body, these sentences are true under exactly the same conditions. By the first condition of the proposed definition, they have the same meaning. Yet an ancient astronomer might understand the first sentence perfectly well without understanding the second, if he does not know that the two expressions refer to the same object. The two sentences differ in what one might call their cognitive significance: they present the same state of affairs under different modes of presentation. A competent speaker may assent to one and dissent from the other without logical error. It appears that the definition, by identifying meaning with truth-conditions alone, fails to account for the dimension of meaning that consists in the manner in which a referent is presented. Two sentences may be true in exactly the same circumstances and yet differ in meaning in a way that matters for thought, inference, and understanding.

definition: A singular term T refers to an object O if and only if (i) T is associated with a set of descriptive properties that O uniquely satisfies, (ii) a competent speaker who uses T intends to pick out the object that satisfies those properties, and (iii) O is the unique object in the relevant domain that satisfies the descriptive properties associated with T.
conditions: T is associated with a set of descriptive properties that O uniquely satisfies
A competent speaker who uses T intends to pick out the object satisfying those properties
O is the unique object in the relevant domain satisfying the descriptive properties associated with T
counterexample: Suppose that a community of speakers uses the name 'Aristotle' and associates with it the descriptive properties of being the teacher of Alexander the Great, the author of the Nicomachean Ethics, and the founder of the Lyceum. Suppose further that historical research reveals that the Nicomachean Ethics was in fact written by a different philosopher, so that no single individual satisfies all three properties. By the proposed definition, the name 'Aristotle' fails to refer, since no unique object satisfies the full set of associated descriptive properties. Yet speakers continue to use the name successfully, and they refer to the historical figure despite the falsity of one of the associated descriptions. The name refers not because some set of descriptions is uniquely satisfied but because there is a causal-historical chain connecting present uses of the name to an original act of naming.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the possibility that reference may be secured by a causal-historical chain of transmission from an original act of naming rather than by the satisfaction of associated descriptive properties, so that a name may refer successfully even when the descriptions speakers associate with it are partially or wholly inaccurate.
passage: One might define reference as obtaining when a singular term is associated with a set of descriptive properties that are uniquely satisfied by an object, the speaker intends to pick out the object satisfying those properties, and the object is indeed the unique satisfier. This analysis captures the intuition that reference is mediated by description, and it has the virtue of explaining how speakers manage to single out particular objects from among many. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the use of proper names. Suppose that a community of speakers uses the name 'Aristotle' and associates with it the descriptive properties of being the teacher of Alexander the Great, the author of the Nicomachean Ethics, and the founder of the Lyceum. Suppose that historical research reveals that the Nicomachean Ethics was in fact written by a different philosopher. No single individual satisfies the full set of associated descriptions. By the proposed definition, the name fails to refer. Yet speakers continue to use the name successfully: they speak of Aristotle, argue about his views, and refer to the historical figure without difficulty. It appears that reference is secured not by the satisfaction of a set of descriptions but by a causal-historical chain connecting present uses of the name to an original act of naming. The definition, by making reference wholly dependent upon descriptive fit, cannot account for cases in which the descriptions associated with a name are partially or wholly inaccurate and yet the name refers successfully.

definition: A name N names an object O if and only if (i) N was introduced in a baptismal act in which the speaker stood in a direct perceptual relation to O, (ii) subsequent uses of N are linked to the baptismal act by a continuous chain of communicative intentions, and (iii) speakers who use N intend to refer to the same object that was singled out in the original baptismal act.
conditions: N was introduced in a baptismal act in which the speaker stood in a direct perceptual relation to O
Subsequent uses are linked to the baptismal act by a continuous chain of communicative intentions
Speakers who use N intend to refer to the same object singled out in the baptismal act
counterexample: Suppose a mathematician introduces the name 'omega' for the first infinite ordinal number. No baptismal act involves direct perceptual contact with this object, for infinite ordinals are abstract entities that cannot be perceived. Yet the name 'omega' successfully names the first infinite ordinal, and mathematicians use it with perfect precision and mutual understanding. The second and third conditions may be satisfied, but the first is not: no speaker has ever stood in a direct perceptual relation to an infinite ordinal. The name nonetheless refers.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the naming of abstract objects, which cannot be singled out by direct perceptual contact, revealing that the requirement of a perceptual baptismal act is too restrictive and that names may be introduced by stipulative definition rather than by ostensive acquaintance.
passage: One might propose that a name names an object when the name was introduced in a baptismal act involving direct perceptual contact with the object, when subsequent uses are linked to that act by a chain of communicative intentions, and when speakers intend to refer to the same object. This characterisation captures the causal and intentional structure of naming as it operates in familiar cases of ostension: one perceives an object, bestows a name upon it, and the name is transmitted through a community of speakers. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from mathematical practice. Suppose a mathematician introduces the name 'omega' for the first infinite ordinal number. No baptismal act involves direct perceptual contact with this entity, for infinite ordinals are abstract objects that cannot be perceived, touched, or pointed at. Yet the name 'omega' successfully names the first infinite ordinal, and mathematicians employ it with perfect precision. The second and third conditions of the proposed definition may be satisfied: subsequent uses are linked by a chain of communicative intentions, and speakers intend to refer to the same object. But the first condition is not met, for no speaker has ever stood in a direct perceptual relation to an infinite ordinal. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon perceptual baptism as a necessary condition, is too restrictive. Names may be introduced by stipulative definition or by descriptive characterisation as well as by ostensive acquaintance, and a theory of naming that demands perceptual contact will inevitably exclude the vast domain of abstract objects.

definition: A translation T of a sentence S from language L1 into language L2 is adequate if and only if (i) T preserves the truth-conditions of S, (ii) T preserves the logical relations that S bears to other sentences in L1, and (iii) a competent speaker of L2 who reads T would draw the same inferences as a competent speaker of L1 who reads S.
conditions: T preserves the truth-conditions of S
T preserves the logical relations that S bears to other sentences in L1
A competent speaker of L2 reading T would draw the same inferences as a competent speaker of L1 reading S
counterexample: Suppose one translates the French sentence 'Vous etes tres aimable' into English as 'You are very kind.' The translation preserves the truth-conditions, preserves the logical relations, and a competent English speaker would draw the same inferences about the speaker's assessment of the addressee. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the French sentence, through its use of the formal 'vous' rather than the informal 'tu,' conveys a register of social deference and formality that the English sentence, lacking any distinction between formal and informal second-person address, does not capture. A dimension of social meaning is lost in the translation, and yet the definition, being concerned only with truth-conditions, logical relations, and inferential equivalence, cannot detect this loss.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require the preservation of pragmatic, social, and connotative dimensions of meaning, such as register, formality, and tone, so that a translation may satisfy all three conditions whilst losing aspects of the original sentence's communicative force that are essential to its full meaning.
passage: One might define adequate translation as the preservation of truth-conditions, the preservation of logical relations, and the guarantee that competent speakers of the target language would draw the same inferences as competent speakers of the source language. This characterisation captures the logical and inferential core of translation with considerable precision. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the translation of forms of address. Suppose one translates the French sentence 'Vous etes tres aimable' into English as 'You are very kind.' The truth-conditions are preserved: the sentence is true in the same circumstances. The logical relations are preserved: the same entailments hold. A competent English speaker would draw the same inferences about the speaker's assessment of the addressee's character. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the French sentence, through its use of the formal 'vous' rather than the informal 'tu,' conveys a register of social deference and formality that the English sentence, which lacks any distinction between formal and informal address, cannot capture. A significant dimension of social meaning is lost. One might observe that this loss matters for communication: the choice of 'vous' signals respect, social distance, or politeness in a way that affects how the utterance is received. It appears that the definition, by attending only to truth-conditions, logical relations, and inferential equivalence, fails to require the preservation of pragmatic, social, and connotative dimensions of meaning that are essential to the full communicative force of the original.

definition: An utterance U is metaphorical if and only if (i) U involves the application of a term to an object or domain to which it does not literally apply, (ii) the speaker of U does not intend the utterance to be taken literally, and (iii) a competent hearer would recognise that the literal meaning of U is false or absurd and would seek an alternative interpretation.
conditions: U involves the application of a term to an object or domain to which it does not literally apply
The speaker does not intend the utterance to be taken literally
A competent hearer would recognise the literal meaning as false or absurd and seek an alternative interpretation
counterexample: Suppose a speaker says, 'The city never sleeps,' intending the utterance metaphorically. The literal meaning, that the city lacks the capacity for sleep, is not absurd, for cities, being non-sentient, trivially never sleep. A competent hearer might take the sentence as literally true in a trivial sense without recognising any absurdity, even though the speaker intends a metaphorical meaning about the city's ceaseless activity. The first and second conditions are satisfied, but the third is not: the literal meaning is not obviously false or absurd, for it is trivially true. Yet the utterance is plainly metaphorical.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for metaphors whose literal meaning is trivially true rather than false or absurd, revealing that a competent hearer's recognition of metaphor may depend not upon the falsity of the literal meaning but upon its pragmatic inappropriateness or informational triviality in context.
passage: One might define a metaphorical utterance as one in which a term is applied to an object or domain to which it does not literally apply, the speaker does not intend the utterance literally, and a competent hearer would recognise the literal meaning as false or absurd and seek an alternative interpretation. This characterisation captures the indirection and the semantic tension that we associate with metaphor. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the class of metaphors whose literal meaning is trivially true rather than false. Suppose a speaker says, 'The city never sleeps,' intending the utterance metaphorically to convey the idea that the city's activity continues without cessation. The literal meaning of the sentence is that the city, a non-sentient entity, does not sleep. But this is not false or absurd; it is trivially true, for cities cannot sleep. A competent hearer might take the sentence as literally true in this trivial sense without perceiving any absurdity. The third condition is therefore not satisfied: the literal meaning is not obviously false but trivially true. Yet the utterance is plainly metaphorical; the speaker intends something beyond the trivial literal truth. It appears that the definition, by requiring that the literal meaning be false or absurd, fails to accommodate metaphors whose force depends upon the pragmatic inappropriateness or informational triviality of the literal reading rather than upon its falsity. A metaphor may be recognised as such not because its literal meaning is absurd but because a literal interpretation would be uninformative or contextually irrelevant.

definition: An utterance U constitutes a promise if and only if (i) the speaker S, in uttering U, expresses the intention to perform a future action A, (ii) S believes that A is within S's power to perform, and (iii) S intends the utterance to place S under an obligation to perform A.
conditions: The speaker expresses the intention to perform a future action A
The speaker believes A is within the speaker's power to perform
The speaker intends the utterance to place the speaker under an obligation to perform A
counterexample: Suppose an actor, performing in a play, utters the words 'I promise to return before dawn' with full theatrical conviction. In the context of the performance, the actor expresses the intention to perform a future action, believes the action is within the character's power, and intends the words to convey an obligation upon the character. All three conditions appear to be satisfied within the frame of the utterance. Yet no promise has been made: the actor has incurred no obligation to return before dawn, and no one in the audience takes herself to have received a promise. The utterance has the form and apparent force of a promise but is void of promissory effect.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the utterance be made in circumstances that the speaker and hearer mutually recognise as non-fictitious and as constituting genuine communicative interaction, so that promises uttered in fictional, theatrical, or otherwise parasitic contexts are wrongly classified as genuine promises.
passage: One might define a promise as an utterance in which the speaker expresses the intention to perform a future action, believes the action to be within the speaker's power, and intends the utterance to create an obligation. This characterisation captures the intentional and normative dimensions of promising with some precision. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the theatre. Suppose an actor, performing in a play, utters the words 'I promise to return before dawn' with full theatrical conviction. Within the frame of the performance, the actor expresses the intention to perform a future action, believes the action to be within the character's power, and intends the words to convey an obligation upon the character. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet no promise has been made in any genuine sense: the actor incurs no obligation to return before dawn, and no member of the audience takes herself to have received a promise. The utterance possesses the form and apparent force of a promise but is entirely void of promissory effect. One might observe that the difficulty lies in the context of the utterance. The theatrical setting is one in which speaker and hearer mutually understand that the words are not to be taken as genuine communicative acts but as performances within a fictional frame. It appears that the definition, by attending only to the speaker's intentions and beliefs, fails to require that the utterance occur in circumstances mutually recognised as constituting genuine, non-fictitious communicative interaction.

definition: A predicate F is vague if and only if (i) there exist borderline cases to which the application of F is indeterminate, (ii) F admits of a sorites series in which no sharp boundary can be drawn between cases to which F applies and cases to which it does not, and (iii) the indeterminacy of F is a feature of the language rather than of the world.
conditions: There exist borderline cases to which the application of F is indeterminate
F admits of a sorites series with no sharp boundary between positive and negative cases
The indeterminacy of F is a feature of the language rather than of the world
counterexample: Consider the predicate 'is a cloud.' There exist borderline cases in which a diffuse collection of water droplets is neither clearly a cloud nor clearly not a cloud. The predicate admits of a sorites series as the droplets gradually disperse. Yet it is not obvious that the indeterminacy is purely linguistic. The water droplets in the atmosphere are themselves distributed in a continuously varying manner, with no sharp physical boundary between cloud and clear sky. The indeterminacy of the predicate may reflect a genuine indeterminacy in the world rather than a mere deficiency of language. The first and second conditions are satisfied, but the third is contested, and yet 'is a cloud' is a paradigmatic example of a vague predicate.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for the possibility that the indeterminacy of a vague predicate may arise from worldly indeterminacy as well as from linguistic indeterminacy, presupposing without argument that vagueness is exclusively a linguistic phenomenon and thereby excluding predicates whose vagueness reflects genuine ontological indeterminacy.
passage: One might define a vague predicate as one that admits of borderline cases, permits the construction of a sorites series with no sharp boundary, and whose indeterminacy is a feature of the language rather than of the world. This characterisation captures the most salient features of vague predicates and rests upon the widely held assumption that vagueness is a linguistic rather than a metaphysical phenomenon. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the predicate 'is a cloud.' There exist borderline cases in which a diffuse collection of water droplets is neither clearly a cloud nor clearly not a cloud. The predicate admits of a sorites series as the droplets gradually disperse. Yet it is by no means certain that the indeterminacy is purely linguistic. The water droplets in the atmosphere are themselves distributed in a continuously varying manner, with no sharp physical boundary between cloud and clear sky. One might argue that the indeterminacy of the predicate reflects a genuine indeterminacy in the world, that nature itself does not draw a sharp line between cloud and non-cloud. If this is so, then the third condition of the proposed definition is not satisfied, for the indeterminacy is not exclusively a feature of language. Yet 'is a cloud' is a paradigmatic example of a vague predicate. It appears that the definition, by presupposing that vagueness is entirely linguistic, excludes the possibility that some predicates are vague because the world itself is vague, and thereby imposes a substantive metaphysical thesis as if it were a definitional requirement.

definition: The meaning of a word W is the mental image that W produces in the mind of a competent speaker, such that (i) two words have the same meaning if and only if they produce the same mental image, (ii) understanding W consists in entertaining the appropriate mental image, and (iii) the meaning of W is private to the speaker in the sense that it is directly accessible only through introspection.
conditions: Two words have the same meaning if and only if they produce the same mental image
Understanding W consists in entertaining the appropriate mental image
The meaning of W is private to the speaker and directly accessible only through introspection
counterexample: Suppose one considers the word 'justice.' A competent speaker may understand the word perfectly well, using it appropriately in discourse and distinguishing just from unjust arrangements, without entertaining any mental image whatever. The word does not evoke a picture in the mind in the way that a concrete noun such as 'horse' might. The second condition fails: understanding 'justice' does not consist in entertaining a mental image. Yet 'justice' is meaningful, and competent speakers agree on its application in a wide range of cases.
missing_condition: The definition fails to account for the meaning of abstract terms that do not produce mental images, revealing that meaning cannot consist in mental imagery, for many meaningful words are perfectly understood without the mediation of any image and their meaning must reside in their role in inference and discourse rather than in introspectable pictorial content.
passage: One might propose that the meaning of a word is the mental image it produces in the mind of a competent speaker, that two words share meaning when they produce the same image, that understanding consists in entertaining the appropriate image, and that meaning is private and introspectable. This characterisation has a certain intuitive plausibility for concrete terms: the word 'horse' may indeed produce a mental image of a horse in many speakers. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the consideration of abstract terms. Suppose one considers the word 'justice.' A competent speaker may understand this word perfectly well, using it appropriately in discourse, distinguishing just from unjust arrangements with confidence, and engaging in reasoned argument about what justice requires. Yet the speaker need not entertain any mental image in the course of understanding the word. The word 'justice' does not produce a picture in the mind in the way that 'horse' or 'sunset' might. The second condition of the proposed definition is not satisfied: understanding does not consist in entertaining a mental image. Yet the word is undeniably meaningful. It appears that the definition mistakes a contingent psychological accompaniment of understanding for its essence. Many meaningful words are understood without the mediation of any image, and their meaning must reside not in introspectable pictorial content but in their role in inference, discourse, and the practices of a linguistic community.

definition: A demonstrative expression D (such as 'this' or 'that') refers to an object O if and only if (i) the speaker accompanies D with a gesture, such as a pointing, that singles out O from the surrounding environment, (ii) the object singled out by the gesture is perceptually available to both speaker and hearer, and (iii) the hearer identifies O as the intended referent on the basis of the gesture and the shared perceptual context.
conditions: The speaker accompanies D with a gesture that singles out O from the environment
The object singled out is perceptually available to both speaker and hearer
The hearer identifies O as the intended referent on the basis of gesture and shared perceptual context
counterexample: Suppose that two astronomers are conversing by telephone about a particular star that they have both been observing on previous nights but that is not currently visible to either of them owing to cloud cover. One says, 'That star we measured last Tuesday exhibited unusual spectral variation.' The demonstrative 'that' successfully refers to the star in question, and the hearer identifies the referent without difficulty. Yet no gesture accompanies the utterance, the object is not perceptually available to either party, and the identification proceeds not from gesture and shared perception but from shared memory and conversational context. None of the three conditions is satisfied, yet the demonstrative refers successfully.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that demonstrative reference may be secured by shared conversational context, prior discourse, or mutual memory rather than by perceptual availability and accompanying gesture, thereby excluding deferred, anaphoric, and discourse-dependent uses of demonstratives.
passage: One might define demonstrative reference as obtaining when the speaker accompanies a demonstrative expression with a gesture that singles out an object perceptually available to both speaker and hearer, and the hearer identifies the referent on the basis of the gesture and the shared perceptual context. This characterisation captures the paradigmatic case of demonstrative reference in face-to-face conversation, where one points at an object and says 'this' or 'that.' Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from remote communication. Suppose two astronomers converse by telephone about a particular star that they have both observed on previous nights but that is not currently visible owing to cloud cover. One says, 'That star we measured last Tuesday exhibited unusual spectral variation.' The demonstrative 'that' successfully refers to the star in question, and the hearer identifies the referent without difficulty. Yet no gesture accompanies the utterance, for the speakers are communicating by telephone. The object is not perceptually available to either party, for the sky is overcast. And the identification proceeds not from gesture and shared perception but from shared memory and conversational context. None of the three conditions of the proposed definition is satisfied, yet the demonstrative refers successfully. It appears that the definition, by tying demonstrative reference to gesture and perceptual availability, captures only the most basic case and fails to accommodate the many situations in which demonstratives refer through conversational context, prior discourse, or mutual memory.

definition: An utterance U constitutes an assertion if and only if (i) the speaker S presents a proposition P as true, (ii) S believes that P is true, and (iii) S intends to induce in the hearer a belief that P.
conditions: The speaker presents a proposition P as true
The speaker believes that P is true
The speaker intends to induce in the hearer a belief that P
counterexample: Suppose a witness in a court of law, under oath, states that the defendant was at the scene of the crime at nine o'clock. The witness presents this proposition as true, and the witness intends the jury to believe it. The first and third conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose the witness does not believe the proposition to be true; the witness is lying. By the proposed definition, the utterance does not constitute an assertion, since the second condition fails. Yet it is manifestly an assertion, albeit a dishonest one. The court treats it as an assertion, the jury receives it as an assertion, and the witness is subject to perjury charges precisely because what was said was an assertion. A lie is a species of assertion, not its absence.
missing_condition: The definition fails to recognise that assertion is a public speech act whose character is determined by the norms governing the utterance rather than by the speaker's private beliefs, so that a speaker who presents a proposition as true in an assertoric context performs an assertion regardless of whether the speaker privately believes the proposition, and a lie is an assertion made in violation of the sincerity norm rather than a non-assertion.
passage: One might define assertion as an utterance in which the speaker presents a proposition as true, believes it to be true, and intends to induce in the hearer a corresponding belief. This characterisation captures the sincerity and communicative aims that we ordinarily associate with asserting. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis may be drawn from the phenomenon of lying. Suppose a witness in a court of law, under oath, states that the defendant was at the scene of the crime at nine o'clock. The witness presents this proposition as true and intends the jury to believe it. The first and third conditions are satisfied. Yet the witness does not believe the proposition; the witness is lying. By the proposed definition, the utterance is not an assertion, since the second condition is not met. This conclusion is difficult to accept. The court treats the utterance as an assertion; the jury receives it as one; and the witness is subject to perjury charges precisely because the utterance was an assertion. A lie is not the absence of assertion but a defective assertion, an assertion made in violation of the norm of sincerity. It appears that the definition, by requiring the speaker's sincere belief as a necessary condition, conflates the norms governing assertion with the conditions for its occurrence. Assertion is a public speech act whose character is fixed by the conventions of the discursive context, and it obtains whenever a speaker presents a proposition as true in an assertoric setting, regardless of whether the speaker privately believes what is said.

definition: The meaning of a sentence is its truth-conditions if and only if the sentence is declarative, the truth-conditions are specifiable in a finitely stated theory, and the sentence is not context-dependent.
conditions: The sentence is declarative
The truth-conditions are specifiable in a finitely stated theory
The sentence is not context-dependent
counterexample: Consider a case in which someone utters the declarative sentence 'It is raining.' The truth-conditions of this sentence — that precipitation is occurring at a particular place and time — are specifiable within a finitely stated semantic theory. Yet the sentence is context-dependent: what counts as the relevant place and time is determined by the context of utterance. The third condition is not met. Nevertheless, it appears that the meaning of this sentence is captured by its truth-conditions, for competent speakers understand it precisely by grasping the conditions under which it would be true, with context supplying the needed parameters. The exclusion of context-dependent sentences is therefore too restrictive.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously excludes context-dependent sentences; for many such sentences, meaning is still best analysed as truth-conditions, with context serving to fix the relevant parameters rather than undermining the truth-conditional approach.
passage: It has been proposed that the meaning of a sentence consists in its truth-conditions, subject to three requirements: that the sentence be declarative, that the truth-conditions be specifiable within a finitely stated theory, and that the sentence not be context-dependent. The first two conditions are widely, if not universally, accepted. The third may be motivated by the observation that context-dependent sentences seem to lack stable truth-conditions, and that the identification of meaning with truth-conditions therefore becomes problematic for them. Yet we should hesitate to accept this restriction. Suppose one considers the sentence 'It is raining.' This is a declarative sentence. Its truth-conditions — roughly, that precipitation is occurring at a certain place and time — are specifiable within a finitely stated semantic theory, provided one allows for contextual parameters. But the sentence is plainly context-dependent: the place and time that determine its truth-value are fixed by the circumstances of utterance. The third condition is therefore not satisfied, and the analysis would deny that the meaning of this sentence is its truth-conditions. This seems an unfortunate result. Competent speakers understand this sentence precisely by grasping the conditions under which it would be true, with the context of utterance supplying the necessary parameters. The truth-conditional account of meaning appears to apply to it without difficulty, once one allows context to play its proper role. We are led to conclude that the exclusion of context-dependent sentences is too restrictive and that the analysis is insufficient as stated.

definition: A proper name refers to an object if and only if the name was introduced by an initial baptism in the presence of the object, a causal chain of communication links the current use of the name to that baptism, and the speaker intends to refer to the object so baptised.
conditions: The name was introduced by an initial baptism in the presence of the object
A causal chain of communication links the current use to that baptism
The speaker intends to refer to the object so baptised
counterexample: Suppose that an astronomer discovers a new planet by observing its gravitational effects on other bodies, without ever directly observing the planet itself. She introduces the name 'Erebus' for this planet. The baptism occurs not in the presence of the object but in the presence of indirect evidence of its existence. A causal chain of communication transmits the name, and subsequent speakers intend to refer to the planet so named. The first condition is not satisfied, for no one was in the presence of the planet at the time of naming. Yet it appears beyond question that the name 'Erebus' refers to the planet, suggesting that the requirement of presence at baptism is too strong.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires that the initial baptism occur in the presence of the object; names may be successfully introduced on the basis of indirect evidence, descriptions, or theoretical postulation without the object being present.
passage: A prominent account of reference for proper names holds that a name refers to an object when three conditions are met: the name was introduced in an initial baptism performed in the presence of the object, a causal chain of communication links subsequent uses of the name to that baptism, and the speaker intends to refer to the object thereby baptised. One might observe that this analysis captures many familiar cases. A child is named at birth in the presence of family members; the name is transmitted through a community of speakers; and those who use it intend to refer to the person so named. Yet one might construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Let us imagine an astronomer who discovers a new planet not by direct observation but by inferring its existence from gravitational perturbations in the orbits of other celestial bodies. She introduces the name 'Erebus' for this planet. No one has seen the planet; the baptism occurs in the astronomer's study, in the presence only of calculations and data. A causal chain of communication transmits the name through the scientific community, and those who employ it intend to refer to the planet so named. The second and third conditions are plainly satisfied. But the first is not, for the baptism did not take place in the presence of the object. Nevertheless, it appears unquestionable that 'Erebus' refers to the planet. We are led to conclude that the requirement of presence at the initial baptism is too restrictive and that the analysis must be revised to accommodate cases of reference fixed by description or theoretical postulation.

definition: A name is meaningful if and only if it has a bearer, the speaker is acquainted with the bearer, and the name functions as a rigid designator across possible situations.
conditions: The name has a bearer
The speaker is acquainted with the bearer
The name functions as a rigid designator across possible situations
counterexample: Consider a case in which a historian discusses Prester John — a legendary Christian king of the East who was widely believed to exist in the medieval period but who, it is now generally agreed, never existed. The name 'Prester John' has no bearer. Yet the historian uses the name meaningfully in sentences such as 'Prester John was a figure of considerable importance in medieval European imagination.' The first condition is not met, since there is no bearer. Nevertheless, it appears that the name is meaningful: the historian's audience understands her sentences, can evaluate their truth, and can engage in substantive discussion about Prester John. The case suggests that having a bearer is not necessary for meaningfulness.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires that the name have a bearer; names of fictional or legendary entities may be meaningful even though they designate no actual object.
passage: It might be proposed that a name is meaningful just in case it has a bearer, the speaker is acquainted with that bearer, and the name functions as a rigid designator across possible situations. These conditions may seem natural enough. Names are, on this view, meaningful because they stand for objects, and the speaker's acquaintance ensures a genuine cognitive connection. Rigidity is added to distinguish names from definite descriptions. Yet we should hesitate to accept the first condition as necessary. Let us consider a historian who discusses Prester John, the legendary Christian king said to rule a vast empire in the East. Medieval Europeans wrote of him extensively, sent embassies in search of his kingdom, and shaped their foreign policy in part around belief in his existence. It is now generally acknowledged that Prester John never existed. The name has no bearer. Yet the historian uses the name meaningfully. She asserts, for instance, that Prester John was a figure of considerable importance in medieval European thought, and her audience understands the assertion without difficulty. They can assess its truth, raise objections, and propose qualifications. The name is not empty of meaning merely because it lacks a bearer. The first condition of the analysis is not satisfied, yet meaningfulness is plainly present. We are led to conclude that the analysis is too restrictive in requiring that a meaningful name have a bearer.

definition: A translation of a sentence from one language into another is adequate if and only if the translated sentence preserves the truth-conditions of the original, the translated sentence is grammatical in the target language, and the translation preserves the illocutionary force of the original.
conditions: The translated sentence preserves the truth-conditions of the original
The translated sentence is grammatical in the target language
The translation preserves the illocutionary force of the original
counterexample: Suppose one translates the French sentence 'Il pleut' into the English 'It is raining.' The truth-conditions are preserved; the English sentence is grammatical; and the illocutionary force — that of assertion — is maintained. All three conditions are met. Yet the French sentence carries a certain register and brevity that the English translation, while adequate by these standards, fails to convey. If the original occurs in a literary context where its clipped, impersonal character is essential to the passage's effect, one may question whether the translation is truly adequate despite satisfying all three conditions. The analysis does not account for stylistic, connotative, or pragmatic dimensions beyond illocutionary force.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require preservation of stylistic, connotative, or broader pragmatic features that may be essential to the adequacy of a translation, particularly in literary or rhetorical contexts.
passage: One might analyse the adequacy of a translation by requiring that it preserve truth-conditions, be grammatical in the target language, and maintain the illocutionary force of the original sentence. These conditions appear to capture the essential features of a satisfactory translation. If the translated sentence is true under exactly the same circumstances as the original, is well-formed in the target language, and performs the same speech act — whether assertion, question, or command — then it seems that nothing of importance has been lost. Yet one might construct a case which reveals that these conditions, though perhaps necessary, are not sufficient. Suppose one translates the French sentence 'Il pleut' into the English 'It is raining.' The truth-conditions are preserved; the translated sentence is grammatical; and the illocutionary force — an assertion concerning the weather — is maintained. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet if the original sentence occurs within a literary passage whose effect depends upon its clipped, impersonal character — a terse observation placed at the opening of a novel, let us say — then the adequacy of the translation may be questioned. The English sentence, though correct by the standards of the analysis, may fail to reproduce the stylistic register, the connotative resonance, or the rhythmic quality of the original. One is led to conclude that the analysis is silent on dimensions of linguistic meaning — style, connotation, tone — that bear directly upon translational adequacy, and that it is therefore insufficient.

definition: An utterance is metaphorical if and only if the speaker says something she does not literally believe, the utterance invites the hearer to perceive a similarity between two unlike things, and the intended meaning diverges from the literal meaning.
conditions: The speaker says something she does not literally believe
The utterance invites the hearer to perceive a similarity between two unlike things
The intended meaning diverges from the literal meaning
counterexample: Suppose a poet writes 'No man is an island.' The poet does not literally believe that anyone has asserted men to be islands, yet the first condition seems satisfied — she says something she does not literally believe. The utterance invites the hearer to perceive a similarity, or rather a significant dissimilarity, between a human being and an island: the point is that human beings, unlike islands, are not self-sufficient or isolated. The intended meaning diverges from the literal. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the utterance functions at least partly through the denial of a comparison rather than the assertion of one, and the hearer is invited to grasp a relation of dependence and interconnection that goes beyond mere similarity. The analysis, by focusing on the perception of similarity, fails to account for metaphors that operate through negation, irony, or the highlighting of disanalogy.
missing_condition: The definition fails to accommodate metaphors that work through negation, contrast, or the highlighting of disanalogy rather than through the perception of similarity.
passage: A familiar analysis of metaphor holds that an utterance is metaphorical when the speaker says something she does not literally believe, when the utterance invites the hearer to perceive a similarity between two unlike things, and when the intended meaning diverges from the literal meaning. These conditions appear to capture many standard cases. When one says that a person is a fox, one does not literally believe the person to be a fox, one invites the hearer to perceive a similarity (cunning, perhaps), and the intended meaning plainly diverges from the literal. Yet we should hesitate to accept the second condition as it stands. Suppose a poet writes the line 'No man is an island.' The poet does not literally believe that the question of whether men are islands is in genuine dispute, and the intended meaning diverges from the literal. The first and third conditions are satisfied. But what of the second? The utterance does not straightforwardly invite the hearer to perceive a similarity between a man and an island; rather, it invites the hearer to grasp, through the denial of a comparison, a truth about human interdependence and the impossibility of complete self-sufficiency. The metaphor operates through negation and contrast rather than through the assertion of similarity. The analysis, in requiring the perception of similarity, fails to accommodate this significant class of metaphorical utterances and is therefore shown to be insufficient.

definition: An utterance constitutes a promise if and only if the speaker expresses an intention to perform a future action, the hearer understands this expression as placing the speaker under an obligation, and the speaker sincerely intends to carry out the action.
conditions: The speaker expresses an intention to perform a future action
The hearer understands this expression as placing the speaker under an obligation
The speaker sincerely intends to carry out the action
counterexample: Consider a case in which a hostage, under duress, tells her captor 'I promise I will bring you the money tomorrow.' She expresses an intention to perform a future action. The captor understands this as placing her under an obligation and expects compliance. She sincerely intends, at the moment of speaking, to bring the money, out of fear for her life. All three conditions are met. Yet one may question whether this utterance constitutes a genuine promise, for the expression of intention and the sincerity of the speaker are products of coercion rather than of free deliberation. A promise extracted by force seems defective in a way that the analysis fails to capture.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the speaker's expression of intention be made freely and without coercion, thereby counting as genuine promises utterances that are extracted by duress.
passage: It is natural to propose that an utterance constitutes a promise when the speaker expresses an intention to perform a future action, the hearer understands this as placing the speaker under an obligation, and the speaker sincerely intends to carry out the action. These conditions appear to capture the central features of promising. A friend says she will return a borrowed book, the hearer understands this as a commitment, and the friend intends to follow through. The case is a paradigm of promising. Yet one might construct a case which reveals the insufficiency of this analysis. Suppose a hostage, under grave duress, tells her captor that she will bring him a sum of money on the following day. She expresses an intention to perform a future action. The captor understands the expression as placing her under an obligation and fully expects her to comply. She does, at the moment of speaking, sincerely intend to do as she says, for she fears the consequences of failure. All three conditions are met. Yet it appears doubtful that this utterance constitutes a genuine promise. The intention is formed under coercion; the obligation, if one may call it that, is imposed by force rather than freely undertaken. A promise, one might argue, requires that the speaker's commitment be voluntary — that it arise from an act of free deliberation rather than from the compulsion of threats. The case thus reveals that the analysis is insufficient, lacking as it does a condition of voluntariness or freedom from duress.

definition: A predicate is vague if and only if it admits of borderline cases, it is susceptible to sorites reasoning, and competent speakers disagree about its application in some cases.
conditions: It admits of borderline cases
It is susceptible to sorites reasoning
Competent speakers disagree about its application in some cases
counterexample: Suppose one considers the predicate 'is a prime number.' This predicate does not admit of borderline cases in the usual sense — every natural number either is or is not prime, with no indeterminacy. It is not susceptible to sorites reasoning. And competent speakers do not disagree about its application, provided they are mathematically informed. None of the three conditions is satisfied. Now consider, by contrast, a predicate such as 'is a small prime number.' This predicate admits of borderline cases (is 37 a small prime?) and competent speakers may disagree. But it is not obviously susceptible to sorites reasoning in the standard sense, for removing one from a prime does not yield a prime — the sorites series is disrupted by the discreteness and irregularity of the primes. The second condition is not met, yet the predicate appears genuinely vague.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires susceptibility to sorites reasoning; some predicates are genuinely vague, admitting of borderline cases and speaker disagreement, without being susceptible to sorites reasoning owing to the discrete or irregular structure of their domain of application.
passage: One might propose that a predicate is vague just in case it admits of borderline cases, is susceptible to sorites reasoning, and occasions disagreement among competent speakers regarding its application. The first condition — the existence of borderline cases — is widely regarded as the central mark of vagueness. The second ties vagueness to the sorites paradox, that ancient puzzle in which the successive removal of small increments appears to transform a clear case into a clear non-case without any identifiable boundary. The third recognises the practical manifestation of vagueness in linguistic behaviour. Yet we should hesitate to require all three conditions jointly. Consider the predicate 'is a small prime number.' This predicate appears genuinely vague: there are borderline cases — one may wonder whether thirty-seven or forty-one qualifies — and competent speakers disagree about its extension. The first and third conditions are met. But the predicate resists sorites reasoning in the standard form. One cannot construct the usual sorites series by successive small decrements, for the primes are irregularly distributed and subtracting one from a prime does not, in general, yield another prime. The iterative structure upon which the sorites depends is absent. The second condition is therefore not satisfied, yet vagueness seems plainly present. We are led to conclude that susceptibility to sorites reasoning, while characteristic of many vague predicates, is not a necessary condition of vagueness, and that the analysis is insufficient.

definition: Two sentences have the same meaning if and only if they have the same truth-conditions, they are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value, and competent speakers judge them to be synonymous.
conditions: They have the same truth-conditions
They are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value
Competent speakers judge them to be synonymous
counterexample: Suppose one considers the sentences 'Every vixen is a vixen' and 'Every female fox is a female fox.' These sentences have the same truth-conditions; they are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value; and competent speakers would readily judge them synonymous. All three conditions are met. Now consider instead the sentences 'Water is wet' and 'H2O is wet.' These share truth-conditions and are intersubstitutable in extensional contexts. However, a competent speaker who is ignorant of chemistry may not judge them synonymous, since she may not know that water is H2O. The third condition fails. Yet it seems that these sentences do have the same meaning in a robust sense — they concern the same substance and ascribe the same property to it — and that the speaker's ignorance is a deficiency of information rather than evidence of a difference in meaning.
missing_condition: The definition relies on competent speakers' judgements of synonymy, which may be distorted by ignorance of empirical facts; it fails to distinguish between differences in meaning and differences in speakers' information about extra-linguistic matters.
passage: One might propose that two sentences share the same meaning when they have the same truth-conditions, are intersubstitutable in all extensional contexts without change of truth-value, and are judged synonymous by competent speakers. These conditions appear to impose a stringent test for sameness of meaning, combining semantic, logical, and sociological requirements. Yet we should hesitate to accept the third condition as necessary. Consider the sentences 'Water is wet' and 'H2O is wet.' They have the same truth-conditions, for water is H2O. They are intersubstitutable in extensional contexts without alteration of truth-value. The first two conditions are satisfied. But a competent speaker who is ignorant of chemistry — who does not know that water is H2O — may decline to judge the sentences synonymous. She may insist that 'water' and 'H2O' mean different things, since as far as she is aware they might refer to different substances. The third condition is not met. Yet it appears that the sentences do share the same meaning in a perfectly good sense. They concern the same substance and attribute the same property to it. The speaker's reluctance to judge them synonymous reflects a deficiency of empirical information, not a genuine difference in meaning. We are led to conclude that the analysis is too restrictive in deferring to speakers' judgements, which may be distorted by ignorance of extra-linguistic facts.

definition: A definite description refers to an object if and only if there exists exactly one object satisfying the description, the speaker uses the description with the intention of picking out that object, and the description is the grammatical subject of a declarative sentence.
conditions: There exists exactly one object satisfying the description
The speaker uses the description with the intention of picking out that object
The description is the grammatical subject of a declarative sentence
counterexample: Consider a case in which a speaker says 'I gave the book to the author of Waverley.' The definite description 'the author of Waverley' refers to Walter Scott; there exists exactly one person satisfying the description, and the speaker intends to pick out that person. Yet the description is not the grammatical subject of the sentence — it is the indirect object. The third condition is not met. Nevertheless, the description plainly refers. The grammatical role of the expression is irrelevant to whether it refers.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously restricts referring uses of definite descriptions to those occurring as grammatical subjects; descriptions in other grammatical positions — object, complement, or oblique case — may refer equally well.
passage: It might be held that a definite description refers to an object when there exists exactly one object satisfying it, when the speaker intends to pick out that object, and when the description occupies the position of grammatical subject in a declarative sentence. The first two conditions are familiar from standard accounts of definite descriptions. The third may seem an innocuous syntactic requirement, ensuring that the description plays a central role in the sentence. Yet the restriction is plainly too narrow. Suppose that a speaker says 'I gave the book to the author of Waverley.' The description 'the author of Waverley' satisfies the first two conditions: there is exactly one person who authored that novel, and the speaker intends to refer to him. But the description is not the grammatical subject of the sentence — it occupies the position of indirect object. The third condition fails. Yet the description refers without difficulty. The speaker successfully picks out Walter Scott by means of the description, and her audience understands whom she means. We are led to conclude that the requirement that the description serve as grammatical subject is an arbitrary syntactic condition that bears no genuine relation to the capacity of a description to refer, and that the analysis must be revised to remove it.

definition: A name designates rigidly if and only if it picks out the same individual in every possible situation in which that individual exists, it was introduced via a definite description that uniquely identifies the individual, and its reference is fixed at the moment of introduction and does not shift with subsequent uses.
conditions: It picks out the same individual in every possible situation in which that individual exists
It was introduced via a definite description that uniquely identifies the individual
Its reference is fixed at the moment of introduction and does not shift with subsequent uses
counterexample: Suppose a child is named 'Margaret' by her parents at birth, not by means of any definite description but simply by a demonstrative act — 'Let us call this child Margaret.' There is no definite description that uniquely identifies the child at the moment of naming; the parents simply bestow the name upon the individual before them. The name picks out the same individual in every possible situation in which she exists, and its reference does not shift over time. The first and third conditions are satisfied, but the second is not. Yet the name designates rigidly by any standard account. The requirement that a rigid designator be introduced via a definite description is too strong.
missing_condition: The definition erroneously requires introduction via a definite description; rigid designation may be achieved by ostension, demonstrative acts, or other non-descriptive means of fixing reference.
passage: An analysis of rigid designation might proceed by requiring that a name pick out the same individual in every possible situation in which that individual exists, that it be introduced by means of a definite description uniquely identifying the individual, and that its reference be fixed at the moment of introduction and remain stable thereafter. The first and third conditions capture what seems essential to rigidity: the constancy of reference across possible situations and across time. The second condition is perhaps motivated by the thought that there must be some definite criterion by which the referent is initially identified, and that a uniquely identifying description provides the most natural such criterion. Yet we should hesitate to accept this requirement. Suppose that a child is named 'Margaret' at birth. The parents do not employ any definite description. They do not say 'Let us name the only child born in this room at this time.' They simply gesture toward the infant and declare 'Let us call her Margaret.' The name is introduced by a demonstrative act, not by a description. No definite description that uniquely identifies the child need be in the parents' minds at all. Yet the name picks out the same individual in every possible situation in which she exists, and its reference does not shift. The first and third conditions are met. The name designates rigidly. We are led to conclude that the second condition — introduction via a definite description — is not necessary for rigid designation and that the analysis must be broadened to accommodate non-descriptive means of reference-fixing.
